It had to be singer/songwriter.”57 While there are still many electronic soundscapes and beats present on the album, she is steadfast in the directness of writing and singing about the most powerful human emotions: love, loss, and grief. It could be considered ironic, but I see it as a bit of a perfect balance, that out of this album came her first foray into the world of virtual reality, the technology of the future. The year of Vulnicura’s release saw the proliferation of virtual and augmented reality technologies, as with the advent of face filters on social media. In 2016, following the release of Vulnicura came Björk Digital, a traveling exhibition that allowed visitors to take a journey through music videos from the album, which Björk and her collaborators had adapted into completely immersive virtual reality pieces. Utilizing multi-channel video installation and VR headsets, viewers were immersed in the digital world she had created in 360 degrees, creating transportative audio visual experiences. I had the privilege of being in Barcelona in 2017 when the exhibition was on view at the Centre de Cultura Contemporània de Barcelona (CCCB) and it was honestly a life-changing experience. It was the first time I had been able to engage with VR technology, the headsets and the immersive-ness of it all and it completely blew me away. I was already such a fan of Björk and the Vulnicura album, and the experience elevated it to a completely new level. I was seeing things I had never seen before. Technology utilized in a way that was elevating the human experience of heartbreak. This was the chance to speculate what that pain feels like outside the realm of our own physical reality. She presented this album like a wound, referencing the Latin of its title, digitally imposed on her latex bodysuit on the album’s cover image. What is a wound if not a void? What is heartbreak if not a void left by the loss of love? Björk and her collaborators offered viewers both a masking of the void, and an exploration of the void itself through these masterful digital artifacts. As described by the CCCB summary of the exhibition, one of the pieces Notget VR “presents Björk as a digital moth giantess transformed by stunning masks created by artist James Merry.”58 After seeing the pieces in Björk Digital, I began closely following the collaboration between Björk and the artist James Merry, who had begun crafting incredible masks for Björk’s live performances and subsequent album art. Merry’s physical masks are unique combinations of 3D printed material, silicone, metal, beads, and more. He also creates masks, or versions of the physical masks, in digital space, to be used as face filters, or within the VR video experiences as described above. In an interview with the magazine Current Obsession, Merry lets us in on his process and inspiration. In detailing the origins of the masks and working with Björk, he describes how Björk utilized them during the Vulnicura tour, where performing such vulnerable songs to millions of people was really aided by her ability to be shielded in some way by a mask. The function of this mask as ornament, beyond being an extension of the album’s creative universe, was actually one of psychological utility for Björk. Merry then describes: I think it has grown since then into something else—a physical expression of an emotional state, revealing and concealing in equal measure… But the idea of the avatar, of playing with outward-facing identity, is something Björk has explored throughout her career—from the Hunter video, to numerous photoshoots, to the All is Full of Love robots, to the recent VR videos…So I see our masks more as a continuation of that new.59 thread, rather than something totally The visual narrative of these masks are so rich, in addition to their stunning aesthetic offering. In the same Current Obsession interview, Merry says, “I think it would also be foolish to ignore just too.”60 the straightforward aesthetic aspect too: sometimes it can just look cool, and that’s enough This concept would make Adolf Loos or J.A.R. Stevenson turn over in their graves—ornament just for the sake of it? A straightforward aesthetic goal? Yes: Sometimes the aesthetic and the beauty and the visual pleasure of ornament and adornment is exactly its function. It’s not so blasphemous anymore. I think in the current state, where aesthetic material is thrust at us from every direction, in real life and in digital space, we cannot always take the time to question or investigate deeper design narratives. Most people don’t. They look at something, they think, “That looks cool,” and move on. And in many cases, that is entirely sufficient. It’s a refreshing expression of honesty from Merry, whose masks are often precisely representative of layered symbolic imagery and embedded with cultural messages. His acknowledgement of the creative importance, as well as the aesthetic cool-ness of something is, I think, very futuristic. It’s the acceptance of two truths, not mutually exclusive, which continues to shape our interpretations of art and design and the rhetorical goals behind them. I was inspired by Merry’s practice to create my own filters, taking on new methods of 2D illustration into 3D modeling, then applied to augmented reality software to create the filters. This project was in pursuit of my investigation into embodied ornament, of what I consider the present and future state of ornament. Ornament embodied is the expression of personal and cultural identity. It is the opportunity to mask the void of the “pulsing unknown,” or fill it, or destroy it, and grapple with the notion of any true representation of self through visual material.

author: Abby Lee
thesis_title: Space, Self, Future
paragraph: Ornament is far more than decoration; it is a dynamic and deeply rooted design and artistic practice that reflects human history, values, and creativity. By revisiting its origins and tracing its evolution, this thesis has demonstrated how ornament’s function has shifted over time, often aligning with social prerogatives and cultural narratives. From its utilitarian beginnings to its dismissal by Modernist ideals, ornament has been shaped by the forces of industrialization, capitalism, and cultural critique. The enduring legacy of ornament lies in its adaptability and range of rhetorical expression. Whether through the intricate metalwork of historical architecture or the expressive digital designs of contemporary artists, ornament continues to serve as a visual language that communicates the humanity of its artisans. Despite efforts to marginalize it by those who shun excess to, ornament remains a vital element of design, capable of bridging past traditions with future innovations. In challenging narrow interpretations of function, this thesis reclaims ornament as a purposeful and meaningful design element. Beyond utilitarian constraints, ornament embodies function in a myriad of ways that contribute to the richness of human experience, and our interaction with art and design. Its layered narratives and symbolic power encourage deeper engagement with material culture, allowing us to interpret and reinterpret the objects in our world. As we navigate the complexities of late-stage capitalism’s digital and material hyperconsumption, ornament offers us a tool for orienting ourselves at the intersections of design, culture, and function. Through this analysis, we can understand the unfixed nature of concepts like function and value. By embracing its potential, we can move toward a more nuanced understanding of ornament—not as superfluous, but as an essential expression of human creativity and cultural resonance. Beyond this written thesis, there is so much design and creative work in process for me that will continue to develop. I hope that, by reimagining the category of “ornament” and engaging with my concept of ornament as communication, I can create work that crosses material and social boundaries. Through the development of this thesis, historical design and visual research have become critical elements of my methodology across many forms of design.

author: Amanda Pyun
thesis_title: Play more!
paragraph: by Amanda Pyun Abstract In this thesis, I explore theories and subtypes of play through interdisciplinary research in psychology, child development, theater, and art. I examine how capitalist, social, and cultural structures inhibit play while identifying conditions that foster it. Additionally, I analyze works by various artists, including myself, who embody a playful attitude, offering insights on how to cultivate playfulness in our lives and empower others to do the same. Introduction According to the Oxford English Dictionary, the verb “to play” is to “engage in activity for enjoyment and recreation rather than a serious or practical purpose.” The same dictionary defines the noun “play” as ‘activity engaged in for enjoyment and recreation, especially by children.’ Why is that? Adults have more responsibilities, but why can't play be as fundamental for them as it is for kids? When asked about my life goal, I say it’s to find my people and have fun with them; they are individuals like me, who also value the joy of playing. I’ve always loved playing—alone, with friends, with animals, through video games, pranks, singing, dancing and more. Yet, growing up, my mom told me I should work twice as much as I relax. My mom, who enjoyed knitting and reading, disapproved of my preference for video games and texting. My Korean-American upbringing reinforced that work should be the main event of life. Job applications expected me to view work as my dream rather than a means to enjoy what I love. The 40+ hour work week in Western society embraces this, while some countries like week.1 Iceland, adopt a four-day work On the other hand, in South Korea, (where my mom is from) the government wanted to raise the work week cap from 52 hours a week to 69 hours a weekends.2 week, including Shouldn’t individuals be able to shape their lives to maximize happiness while maintaining productivity?

author: Amanda Pyun
thesis_title: Play more!
paragraph: 1Wilkins, Brett, "How's Iceland's 4-Day Work Week Working?", https://www.commondreams.org/news/iceland-4-day-workweek 2Chen, Heather et al. “South Korea's Controversial Proposal to Extend the Work Week to 69 Hours Sparks Outcry,” CNN, https://www.cnn.com/2023/03/18/asia/south-korea-longer-work-week-debate-intl-hnk/index.html As I neared entering the workforce, I feared losing my freedom to play. Play is often treated as a reward rather than a necessity. But in reality, it enhances well-being, mental health and value.3 relationships. Play doesn’t need to be productive in a capitalist sense—it has intrinsic The real-world stakes of neglecting play as an important part of life relate to the quality of life of play-deprived young adults. We as individuals have the agency to assign purpose to our lives, as we don't inherently have a purpose, so why can’t some choose to focus on that as a life goal like some choose to focus on their careers? This thesis explores play as more than recreation. It’s a vital, lifelong source of joy, creativity and self-connection. I will examine theories of play, societal structures that restrict it and artists who embrace play as resistance.

author: Amanda Pyun
thesis_title: Play more!
paragraph: Chapter 1: What does play mean to philosophers and artists? "Go play!"—a phrase commonly said in childhood but when was the last time you heard this in adulthood? Play takes many forms: social, spontaneous, structured, unstructured, curious, purposeful. Scholars like Peter K. Smith defines play as voluntary, flexible and done for its own sake.4 culture.5 Others like Johan Huizinga, argue that play is central to Scott G. Eberle highlights the core qualities of play: purposeless, voluntary, fun and outside the ordinary. Meanwhile, Stuart Brown challenges the idea that play is not purposeless because it’s key to our species' success. He argues that play helps us succeed because it stimulates our development.⁵ creativity and furthers our What I take away from the words of these philosophers is that play is an important methodology for nurturing our curiosity, zest for life and imagination. It’s essential to take the time to do those things because it fulfills us even if it doesn’t seem that productive to some people. Designers also see play as essential. A designer who goes by the name of Tomos describes how playfulness fosters creativity. Thespian and creative consultant Gary Izzo calls interactive imagination.6 theater “the art of play,” likening it to childhood That insight resonates with my childhood. As a child, I imagined worlds with friends by storytelling, setting rules and 3Robinson, Lauren et al. "The Benefits of Play for Adults," Helpguide, https://www.helpguide.org/mental-health/wellbeing/benefits-of-play-for-adults 4“Play.” edited by Peter K. Smith. Encyclopedia on Early Childhood Development. https://www.child-encyclopedia.com/play 5Tomos. “Design Play” Medium. https://medium.com/@tomos.design/design-play-e7e90ce895e5 6Izzo, G. (1997). The Art of Play: The New Genre of Interactive Theatre. Heinemann embodying characters. Looking back, play was a collaborative, theatrical act. This ties into theater’s role in my life as an essential source of my emotional expression; through singing and performing, I found an outlet for feelings I struggled to verbalize. Play is present during all of that creative self-discovery, which is what makes it so important–and fun! Overall, play isn’t just stimulating for our individual creativity, growth and happiness; it also helps us explore all of these things with other people. Socializing and collaborating add to the fulfilling nature of play. This is why play is so important to me and why I want to encourage others to value play as something we not just want but something we need. For the purpose of my thesis, I define play as any activity that brings you joy in the present and in the long run for the sake of enjoyment rather than for any external practical goal. The main (and sometimes only) goal of play is to make you happy. It can be spontaneous, creative, restorative, silly, intentional, social and deeply personal. Play is an equally fundamental part of a fulfilling life at any age.

author: Amanda Pyun
thesis_title: Play more!
paragraph: Chapter 2: Restrictions on Play in Adulthood Work, societal expectations and stigma often push play to the sidelines. Many believe free time must be earned through productivity. Whenever I watch a show, take a nap, text friends, or play video games, I’m often told (by my mom or other authority figures) that I’m wasting time and I should do something useful instead; for example, read a book or study because that is what will get me a job and thus, earn money and approval. I know those are great things to work toward, but I also highly value my leisurely activities because they are my way of playing. They allow me to relax, recharge and connect with others, and in doing so, they make me happy. In my experience, society prioritizes measurable productivity, favoring things like profit, social status, or fitness goals over leisurely hobbies like playing video games or lounging. Gaming is a great type of play that illustrates how joy can be underrated. For example, I conducted a study on seven of my friends who, like me, are in their 20s and are avid video gamers. First, I gathered information on how much they game and how they feel about the experience. The majority of people expressed that they enjoy the team-building and social aspects of multiplayer gaming with their friends, with that being one of their favorite ways to blow off steam (same as me). Despite participants of the study reporting putting hundreds, even thousands of hours into games, they felt like they needed to game less because it “isn’t productive,” as John, one of the participants phrased it. John wasn’t the only one who felt this way. Five out of seven participants expressed that they felt torn because gaming brought them closer to their friends, but they also had many other commitments like work and school that they felt needed to prioritize, which led to gaming as little as possible, even at the cost of affecting friendships and leisure. I couldn’t help but feel that the participants’ sentiments signified that they didn’t find much value in the joy they got from playing. Surprisingly, video games have a lot of hidden benefits, even quantitative ones. “One long-term study published in 2013 showed that children who played strategy-based games showed an improvement in problem-solving skills, and thus, year.”⁹ they tended to get better grades—the next school Although this is a great unexpected outcome of gaming, I believe having fun is valuable by itself. To my point, studies have shown that some video games can boost mood and make for better heart rhythms—a sign that they stress.7 may also help relieve In my opinion, qualitative value—joy, spontaneity, exploration—matters just as much as quantitative output like money or practical skill-building. Stress relief shouldn’t only be a tool for getting back to work, it should also be the end goal in and of itself simply because it makes space for joy. The tension between play and productivity reflects a cultural mindset that undervalues joy unless it generates prestige and capital. But I’d like to turn that mindset on its head and propose that work, school and practicality distract us from playing just for the sake of enjoying ourselves.

author: Amanda Pyun
thesis_title: Play more!
paragraph: Chapter 3: Resistance Through Art and Play Let’s look into some amazing artists who use play to challenge societal norms. Pilvi Takala’s Trainee8 The is a covert performance art mission, the premise of which disrupted corporate expectations by doing nothing productive at the prestigious financial company Deloitte, prompting discomfort among employees. Takala would spend all day in the elevator or stare blankly at the computer screen. She would socialize with coworkers and lounge in the break

thesis_title: Play more!
paragraph: 7“9 Reasons to Give Video Games a Try,” GEICO Living, https://living.geico.com/home/technology/9-reasons-to-give-video-games-a-try/ Takala, Pilvi. “The Trainee” https://pilvitakala.com/the-trainee room, totally vexing employees. She even got scary corporate emails addressing her unusual behavior:

author: Amanda Pyun
thesis_title: Play more!
paragraph: You can feel the anger of the email sender. It’s understandable; Takala’s actions could be interpreted as making a mockery of the Deloitte work culture. It’s almost as if Takala were playing office, like a child pretending to be a doctor or chef. She took this form of play that is usually reserved for children and performed it in an adult environment. The dissonance she caused was her way of resisting the seriousness of Deloitte. Takala’s stunt disrupted people from their productivity, which is a sin in grind-culture. Similarly, during the 2017 solar eclipse, millions stepped away from work to witness a rare celestial event, momentarily prioritizing wonder over productivity. The eclipse lost US productivity.9 While this monetary employers approximately $700 million due to the pause on loss was enormous, Senior Contributor to Forbes, Jack Kelly, brings forth many points for why it wasn't really a loss at all. He mentions how it was good for “long-term morale,” “employee engagement” and “social connection.” But, I also want to emphasize my belief that the once-in-a-lifetime eclipse didn’t need to be economically beneficial in order to be worth skipping a few hours of work for. Work will always be there, but this eclipse only comes every 20 years.

author: Amanda Pyun
thesis_title: Play more!
paragraph: 9Kelly, Jack, "How Lost Productivity During the Total Solar Eclipse Is Actually a Win for Employers," Forbes, 9 April 2024 Things that break up the day-to-day routine, like an eclipse, make the world a little brighter. prank10—a Artist Sunday Nobody’s sculptural dumpster secretly containing a whimsical diorama of raccoons in trench coats at an art museum—transforms the mundane into the playful. His work reminds us that play can emerge unexpectedly, breaking the monotony of daily life in a hilariously refreshing and unexpected way.

author: Amanda Pyun
thesis_title: Play more!
paragraph: Hidden public sculpture displayed for 24 hours by self-proclaimed “meme artist” Sunday Nobody. Photo source: @sunday.nobody.art on Instagram.

author: Amanda Pyun
thesis_title: Play more!
paragraph: Chapter 4: My Design Process I myself brought the practice of breaking up the ordinary into the classroom as part of my Thesis workshop. I conducted an activity inspired by theater bonding and warm-up exercises. These exercises are done in rehearsals to bring the cast closer together while exercising creativity. In a class of about twelve people (including the professor), here were the guidelines for the exercise: 10Nobody, Sunday. Instagram, instagram.com/sunday.nobody.art.com Step 1. Stand in a circle facing inwards. Make sure you have enough elbow room to move around. Step 2. Player one will mime any physical motion/action. Step 3. Player two (the person to the right of Player One) will ask, “Are you ___?” Fill in the blank with something completely different than what it looks like Player One is doing. (Don’t take time to think, say or mime whatever comes to mind!) Step 4. Player One, say “yes” no matter what Player Two asks, but do not change your miming. Step 5. Player Two will mime the action that they said Player One was doing. Step 6. Repeat with the next person to the right. Some key takeaways I observed from the workshop and feedback I received from the participants were that there were many awkward laughs and smiles due to the game’s silly and improvisational nature. Having the participants stand and anticipate their turn to mime/improvise engaged them. Their After the first round, participants were noticeably more relaxed and amused. Some reported that it was an interesting experience because it was unlike anything we usually do in class normally. Most agreed that it gave them nostalgia for primary school games that stimulated their imagination and thinking skills. They enjoyed the interactive and absurd elements because it woke them up in the late-night class, which is more than I could have hoped for with this exercise!

author: Amanda Pyun
thesis_title: Play more!
paragraph: Next, I designed a series of sculptures made with fabric and miscellaneous items that I displayed in the Pratt Graduate Communications Design department’s Graduate Inquiry Exhibition. The key to these pieces was in the making process. I aimed to create without forethought or figuring out the meaning of the materials. I gathered materials I had on hand, like old clothes, safety pins, thread, rhinestones, and googly eyes. I didn’t think too hard about what materials I was choosing, what they meant, or what I was going to do with them. I tackled the making process similar to how I remember crafting as a child: without a plan. I channeled a playful and carefree energy as I draped the fabric, decided what materials went with which piece, and arranged them together. The result was a generative exploration of what it means to play, free from inhibitions, planning, and order. Through this process, I discovered that it was harder for me to let go and create without thinking too hard than it was for me to logically plan out a design. I ended up with zany pieces that I discovered meaning in after they were finished. This process also allowed my peers to interpret the pieces in their own ways and reveal their experiences and subconscious thoughts. That by itself was a great discovery of how play brings people together to understand how play is not only an act of leisure but one that nurtures creativity, whether collaboratively or individually.

author: Amanda Pyun
thesis_title: Play more!
paragraph: For my final thesis design, I created an unconventional deck of cards that draws inspiration from my work throughout the thesis process, either by repurposing pieces or incorporating their influence into the card designs. Instead of a bound book, I made unbound cards that have an image on one side and text on the other. Because it is unbound, the cards don’t have an inherent order or even a front or back. The freedom of the form captures how liberating play is. The deck of cards itself is a nod to gameplay. I divided the deck into chapters. In one chapter, there are scanned images of my fabric sculptures with lines from a poem I wrote about the theme of play written on the reverse. In another chapter, there are images of destroyed dog toys with catchphrases on the reverse. Another chapter–my favorite–features scans of mini zines I made with playful prompts on the reverse for the reader to try at their leisure. Readers can also write in their own prompts for future readers to choose from. These cards live inside of a box that invites people to engage with them in a fun and explorative way either individually or collaboratively. The cards I designed invite people to break up their mundane routine with the contemplative silliness they’ll find within the deck. It provides a hands-on experience of how play can be generative and creative but at the same time, helps people recognize its stand-alone value as something that promotes joy.

author: Amanda Pyun
thesis_title: Play more!
paragraph: Conclusion Play is everywhere if we choose to embrace it. It’s not just a reward or a tool for productivity—it’s a fundamental part of a fulfilling life that is just as important as work, if not more. If being fun-focused isn’t your thing, that’s okay too. You should live your life how you want to, whether that means prioritizing work, prioritizing play, or equally valuing both. We may work to survive, but we play to live! My improv workshop can be turned into a club that uses similar exercises to keep people in touch with their relationship with play. The deck of cards I designed promotes whimsy and inspires people to celebrate that. Creating this deck taught me unobvious ways to express playfulness. This deck also showed me that people light up when they experience zany exercises or visuals, showing that almost everyone needs that reminder that life isn’t so serious. With this knowledge, I can continue to design to bring out people’s inner child, empower their goofy side, and reinvigorate their zest for life. For example, I could publish visual self-help books, a mascot-led social media series, or a playful installation set in an environment that disrupts the daily routine of adults (like a professional area) that brings people joy, all while sending the same message as my thesis. When was the last time you truly played? If you can’t remember, it’s definitely time to start. So see the eclipse. Find raccoons in the dumpster. Make a mess. Go, play!
