author: Janhvi Trivedi
thesis_title: Visualizing Eternity
paragraph: Do you feel time moves fast? Don’t you think 24 hours is not enough? Like I need the day to be 36 hours long? Or maybe 40 on a good day? That sounds about right. Right?! It has been more than a year since I came to New York and this one year feels like a blink of an eye. A lot has happened in the last year, it’s almost like I am a new person, maybe not entirely, but in part. The interpretation, imagination, and perception of time are deeply intertwined with my experiences. In my journey, I've often challenged conventional notions of time, engaging with alternative perspectives beyond linear thinking. My imagination frequently transcends the typical flow of time, blending past, present, and future into a cohesive narrative of my life experiences. This interplay between my interpretation of time and my perception of events has given rise to profound and thought-provoking realizations about my existence. My understanding of time has been shaped by my ideology of “How does this make me feel?”. At times, I perceive time as an object, other times it’s just a feeling, a feeling I am trying to find words for you to understand and relate to what I talk about. These diverse perspectives on time have significantly influenced my sense of feeling things while experiencing and understanding them. By delving into the subjective and experiential aspects of time, I've gained a deeper understanding of my own experiences, offering myself unique and insightful perspectives that transcend the boundaries of scientific inquiry.

author: Janhvi Trivedi
thesis_title: Visualizing Eternity
paragraph: 1. How does the feeling of feeling feel? 2. Does the environment change my perception of time and how I perceive it?

author: Janhvi Trivedi
thesis_title: Visualizing Eternity
paragraph: I’ve always felt a type of way when I think or listen about things that are considered “weird” or out of the norm for the people around me as if it were calling out my name. I knew I was different when I felt at peace when I cried and felt heavy in my heart when I smiled. I could taste my lungs, fight my sight, see my gums, and count my skin. Isn’t that strange? Like it’s a never-ending story, of those strange feelings, It’s known but untold. Time has always been a feeling for me and never the numerical digits. It has always been different experiences that I’vefelt throughout the day that made me feel what time was. The beginning, the middle, the end, where am I stuck? Running from the reality, where am I buzzed? Am I buzzed? Or am I dust? I don’t feel the “feeling”, Am I rust? I've just begun writing. Can I call myself a writer already? Maybe it's too soon! It's incredible to think that it feels like just yesterday when I had no idea I could write. Someone asked me recently, “How do you write?” and I thought about it because I never did before, after 10 minutes I said, “I feel it, I talk to myself about it in my head. I always did that, maybethis is the first time I started sketching my thoughts onto the paper.” And I realized that’s how I function, I need to feel it first and understand it before taking action on it and that’s how I make it better! Like it was just yesterday, Or like it was today, I have it on my mind, but my words stumbled up on me. I know you are there, you know too! Come out and show the world, the magic that you keep a secret. Come out and show the world my point of view. I sing random words in my head, and they form a string of letters. Illusions are the same to me. My thoughts mix the reality and the things I daydream about. It is hard to identify sometimes if what I am thinking/ remembering is real or a product of human cognition. The feeling of feeling like I am deep in the middle of the ocean, and I don’t know how to swim, like a toddler lost in the fish market, like you sleep but not able to wake up but somehow you are floating in the air and can see your body sleeping on your bed from a bird’s eye view! Everything is scary but makes me feel amazed at the same time. Is it me or are my spirits wandering around, flowing with the whispers of winds? It makes sense, all of it. In my head everything is perfect. How do I explain that not wanting to exist is not suicidal? I just want to live with the heavens and the earth’s Not in between them, But with them. I want to be that grain of sand I want to be that mist of air I want to be that star that you see blinking on a night. That’s existing for me. This? For this, I haven’t found any word yet. Every day I wake up in Dark Grey. I move through the dark grey, coming back home by Lemon Yellow. By Dark Yellow, I’m finished with my short nap and get involved with my homework. I’ve perceived time in that sense since I was 6 years old, weird right? I perceive time differently than most people, as for you it’s numbers, minutes, and seconds, but for me, it’s colors and how that moment makes me feel. I had the same day every day for almost 15 years of my life, it felt like nothing was interesting and I hated it. These emotions affected my perception of time in terms of mood and the color that I see and “feel” at that given moment. Imagination and perception of time, for me: Every day for me was the same. I was starting with waking up early in the morning to get ready to leave for school, coming back home and taking a short nap, having high tea, and waiting for dinner, while I studied the whole time in different locations. This was my life for 15 years straight and it felt like nothing was interesting and I hated it. I perceive time differently than most people, as for you it’s numbers, minutes, and seconds, but for me, it’s colours and how that moment makes me feel. These intense emotions as a child affected my perception of time in terms of mood and colors. I have different mental illnesses and I find it extremely funny. Something in me triggered in the Dark Yellow very frequently which made me feel things on extreme levels. This is why I associate time with colors I continuously saw every single day for more than 5 years at the same time because of the way the sun had its rays coming into the room through the curtains. Everything is connected, the way I breathed at that time, the way I moved, I felt, I walked, I talked, and also the way I saw, the most important one of them all. My hyper attention/ awareness of details about myself tells me more stories about myself. It gets a little tense for me when other people are involved in activities with me as it's hard to be particular and be on time for me. Do you want me to be somewhere at 10 am? I will be early or late, definitely late, but never on time. My brain finds it hard to understand the movement of the clock’s hands. If you want me to be somewhere at 10 am I have to rethink and count the time again and again and again every two minutes from the very start to understand that 2 or 3 minutes have passed. Honestly, it's quite hectic. Not just time but alongside it I have to understand the moment, like when I have to travel and reach a certain location I have to recount and analyze the route the same way I analyze time (repetitively). 6 am to 10 am Dark Grey: Dullness, less energy, bad mood 11 am to 2:30 pm Lemon Yellow: Too bright, expected to feel fresh, hungry, sleepy, lazy 2:30 pm to 5:30 pm Dark Yellow: Gloomy, dark, sleepy, foggy brain, stressed, lazy, low mood, lowkey sad, body feels like it is inflating like a ball, surroundings move slowly and I move very fast. 5:30 pm to 7 pm Blue and White: Happy, hungry, a little calmer, love 7 pm to 11 pm White: Happy, love, comfort, sleep time, calm, good heart rhythm It seems like it’s a problem for me to perceive things differently, but the truth is that everyone perceives things slightly differently. How does it feel to read how someone you know or a stranger feels this way to you? One thing this experience has taught me is to be empathic and understanding toward others’ feelings and experiences. It's ok to be different, we all are different, and it wouldn't make sense if we weren’t! Yes, it's hard to explain to people these weird feelings which are personal to me and a little fragile. But are they fragile? Am I strong enough to let this be out in the world but "allow" people to see me differently? I've always experienced that people take me talking about this as a "stupid complaint" or a "rant" and take it in a joking way, I'm never taken seriously, but that's ok, I know it’s hard to understand something which you haven't experienced before. But isn't that the fun part? To be inside someone's bubble of thoughts?It's looking so cool in my head right now, I wish I could show you, wait, maybe I can?!

author: Janhvi Trivedi
thesis_title: Visualizing Eternity
paragraph: I was sitting on a rocking chair, the room was dark. You could hear the crack sound made by the rocking chair. I was old and alone with no one by my side, I had long white hair made in Indian-style braids. I was wearing a white saree with tiny light pink flowers on the border. We wear white sarees after our husbands die. I was watching the show SaReGaMaPa(one of the most popular singing shows in the 2000s in India) on my box TV which was kept on a brown wooden shelf specially made for those box TVs. I to this day remember all the details of that room from the bedroom on the left to the living area to the khatla (traditional wooden bed) on the right of the chair. I saw the final episode while rocking in my chair and my eyes slowly started to close, no I was not sleepy, it was different, and I knew it was time. I remember the feeling of everything closing around me. I remember the feeling of knowing that it was over. I remember those 10 seconds before I realized it was the end for me. Isn’t it strange? I have this very vivid memory that I keep talking about ever since I learned how to speak, but my parents don’t believe me. Memories play a big role in my life. I don’t remember most of my life after the age of 5. Weirdly I remember things from before I was 5, or maybe my brain just made up those memories, not sure. My life has always been a different experience that I constantly compare to everyone else’s around me and hurt myself and my inner child. She was young, brave, shy and scared. She was forced to become an emotionless, angry, and strident only child who protects herself and her family. I remember being the center of attention for always failing in school. Yes, I was that stupid kid who was always in the art class and didn’t know Science, Math, or Social Studies and always failed in school. The constant failing never embarrassed me. It gave me a chance to see things differently than people do, I was always doing art, I will always do and nobody could or can stop me. That’s my strength. I never cared what 14+27 was, cause I never saw those numbers together. The constant comments made me doubt myself into thinking I was lazy and I was not studying enough even to pass, yes I was not studying enough but that was because I couldn’t see those numbers or those big chapters in the history books or those formulas in the chemistry class. I never knew I was facing these issues as it was never normalized. "I experience the flow of time differently from most people." - Lilia As I started growing older, I stopped feeling bad about myself and started to see the duality of reality. I saw how people see the world differently. I saw how people are shaped to see the world differently than others, so some people go in a good direction, some bad. And nobody can control it. I started feeling empathetic towards the bullies, my anger was left behind or buried deep inside. Though somewhere my inner child was still feeling the anger and crying, I comforted her by promising her to make things better and safer for her. The duality is right in front of you, just open your eyes and see it. Every single human being has a different perception of things, as I have of time. That’s the “Duality” I keep talking about. Isn't it? You might see the time as 1 o’clock or 1 pm or 1 in the afternoon, however, you want to say it. But I see Lemon Yellow, I feel the brightness hurting my eyes and hunger growling in my stomach, that’s 1 pm to me.

author: Janhvi Trivedi
thesis_title: Visualizing Eternity
paragraph: We start our day and end it with different sounds around us. From people talking to walking, birds, or even air. I sometimes hear electricity too and it gets under my skin. Do you remember the sounds? How did it move around you? How it echoed? How did it move you away? Was it inspirational? Emotional? Chaotic? How does sound make you feel? It always makes me wonder if people feel sounds the way I do. The making of my proof of concept for this thesis started with me experimenting with different sounds. I mixed existing audio to create different sounds of different genres. I observed how they made me feel. The chaos was real! I had a full-blown panic attack for 3 hours straight, felt like I was high on a gallon of coffee! Not a very pleasant feeling I must say. Sounds are the most triggering thing for me. I cannot bare noise, it makes me angry but on the other hand, I am a very loud person in general. My Asian mom would punctuate me at least 5 times a day and ask me to lower my voice. I don't realize when I might be overly loud for my environment. Back in India, everyone is loud from where I come from. I live on the 11th floor, but if my family would be visiting us you would hear our laughter on the 3rd floor. Sounds play a major role in my life in both negative and positive perspectives. I need the sound of silence around me. I crave silence, unusually like my sanity depends on it. I need my surroundings to be pin-drop silent (the phrase I have heard my whole life). I grew up in one of the fastest-developing cities in India. The chaos was everywhere, people screaming, fighting, gossiping, vendors selling vegetables, people driving 50 kinds of different vehicles and the traffic was just crazy! In between those sounds are my core memories. Climbing Java Plum Tree that was literally in the middle of a busy road and fainting midway while climbing. I have chaotic memories and the sounds around me remind me of those memories that I forgot existed. It makes me sad that I don’t remember most of my life, I wonder all the time how my life was even though I was the one who lived it. I confuse memories and dreams and I cannot identify what happened. Sounds "The butterfly counts not months but moments, and has time enough." - Rabindranath Tagore trigger my memories, and tell me how I felt in that exact moment that occurred on 9th September 2015. Sounds are poetry to me, the poetry that travels with me through the beginning of my time.

author: Janhvi Trivedi
thesis_title: Visualizing Eternity
paragraph: Ever since I was a child I could predict things before they happened. I could tell you if something you’re doing is good for you or not. I would tell people to stay away from certain people. I was always seen as a strong opinionated person about things around her. I am very protective towards my close ones, which most of the time came out as my jealousy or insecurity. On May 17th, 2020, I had a dream, asking me to read Tarot. I am a believer in Astrology and Tarot reading, but always had my inner doubts as to how this works or is even real. But my faith in Lord Krishna always told me to have trust in things I see or don’t. That dream changed my life. It was in the middle of the very first pandemic, no delivery services were available for me to buy the cards and learn to read them. My savior, my dad, printed the cards out at home for me to at least start learning them and placed an order for the cards on Amazon at the same time. The interesting part is, I knew nothing about tarot, didn’t even know what exactly these cards looked like, just had an idea from all the sessions I had with one of the very senior Tarot Readers in the city. I saw them from this close for the very first time. I tried reading them for fun without understanding how they work, and guess what, I knew exactly what the cards wanted to tell me. Like someone was talking to me who was not visible, but they were whispering in my brain telling me the right answers. You know how I know the answers were right? What I read in the cards that day was opposite to what was happening in my life at that point, but within the next 5 hours everything I read happened, out of nowhere, I was surprised, and my parents were too. I kept reading cards since that day. Today, 25 November 2024, I am confident with what I feel and say. I know if I read something it is going to happen. I trust in the power of the invisible, sounds cliche I know, but it is what it is. I use this gift for the people in need. I choose to use this gift for the people who are lost, confused, and need help desperately. I choose to help the good. Now you will be thinking how this is related to what I was talking about the whole time, right? Trust me it is. When I read cards, I visualize the answers. I SEE the “right” answer. From simply talking to doing the most diligent task, I have to visualize it before I can perform it, and it’s not even like I try it, it just happens. Everything I do I’ve already imagined it, not once, not twice, but like a million times. "When she calls you a coward, hit the deck." - Lilia

author: Janhvi Trivedi
thesis_title: Visualizing Eternity
paragraph: What do you see when I say May 8, 2020? I see the calendar of the whole year, in a black infinite room, the calendar letters and numbers are floating in the air in white color. It zooms into the month calendar as soon as I hear the specific date and month. Then it circles the specific date with a red pencil (gives that sketchy effect for the vibez). It has always been like this ever since I remember. Dr. Gregory House says memories are induced by smell (House, S5. Ep15, iykwim ). But is it for you? I think he was right about it. Park Avenue; Voyage, is the perfume that reminds me of my early teenage years. It is one of my favorite perfumes for that very reason. Every time that fragrance wanders around me it forces my mind to play the movie before my eyes. It makes me so happy, it makes me remember and feel what innocence is. Envisioning reality is a part of my life. It structures my existence. Helps me survive the knowing and unknowing.

author: Janhvi Trivedi
thesis_title: Visualizing Eternity
paragraph: Have you walked looking down, stepping on the tile keeping your foot at the exact place you kept on the last tile? Every single time you walk? What about talking to every single person in your life differently and mimicking their personality and speaking style without even noticing, which confuses you into believing you don't have a personality of your own? This also means if someone doesn't like it, it's most likely because they don’t like themselves. What about not being able to remember someone if they are not in front of you for more than 4-5 days? Thank god I still remember who my parents are, maybe because they call me every day. I forgot my roommate that I lived with for the last 15 months when she took a trip back home for a month, and every time I had to talk to her I had to scroll down my WhatsApp and look for her face to see what name was written beside it. In July 2023, right before coming to New York, I decided to go for therapy and give it a try for the 4th time. I knew this lady, a Criminal Psychologist with an experience of 10+ years, invited me to try a session with her. I agreed, and went with my mom to her office, the travel was a 30-minute drive from my place but because of the heavy rains, it took more than an hour for me to reach. I was nervous, I wanted it to work out badly, I was desperate you must say. I went to the office for 20 minutes for the session as I was late for the appointment. The office was on the 6th floor, fully made of glass,I could see the rain falling from the clouds, and I could see the sky getting dark with gray clouds at Lemon Yellow. My heart was beating fast, I could feel the pounding on my outer skin, my eyes, and my ears for some reason. We started to talk, and my habit of deeply analyzing body movements and facial expressions while I talked changed how I started to speak. I usually blabber but the moment my brain analyzes that the opposite person is not interested, or doesn't like what I am saying, or thinks it's not a big deal, or any other thing that my brain analyzes I start to act according to it. The same thing happened in the session, I was asked questions about my childhood, a very sensitive topic for me, I started to talk about it, but I could see a small grin on her face, I could see that she thought it was not a big of situation or maybe not, but that what I could feel at that time. I started looking out of the window to ignore her face, but her words were harsh and cut-throat, I got hurt and felt the feeling of isolation. My eyes started focusing on the 10-story building that I could see which was very far away, suddenly the building fell! High tide waves pushed the building with it! The sky got darker likeit was nighttime, and I started hearing people screaming! The waves were coming closer and closer, I was afraid but couldn't move, my body was stuck on that sofa. I looked at her to confirm what I saw but she was sitting there as if nothing was happening. Her eyes got bigger and the grin was getting creepier. I was not feeling good! I ran out of that place and my mom followed me. The moment I reached the first floor and came out of the building everything was normal! The day became sunny as if the dark clouds were never there. The roads were almost dried out because the rain stopped 20 minutes ago! I realized nothing that happened in those 20 minutes was normal. The session triggered it. I gave therapy one more chance from August to September this year, it made my mental health worse than ever. Seek real help in December 2024 which made it so powerfully calm. I’ve been in therapy for 7 weeks now and I think it is helping my everyday life. I finally found the right therapist for me. After clearly struggling for years. The medicines and the talk therapy are helping me make my understanding of reality simpler little by little. It might take years before I start living “normally” (defining normal in the sense of what others think it is). I am ok and happier. Taste How do you realize if you like something in your mouth? The taste bugs crawl around telling you if you feel the spice or the sourness. It tells you how the food you eat tastes, right? That’s how it’s supposed to be, that the “normal” but at this point you know I am not “normal”. I was 5 years old, riding on a scooter with my dad, I was standing in the front and my dad and I were talking about how he would buy the painting kit on the way back home. I was so excited, and couldn’t wait to go home. I kept looking around at all the people and buildings fantasizing how as soon as I reached home I would be able to play with paints. But something odd started to happen, the moment I would focus on the thing passing by I would feel this weird taste in my mouth. Does that sound real? No right! I understand that sounds absurd but trust me when I say I taste everything. The sand, the concrete bricks on the building, the ground of the playground, the seats of the bicycle, the railings of the bridge, and whatever else and everything. I cannot make a list, I literally cannot. As a kid, I thought this is what everybody feels when they look at things. I never talked about it out loud as I never felt the need to until I grew older and I confided in my best friends and told them about this. Both of them just laughed! But that's when I got to know people don’t feel the same way. I told this to my therapist and she was surprised, didn't know what to tell me. I have had a lot of conversations where I have talked about things people are not comfortable with or cannot answer about and that does it for me. Talking about me tasting random ass shit off the wall, pardon my French, is one of them and I find it funny. Smell Memories are part of your everyday life (like I was talking about earlier), you live - you love - and you breathe, everything is part of your past, or it eventually becomes. I tend to forget my past, good or bad. I forget people too, friends, family, and that one good stranger I met on the subway who helped me by stopping the train for 10 seconds! I forget stuff. I don't remember what I did in 2014 when I was in Vadodara, India with my very small family of three. I’d like to sit and think about it, my happy and sad moments, I do have a lot of them. But one thing I for sure remember, or my brain does, are those beautiful smells/ fragrances that I got a sniff of at “that” particular time. Have you ever just stood on a crossing road waiting for your turn to reach your destination and someone passes by; you get that smell of your best friend that you haven't seen in months? Or your father that you’ve been missing for weeks but can't visit? Or your first love that you forgot about? Does it happen to you? Because it happens to me. Isn't it weird how one smell can make you imagine a whole part of your life? I do have a lot of questions hovering around but what to do my mind works in a certain way. I don't hope to get an answer to everything but do like to think about things people don't usually discuss. Twenty Three years of my life and I barely remember anything. I have this crippling fear that I won't have stories to tell my grandkids like my grandparents tell me right now. It’s really hard for me to even remember what I had for my breakfast or even the most important conversation I had with someone. But smell is something we don't remember but imprint. I watch a lot of dramas. Have you seen those scenes where the main female lead of the drama walks by the male lead and he smells him and gets all bubbly? Ahaha, I love those scenes, they are so romantic. Haven't we all dreamt of those scenes happening to us in real life? Or is it just me? Different fragrances have different effects on me and my intricate memories and I hope you all have the same effect as I do.

author: Janhvi Trivedi
thesis_title: Visualizing Eternity
paragraph: I walked towards the exit and saw my father and my boyfriend standing there waiting for me to meet them, after one and a half years. It was weird, unreal for some reason. Like I wasn't able to feel it happening. That wasn't ok to me. For the very first time in my life, I realized I was not ok with how I felt. I felt lost while being with my loved ones, the continuous threat of this moment not existing felt like a death trap. I was depressed but tried hard to not show it to my family. They were so excited to see me after so long. I tried to keep it low like it meant nothing to me to feel like I was in a movie or a game. I missed them so much, I was touch deprived of them. But they didn’t feel real! At first, I thought this was happening because I met them after so long, I never knew before how it is to be without them and that made me think it was the reason for my feelings at that point. It was extremely scary, regardless of the reason. I didn’t wanna feel this way. I decided to meet a psychiatrist finally after years of contemplating. I talked to him for hours telling him how exactly I’d been feeling forever at that point. He was surprised at how I had been living my whole life without any guidance or help.

author: Janhvi Trivedi
thesis_title: Visualizing Eternity
paragraph: I knew I was at the right place. I could feel that this time I would be alright. I felt lighter going back home that day like a very big responsibility has been lifted off my shoulders. It felt like for the very first time I made such a big decision for myself.

author: Janhvi Trivedi
thesis_title: Visualizing Eternity
paragraph: After years of different kinds of struggle, I am now finally understanding what the feelings of feeling I have been feeling. The experience has been beautiful. I hope I was able to express my experiences properly to the readers. The environment changed a lot, my perception of time, my imagination of reality, my interpretation of memories, and my expectations of the future. The whole experience graded my life in different ways. I have had bad and very bad days but all I know is I feel utterly grateful to be living my life differently from most people, experiencing things differently.

author: Janhvi Trivedi
thesis_title: Visualizing Eternity
paragraph: It was a mysterious poetry that was yet to be written. A life worth living. I hope you see me differently now. I hope you find me weirder.

author: Jiayin Ding
thesis_title: Whose ruler rules?
paragraph: “How does measurement act as a bridge between human perception, objects, time, and space, and how does the construction of these elements shape our understanding of reality?”

author: Jiayin Ding
thesis_title: Whose ruler rules?
paragraph: “How do the objective and subjective dimensions of measurement evolve in response to cultural, historical, and scientific changes, and how do they shape the criteria that govern our perception of reality?”

author: Jiayin Ding
thesis_title: Whose ruler rules?
paragraph: “From a positivist perspective, the observer effect of quantum mechanics challenges our assumptions about objective reality, revealing how the measurement process affects the object being measured. However, how does this mean that our reality is inherently indeterminate or simply a limitation of our ability to measure?” [Introduction]

author: Jiayin Ding
thesis_title: Whose ruler rules?
paragraph: In 2017, when I arrived in the United States, I experienced, for the first time, the discomfort of “losing precision.” Fahrenheit, feet, gallons—these unfamiliar units became obstacles in daily life. Each conversion felt like a small disruption to my habitual ways of understanding. Yet, this inconvenience went deeper, touching on my sense of identity. I no longer fully belonged to a world defined by familiar systems but instead found myself suspended between two measurement paradigms. This discomfort led me to question: What is measurement? Why can’t measurement systems be universal? Most importantly, why did I instinctively resist new systems? This resistance wasn’t just about the challenge of adaptation—it stemmed from the realization that measurement is an invisible bridge between me and the world. When the bridge shifts, it unsettles my understanding of both myself and others. Measurement, often seen as neutral and objective, is more like a piece of clay—malleable and shaped by culture, history, and power. From the geometric surveys of ancient Egypt to the metric reforms of the French Revolution, measurement has never been just a tool to record the world. It has also served as a symbol of social order. Historical narratives reveal that the metric system was not simply a scientific advancement but a political and cultural project, designed to consolidate national authority through standardization. Meanwhile, the persistence of the imperial system reflects resistance to globalization and an adherence to tradition. Behind every measurement system lies humanity’s effort to define “who we are,” as well as whose standards dominate. For me, these limitations became especially clear in my work as a designer, where precision tools are essential, whether calculating distances for exhibit installations or adjusting pixels for digital interfaces. Yet, even the most accurate tools cannot override the influence of my perspective and cultural background on measurement outcomes. For example, when designing an interactive installation, I might calculate projections based on the “standard” viewer’s distance. But this “standard” often overlooks variations in height, perspective, or cultural context. These biases force me to reexamine measurement not as a purely scientific practice but as a relational activity—one that connects the measurer and the measured. This relational view of measurement resonates with the observer effect in quantum physics. Quantum theory shows that observation not only records a system’s state but also alters it. Measurement is like a lake: when I throw a stone, the surface is disturbed, and my perspective shifts—the ripples are both the result of my action and the product of my observation. This philosophical lens challenges traditional notions of measurement as an objective tool and highlights the inseparability of observer and observed. In the quantum world, reality is dynamic and relational. The same is true of human society’s measurement systems. Measurement is not a fixed truth but an interactive process shaped by power, culture, and history. This insight provides a profound perspective for my research: measurement does more than record data; it shapes identity and constructs the frameworks through which we understand the world. My research seeks to uncover the power structures and implicit assumptions embedded in measurement systems. While standardized measurements facilitate communication, they often disregard diverse cultural and personal experiences, marginalizing alternative perspectives. By questioning these systems, perhaps we can redefine measurement as a more inclusive tool. This exploration shifts measurement from a one-way tool to a two-way relationship. As social beings, we must recognize that measurement not only defines the world but also shapes how we see ourselves. Quantum philosophy reminds us that we are not passive observers but active participants in shaping reality. By deconstructing and reconstructing measurement systems, we can redefine our identities and rebuild our relationships with the world. At this point, measurement is no longer a fixed scientific attribute. It becomes a dynamic network connecting self, others, and the world. Every act of measurement carries humanity’s desires, cultural legacies, and the construction of identity. How might we enhance the diversity of measurement or give it new meaning by rethinking these hidden relationships? Can measurement evolve beyond being a symbol of power to become a bridge to diversity and understanding? [Chapter 1] How is measurement evolving?

author: Jiayin Ding
thesis_title: Whose ruler rules?
paragraph: Measurement can be seen as a human-constructed "framework," a tool to classify, divide, and understand the world. By creating standardized expressions of objects, time, and space, measurement forms a bridge in human comprehension of reality. Its initial purpose was to meet practical needs in daily life, such as trade, construction, and agriculture. Systems have evolved and adjusted over time, and they’ve become indispensable tools for shaping and aligning with society's fundamental expectations. But they have not fully captured reality’s complexity. Human measurement systems are deeply influenced by our anthropocentrism, with their principles primarily centered on human efficiency. This approach has led to high efficiency, simplifying various phenomena but also reducing sensitivity to individual differences, unique environments, and species. For instance, time is divided into seconds and minutes, dulling the human connection to natural rhythms, while space is quantified in standard units, diminishing the local specificity and personal experience of spatial dimensions. While anthropocentrism has driven technological advancement, it has also abstracted measurement into a system centered on efficiency and norms, gradually evolving into a “shell” around human thought. This framework's origins can be traced to early civilizations, where measurement systems were often based on human physical dimensions. For example, ancient Egyptian cubits used the king's forearm as a measurement unit, and ancient Roman feet were based on soldiers’ steps. These body-based units reflected human intentions to control and order the natural world, emphasizing the human scale of measurement systems. Yet as needs grew, humans sought standardized tools beyond the limitations of body-based measures to address more complex realities. Ancient Egyptian rope-stretching offers a compelling example of such an extension beyond the human body. After the Nile flooded and erased land boundaries, reestablishing them became essential. Rope stretchers, as they were called, used knotted ropes to demarcate these boundaries. They treated the rope as an extension of their own bodies, with specific knots acting as markers for land division. In building projects like the pyramids, these ropes were also essential to ensuring foundational precision. This work not only elevated measurement as a technical skill but also conferred social status upon those who mastered it. Egyptian surveyors not only held the responsibility of land division but also assessed taxes, thus holding prestigious social positions. Across civilizations, measurement systems were established not only for efficiency and control but also deeply rooted within the social, religious, and political frameworks, carrying the influence of power and belief systems. Measurement, therefore, emerged as an institutionalized force, reflecting each civilization's ruling ideologies and authority structures. Similarly, in the modern era, the metric system embodied clear cultural and political intentions. During the French Revolution in the 18th century, the metric system was adopted not only as a scientific advancement but as a “universal” standard that conveyed ideals of equality and unity. As historian Ken Alder notes, the metric system was propagated to transcend regional and social differences, using science as a borderless language to reshape societal order. Although scientifically advantageous, its promotion carried Enlightenment ideals and nationalist elements that bolstered France’s cultural influence over other countries, embedding French ideology within its perceived objectivity. In reality, standardized measurement systems bring more than surface practicality; they wield a regulatory power that upholds particular cultural or political systems. The French metric and British imperial units, while superficially just different measurement units, reflect the shifting dominance of France and Britain in distinct historical periods. These standards, through systemic standardization, form a “universal language” in global economic and technological collaborations. However, this standardization subtly reinforces certain power [Chapter 2] What if we change our perspective?

author: Jiayin Ding
thesis_title: Whose ruler rules?
paragraph: “There is no objective reality, only a rational one,” states Carlo Rovelli, a professor of quantum physics in his book, Reality Is Not What It Seems: The Journey to Quantum Gravity. This quote opens up a black hole of existence for my topic. It presents a subjective view of reality, emphasizing that our understanding of the world is not a direct reflection of objective reality, but is based on the structure of reason and the way we perceive it as observers. According to the pillars of epistemology, all human knowledge and understanding are constructed through the senses, the mind, and language. In other words, it is limited by human cognitive and linguistic systems. What we call the “objective” world is often only a “subjective reality” filtered through experience, logic, and reason. This suggests that what we perceive as “objectivity” is actually the product of rational interpretation rather than pure, unprocessed existence. One intuitive example is the fact that, due to different biological systems, the colors we perceive as humans are completely different from other animals. That is to say that color is not an inherent property of the external world, but rather its interpretation of a particular light wave signal. Without an observer (or visual system), so-called “color” does not exist. The “color” we perceive is not an objective fact when unprocessed by our senses and cognition, but a “rational reality” constructed by our sensory system and brain. At the same time, as I explored quantum physics more deeply, I realized that the presence of an observer affects the results of a measurement. For example, in a double-slit experiment, an electron or photon will exhibit particle properties when observed, but fluctuating wave properties when unobserved. This implies that “reality” itself is influenced by observation, and therefore, there is no pure, independent “objective reality”. This observation-influenced reality can be understood as a “rational reality”, i.e., a structured reality based on the way we observe and measure. The existence of these facts verifies that the act of measurement is anthropocentric and that it exists in human society as a subjective consciousness that facilitates our lives in the form of experience. With the advancement of globalization, the world gradually came to accept uniform standards, particularly in measurement units and timekeeping. It seemed that the results could be “objective and precise” when they were certified by the majority of the group. However, this so-called “objectivity and precision” is a product of consensus. The process of standardization does not erase the subjectivity of measurement, but rather, through negotiation and collective agreement, it solidifies certain measurement methods and unit systems into a “universal,” accepted reality. However, this reality is still constructed within a set of rational frameworks that are nonetheless not completely free from the influence of the observer. For example, although concepts such as time and distance have global standards (such as seconds or meters), they can still take on different meanings and understandings in different cultures, historical contexts, and personal experiences. In some aboriginal cultures, time may be viewed as a cycle or flow, rather than a linear or discrete unit. Similarly, “time” in quantum physics is an ambiguous dimension that at very small scales does not exactly follow the linear passage of time defined by classical physics. All of this suggests that despite the operation of a set of global standards, the experience of reality is still pluralistic and subjective. Another implicit role of standardized measurement lies in the representation of power relations in human societies. Harmonized measurement standards make social governance, economic trade, and technological development feasible, bringing with them the possibilities of efficiencies across local and global systems. At the same time, however, standards are also exclusionary and controlling. For example, in the process of industrialization, Western standards gradually became dominant around the world, and traditional ways of measurement in different regions and cultures were gradually marginalized or even disappeared. Such a process makes standardization an invisible cultural force, pushing certain worldviews and ways of thinking around the globe and shaping human perceptions of “objective reality”. This leads to a key question: Can or should we pursue a more inclusive measurement system? How can we shift the definition of measurement from “objective truth” to “relational presentation”? I argue that the purpose of thinking about this question is not to overturn and reconstruct existing measurement systems, but to help us understand the complex relationships between different cultures, ecologies, and individuals. Therefore, this study proposes more about how to accept and recognize the utility of systematic, scientific, and evidence-based measurement systems, while acknowledging the limitations, biases, and prejudices based on human perception. This is where I get confused about the “individual” and the “binary thinking that breaks down ‘dualism’”. [Chapter 3] What would happen if standards were reimagined?

author: Jiayin Ding
thesis_title: Whose ruler rules?
paragraph: Morris's Three Rulers made me meditate on the deeper possibilities of simplicity rather than a reliance on complexity for an artwork’s visual impact. The three rulers, though all designed to be 36 inches in length, are visually distinct. In the work, the standardization of scale is shifted from a universal rule to one expressing the artist’s experience. This subtle difference directly challenged my preconceived notions of the world, and I felt the shock of a familiar order collapsing. However, this “collapse” is not mere disintegration, but rather the opening of a door to a more beautiful and richly diverse world. The work makes me realize that this is precisely the direction I want to pursue: to profoundly redefine the meaning of measurement. Like Rick Salafia's work, Instruments, the infinite deconstruction of the simplest measuring tools can also provoke viewers to think about the seemingly taken-for-granted act of measuring in a new way. Through the mundane object of a ruler, he reveals the depth of measurement as a fundamental way of constructing human experience. This inspired me to revisit the nature of measurement: measurement is not just a tool or an act, but a language, and a way in which human beings can try to define order. It is an antidote to the measurement system of modern society, while seemingly scientific and objective, reduces complex reality to a single system of rules, thereby compressing cultural diversity and individual experience. From these non-conventional perspectives, measurement does not just describe reality; it shapes it and even dominates human cognition and behavior. If we deconstruct this widely accepted standardized system, can we find a way of measurement that is closer to individual perception? Is it possible to make measurement an open and inclusive behavior again, rather than a mandatory norm? These questions push me to rethink the possibilities of measurement. At the same time, I realize that simply rejecting traditional standards does not really bring us everything. Standardized measurement systems are an important cornerstone in the development of human civilization, laying down the order and efficiency of modern society. However, the establishment of these standards also means the exclusion of certain possibilities. Therefore, by deconstructing these rules and discovering those overlooked possibilities, I hope to design a more diverse and flexible measurement paradigm. This paradigm no longer sees measurement as a monolithic act but transforms it into a relational exploration: a process of constant interaction between observer, environment, and object. For the sake of better narrative logic and visual effect, I try to find a measurement system that can have objective measurement accuracy and subjective perception at the same time, and finally, I decide to use time as a central entry point to start the reconstruction of measurement. Clocks and calendars provide a precise rhythm for our lives, but behind this precision lies a simplification and abstraction of the essence of time. We follow the rules of standard time to organize our schedules and make plans, but we often neglect our individual perception of time. More so than in the last century, contemporary people live in different time segments: they go to work at nine in the morning and leave at five; each class lasts three hours; appointments with the doctor are booked for noon on Saturdays, and so on. Time seems to translate into our lives in these segments. But many of you may have also experienced the feeling that time seems to fly by when we are focused on something, and that time seems to take forever when we are waiting with nothing to do. This subjective passage of time is dynamic, non-linear, and depends on emotion, mental state, and environment. The meaning of time also varies greatly for people of different cultures, occupations, and ages. Through the universal but complex phenomenon of time, we can explore more intuitively the diversity of measurement in individual, collective, and even overall human experience. Time is both definable and difficult to define, and it is this paradox that makes it an ideal medium for experimentation. [Chapter 4] like？（Survey Making） What would we look &

author: Jiayin Ding
thesis_title: Whose ruler rules?
paragraph: The concept of time measurement is deeply connected to everyone’s life. Even though we may live in the same time zone, each individual’s experience and definition of time can vary significantly. With this understanding, I gathered responses from people around me about how they perceive and measure time, asking the following guiding questions: ● How do you measure time in your daily life? ● What do you think is a metaphor for time? ● How would you define the concept of wasting time? ● From an objective perspective, what would an ideal time-measuring tool look like? The responses fall into two categories: objective and subjective. Some participants questioned the rigidity of clock-based time and expressed a preference for personalized or natural markers, while others found comfort in the control offered by structured time planning. A recurring theme in many responses was the role of light in time perception—marking time through the natural cycle of sunrise and sunset, one of the most primordial ways humanity has understood time. Another intriguing observation is that people often only become aware of time’s existence at the moment it is consciously observed. This phenomenon aligns closely with the “observer effect.” Without objective observation, our perception of the passage of time is often subjective and fluid. For instance, when we are deeply engrossed in activities we enjoy, time seems to fly by; yet during moments of pain or struggle, time feels interminable. It is only when we check a clock or glance at a phone screen that time reverts to its objective definition. Thus, while we ostensibly share a global 24-hour cycle, each of us may exist within our own “subjective time zones.” These “subjective time zones” not only vary between individuals but also shift within a single person depending on context and modes of thought. From the collective human perspective to fragmented group dynamics, and ultimately to the individual experience, time perception can be seen as an unfolding narrative. Each layer reveals the diversity and complexity of time measurement: from the apparent uniformity of shared human time at the collective level, to the interpersonal dynamics within groups, where individual perceptions of time can shape and influence one another, and finally to the personal level, where time becomes highly subjective and fluid, stretching or compressing based on emotions and activities. Building on this foundation, I propose the concept of a “composite time ruler” to conceptualize twelve hours across different modes. This flexible framework uses non-standardized markers to represent how individuals perceive the flow of time in various contexts. Time inherently possesses collaborative and social dimensions. Twelve hours can be deconstructed into overlapping schedules—for work, socializing, and rest —that represent the intertwined rhythms of our collective actions. These overlapping layers can be visualized as a “time ruler,” symbolizing the multifaceted nature of shared time and the tensions and harmonies inherent in collaboration.

author: Jiayin Ding
thesis_title: Whose ruler rules?
paragraph: On the grand scale of all humanity, the understanding of time has evolved from natural cycles to mechanical systems and now to digital constructs. While modern time is meticulously standardized into precise units, the cycle of sunrise and sunset remains the most primordial boundary of twelve hours. However, this boundary is not absolute but rather the result of human interaction with the environment. The standardization of modern times, represented by rigid scales, simplifies the complexity of time into linear, measurable units. Yet behind these standardized rulers lies humanity’s reshaping and regulation of natural time—a consensus formed by the few but accepted by the many. Light serves as an ideal medium connecting these models, symbolizing both observation and attention while also being humanity’s earliest tool for measuring time. I plan to integrate the presence of light with the concept of scale, visualizing the twelve-hour framework across different modes to explore the relationships between individuals, collectives, and nature through the lens of time. By weaving together the personal, collective, and human dimensions of time measurement, this project aims to create a comprehensive visual language that responds to the multiplicity and richness of the theme of time measurement.

author: Jiayin Ding
thesis_title: Whose ruler rules?
paragraph: And so, what exactly is measurement? What does it mean to measure? It can be understood as a dynamic, relationship-based behavior—one that adapts and adjusts as observers shift, contexts transform, and situations grow more complex and diverse. This reimagining does not seek to replace existing measurement systems, but rather to complement and expand them. By embracing measurement as a fluid, multidimensional language, we open the door to systems that are more inclusive, resilient, and reflective of the intricate realities we navigate daily. The essence of the four chapters outlined above lies in redefining the purpose of measurement: moving beyond quantification and standardization to foster a deeper understanding of the diversity and subjectivity inherent in complex realities. By intersecting art, philosophy, and science, this thesis envisions measurement not as a rigid technical tool but as a creative practice—an evolving process that bridges disparate perspectives and enriches our shared understanding. However, the current structure of the thesis still unfolds within a vast narrative, exploring broad conceptual themes. Moving forward, I aim to ground these ideas in the intimate, everyday interactions that shape how we measure and are measured—whether in person-to-person dynamics, person-to-object relationships, or object-to-object exchanges. By refining the concept from a more hyper-conventionalized and immediate perspective, I hope to uncover the subtle, often overlooked ways measurement operates in our lives, weaving the abstract with the tangible to reimagine measurement as a living, relational act. author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: DES-760B-09 Graduate Seminar B 24FA By Jiayue Wang

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: Abstract advancements1, In an era defined by rapid technological the exponential proliferation of images, hyper-connected digital landscapes, and algorithm-driven narratives have blurred the boundaries between reality and illusion. While technological expansion continuously enlarges our world, individuals remain constrained by their finite perception and cognitive limits. Thus, a stabilizing anchor— a belief system—becomes essential. This project investigates the idea of instilling a personal belief system as a framework to systematize the dynamic external information by redefining individuals’ internal perception of time and space. This is intended to serve as a bridge that grounds external reality and internal consciousness, enabling individuals to navigate the overwhelming flux of blurred realities. Much like a camera obscura, which inverts and reframes the external world through a lens, it offers a coherent internal image at the imaging surface amidst the darkness in the original box. Introduction As a member of Generation Z, whose life is bound to the rapid development of modern technology, I often find myself overwhelmed by the explosion of external information. I was born into a family with contrasting beliefs—my mother, a devout Buddhist, often brought me to temples, while my father, a firm materialist, believed only in science. As a child, I found solace in religion, but as I grew older, I began to question the doctrines that once comforted me. In high school, I turned to science, hoping to grasp reality through logic and reason. Yet in college, I realized that even science has its blind spots—it can measure, but it cannot fully explain being. These shifts left me feeling unanchored. I began to wonder: is there a system—beyond religion or science—that can help individuals understand their existence? This inquiry marked the beginning of my exploration into the idea of a “personal belief system.” Belief, as one of humanity's most universal and profound spiritual activities, governs and directs other conscious activities. It is the self-aware recognition and active adjustment of human existence and its relationship with the external world.2 Society often imposes a unified belief system on us. In the Chinese context, academic achievement is seen as the only path to a good job, and a job becomes the sole measure of success. This system doesn't suit everyone. I once internalized this mindset—when I struggled academically in primary school, I questioned my self-worth. In developing this thesis, I wanted to challenge that narrative and understand the meaning of my own existence. I began to realize that each of us already holds a personal belief system, shaped by our experiences and identities. We simply need to ask and listen inwardly: “What do I believe?” For example, I first believed in religion, but nowadays, I believe science can help me explore the outside world to some extent. However, science can’t prove the existence of many mystical ideas, so that can give me another direction to understand what is going on in the outside world. The area between science and mysticism is one constant of my belief system. The importance of having a personal belief system In today’s fast-paced world, where attention is fragmented and identities are fluid, personal belief systems have become more essential than ever. They help individuals bridge the gap between overwhelming external information and internal consciousness. In this thesis, I define belief not as dogma or organized religion, but as a flexible structure of perception—something rooted in the individual's lived experience, shaping how one sees and understands the world. As suggested in the book Making Room for Thought, “In film, on television, with computers, in books, the ways of seeing transform us.”3 If we just take things at face value, our belief system is not our own belief system. In this sense, a belief system becomes a way of seeing, shaping our understanding of the meaning of existence, which also functions as a lens that through filtering external information organizes our internal perception, allowing us to navigate the overwhelming flux of mediated realities, to see the world more clearly and recognize ourselves within it. Thesis statement This thesis will examine a personal belief system using the camera obscura as a metaphor to show that the relationship between our internal consciousness and external information is not a static doctrine but a dynamic process—one that filters, transforms, and projects external reality in our internal understanding, through the lens of a personal belief system, which can help us see and think the external existences as their essential form. After interacting with the external, we can see ourselves clearly. By a hybrid methodology that blends visual experimentation, poetic narrative, and philosophical reflection, this project proposes that belief systems serve as interpretive frameworks—fluid, layered, and deeply subjective—that guide individuals in making meaning within an increasingly fragmented and mediated world.

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: Central terms · Definition of Terms [Belief System] noun [Belief]4 - - The feeling of being certain that something exists or is true. - [System]5 - A set of connected things or devices that operate together. In the context of this thesis, a belief system is an evolving cycle, which continuously integrates perception, experience, and interpretation. A belief system begins with sensory encounters that shape initial understanding, then inform new experiences, and finally create an iterative loop of meaning-making. - Zooming in on the timeline, the belief system is seen through an inherently temporal lens, existing within the cycles of past, present, and future. - Zooming out (away from the timeline), the belief system is a structured, logical process that mediates and restores the dynamic interaction between external information and internal consciousness. For example: 1) (Algorithm) In our daily life, when we see a social media post of some voice related to healthy eating for the first time, we form an initial impression of a healthy eating method. (Sensory Encounter) The data system in the computer notices we stop at this post; it will repeatedly expose us to similar content which might influence our behavior consciously or unconsciously, such as adopting sustainable habits or changing consumption choices. (inform new experiences) These actions reinforce the belief that "healthy food is important to the body," leading to deeper engagement like seeking more information related to healthy food, further shaping our lifestyle (Iterative Loop of Meaning-Making). 2) Another example in our daily life, we usually see edited or filtered images of influencers with "ideal" body types on social media, forming an initial impression of beauty standards (Sensory Encounter). Repeated exposure leads to dissatisfaction with our own appearance, prompting extreme dieting (inform new experiences). This anxiety reinforces the belief that "thin equals beautiful," affecting self-esteem and mental health, potentially leading to eating disorders or anxiety while continuing to consume similar content, creating a harmful cycle (Iterative Loop of Meaning-Making).

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: [Camera Obscura] noun - [Box - physical container] + [Lens - eye] + [Imaging surface - produce an exact picture of something] In the context of this thesis, the camera obscura is rooted in the physicality of pinhole imaging wherein it captures light, time, and space, projecting them onto an internal surface. The Camera Obscura serves as a metaphor to visualize the process of a belief system. The camera obscura does not present reality itself but a filtered projection, our perception of people is also a construction—a belief system shaped by what we focus on, how we interpret their actions, and the personal lens through which we "see" them. For example: When we use the camera obscura model to observe a person, the image projected inside appears inverted and blurred, requiring interpretation to make sense of it. This mirrors how we perceive individuals in real life—our understanding of a person is never absolute but shaped by fragments of interactions, past experiences, and shifting contexts. To be more specific, if we see someone through one specific event, we might define them based on that single moment—a kind act makes them seem generous, and a mistake makes them appear careless. However, when viewed through different experiences and perspectives, these impressions shift, much like how adjusting the aperture or lighting in a camera obscura alters the image. · Metaphors 01 The Hourglass Mode By distilling a visual expression that captures the dynamic flow between external information and internal perception, I identified the belief system’s position—an intersection where these realms converge and transform. This model serves as the foundation for understanding the cyclical nature of meaning-making in existentialism, illustrating how individuals through belief systems process, internalize, and reframe the reality between the external universe and the internal universe.

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: Research Questions 1. How does a belief system, as a way of seeing the world, mediate and influence an individual's dual perception between material reality and subjective consciousness? 2. How does establishing a belief system enable individuals to construct new frameworks of reality and explore the meaning of existence?

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: Research Motivation The origins of this research method (Experimental narrative writing) stem from my exploration of belief systems, where I encountered a challenge—belief, as a theoretical construct, often remains abstract and difficult to communicate effectively. Through experiments7 research investigations and creative I realized that abstract expressions alone fail to illustrate how a belief system operates and influences perception. Thus, I analyzed my mindset shifts related to belief and developed a structured understanding of the different topics of the belief system’s formation. Embodying these ideas through a concrete narrative, this evolution is directly mirrored in the writing’s protagonist–Aether–whose journey unfolds through encounters with items that serve as physical and mental touchpoints. The novel, in this sense, rather than treated as a purely fictional construct, is positioned as a research tool—an introspective case study where my own lived experiences serve as an individual sample within a broader human context, functions as both a narrative inquiry, by transforming these experiences into a poetic narrative, the experimental narrative grounding the abstract mechanics of belief formation into tangible experiences. Methodology The theoretical foundation of this research draws from Buddhist philosophy (particularly early interpretations of the Diamond Sutra), Sartrean existentialism, and visual culture studies. While The Diamond Sutra informs the nature of perception, Sartre’s existentialism provides a framework for understanding belief as an active, self-defined process rather than a fixed entity. Additionally, visual culture theories help contextualize how modes of seeing shape individual consciousness. These theoretical perspectives are put into practice through a multi-layered approach: - The Camera Obscura Experiment: Aether constructs a camera obscura by herself, introducing the idea of seeing as an active, interpretative process rather than a passive reception of reality. - Narrative Development: The experimental narrative unfolds in a fragmented, nonlinear manner, mirroring the way belief is formed through scattered experiences rather than a singular, linear revelation. - Symbols Encounter: Through a fragmented, nonlinear manner narrative, mirroring the process of constructing and deconstructing the belief system is formed through scattered experiences rather than a singular, linear revelation. Also try to visualize the Zen philosophy ‘seeing mountains as mountains, seeing mountains as not mountains, and seeing mountains as mountains again.’ Impact This research contributes to broader academic discussions in several ways: - Expanding Narrative Forms: It proposes a new method of theoretical inquiry by integrating philosophical research with experimental storytelling, demonstrating how fiction can serve as an academic tool. - Challenging Traditional Perception Models: By framing belief as a mode of seeing, this research argues that a belief system is not merely a system of thoughts but a perceptual framework that mediates human experience. - Encouraging Self-Exploration: This research aims to empower individuals to construct their understanding of time, space, and existence—free from rigid doctrines and external impositions. By engaging with both external information and internal consciousness, individuals can develop a belief system that is fluid, self-defined, and uniquely attuned to their realities.

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: Everything I have told you about the journey is true. The map was not created out of thin air, but actually existed in the world, and also deep within me. What you have heard are fragments that I have picked up from the experience of actually making the journey. I did not intend to, and could not, create it. Welcome to an unfinished chapter. 我所告诉你关于那趟旅程的一切，其实都是真的。那条路并不是凭空创造出来的产物，而是 实实在在存在于世上，存在于我内心的深处。你所听见的，都是我从实际造访的经验中撷 取的片段。 我无意，也无法创造。 欢迎来到一个尚未完成的章节......

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: - In a Dark Box Where should I begin? A paper lies before me, the story upon it was written long ago. I am but the hand that records all the things happening at this time. There is a murmuring voice, carrying its whispers from the darkened corners of this space, each telling transforms the answer I am trying to find, shifting it with every breath. As the voices reached my senses, their volume and resonance guided my perception. I subconsciously estimated their distance, mapping their positions in the darkness. I was gradually convinced that I was enclosed within a dark box—a space defined not by walls, but by the way sound moved, echoed, and faded around me. These voices would become the echo that reverberates endlessly, weaving the known anew, offering not an answer fixed in form, but a fluid path where discovery lies in the act of perceiving. Perhaps I should start from the end—for all things begin at the end; when there is nowhere left to go, that is when everything truly begins. My story is a riddle AS A RELATIONSHIP —— As a cipher/a phrase/ a paradox AS A CONDUIT —— As a point / a line / a pattern AS …… —— will not simply be the beginning. As I leave behind this record, I am destined to begin again, endlessly tracing the boundaries of an invisible labyrinth—a dark box—I may never truly depart. 在暗箱中 — 我该从何处开始？ 一页空白的纸铺展在我面前，然而，这个故事早已被书写。我不过是记录正在发生的 一切的手。 那个低语的声音，它的回响来自这个空间幽暗的每个角落。每一次诉说，我追求的答 案便被改变，每一次呼吸，形状便被重塑，或许最终，它成为回音，在时间的罅隙中 无尽回荡，重新编织已知的世界。当声音传入我的感官时，它们的音量和共鸣引导着 我的感知。我下意识地估算着它们与我的距离，描绘着它们在黑暗中的位置。 我逐渐相信，我被封闭在一个黑盒子里--这个空间不是由墙壁界定的，而是由声音在 我周围移动、回响和消逝的方式界定的。 那个声音给我的不会是一个固定的答案，而是一条流动的路径——在感知的瞬间， 发现才得以发生。 ··· 或许，我应当从结尾开始——因为一切都始于终点。 当无处可去时，那才是真正的起点。 我的故事是一则谜语 是一种关系 如暗号，如短句，如悖论。 — 是一条通道 — 如一点，如一线，如一脉相承的纹理。 是…… ——不会只是一个开端。 而当我留下这份记录之时，我已注定再次启程。无休止地描摹这座无形的迷宫—— 一座暗箱——或许，我永远无法真正离开。

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: - Carving a Hole Perhaps it is not a question of what we carve, but what we seek to uncover. (A yearning as ancient as the first hand that brushed against stone, searching not to destroy but to reveal what lies beneath. ) [...] · The act of carving: is not a destruction but a passage, a timeless action, existing outside the boundaries of a linear narrative. · A hole: is an absence, and yet, it defines everything around it. is the eye of the storm, the center of gravity, a connection of existences. is the answer to the absence, or is it just a void? · Carving a hole: is not only for a void that creates a form but also to invite contradiction: an emptiness that speaks, the silence that echoes, the darkness that illuminates. · What lies beyond the hole? Each cut reveals another layer, a hidden thread weaving the internal and external universes together.

paragraph: — 开凿一个洞 或许，问题并不在于我们雕刻了什么，而在于我们试图揭示什么。 【那是一种亘古的渴望，如同最初触碰岩石的手，不是为了破坏，而是为了揭开其下 隐藏的世界。】 雕刻的行为: · 并非毁灭，而是一种通道，一种超越线性时间的永恒动作。 一个孔洞: · 它是缺失，然而，它定义了其周围的一切。 它是风暴的眼，是引力的中心，是存在之间的连结。 它是关于缺席的答案，抑或，它只是虚无？ 雕刻一个孔洞: · 不仅仅是塑造形体的空缺，更是对矛盾的召唤： 使虚空诉说，让沉默回响，让黑暗透进光亮。 孔洞的彼端是什么？ · 每一次切割，都揭开一层新的表面，一条隐藏的脉络，交织着内在与外在的宇宙。

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: - Embedding a Lens Perhaps it is not a question of what we see on the outside, but how we choose to frame it. (a yearning as ancient as the first gaze toward the horizon, not seeking to impose a view but to reveal what is hidden within and beyond. ) [...] · The act of embedding: is not an intrusion but an alignment, a convergence of the seen and unseen. To visualize a dialogue between presence and perception. · A lens: is a boundary revealing clarity through its limited view. is a definition through a fragment that redefines the infinite. is a question about whether it is a way to see, or a way to be seen. · Embedding a lens: a vision that both narrows and expands, a focus that sharpens and blurs, an observer that observes and is observed. · What lies beyond the lens? Is it the truth, or merely another reflection refracted through layers of belief? Each glimpse through the lens peels back another veil, another story, another self.
