author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: It is the lens of the camera obscura, through the window, the boundaries of time and space emerge, and then the belief system collapses - a new perspective is born. 嵌入一枚透镜 — 或许问题并不在于我们看见了外界的什么，而在于我们如何去框定它。 【那是一种如同第一次遥望地平线般古老的渴望，不是为了强加视角，而是去揭示隐 藏的，超越可见的。】 · 嵌入的行为： 并非入侵，而是对齐，是显现与隐匿的交汇点。 可视化了存在与感知之间本就潜藏着的某种对话。 透镜： · 是一道边界，透过自身的局限，显露清晰。 是一种定义，以一片微小的玻璃，重塑无垠的世界 是一个问题，它是通向观看的方式，还是通向被观看的方式？ 嵌入一枚透镜： · 既收束又拓展视野，既锐化又模糊焦点，既是观察者，也是被观察者。 透镜的彼端是什么？ · 是真相，还是仅仅又一层被信念与感知折射后的映像？ 每一次透过透镜的凝视，都是一层帷幕的剥离，一个故事的重塑，一次自我的揭示。

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: 在暗箱的孔洞中， 透镜是一种象征。它破碎了存在，又重组了它的叙事。 我不禁思考： 我嵌入它，是为了更清晰地观察，还是为了扰乱现实的真相？

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: 它是一枚暗箱之镜， 是一扇窗口，透过它时间、空间得以显现，于是信仰的界限崩塌，一个全新的视角诞生。

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: - Introducing light Perhaps it is not the light itself that draws us in, but the way it reveals what we cannot see (a yearning as eternal as the first gaze toward the sun, not to claim it but to witness its reflection in all things. ) [...] · The act of entering: It exists beyond the boundaries of physicality, offering a certain clarity, or even the outline of the truth I am questioning. · A light: only exists in contrast to shadow. As a line that unfolds the pulse of existence - separates perception and reality. destiny.9 Within the column of light, all is · Introducing a light is to invite duality: If light never arrived, darkness would be the embodiment of light. · What lies beyond the light? is the end of light or the beginning of darkness, maybe it is the fragments of truth Beyond the column of light, all is unknown.

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: In the Lens is standing in the space between exposure and concealment. I wonder if the light itself the revelation, or is it the illusion we chase? It is the light of a camera obscura, the beam through which existence is illuminated. 引入一束光 — 或许，吸引我们的并非光本身，而是它所揭示的——那些我们无法看见的事物。 【一种如初次仰望太阳般永恒的渴望，并非为了占有，而是为了在万物中见证它的倒 影。】 [...] 引入行为： · 它超越物理的界限，给予了一道锋利的清晰，一条边缘线关于我始终无法捕捉的真相。 光： · 唯有在阴影之中，方才显现。 它是一条线——存在的脉动，划分了感知与现实。 在光柱之内，万物皆是命运。 · 引入光 意味着接受二元性：若光从未降临，黑暗本身便是光明。 光的尽头是什么？ · 是光的终结或黑暗的开端，也许是真相的碎片。 光柱之外，一切皆是未知。

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: 透过镜头 是站立在曝光与隐匿之间的缝隙。 我不禁思索 光本身是否即是启示，还是我们穷尽一生追逐的幻象？

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: 它是暗箱之光， 使存在得以显现的那一道光束。 - Imaging a Scene Perhaps it is not a question of what is real, but what we decide to illuminate (a mystery as enduring as the first shadow cast by firelight, transforming absence into form. ) [...] · The act of imaging: is a transformation, a passage through the blurred boundary between presence and absence — presence dissolves into absence, while absence manifests into presence. · A scene: is a map of existence, charting the unseen connections between internal and external reality. · Imaging a scene: is an invitation to step beyond mere form to see the intangible: — the echo of time in the folds of space, — the weight of memory suspended in motion, — the infinite expanse of uncertainty that lies between. is to invite duality: If light never arrived, darkness would be the embodiment of light. · What lies beyond the scene? Is it the essence of the thing itself, or the shadow of our gaze projected onto it?

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: In the light, the scene becomes both a mirror and a labyrinth. I wonder if I am creating an image of the world outside or reframing the shifting landscapes within.

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: It is the image of a camera obscura, Through the aperture, existence is reimagined, reframed, and made whole. 印出一幕场景 — 或许，问题的核心并非何为真实，而在于我们选择点亮何物。 【这始终是一道未解之谜，正如火光投下的第一道阴影——将虚无化为形体。】 [...] · 成像过程： 是一种转化，通过穿过存在与缺席之间的模糊交汇的对话，使他们两者相互转换存在形式 场景： · 是一幅存在的地图，描绘着内在与外部世界之间未曾可见的联系。 印出一幕场景 · 是对超越表象、窥见无形的邀请 是时间回响于空间褶皱， —— 是记忆悬浮于运动之中， —— 是介于可知与未知之间，无限不定的辽阔。 —— 影像之外，何物存在？ · 是事物本身的本质，还是我们目光投射出的幽影？ 在光中 场景既是一面镜子，也是一座迷宫。 我不禁思索： 我是在创造外部世界的影像，还是在重新塑造内心不断变幻的风景？

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: The Origin of My Name M: Do you know the origin of your name, Aether? (I shake my head, waiting for her to tell the story.) M: Your father wanted to honor your grandfather. He was deeply fond of Aristotle’s philosophy. (She speaks softly, a gentle smile appearing on her face.) M: Aristotle described "Aether" as the purest, intangible substance in the cosmos, connecting all things and guiding them in perfect harmony. A: But, after Einstein’s theory of relativity, the concept of "Aether" was disproven—replaced by spacetime. Science has shown it doesn’t exist. Dad knew that, didn’t he? Why would he still…… M: Aether, does something proven "nonexistent" by mainstream belief truly have no meaning or value at all? (She pauses, meeting my gaze, then speaks softly.) M: To your grandfather, Aether was more than a scientific theory—it was a symbol of connection and infinity. And to your father and me, it serves as a reminder: we hoped your name would help you remember that you are a boundless existence, yet as deeply rooted in existence as the universe itself. (I reflect for a moment.) A: But, Mom, my name is only a reflection of my existence. It’s like a vessel, holding my past, projecting how others perceive me, but it does not define who I truly am. (She smiles again—this time, there is a glimmer of pride in her expression.) M: That’s right, my dear. It's merely a story that helps you understand who you are. [...] 09·12 Today is my 23rd birthday. My mother shared a small story about my name—Aether. Do things that are deemed "nonexistent" by mainstream knowledge truly lack presence or value? I don’t like using my given name. I’m not sure why, but I find it easier to call myself "Zero" or something else. It could be anything—just not Aether. My real name (if it even is real?). Every time I hear my name, it’s as if I am, in some unknown way, reaffirming my existence—like tracing an invisible thread that weaves through life. My name…..Aether…. Oh! Look how "Aether" and "A-other" resemble each other! [The perceiving subject "a"—the external "other"?] And "Aether" even sounds like "Author." My name is quite mischievous, isn’t it? :) Aimless Wandering 6:00 AM The morning light is warm, interwoven with birdsong and the faint hum of my alarm. I slowly open my eyes, letting a sliver of sunlight seep into my vision. My body feels heavy, bound by an invisible force. I turn over, staring at the ceiling in a daze. 12:00 PM Sunlight filters through the trees, casting dappled shadows. I wander aimlessly, letting the light guide my direction. Sitting on a bench outside a bakery, I open my notebook and casually sketch an egg nestled among the stones of a small warehouse—choosing one at random... 12:00 AM Back home. I turn off all the lights, leaving only a narrow gap for moonlight to seep through the darkness. The projection begins. An inverted world appears on the wall—faint, flickering. Light and shadow weave a fragile image. The trembling projection responds in silence—not all answers reveal themselves immediately.

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: 09·18 What day is it today? Each day seems to repeat: waking, eating, studying, conversing, sleeping again. I need to break this cycle. But how? My thoughts scatter, unable to form a clear picture. Today, I will break it. The routine controls me every moment. A sudden thought arises, during my painting time — Perhaps, only in stillness can clarity be found. Then, how can I capture the fleeting moment? (The ink lingers. There is no answer.) Tonight, I let the moonlight speak for me, she told me that I don’t need to find the one ultimate truth. my purpose is to chase the fleeting, to embrace the undefined beauty. But, right now, I long to capture something… Questioning Backwards Over breakfast, I ask my father: A: Dad, why do I exist? F: Don’t overcomplicate things. Life is far simpler than you think. Do what you’re good at, stay focused, and meaning will emerge naturally. (My mother sets down her teacup, her voice gentle yet firm) M: Aether, have you ever tried praying? You know, Some answers are not created but received. A: I’ve tried everything. (I murmur when father folds his newspaper.) F: Not all answers reveal themselves immediately. And some questions, perhaps, don’t need answers at all. A: But what if I’m asking the wrong questions? M: My dear, there are no "right" or "wrong" questions—only the answers that belong to you. What is it that you wish to do? (I pause in thought.) A: I want to stop everything, and then I might find a moment when all layers of existence align. Even if it’s just for an instant, I want to see everything clearly. [...]

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: 10·16 If I am neither defined by thought nor shaped by action, then what am I? Why am I here? I once believed I was searching for freedom and truth, but somewhere along the way, I strayed from the intended path—whether by choice or by fate. Religion once gave me comfort—it was the faith my mom had bestowed upon me. But its doctrine could not contain me. I felt its constraints—its rules, its conditional love. I could not find myself within it. Technology is kind of my father’s belief, I used to enjoy the infinite information it brought, yet within it, I found absence—the chasm between understanding and expression, the disparity between the measured world and the world as I perceive it. External knowledge is forever incomplete, and my perspective is always limited. I gradually noticed that I exist within the in-between—between faith and doubt, the known and the unknown, fractures in time. Belief in god and belief in technology are all parts of my belief, but what is the combination of them? What belief system truly belongs to me? I feel trapped in a place that does not belong to the past, nor does it lead to the future—it is a liminal space where all things collide, shift, and blur. And I have become a paradox unto myself. At some point in my growth journey, I lost my grasp of the "truth" of the world. I need to step forward—out of this fog. I do not know where I am going; I do not know what I truly need; I do not know how to arrive, feeling I am drifting in a fragmented universe. So I know—it must be now to start my adventure. Experiment—Camera Obscura I turned off all the lights, leaving only a sliver of moonlight filtering through the slightly parted curtain. (Once again.) A blurred reflection emerged on the wall—trees, rooftops, clouds, inverted and hazy. (Once again.) I lay on the floor, gazing at this projection, thinking I was in a big camera obscura. (Once again.) I waited for nightfall. (Once again.) Staring at this small box of darkness, (Pondering how to enclose the external world within it.) I need light to enter… Perhaps, carving a hole! I need a fleeting moment held in stillness. (Once again.) Let the light pierce the integrity of darkness. I need to weave a net for it… (Once again.) Perhaps, Using a sheet of paper…… [...] 10·21 I picked up the wooden boards beside me, trying to construct a boundary. Within this boundary is a space as if extracting fragments from the ruins of time, piecing them together into a box, which is a vessel of darkness... a space that holds nothing, yet contains infinite possibilities. I coated the box surface with ink, then covered it with fabric, as if reenacting the explorations of my past — I sought to fill the void, only to discover that emptiness itself harbored power, and I couldn’t fill it. I carefully pierced the box with a blade, like an ancient stargazer slicing through the night sky in search of constellations. A tiny hole, like the eye of the cosmos. Light penetrated, yet could not escape, trapped like memories behind the filter of time. I waited for the light to seep through the tiny aperture, imprinting the world onto paper. Light dances upon the paper’s surface, like a ghost gliding over a silent poem… It is fragile and weightless, yet capable of capturing the illusions of the external world. Not enough, the image can’t emerge. There is just the path of the light’s escaping……. Experiment— Capturing Light I waited for nightfall. (Once again.) If light alone is not enough, Perhaps I need a way to shape its path. I found an abandoned magnifying glass. (Searching……) Carefully suspended it above the aperture. (Adjusting.) The image sharpened. (Change happening) It was no longer just a blur of light, but a fragment of the external world. [...] 12·18 Today, I finally figured something out! I embedded a lens into my dark box’s hole. And the image finally emerges! The tree outside my house is swaying gently in the wind—whispering and dancing alone against the passing of time. I see… its echo, rendered in shadow. On the paper inside the dark box I constructed, its shape emerges, but softer—blurred, as if movement itself had been forbidden. Time stands still. The tree lingers in light’s memory. Yet, it was still imperfect. The lens’s adjustments introduced distortions—straight lines bent, reality stretched into something dreamlike. And strangely, this distortion comforted me, as if imperfection itself was the essence of truth. This practice inspired me that the lens not only shapes light but also shapes my perception of it. Clarity and distortion are not opposites but coexisting ways of seeing. I stood still, watching the reflections of light and shadow inside the box for a long time. I don’t know, just a thought fly by — perhaps what I truly seek is not the external world, but that point where light and shadow converge—the boundary between the visible and the invisible. I was no longer the creator but merely a witness—of perception, of light, of a silent dialogue between presence and absence. Now, with my tools in hand, I will find my answer! Letter & Bag Dear Mom and Dad, I guess I figured out what I need right now. I have decided to leave, to search for an unspeakable answer, an intangible existence. My soul calls me day and night to explore my true self in an unknown realm, to feel the truth hidden beyond everyday life. I need to capture more and find my focus distance. As for the past, I am grateful. You have given me the best love in this world. You taught me how to find happiness in comfort and peace in instability. However, the meaning in the traditional sense is not the same as my meaning. As for now, I can no longer escape the deep, inner questioning of "existence." Every day, I live in the cracks of these questions, my soul feels trapped in a black box, lost and confused. Finally, the light goes inside… But I still need more. In recent days, I have been looking through my camera obscura. In one day, I placed an egg in front of it, then a stone. At first, they seemed different—fragile and solid, organic and inorganic. But as the light shifted, their shadows blurred, their outlines merged. At one point, I couldn't tell them apart. If even their forms are not fixed, then what about everything else? I suddenly felt that what I see is never absolute. That maybe, the world itself is fluid, undefined. That is why I must go. I am afraid—afraid of leaving this familiar world, afraid of leaving your embrace, afraid of the unknown confusion. But what terrifies me more is that if I stay here, I will never truly know who I am. Please do not worry about me.I will carry your love with me on this journey. Your care is constantly reminding me that no matter how far I go, my roots will always be here. Please trust me.I am not escaping, but searching for my place. I believe I will find a moment when I understand what "existence" looks like and its meaning. I will capture moments of "existence" through my camera obscura, and this time, it will be my dialogue with myself. I do not know what the future holds or what I will ultimately discover. But I know this is a journey I must take, one that I must experience.

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: With all my love, Aether A Bag I checked my backpack one last time. I kept my father’s knife. It’s small but sharp, carved the wood into memory and stories. I slipped it into a side pocket, its weight grounding me. I kept my mother’s compass. Its needle wavered slightly as I held it, As it were pondering the next direction. I kept my journal. Its pages held my unspoken thoughts, sketches of fleeting moments, and fragments of riddles yet unsolved. Lastly, I kept my camera obscura. All my stories started with it. For it, I will find the perfect focus. For me, I will find the meaning of my existence. I checked my backpack one last time. Then my adventure began…

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: (即心即佛，即本解脱） An Egg The first time I saw the egg, the sky had not yet darkened. [The earth in the courtyard was still damp, the air carried the lingering moisture of the night before. Wild grass climbed the wall, its tangled vines casting twisted, elongated shadows that seeped through the cracks as if intent on consuming the space.]10

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: I try to get the egg by brushing away the soil. [The damp grains clinging to my fingers, seeping into my palm. The air thickened, like time on the verge of solidifying. ] I reached out toward the egg, laying still beneath the moist earth as if it had emerged from the darkness itself — Cold to the touch, my fingers met only a thin layer of cold light. The egg stood at the center of my vision, light gently tracing its fragile shell— Like water meeting the stone of a riverbank… It was a promise of existence, yet so illusory it seemed ready to dissolve into the darkness. To be touched by another is to truly exist. The egg doesn't have any boundaries, like something untouched by the chisel of time; a universe not yet hatched; an answer I had yet to find…… I held my breath, listening to its voice — the faint echo of liquid shifting inside as the egg was moved. It floated in the light, at times cracking open, as if ready to contain all things—a silence softer than darkness itself. Sometimes it exists as its whole — a vessel of time, filling the gaps of what once was, carrying the possibilities of what is yet to come; sometimes it's the fragments of the time, dividing light from shadow like the edge of a blade. The camera obscura swallowed light and shadow, folding memory upon itself. Then, there is no existence of past, now and future; there is an egg, and also exists as a stone. [......]

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: A Tree (see a mountain as a mountain) I lay beneath the tree for a long time. [Sunlight filtered through the gaps in the leaves, casting dappled patterns on my face. The wind passed through the branches, making the shadows sway. I feel chill.] The breath of the tree surrounding me—the dampness of the soil, the freshness of the leaves, the bitterness of the bark—the smell is so real. [In my memory] When I was 10 years old, it’s my first time climbing a tree. I stood under the tree, and told myself “See, it’s just a tree.” Stepping carefully onto the thickest branch, I made my way upward. Someone: What’s up there? Aether. A: the tree. S: You just see a tree? Even if you are in the tree? A: Yes, I am in the tree and I see…. [An egg nestled among the branches—like a relic of time itself. ] A: An egg! S: Just an egg? I reached out to touch the egg, its surface was smooth and warm, yet the shape looked like a rock. within, it seemed to hold countless fractured specks of light. A: It is an egg, or maybe it is a rock….. S: Take it. It is your story. A: What’s your mean? I got silence as the answer. Then I climbed higher. I was so focused on seeing the trees that surrounded me that I didn't even notice that my hand was cut by its texture. [The tree’s form blurred, and the world around me grew unfamiliar. The leaves lost their color—fading from deep green to ashen white, then to something transparent. The wind grew heavy, each sway of the branches like the breath of some vast, unseen presence.] The tree spread in all directions—each branch carrying a vision of the future, each root entwined with a different past. I saw my childhood in my small courtyard with an egg resting in my palm. [Present] Within the camera obscura, the image of the tree emerged slowly. Each patch of light concealed a faint, circular shadow—something unhatched, something waiting. I stared in front of the tree again. The image of the tree neither broke nor changed, simply existing in silence—as if time had paused, as if it were an unfinished chapter of a story. The wind swept through the canopy with an egg resting in my palm. A Herb (see a mountain not as a mountain) I sat among the herbs. [The air is thick with their bitter scent. The wind stirred the leaves, making them tremble like whispers in the silence. They grew in the cracks of the earth, their roots buried deep—fragile in appearance, yet stubbornly reaching for the light.] I crushed the herb between my fingers—its sap seeped out, staining my palm as its color. A flavor so bitter that it made me feel sad surrounding me, and I saw the gradual recovery of the wound on my hand. [There is a funeral, one prepared for someone whose name I did know. Seeing the mourners, listening to hushed laments, hearing the dull thud of soil falling onto the coffin. There's a herb that falls where it's buried.] Someone: What is this? Aether. A: A herb, or more than that…… S: What is its name? A: Poison and cure S: I mean its specific name. A: I don’t know and I don’t need to know. It’s just itself. [The herbs wither under the scorching sun, bow under the weight of the storm, decay into the soil. Yet they left behind seeds, waiting for their next awakening—a cycle repeating endlessly, an inescapable fate. As same as the tree I met, but at that time, I could only see its surface, the structure of its physical being.] [Present] Within the camera obscura, the image of the herb flickered and overlapped, like echoes of time. like wounds that had never fully healed and it was blonding again and again. I stared as the light passed through it, casting dim silhouettes onto the background. Every edge of every leaf shimmered with a blurred glow. I am a witness of the funeral or I am the one who is been buried?

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: Earth (see a mountain as a mountain again) I have been lying beneath the earth for a long time. [The wind sweeps in from the horizon, lifting fine dust into the air, blurring the boundary between heaven and earth. The soil is dry, its cracks branching outward in silent expansion, like veins stretching across an unseen body. ] I am listening to the fissure holding traces of the past, every speck of dust carries a story yet to be told. My body touching the earth, a sense of peace full of me, when the earth covers all, the dust settles. S: Who are you bidding farewell to? A: …… I witness it as I experience it, a hollow weight in my chest, yet no true sorrow. I assume this farewell belongs to someone else, that this ritual is for a life already gone. But perhaps the one being buried is me—an unnoticed fading, a self once desperate for answers. S: Who are you bidding farewell to? (Again.) A: It is belongs to me S: Who is burying you? A: Myself. [Present] Within the camera obscura, an image of earth slowly emerges. The earth seeps into the openings, filling the empty spaces, as if the land itself is gently swallowing what remains of its fragile softness. I saw a broken egg lying half-buried in the soil, its cracks branching like riverbeds, light and shadow drifting through the fractures. The edges of the eggshell curl slightly, its broken contours gradually veiled by the rough grain of the soil. A solid rock rests quietly in the egg, as though it has slowly emerged from the depths of the earth.Its once-smooth surface grows dull, layered with hardened sediments, like time itself congealing. The jagged edges sink beneath the earth, the egg’s curve collapses inward, until its last vestige of softness is completely encased in stone.

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: The wind moves across the barren land The soil ceases its murmur The image freezes The funeral is complete (即心即佛，即本解脱） A Rock The last time I saw the rock, dawn had arrived. [The dampness of early morning clung to the earth, carrying traces of the night’s lingering chill. Wild grass still climbed the walls, vines hanging low, their shadows stretching into the cracks, as if seeping through the fissures, slowly consuming the space.] I brush away the soil. Fine particles entwine, a faint roughness pressing against my fingertips. [The air grows dense, as though time has been compressed into a single, heavy fragment. And there, embedded in the layers of earth, the rock rests in silence—stern, unyielding, like a relic descending from the depths of time.] I hear its voice — the sound of stone rolling, its friction against the ground. I touch its texture — nestled within the broken contours. It had always been there. Perhaps it was the first thing that ever existed. The rock remains still within the light, yet at times it fractures, breaking into sand, returning to dust, sinking deeper than darkness itself. The wind swept through the leaves, the shadows rippled. I looked down and saw the stone resting in my palm. Light passes through the lens, casting an image. The world turns. The camera obscura swallowed light and shadow, folding memory upon itself. Then, there is no existence of past, now and future; there is a rock, and also exists as an egg.

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: The camera obscura swallows the final images, dissolving the imprints of time within its depths. Here, all causes and consequences converge, and all questions fall silent. At this moment, I understand — the egg and the rock had never changed, they were never waiting to become something else; they have always been both: soft and whole, vulnerable and complete; they are not opposites but different faces of the same truth. My friend, the moment we stopped seeking, we arrived. In stillness, seeing is not a matter of chasing light, but of receiving it. Thinking is not the absorption of external information, but the filtering of resonance already waiting inside. Within us, the egg and the stone were never separate. Perhaps, we were already free when we met ourselves.

author: Jiayue Wang
thesis_title: Seeing Through the Camera Obscura: Constructing a Belief System & Exploring Existence
paragraph: It is the egg, it is the stone. It is the past and it is the future. 它是鸡蛋，它是石头。它是过去，也是未来。

author: Jiwon Kang

author: Jiwon Kang
thesis_title: The Future of Remote Work
paragraph: Since the early 2010s, MR devices have been commercialized, The future of remote work expanding their applications from specialized industries to those aimed at general consumers. In the 2020s, major companies

author: Jiwon Kang
thesis_title: The Future of Remote Work
paragraph: such as Meta, Microsoft, and Apple have been releasing MR devices, exploring ways to integrate MR into everyday workflows, and developing related content. The Future of Remote Work explores how future remote workers communicate and perform tasks in MR environments. In these environments, remote workers can utilize digital elements that mimic real-world principles, allowing for a more immersive and expanded work experience. This concept is presented as a video simulation, showcasing various situations from the perspective of Maria, a remote worker, as she navigates her common workday in an MR environment from 8 AM to 4 PM.

author: Jiwon Kang
thesis_title: The Future of Remote Work
paragraph: The advancement of technology has always been closely linked to greater improvements in work efficiency. Since the emergence of Mixed Reality (MR) technology, various attempts have been made to utilize it for enhancing work productivity. By enabling immersive, three-dimensional experiences that go beyond traditional flat screen interactions, MR has been actively adopted in various professional training institutions and industrial settings. For example, NASA has leveraged MR technology to provide astronauts with cost-effective and safe training environments for equipment operation. In the medical field, MR allows surgeons to view MRI scan images in 3D, thereby enhancing surgical precision. Additionally, in industrial settings, 3D models are being used to facilitate architectural design reviews and planning. Furthermore, users can access multiple screens and customized Chapter 01 virtual environments from anywhere at any time. In a work setting, MR offers enhanced interaction compared to standard video

author: Jiwon Kang
thesis_title: The Future of Remote Work
paragraph: conferencing, allowing employees to engage more actively in communication and share complex ideas more effectively, thus increasing productivity. Meta also notes that users can collaborate more efficiently in real-time within the same virtual space. Lastly, MR provides experiences that closely resemble reality, making it useful for practicing skills in realistic scenarios.¹ In summary, Meta suggests that the MR’s advantages include natural interaction, provision of realistic experiences, and immersive communication. Having explored the potential of MR in enhancing user experience, the next step is to examine how this potential is being realized through the devices released in the market. To understand this, I analyzed the MR devices from the three major companies. Mixed Reality (MR), a technology that combines AR and VR, blends virtual and physical elements, allowing users to interact with both digital objects and their physical environment. As MR continues to evolve, notable devices such as Meta’s Meta Quest, Microsoft’s HoloLens, and Apple’s Vision Pro have recently been introduced to the market. Among these, Meta, a leading player in MR, highlights the potential of the technology on its website. According to Meta, MR provides a highly immersive experience, making users feel as though they are physically present in the MR environment. Additionally, they emphasize that avatars enable more natural conversations. Unlike traditional video conferencing, avatars allow for freer communication, including non-verbal cues and side conversations. First, Meta provides access to its services through the Meta Quest Figure 3 device, with the most notable application, Horizon Worlds. Meta’s MR services focus on entertainment and communication content, such as VR gaming, social experiences, virtual workplaces, and fi tness. While the platform off ers a variety of entertainment and communication content, the inconvenience of using physical hand controllers and the lack of AR features are noted as drawbacks. Second, Apple provides its services via the Vision Pro device, utilizing iOS software with a focus on work-oriented productivity tools and spatial computing. Its strengths lie in multitasking through multiple virtual screens and natural controls via eye and hand tracking. However, its high price and lack of entertainment content are identifi ed as disadvantages. Lastly, Microsoft off ers services through the HoloLens device, which operates on Windows 11 software and focuses on remote collaboration and training in industries such as manufacturing, healthcare, and education. It enhances effi ciency by overlaying digital information in industrial environments, and its natural controls, such as eye and hand tracking, provide a notable advantage. Nevertheless, the high cost and limited applicability in specifi c fi elds present signifi cant limitations. The analysis of the three companies revealed that each had distinct purposes and strategies for utilizing MR. Meta prioritized communication and entertainment, Apple focused on spatial computing and work-oriented productivity tools, and Microsoft emphasized remote collaboration and professional training. However, a key commonality among all three was their active use of MR for work-related content. Their shared goal was to bridge real world environments and experiences with virtual spaces to enhance productivity. I believe there is great potential in these companies’ use of MR Chapter 02 technology to improve communication among remote workers. This is because the number of remote workers continues to rise,

author: Jiwon Kang
thesis_title: The Future of Remote Work
paragraph: and many workers struggle with having eff ective communication with their colleagues. However, practical solutions to address these challenges are still lacking, making it an urgent issue that requires further discussion. As supporting evidence, according to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, one in fi ve (19.5%) workers in the United States is currently working remotely, as of August 2023.¹ Additionally, the online work marketplace Upwork predicts that by 2025, around 32.6 million Americans, or 22% of the entire workforce, will be working remotely.² In light of this research, remote work has become a common employment model, and the number of remote workers is expected to keep rising.

author: Jiwon Kang
thesis_title: The Future of Remote Work
paragraph: For interaction, I chose to integrate non-verbal communication methods. This approach imitates the way communication works in the real world. In the simulation video, users can use hand gestures as a form of non-verbal communication. I chose hand gestures because they are the most basic and intuitive form of communication, allowing users to convey various meanings simply. When users engage in conversation with others and use hand gestures, the MR device will capture these gestures and display them to others. If these concepts can enable interactions experienced in the real world to be realized in MR environments, I believe it will narrow the boundary between reality and the virtual world, allowing MR technology to evolve from a mere tool into a more natural and intuitive medium for communication. The simulation video is presented from the perspective of the persona, Maria. Maria is a 31-year-old product designer living in Brooklyn. She works remotely and uses MR to design products and communicate with her colleagues. She participates in daily virtual meetings, sharing feedback and collaborating with her team throughout her remote work. To maintain realism while incorporating the convenience of digital Chapter 04 features, these items are designed with interactive interfaces layered on top. For instance, when checking emails, users can

author: Jiwon Kang
thesis_title: The Future of Remote Work
paragraph: take a folded paper from a digital mailbox, unfold it, and interact with the email interface displayed on the paper. Furthermore, users can freely interact with these items, rearranging the items’ positions or adding new ones to customize their virtual desk environment. As a result, users can create a desk setup that matches their preferences—whether it’s neat, creatively decorated, or intentionally cluttered. The personalized MR desk environment can also be saved and used in various locations, such as cafes or libraries, not just at a personal workstation, making it adaptable to diff erent scenarios. When communicating with colleagues, users can enhance their interactions by using 3D hand models that imitate real hand gestures. For example, during a conversation, users can point to themselves by raising their hand or using a fi nger, without Simulation Video The Future of Remote Work is a 2:30-minute video clip that the need for text or voice. Additionally, they can use their hands captures 10 diff erent scenes from the perspective of the persona to pick up objects and pass them directly to colleagues. This Maria, depicting her remote workday from morning to afternoon. provides an experience that makes them feel as if they are in the same space as their colleagues. In the MR environment featured in the video, users can interact with 3D items modeled after real-world objects. These items not only replicate the appearance of their real-world counterparts but also function similarly, allowing users to use them as they would in real life. For example, users can organize documents in a virtual binder that resembles a physical one, fl ipping through the pages to locate specifi c documents.

author: Jiwon Kang
thesis_title: The Future of Remote Work
paragraph: Regarding haptic feedback, current MR devices can generally be categorized into two types: those that rely solely on hand gestures, such as Vision Pro, and those that use hand controllers, like Meta Quest. When using controllers, users can feel vibrations and press buttons, providing a form of indirect tactile feedback. However, this remains a simulated experience and does not replicate the sensation of physically touching objects. I eventually found it in a corner of the terminal, jam- med into one of those boxes where they make you put your luggage for measuring. They had broken the backrest. And while I have trunk control, imagine someone who doesn’t. If you break their backrest, they can’t sit upright. In that case, it took me over a year to get the airline to pay for the damages, and I even had to involve lawyers. author: Jose Pena Inclan
thesis_title: Accessibility Activism Through Design As Satire
paragraph: By Clara, a motorized wheelchair user What I’m complaining about now is that, on the paper they give me when I check in my wheelchair, luggage, and other items, they ask for a series of details, like what type of battery I have and how to dis- connect it. But, for example, they don’t explain what exactly needs to be filled out. My parents, for instan- ce, don’t speak English, and when we asked what the form was, since it was the first time we had seen it, the airline staff at the counter didn’t know either. They had to call other people because they didn’t know what to do with the document. Aside from the section I have to fill out, there’s information for the person hand- ling my wheelchair—basically, the person responsible for transporting it when I get off the plane. Then, there’s another part that needs to be filled out by the person recei- ving it upon landing. Going back to the issue of the wheelchair: they place me in my seat, take away the aisle wheelchair (as I call it), and then take my per- sonal wheelchair as well. My wheel- chair goes with the luggage ●

thesis_title: Accessibility Activism Through Design As Satire
paragraph: By Maite, a blind woman with Parkinson's Having mobility issues and tra- veling by plane is a nightmare. I also have Parkinson’s, so moving around isn’t easy. Airports often bring out a smaller wheelchair to take me onto the plane because my own doesn’t fit. But then, ins- tead of helping me to my seat, they just left me there. If my seat is by the window, I have to crawl over the other seats to get there because no one helps. Can you imagine? Crawling to your seat while people watch? It’s humiliating. And if I’m in the aisle seat, every time someone needs to get out, they step over me. It feels like we’re treated as car- go—shuffled around without any real care or understanding of what we’re going through. It’s not just frustrating; it’s dehumanizing ●

As seen in the Saturday Night Live sketch about Alaska Airlines, satire doesn’t just point out problems; it makes them impossible to ignore. Likewise, experiences like Ome- ga Mart draw attention to broader systemic issues under the guise of entertainment, leaving visitors with questions about the systems they in- teract with daily. For accessibility activism, this approach can create a ripple effect. By raising awareness among the public, satire encourages people to think differently, ask questions, and, eventually, demand better solutions. It may not change an ar- chitect’s mind overnight, but it can create the societal pressure that compels decision-makers to rethink their priorities. In this sense, satire doesn’t just entertain—it plants the seeds for change. author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: Graphic design has always been in the business of prompting behavior. However, as the desire for e(ciency has trumped all else, the cost of consumerism as currently practiced has not only been a loss of craftsmanship, but the loss of both the ability to listen deeply and to connect with others despite personal di)erences. A survey of the Industrial Revolution and the motivations behind many innovations since reveals a distinct attitude in the Western world that conflates progress with e(ciency. +e graphic design industry, as a creative ,eld uniquely involved in the internal workings of capitalism, has been directly impacted by these wider cultural and economic shifts. As current corporate design practice places less emphasis on the iterative process than ever before, a recultivation of the value of slowness is crucial for design to continue being seen as a valued skill. +e importance of iteration is its role in facilitating creative freedom through a multi-step explorative process. It calls for more than a “good enough” mentality and instead creates the space needed to produce excellence in work. +is thesis examines the roots of current understandings of e(ciency and modernization and calls for designers to reestablish the values of creativity and responsibility in the design process due to its role in human communication. In analyzing the design process, this thesis focuses on the need for empathy and pause in current practices, then discusses potential methods and strategies of moving forward into more iterative and empathetic design processes.

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: One of my favorite memories from my time in graduate school is of a design critique. Anyone in the design ,eld, or who has ever received constructive feedback on any project, is probably laughing right now—in what world is a design critique something remembered fondly? But this wasn’t a typical crit session. At the end of my second semester, everyone in the program had to submit a thesis proposal booklet that included research questions, key terms, abstract, literature review, visual research, and plan of action for the following year. In preparation, three of my classmates and I staked out a classroom, flipped on the projector, and spent our Friday night pouring over each other’s booklets. One by one we displayed our work at large scale on the wall, searching for misalignments and typos. We stopped once to get snacks from the campus dining hall, and one other time for a quick dance break, but otherwise packed as much review time into the night as possible. Spread by spread, paragraph by paragraph, we worked through the four booklets to check for errors, ask questions, and discuss the ,ner points of layout design. About one hundred pages later, around two in the morning, the table was scattered with test prints, empty co)ee cups, charging cords, snack wrappers, Xacto knives, and highlighters. We gathered up our belongings and trekked home, exhausted in every way possible but delighted at the work we had accomplished. Design is a time consuming ,eld. At its worst, it’s a constant downward spiral of comparison and competition with deadlines constantly looming and projects piling up at an alarming rate. At it’s best though? Design is a chance for human connection, both through its process and its end product. +at Friday night design session was as full of laughter and encouragement as it was of honest feedback and high standards. Graphic design’s problem solving focus provides numerous opportunities for creative solutions to impact the people and culture around us, just as design itself is impacted by culture. +ough marketing products and services existed, in one way or another, since long before the printing press, the Industrial Revolution marked a distinct turning point in the way that craftsmanship and the making and selling of goods was approached. Large scale production and the availability of cheap goods began a long journey that, with the rapid acceleration provided by digital technologies, has culminated in the hyper-consumerist lifestyle predominant in the western world today. Many current methods of making overestimate the possibil- ities of e(ciency and multitasking while failing to account for the human need for pause, thoughtfulness, and connection. Graphic design has both impacted and been impacted by these larger shifts. It is a creative ,eld, where curiosity and experimentation should flourish. However the hurriedness that has in,ltrated the ways that corporate design is often conducted today has narrowed the space required for this creativity to flourish. One of the biggest changes has been the introduction of digital tools, such as Adobe and Figma, into the design process. +ese tools have opened many doors for creativity and possibility for new kinds of design, yet they have also contributed to the consumerist feedback loop of unoriginal and at times unethical design. One new idea breeds a thousand imitations striving for the same e)ect without consideration for whether or not the application is appropriate or not. As deadline piles upon deadline, it can often feel that those outside the design world, and some inside it, don’t understand that Adobe InDesign isn’t a magic wand one can wave and produce a brilliant pamphlet at the snap of a ,nger. Choosing colors? Takes time. Pairing fonts? Takes time. Editing photos? Takes time. Information flow? Takes time. And don’t even get me started on kerning. +is change in the design industry has a direct impact on the ways the general populace communicates. As three designers wrote in a study on design practices, “+e current models of production, distribution and consumption have stimulated and been stimulated by a culture of overconsumption and exhaustion of the natural resources.”! From ,ve second sound bites to clickbait headlines, Western culture as it currently operates actively perpetuates a culture of restlessness and quick ,xes. +e speed at which consumption now functions results in a design process in which real empathy is often neglected for the sake of keeping up. In a market where social media sets the timeline, it is more pro,table to produce something o)ensive rather than nothing at all. +e prioritization of e(ciency, modernization, and digitalization that was supposed to create a society of leisure has instead created a more deeply felt scarcity of ways for people to ,nd human connection, appreciate the world around them, and thoughtfully consider unfamiliar ways of thinking. However, though design has often been used as a supporter of our hyper-consumerist tendencies, it is also true that design holds the potential to cast a vision for a more empathetic and thoughtful future. In a world where human communication is increasingly mediated by technology, the way communication is designed is increasingly influential. Reincorporating empathy and slowness in the iterative process can change not only the designer’s perspective on their work, but the very work itself and what is being communicated to whom. By its very nature, design is a problem solving process that requires a close attention to detail, a willingness to listen to others’ perspectives, and a motivation to ,nd creative solutions. Each of these skills are ones desperately needed in our modern western culture. In short, to be a responsible designer in the modern western world, one must intentionally cultivate empathy for others and purposeful slowness in iteration throughout the design process.

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: & 1 M. Gasparin, W. Green, and C. Schinckus, “Slow Design-Driven Innova- tion: A Response to Our Future in the Anthropocene Epoch,” Creativity and Innovation Management, vol. 29, no. 4, 2020, pp. 551-565, doi:10.1111/caim.12406.

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: Human beings are inherently social creatures, which means we are de,ned by our methods of communication, whether they be cultural, linguistic, or visual. In an age where social media, algorithms, and visual language can make or break a conversation, the work of designers and brand strategists has a direct impact on the way human interaction takes place. One sloppy logo update can make a company into a warning for generations to come and one potent album cover can become the iconography for an entire summer. Now, being a designer myself, I may be biased. I wouldn’t be in this ,eld if I didn’t deeply believe that it was important. Nevertheless, anyone who lived through the Covid-!9 pandemic and the months- long quarantine it caused would agree that human connection— human communication—is one of the most important parts of a healthy life. Today, a variety of communications technologies such as text messaging, social media, video chat apps like Zoom, and others, often mediate our conversations. As they increase in number and usage, their designs, interfaces, and commercial intentions play more and more of a role in our conversations. Why is it that a green text bubble causes so much pain? How is it that seeing mail in the mailbox creates a moment of joy, even if only brief, while yet another email advertisement is a drudgery? While these are only a few examples of how design mediates conversation today, design’s primary role in society is always to establish communication, which means it is directly impacted by the ways that society changes. As Western culture’s expectations for business and life have sped up to an ever increasing pace, it has demanded faster and faster communications to facilitate capitalism, yet we as a culture seem to value thoughtful design less and less. People scurry past the café where I sit, faces buried in their scarves and ,ngers shaking as they type out hurried texts, heedless of the world outside their phones. It’s justi,ed now, when the icy wind always ,nds a way down your collar and you can’t feel your nose, but the cold weather only reaches so far. Indoors, though the climate could often be considered tropical, slowing down is for the weak. “How are you doing?” is considered a pleasantry and the less conversation that follows, the better. Laptops and phones hum with buzz words like efficiency, bandwidth, and information ar- chitecture. Creativity is only welcomed when it increases sales. Time is money, and there’s never enough. If New York, the arts capital of the world, is any kind of accurate touchstone for the state of human communication, there’s fair reason for concern. How did we even get here? A LITTLE BIT OF CONTEXT historical factors influencing today’s society At di)erent times in history and across di)erent cultures, the idea of progress has meant many di)erent things. But in the last few centu- ries of the Western world, it has become almost synonymous with e(ciency. However, equating these two words implies a value judg- ment. “Progress” is a word with a fairly broad application, conveying forward movement, improvements in a given topic, situation, or chal- lenge. “E(ciency,” on the other hand, almost exclusively refers to a speeding up of things. It denotes the idea of putting in the least possible e)ort for the maximum possible result. Progress communi- cates improvements, which may or may not be more e(cient, and e(ciency may or may not be an improvement as long as it is faster and costs less. For example, in !9!3 Henry Ford rearranged his factory. Previously, each team of workers built a singular vehicle in its entirety before moving onto the next one. Ford’s changes created what we now call the assembly line, where each worker is assigned one task that they repeat on every vehicle that goes through the factory line. +is rev- olutionary change allowed Ford to produce a vehicle in half the time with the same number of workers.! Which also meant they could sell more without raising the price of production. Historically, furniture, clothing, tools, and other objects were crafted by hand by individuals or small teams that followed a singular object throughout the entire process of creation. Carpenters,

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: “Steam! Steam! Steam!” Lithograph Accompanying Song Sheet Sung at c.183&4 Vauxhall Pleasure Gardens and in Music-Halls, stonemasons, bakers, and watchmakers were each expected to be experts from sourcing materials to repairing work done by others in the ,eld, and with the founding of guilds in the !-th century, were formally held to high standards of excellence for their work. Ford’s assembly line exchanged this need for individual excellence and com- munal accountability for a systematic means of assembly that simul- taneously was faster for production and easier to learn, but also decreased worker satisfaction. “+e tedium of moving-assembly-line work proved to be more than many employees could tolerate. Mas- sive turnover prompted Ford to announce the celebrated .5 workday in January !9!/.”- His assembly line was not the ,rst innovation prompted by the need for e(ciency: Ford’s vehicle production lines were inspired by the arrangement of meat packing factories that carried animal car- casses on moving tracks.3 Even before Ford, the Industrial Revolution, of which Ford’s assembly line was a culmination, was prompted by the invention of the steam engine, the era’s trademark of speed. In many ways, striving for e(ciency is bene,cial to society: it has led to far fewer food deserts, the ability to communicate quickly across vast distances, and any number of modern conveniences that are so seamlessly a part of our day to day lives that we only consider them when an issue arises, such a hot water heater breaking, or our favorite snack being unavailable at Trader Joe’s. However, the conflation of efficiency with progress is a dangerous assumption that has also opened door after door for the abuses of knowledge, money, and power. +ough Ford reached his goal of creating a vehicle accessible to the average American family, his success prompted a distinct shift from using workers pro,cient in multiple skills to workers assigned to a single

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: repetitive task, which signi,cantly shaped how Western culture now understands craftsmanship. Despite worker burnout and turnover, the assembly line became not only a permanent ,xture of Ford’s factories, but the standard across multiple industries, helping to cement the im- portance of mass produced goods in American culture. Small workshops and hand crafted goods, though regaining pop- ularity in recent years, are more often than not marketed or perceived as luxury goods with a price tag to match. When craftsmanship is seen as a luxury good, it is more than just quality that is being sold at a premium—the luxury consumers pay for is the craftsman’s time, skills, and the years of training he possesses. To borrow a story from the art world, legend has it that when an admirer recognized Picasso in public and asked for a quick sketch, the artist drew a portrait on a napkin and asked for a million francs. Horri,ed, the admirer ex- claimed that it took him only ,ve minutes to do the drawing. Picasso reportedly replied, “No, it took me /0 years.”5 Whether this particular story is true or not, it is only one in a plethora of tales with the same message: excellent work requires time and dedication to one’s craft. Ford’s purpose in the new division of labor was to save time. When each worker only does one task, the overall process speeds up, but workers understand less about the actual product. +is shift began the devaluing of workers’ knowledge that by the mid-twentieth cen- tury, resulted in a new emphasis on speed in manufacturing that soon became a prioritization of speed and e(ciency in society as a whole. A prime example of this is the rise of futurism and fascism in Italy (!9---!9/3), coming on the heels of the Industrial Revolution. +e newly popularized technology of motorbikes across Europe was accompanied in Italy by a new intensi,cation of rightwing beliefs, a decrease in the perceived value of “weaker” people, and an obsession with the aesthetics of speed. Futurism began in Italy with the publication of Filippo Tommaso Marinetti’s publication of in !909. Set against The Futurist Manifesto the backdrop of rising unrest at Italy’s lack of modernization com- pared to other European countries, Marinetti’s manifesto called for a violent wiping away of all tradition and art of the past in order for the new to emerge. “Have faith in progress, which is always right, even when it is wrong, for it is movement, life, struggle, and hope.”6 +e progress of which he spoke was heavily based upon the glori,- cation of “the Machine,”7 which he believed “made all previous art irrelevant. Futurism will hymn the hard new beauty.” Influenced by cubism and Étienne-Jules Marey’s photographs,8 Futurist artists em- phasized vibrant colors, dynamic movement, and the lack of a static individual in favor of the glori,cation of speed and innovation. Futurist visual culture reveals Marinetti’s vision for a new societal order that emphasized militarism and was closely linked with Benito Mussolini’s fascist regime.!0 In fact, Marinetti “created the idea of the art movement as a quasi-ideological group. Where previous ‘isms’ were facetiously named by their critics, futurism announced itself.”!! He worshiped “the love of danger, the habit of energy and rashness”!- that were all made possible by the automobiles, planes, radios, and other technological advancements rapidly changing day to day life around the world. Marinetti frequently used religious and sexual language to discuss automobiles and steam engines and calls for individuals to give way to the youth, to despise the old and weak, and to glory in war as a cleansing of the earth. “Only speed can kill o) the noxious, nostalgic,

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: 1(1814 “Uomo Nuovo” (New Man) by Mario Sironi, sentimental, paci,st, and neutralist Moonlight,” he wrote. “Italians, be speedy, and you will be strong, optimistic, invincible, immortal.”!3 In his mind, the future was to rule and the Machine was its hand. +e Italian poet called for a blind acceptance of technology and prog- ress no matter the cost. +ough Marinetti ultimately failed to ignite a golden age of speed in Italy, much of his ideology found its way into other modernist movements in their worship of immediacy and dynamism, the fasci- nation with the Machine, and the rejection of tradition. +e mediums may have changed from paint to photography, from automobile to arti,cial intelligence, yet many of these same themes still shape cul- ture today, as demonstrated by the rapid acceptance of social media, the loyalty given to major tech companies like Apple, and the unend- ing search for a more e(cient workflow. Clearly, the Industrial Rev- olution, Futurism, and embracing e(ciency as the highest form of progress have impacted the way that capitalism and communication are conducted today.

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: HURRY CULTURE how putting efficiency !rst impacts us today Similar to the way that the rapid modernization of the Industrial Revolution and the Futurist movement (with the help of World War II) changed the day to day functioning of the average employee, the in- troduction of personal computers in !956 added to the hyper-special- ization of labor, increased the speeding up of production, and contributed to a speeding up of information transmission. Communication that would have previously taken months, days, or hours now happens all but instantly. +e artist Mairead Ni Chroinin writes that “mobile and interactive technologies are sold as technologies of progress and ef- ,ciency, that can help ‘speed up’ aspects of life by a)ording instant communication, immediate access to information, and the ability to carry out certain tasks ‘on the move.’”!5 Modern innovation has drastically changed the abilities of sur- geons to save lives, for food to reach those in need, and for people to escape dangerous storms before they hit. On a much smaller scale, technology allows busy parents the flexibility to work around their children’s schedules, for a sick college student to order soup, and for friends to make sure everyone gets home safe after a party. People in the western world have never had better access to resources, knowl- edge, leisure time, or human connection. However, most people feel like they would be better o) with more money, would say that they need more time to rest, and many express feelings of isolation or depression. As Chroinin points out, those who have constant access (via the internet) also possess the ability to be accessed. +is creates an increased demand on our time, en- constantly ergy, and mental capacity that human beings simply cannot handle. How often has a text message or Teams ping interrupted your workflow? How often have you gotten to the end of the day and felt like you got nothing done because all you did was send emails? How often have you gotten overwhelmed by reading the news and walked away more depressed than you started? Or felt guilty because you haven’t been following the news at all for that exact reason? Alternatively, when was the last time you stepped outside and just stared up at the sky? When was the last time you sat in silence for more than a couple minutes, or even seconds, before you reached for a distraction? When was the last time you considered all of the things you’re grateful for? +e Western World today is one ,lled with social media cleanses, studies on the bene,ts of white noise to reduce stress, and escapist dreams to sheep farms in Ireland. But our poor health, isolation, anxiety, and depression aren’t all just the fault of “technology” or “so- cial media.” Rather, the root of our issues is a cultural mindset that changes the way we interact with these digital technologies, the peo- ple around us, and even our mental health and sense of identity, to name a few examples. Our culture’s glori,cation of e(ciency and digitalization has created a society marked by hurry and worry. hurry culture & its implications A term I’ve coined to refer to this phenomenon is “Hurry Culture.” As a broad term for a cultural state of mind, it encompasses two main points: !. +e prioritization of individualistic hyper-consumerism and constant, instant grati,cation -. +e stigmatization of slowness. It’s characterized by the unstoppable urge to update your iPhone every new release even though your current device remains in like-new condition. It’s the misconception that fast food or precooked meals are cheaper than a well planned meal of fresh vegetables. It’s scrolling facebook while a tv show plays in the background because neither one alone provides enough dopamine anymore. It’s the discomfort of sitting, walking, or driving without a podcast playing because that would make it “wasted” time. Today, a high value of slowness is coun- terintuitive, and often outrightly rejected, despite the implications to our wellbeing. As Jessica Stillman wrote in an article for Inc.com, “leav- ing whitespace in your calendar feels like an indulgence…but constant business actually prevents us from working at maximum e(ciency.”!6 Historically, tools of e(ciency were marketed as giving the cus- tomer more leisure time away from their work. While the same tone is used in many of today’s advertising schemes, the messages have a subtle di)erence. From ,nances to grocery shopping, many adver- tisements today focus on the ability to accomplish tasks while one is doing other tasks or watching their kids or even on vacation. At face value, this newfound ability for multitasking seems like the epit- ome of progress, but it insteads actively designs towards a culture of hurry; without rest, creativity and the ability to problem solve actively su)ers.!7 Further, not only are we actually getting less done than we’re led to believe, but we’re thinking less deeply as well. One side e)ect of Hurry Culture is the craving for simplicity in the midst of an overwhelming society. Opening Instagram, Facebook, X, or TikTok individually is like trying to drink from a ,rehose, and most smartphone users have multiple if not all of those apps. In the bombardment of world events, fashion trends, and “hacks,” we as a culture have lost the ability to hold true complexity and wrestle with the tension critical thought requires. +e human mind has trouble understanding gray spaces; it is much easier to follow Immanuel Kant’s First Categorical Imperative, “Act only on a maxim that you can consistently will to be a universal law.”!8 In other words, something is wrong if it’s always wrong, or right if it’s always right. While universalizability is useful in theory, it is dif- ,cult to apply with empathy to a world full of complex human beings that are comprised of many shades of gray. Walk into a store or watch a couple of advertisements and you’re assaulted with a variety of products that each claim to have the an- swer to your health, popularity, or ,nancial struggles. With so many voices crying for attention, it makes sense that those with the clearest and simplest messages are the most heard. From Nike’s “Just do it.” to Disneyland’s “+e Happiest Place on Earth” a crystal clear com- munication of what a consumer gets from the transaction is essential, and if there are emotions involved, even better (as an ironic side note, Google’s tagline is “Do the right thing”).!9 Under the pressure of Hurry Culture, further investigation by the consumer is often over- looked; make a decision and move on to the next thing. At times, further full transparency is impossible to ,nd, and changing one’s mind is heavily discouraged, as anyone who has tried to cancel an Amazon or Netflix subscription knows. In calling for snap decisions, applauding multitasking, and pro- viding constant entertainment, Hurry Culture’s prioritization of individualistic hyper-consumerism has actually hindered the life of luxury and leisure it was trying to create. A quick survey of modern Western culture shows that in the search for comfort and ease, we’re losing the ability to create innovative solutions, wrestle with complex ideas, and to empathize with those di)erent than ourselves. As Ross

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: HURRY CULTURE IN DESIGN so, what does all of this have to do with graphic design? +e assumption that e(ciency is always bene,cial or the best option has oversimpli,ed the way we see the world, and thus has influenced the way we design. As Sherry Turkle described in her -0!- TED Talk, inhabitants of the digital age are far more likely to settle for constant yet shallow intake instead of pursuing nourishment through deeper beauties or intentional connection with others.! Over a decade later, we are only beginning to see the implications of what growing up with an overabundance of information and entertainment means as teachers report increasingly shortened attention spans and rapidly declining lesson comprehension.-

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: In short, Hurry Culture has created an overly dichotomized world and an audience that wants immediate, clearcut answers. Consumers rarely have the mental or calendar space for complexity nor the energy to critically engage with everything they encounter. According to a study done in -0!/, “the average visitor spends !5 to 30 seconds in front of a work of art.”3 If this is how much time people spend in a museum, a space created for lingering and contemplation, how much faster is their average day to day? Now to be fair, the world of design and advertising have heavily contributed to this world. Every adver- tisement was created by someone, and branding campaigns don’t spontaneously appear out of thin air. However, there is a common misconception that Adobe is a magic wand and that Canva is a fairy godmother. +e “I want it and I want it now” attitude cultivated by Hurry Culture has fundamentally devalued the intentional thinking, extensive research, and creative process of well crafted design. To many, throwing away all technology and returning to the world of printed advertisement and face to face communication is the best, or at least the easiest, solution. Yet, if Shakespeare has taught us anything, it’s that misunderstanding, miscommunication, and mis- representation happened long before the invention of the smart phone or Facebook. As discussed in chapter one, the issue is less the technology itself and more the cultural mindset of hurry predomi- nant in the modern western world. So rather than setting ,re to Meta’s headquarters, we need to rethink our approach to creating and consuming. As designers, we need to thoughtfully consider how design has played a part in intensifying Hurry Culture and how that can be reversed through creative means.

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: “Wellbeing Notice! Let’s normalize these. Love this!” -&-44 Jen V., April 3&, PAUSE AND EMPATHY why responsible design requires empathy Although some public ,gures and groups have begun to speak on our need to combat Hurry Culture with a revaluing of slowing down, they typically focus on the ways that pausing bene,ts the individual in their private life. It increases mental and physical health; it’s better for the planet; it gives more time for personal community. +ere are some individuals and companies that have begun to implement as- pects of slowness into corporate life, as demonstrated by the recent conversation around an X (previously Twitter) post highlighting a boss who included a “Well Being Notice” at the end of his email./ Still, true attempts to combat Hurry Culture on a communal or cor- porate level are few and far between. With design’s direct role in consumerist habits, the role of a de- signer is one ,lled with responsibility for the work they create. In the digital age, when nothing can ever really be deleted, this responsibility is even greater. However, to rephrase Spiderman’s uncle Ben’s wise words, with that great responsibility comes a great privilege. Art has for centuries been in the business of changing the trajectory of cul- ture, from the Renaissance’s shift towards secular themes and the study of accurate anatomy to the Impressionists’ careful consider- ation of mundane people and events. Although typically seen as much more of a practical craft than a ,ne art, design still belongs to this rich history of cultural interpretation. Design’s close connection and interweaving with the inner workings of western capitalism puts designers in a unique position to act as advocates and to discourage the momentum of Hurry Culture. Positioning designers as advocates is more than simply champion- ing human centered or transformational design tactics. Rather, to be a responsible designer requires a cultivation of understanding and empathy that in turn leads to a desire or need for purposeful slowness in the design process. +e role of a designer is, at its core, to help others see the world, a problem, a product, in a new way. One of the trademark distinguish- ers of design is that it is created to establish a dialogue between two entities, whether that is between two people, a person and a brand, or two brands. As designers we are taught to think about the person on the other side of our design and use their perspective as the start- ing point for communication. What are their needs? +eir desires? +eir frustrations? And then, how can the product we are making or marketing meet those needs, ful,ll those desires, or calm those frustrations? Only when those questions are answered will design successfully make or market a product to the rest of the world. +e use of personas in UX design is a great example of this. Personas create a general pro,le of the type of person who may be interested in a product. A persona’s main goal is to be speci,c enough to research their desires and pain points, while still being broad enough to capture a wide audience. Typically encom- passing general demographics, habits, and interests, personas help a designer determine how to best reach their target audience, capture their attention, and convince them of the brand’s message. When done with careful research, personas can help product design- ers truly make a di)erence in customer’s lives by understanding where their target audience is coming from. On the other hand, when done without adequate resources or time devoted to understanding users, the use of personas can quickly become a list of assumptions or even stereotypes. +e key di)erence between these two scenarios is the ability to develop empathy for real people. +is requires a vulnerability and willingness to be wrong: as Atticus Finch said in To Kill a Mockingbird, “You never really understand a person until you consider things from his point of view… until you climb in his skin and walk around in it.”5 To communicate a new perspective, one has to see from a new perspec- tive, and to see from a new perspective, one has to be still and observe. you can’t have empathy if you don’t slow down If a designer’s job is to understand how consumers see the world and help them see it in a new way, then designers also need to be able to see the world in creative and interesting ways. By its very nature, this requires time: “A -0!/ study in the Journal of Experimental Social Psy- chology found that bored people ‘are more likely to engage in sensation seeking’—that is, to look for activities or sights that engage their minds and stimulate the brain’s reward centers. +ese people are more prone to ‘divergent thinking styles’—the ability to come up with creative new ideas. ‘+us, boredom may encourage people to approach re- wards and spark associative thought.’”6 +ere is a sort of deeper listening—a willingness to pay attention to small details, to comprehend and retain information rather than merely hearing just to respond—that is necessary to truly reach an audience. Reflecting on Colline Notre Dame du Haut, a chapel he designed in Ronchamp, France, Le Corbusier said, “Never for a min- ute did I intend to make it an object of wonder. My preparation? Sympathy for the other, for the unknown, and a life led in the bru- tality of existence.”7 As the architect himself notes here, one of the reasons his chapel was such a success is because he took the time to listen carefully and deeply both to the people sponsoring his project but also the location and legacy of worship on that hill.8 For designers, slowing down needs to be more than taking time to rest, to recharge before heading back to work. Rather it must be an inherent part of any responsible designer’s routine, crucial for achiev- ing any level of empathy and longevity. In an industry increasingly overwhelmed by the constant deadlines and rush pushed by digital technologies and social media, how then can intentional slowness be reincorporated into design practice? +ankfully, the very process of design provides an opportunity.

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: Every graphic designer has a slightly di)erent process, but somewhere in the process of every experienced designer is either a period of setting the project aside or the decision to revisit ideas multiple dif- ferent times. For some it’s at the beginning, taking time to truly absorb the project brief, to let the details stew in the mental conglomeration of previous work, passive observation, and life experience. For others it’s in the midst of the drafting process, working furiously to get ideas on paper then stepping away to wipe the slate clean before seeing the project anew. Sometimes it’s a conversation with roommates or clients or strangers. Sometimes it’s taking a nap. Taking this time to pause allows the designer to forget the project for a moment, to gather new inspirations and solutions, to take time to rest. In whichever way it appears, this pause often involves obser- vation or leisure before returning to the project with an eye for eval- uation. +is allows them to come back to the task with fresh eyes that will notice small mistakes or see new opportunities that they wouldn’t have seen otherwise. I studied copyediting in college and the most repeated mantra of any credible author or editor is to walk away from the project between drafts: David Ogilvy, often referred to as the Father of Advertising, said, “Never send a letter or a memo on the day you write it. Read it aloud the next morning—and then edit it.”9 Our brains are wired to recognize patterns and to organize infor- mation, however that can be detrimental to the creative process. Much like writing, a designer needs a mental refresh if they wish to fully explore the potential of an idea. Iteration, or revisiting ideas multiple di)erent times, is similar to taking a pause in many ways, but is inherently a more “productive” step in the design process. While pausing is marked by a complete lack of active production, iteration produces many, many di)erent potential solutions in a wide ranging exploration of ideas. While it- erating, a designer creates and creates and creates, making both large

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: and small changes in color, spacing, typography, shapes, and size. While pause and iteration have their di)erences, there is much over- lap in the way they are approached in the modern, western, consum- erist design process—they are both frequently overlooked.
