author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: ITERATION & EXCELLENCE iteration’s importance to excellence in design +anks to client-designer relationships and the need for last minute edits, there always has been and always will be some form of iteration in the design process. People are flawed, minds are changed, colors are critiqued. However, the kind of iteration I am discussing describes the hours spent in front of a notepad sketching a hundred di)erent “h”s in order to ,nd that one spark of an idea waiting to burst into life. It’s the getting lost in your own document because there are too many logos that are each ever so slightly di)erent from one another that neither Adobe nor your brain can handle it anymore. It’s the eye-blurring, mind-frying, wrist aching moment of waking out of a stupor and realizing you found a beautiful, wonderful solution to the design brief that you wouldn’t have arrived at if you hadn’t spent all that time drawing little circles. Nonetheless, the vast majority of those explorations will never see the light of day and as a result are often seen as a waste of time, energy, and resources. +e drafting and ex- ploration parts of the design process are commonly shortened to the bare minimum in order to speed up the timeline of a product while retaining adequate time for client feedback and edits. Clients are thrilled with stories of iconic logos being drawn on napkins during ideation meetings: really, design is simple; it doesn’t take time. But the reality is that more often than not, grand inspira- tion is the result of layer upon layer of work rather than the spur of the moment doodle. Paula Scher’s Citibank logo was a result of her years of experience and labor of love. In essence, that logo was the ,nal result of hundreds of other logos she had worked on throughout her career. As a designer develops, their iterations may become fewer, as “they have less bad ideas they need to get out ,rst” according to one of my professors, but iterations will always be important.

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: iteration as a mark of a healthy designer My ,rst semester of my undergraduate studies, I tried to double major in graphic design and English literature. I met with multiple professors that fall semester, but ultimately by the spring was dis- couraged from the idea by my advisor, probably for the better. +at meant that in the midst of the Covid-!9 quarantine, I had to decide the course of my entire future. I was struggling to keep up in my foundational design classes, and deeply missed spending hours at a time writing. In a mild existential crisis, I emailed one of my design professors and basically asked him if it was worth continuing to pursue design. Although long lost to the ethers of the internet, I’ll never forget his kind response. He didn’t tell me what to do, and he certainly didn’t make any promises that I would “make it” as a designer, he simply told me that design takes work. It’s a problem solving process, and that if I was willing to put in the work to think critically, to try again and again and again to create visual solutions, then I would do okay. In more words, he told me that my skill as a designer depended on my willingness to iterate. He was right. +ere is a learning through working, iterating, and making that can’t be learned just by choosing options or going with the ,rst solu- tion that comes to mind. Although this story is just my personal experience, many of my peers and other more experienced designers would agree: there is an understanding of a subject that comes through participation that can’t be taught by observation. Commu- nication is a complicated, and often messy, process that requires a deep level of empathy that can be reached only by time spent with the subject. By condensing the iterative process, or cutting it out of design practice altogether, both innovative solutions and empathetic nuance is lost. Additionally, as practical as graphic design is, it is ultimately a cre- ative ,eld, and like any artist, designers tend to put a piece of themselves into the work. No matter what another person may say about a project, no matter how excellent the work is considered, when a designer feels rushed, there tends to be lingering dissatisfaction with the project. Over the long term, this can lead to burnout and frustration as their creative muscles aren’t allowed to be worked properly.

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: PRACTICAL STEPS how can this be put into practice? As part of my research, I explored di)erent methods, tools, and strat- egies that designers could implement to reprioritize iteration and pause in the design process. In the modern design industry, it can often be easiest and fastest to jump straight from receiving the design brief to working in Adobe, Figma, or Canva. Most of the designers I know or have interviewed typically do a couple sketches on paper ,rst, but the most signi,cant chunk of time is spent working with digital tools. +ough rarely discussed outside of foundational art classes, working with digital tools is itself a design choice that has its freedoms and its limitations just the same as choosing a typeface or paper size. +e digital nature of these tools impacts the way a designer approaches the project and often adds to the outsiders’ illusion that designing something is a fast and simple process. To counteract this perception, I created a list of some of the many methods and materials for making that visually represent the amount of time it takes to create a piece. Further, each of these methods ask the designer to step away from their digital tools and engage with physical objects before immediately choosing their preferred default design software.

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: MATERIALS Cyanotypes are traditionally created through a process of mixing photosensitive chemicals and exposing them to sunlight. +is process can be signi,cantly sped up using a UV light, however even then cyanotypes take anywhere from ten to twenty minutes to fully de- velop. After developing, the chemicals must be rinsed o) and the paper hung to dry before the process is complete. Minimum Time Needed: -0 minutes +is method goes back to the earliest days of graphic design. Letter- press printing requires a meticulous arrangement of metal type pieces and careful handling of spacing, font, and sizing. Once the type is arranged prints can be made, which result in a texture and typo- graphic quality that is impossible to replicate with laser printers. Minimum Time Needed: ! day

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: 5& Watercolor is one of the most freeform and unique painting methods because it requires artists to work from light to dark. Additionally, because it is a thin, water based solution rather than a thicker, oil based medium, it tends to be more unpredictable and defy control. +is kind of painting requires forethought and patience, counterin- tuitive to most digital tools. Minimum Time Needed: -0 minutes Embroidery has long been a method of decorating clothing and add- ing flourishes to other goods like handkerchiefs and pillows. +ere is a wide variety of stitches, knots, and patterns to choose from, not to mention the rainbow of colors available. While traditionally em- broidery is a textile art, the techniques can also be used on printed pieces and sculpture. Minimum Time Needed: - hours ! Monoprints and Linocuts are both methods of printmaking that require careful planning and layering of colors. Linocut printing is done by carving a design into a substrate that is then coated with ink, and multiple prints can be made by reapplying ink. On the other hand, monoprinting is created by coating the substrate with ink and removing the ink to create the design. Because of this technique, monoprints can only be printed once or twice before requiring a fresh application of ink and recreating a design. Minimum Time Needed: -0 minutes

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: Ito1 “Onion Fatigue” Linocut Print by Katie +ere are many di)erent processes that can be used to create botan- ical prints, however the most common involves boiling or steaming leaves and other plant materials on fabric or paper. +e boiling water extracts natural dyes from the plant matter while also capturing the patterns and shapes in which they are placed on the substrate. Minimum Time Needed: ! hour Rust printing is in many ways visually similar to cyanotypes, but the process is very di)erent. To create a rust print, metal scraps are lay- ered between sheets of vinegar-soaked paper. +e process takes two to three entire days for the metal to oxidize and develop the orange rusty color which then bleeds onto the paper. Minimum Time Needed: - days Pointillism is done by applying small dots of color that together form an image. +is technique doesn’t allow for mixing colors before ap- plication but rather by applying the raw tones to the canvas in such a way that they visually blend together. +is method requires careful sketching beforehand to understand where each color will be placed and with what proximity. Minimum Time Needed: 3 days Polaroids have gained much popularity in the last few years for their retro aesthetic and authentic feel. +e manual process of inputting the ,lm, accounting for lighting and waiting to see the result requires patience and flexibility and is a very di)erent process than the kind of photography many are accustomed to thanks to smartphones. Minimum Time Needed: !5 minutes Wet plate photography is a technique pulled from historical photog- raphy that requires the materials to be prepped, exposed, and devel- oped in a short yet intense window. Collodion can be used dry but requires a much longer exposure time, which is why the wet plate process was invented. Because of the quick timeline required by the wet chemicals, a photographer needs to understand the process inside and out to achieve the proper results. Minimum Time Needed: !5 minutes Texture rubbing is a relatively easy methodology. In its simplest form, it is using a wax crayon to capture the texture of whatever is beneath a piece of paper. However, even this process requires thought and exploration to ,nd interesting textures and apply the crayon to the surface with the proper amount of pressure to capture details without smudging them. It is a process that can’t be replicated digitally with- out losing its spark. Minimum Time Needed: 5 minutes +is method is a drawing technique that is used to help artists learn observation skills and attention to detail. +e artist draws an object

paragraph: with a single continuous line all without looking at the paper. It re- quires focus and a deep awareness of spatial relationships, both in the three-dimensional object’s space and on the two dimensional paper. Minimum Time Needed: - minutes Screen printing is a common process used for all sorts of fabric goods but can also be done on paper to create interesting textures and lay- ering. +e process involves preparing a mesh screen with the design according to each individual color then using the screen to apply ink to the desired surface. Minimum Time Needed: 7 days Shibori Dyeing is a Japanese technique where one tightly bunches or folds fabrics to create patterns when dyed, typically with indigo. +e patterns are created as the folds create a resist that prevents the dye from reaching all of the fabric and can be done with rubber bands, stitching, or wooden strips to achieve the intricate designs. Minimum Time Needed: ! hour Batik fabric dying is one of the oldest techniques in history. It involves decorating fabric with hot wax then soaking the fabric in dye. +e wax acts as a resist such that when it is removed after the dying process, a pattern is revealed. +e process can be repeated multiple times for a variety of pattern and color e)ects. Minimum Time Needed: 3 days Encaustic painting is another process that involves working with hot wax. Unlike batik dyeing, the wax is mixed with pigments to create di)erent colors and then used like paint. +e wax is layered to achieve di)erent textures and colors, then traditionally a hot tool is passed over the wax to “burn in” the colors and create an even surface. Minimum Time Needed: - days + 6 months to cure +is glazing technique involves gathering wood ash, sifting through it to remove large chunks, rinsing the ash to remove other impurities, then grinding the ash to a ,ne powder and adding to a base glaze. +is glaze is applied to ceramics like other glazes. When ,red, the wood ash creates unique textures and colors as it melts and bonds to the clay. +ere is a huge variety of options within the process, from the kind of wood used for the ash to the ability to use the ash alone as a glaze. Minimum Time Needed: -.5 weeks For example, cyanotypes are traditionally created through a process of mixing photosensitive chemicals and exposing them to sunlight. +is process can be signi,cantly sped up using a UV light, however even then cyanotypes take anywhere from ten to twenty minutes to fully develop. After developing, the chemicals must be rinsed o) and the paper hung to dry before the process is complete. Similarly, texture rubbing is a relatively easy methodology. In its simplest form, it is using a wax crayon to capture the texture of what- ever is beneath a piece of paper. Yet, even this process requires thought and exploration to ,nd interesting textures and apply the crayon to the surface with the proper amount of pressure to capture details without smudging them. It is a process that can’t be replicated digi- tally without losing its spark. +e second list I created was of strategies for designers to apply throughout the process. Each strategy aims to help the participant consider their work from a slightly di)erent, and hopefully new, point of view with the end goal of providing new inspiration, understanding the client more, or taking time to sit with design decisions before moving onto the next.

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: STRATEGIES Words and images have di)erent purposes, strengths, and weak- nesses, they communicate di)erent things. By creating a word based mood board, you’re opening the door to ideas and pathways that images wouldn’t. Instructions: !. Gather words relevant to your project. -. Consider ,ve senses, emotions, philosophical, and spiritual words. 3. Nouns, verbs, adjectives, adverbs, pronouns, preposi- tions, exclamations, etc. /. Find words from other languages. 5. Arrange the words in interesting combinations to ,nd new associations. 6. If stuck, start by describing visual objects. Design projects are about communication, which means that they have a tangible message to a physical someone in a physical space. +e space that message lives in has the potential to change the way it is approached and understood. Walking around in that physical place allows you to understand your messaging a little bit more. Instructions: !. Go to a physical place where your audience will come across your design. -. Take notes of the small details of the place; make sure to engage all ,ve senses. 3. Who are the primary people that will spend time here? /. What is unique about the space? How might the place take away or add to your message? Despite the abundance of digital tools to speed up our design process in today’s world, there is something that can only be learned by getting your hands dirty. Sketching provides di)erent sorts of freedoms and limitations than working with a pen tool on a screen, by taking ad- vantage of the di)erences, it will allow you to explore new ideas. Instructions: !. Pick up a pencil and a piece of paper. -. Sketch freely and widely, everything that comes to mind. 3. Don’t censor your ideas just yet. /. Set a numerical goal of doodles to reach before you allow yourself to switch to another mode of making, the more the better. A conventional storyboard, usually images highlighting key plot points, visually outlines a set of key moments in an animation or interaction. Although design is primarily a visual practice, designs are never received as solely visual artifacts. Storyboarding with other senses allows you to consider what other avenues of design and place are available to you. +e medium of the design is just as important as the design itself. Instructions: !. Set up a conventional storyboard but instead of sketching visuals, think through how someone might experience it with each of the ,ve senses. -. How does sound, touch, taste, or smell change through- out the artifact’s lifespan or use? 3. How might you communicate those ideas of touch, taste, etc. visually? Information mapping is a common technique used in UX and other heavily research based design processes that seeks to understand and provide avenues for guiding an audience through information. +is strategy seeks to help designers understand not just how information is perceived in order, but how an audience might understand information

paragraph: 6& emotionally because of a given order. How someone feels about in- formation is just as important as how they feel about it. Instructions: !. +ink through the hierarchy of your design and how it makes the audience feel. -. How might someone be feeling before, during, and after they come across your design? Regardless of the kind of design being done, whether product design, social media work, book layouts, or something else, a design always lives in a physical space. Understanding that space is important to understanding how a design will be perceived. Instructions: !. Gather a wide variety of supplies: cardboard, paper, sticky notes, tape, rubber cement, paint, crayons, etc. -. Use those supplies to recreate the space where your ,nal design will live. 3. How does recreating the space change your perspective. /. What does it mean to interact with your design in a tactile manner? 5. Does it change the message or tone? Historically, di)erent shades, tones, and hues of color were created by mixing pigments by hand. By stepping away from the computer and mixing paints or pigments by hand, you will gain a better understanding of how colors mix and change one another. Colors you didn’t expect will appear, pushing you to step past your initial assumptions and color choices. Instructions: !. Gather paints or pigments that allow for easy mixing, as well as a white and/or black sheet of paper. -. Try di)erent combinations – even if you only have the three primary colors, there is a world of di)erent hues and tones to explore. 3. Mix colors you wouldn’t normally mix and think about how each would pair with others you’ve created. Design decisions should be made with thought and intention, but in the hurry and rush of deadlines, it’s often easier to stick to habits or intuition. +is strategy takes the decision completely out of the de- signer’s hands then asks them to create a justi,cation in order to prompt deeper thoughts on the ,nal decisions. Working with design decisions that aren’t your own provides practice in working outside of your normal habits. Instructions: !. Use a dice, game spinner, online random picker, or AI tool to randomly decide what colors, type, medium, layout, and spacing to use. -. Design according to the random decisions and write a few bullet points for why each decision might work. 3. Repeat this process several times to explore avenues you wouldn’t have thought of previously.

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: Texture Rubbing +ere is a wealth of typefaces available online, and every designer’s temptation is to spend hours scrolling for the perfect one. +is strategy seeks to side step that by encouraging you to start from scratch and create your own lettering. +e time and place for using digital fonts is often, however they should be chosen with inten- tional thought and precision, which drawing type by hand ,rst can help provide. Instructions: !. Start with a blank page and a pencil or pen. -. Write down a list of feeling words: what should the typeface evoke? 3. What should it communicate through its visual forms? /. How could the letters ,t together in unique ways? 5. Start sketching until you either have a ,nal form or know exactly the typeface you need. Most design projects are some combination of image and text. Digital tools make selecting text and creating large color ,elds as simple as pressing a few buttons, but throughout history it was much more complicated. Creating designs through collaging messages opens doors for new ideas and interesting combinations. Instructions: !. Gather old newspapers, magazines, packaging, images from your day to day life. -. Create or recreate your design project using only what you can ,nd around you. 3. What kinds of innovations do these limits encourage? /. What new additions or angles have you found based on the collage? Like many other traditional crafts, hand binding or packaging books, flyers, appliances, and other goods are most often left to machines due to their e(ciency. However, taking the time to understand how di)erent products and their ,nishing touches ,t together is an im- portant part of the design process. By creating the binding or pack- aging of a design by hand, you’ll gain a better understanding of how the many pieces ,t together and how the designs of each part com- plement the others. Instructions: !. Print out your design and trim it to size. -. For books and flyers, bind the pages together and ex- periment with di)erent methods. 3. For other products, spend time creating the box or packaging and seeing how it presents the product. With deadlines pressing in on every side, it can be easy to forget that we are physical beings with real needs. Sometimes the best thing we can do for a project is give our mind a rest and let the ideas stew in the background. When we step away from our work it not only helps us come back with new energy but the time away often gives us better perspective on the scale or message of the project. Our minds don’t just shut o), but instead will continue puzzling over the design subconsciously so that when we return we have new ideas and are able to ,nd mistakes more easily. Instructions: !. Close your laptop. -. Put the sketchbooks away. Get outside, read a book, or do something else that helps you feel refreshed. In the world of social media, so many design projects are created to go in small spaces and comprehended in seconds. Familiarity with a medium is in many ways a good thing, but it can also lead to creative block and boredom. Experimenting with large scale designs can lead to new ideas and a renewed excitement for the project. Instructions: !. Find a large scale design avenue, whether it be a poster, billboard, café mural, or another setting and create a design related to the brief. -. Explore using the logo, text, colors, and other visual elements in a variety of scales and cropping to ,nd new inspiration. Every design project has a speci,c message it’s trying to communicate, which means that it also has a target audience it’s trying to reach. Designs should cater to that audience’s needs, desires, fears, and frus- trations. By turning the table and encouraging designers to design for those not in the target audience, it encourages deeper thought on who the target is, what they really want, and why. Instructions: !. Write down everything you know about the target au- dience of your project. -. Who is NOT in the group? 3. Write down as many things as you can think of in the non-target audience. /. What kinds of design would stand out to them? What are they looking for? As a form of communication, an e)ective design needs to be clear and to the point. Text outside of the required brief is usually discour- aged in order to prevent confusion. Nonetheless, text and other visual elements have the potential to add a richness to a project when used well. +is strategy encourages a thoughtful maximalism as a means of exploring new ways of thinking. By adding to the design, it en- courages you to consider the existing hierarchy and just what the audience will actually desire to learn from the design. Instructions: !. Take your design and experiment with adding more information or visual cues. -. How does it change the meaning? 3. What does repetition add or take away from the design? /. Where does the new information ,t into the hierarchy? Most projects come with a speci,c set of written information that must be on the ,nal product, such as dates, locations, and descriptions. However there are more ways to communicate than just text; this strategy seeks to encourage the designer to explore communication without explicit text. By removing elements of the design, it forces you to rethink the hierarchy and importance of the project’s infor- mation. It will push you to consider how you can convey the same message with fewer elements. Instructions: !. Take your design and experiment with removing dif- ferent pieces of information or visual cues. -. How does it change the meaning? 3. What does the missing information do to the audience? Each strategy seeks to encourage the designer to think about their design in a new way. For example, most project mood boards focus on the visual feel of a project, the colors, the type, the vibe. Words and images have di)erent purposes, strengths and weaknesses; they com- municate di)erent things. By creating a word based mood board, it opens the door to ideas and pathways that images wouldn’t necessarily reach. While a mood board is a strategy that would be applied towards the beginning of a design process, other strategies could be applied later on or at multiple di)erent points. Working with pigments and man- ually mixing colors is something that could be used at the beginning to explore and gather ideas and also reapproached later in the design process when ,nalizing color selection. During the brainstorming phase this strategy would take on a more exploratory and playful tone, while later it could be approached more scienti,cally. At both points it pushes the designer to consider their project from a new angle and to discover new ways of thinking and designing through the making process. HANDBOOK

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: Very few of the methods or strategies mentioned are ones common to graphic design practice, however each has something valuable to o)er. At their base level, they slow down the design process, which, beyond my previous ,ndings, numerous studies have shown to aid creativity: “by slowing down, some participants found that they de- veloped a more ‘focused attention,’ discovering newfound distinctions and rich di)erentiations in the world around them”- and “[s]cience shows that boredom actually increases creativity, as do activities like long walks (and showers) that demand just enough attention to allow our minds to wander.”3 To document these ,ndings, I created a booklet to share at Pratt Institute Communication Design’s Graduate Inquiries Exhibition Fall -0-/—Pause: A Designer’s Handbook for Slowing Down. +e booklet is divided in half with each half containing either the mate- rials or the strategies listed above. To distinguish the two, I designed the booklet with two orientations: the top of the page on the Materials half is the bottom to the page on the Strategies half. +e center spread is a cyanotype full-bleed image that serves as an example of pausing as well as a divider between the Materials and Strategies. +is kind of organization also provides the dual purpose of asking the reader to slow down a moment and physically engage in the reading as they transition from the Strategies to the Materials or vice versa. Across the booklet, each spread contains one of the above Mate- rials or Strategies with their descriptions or instructions. Each

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: 7& Material also has an image as a visual example of the technique. In keeping with the theme of slowing down, the 3x5 inch booklets were produced by hand from printing to cutting to binding. Printed in black and white on newsprint, I was able to make one hundred copies, which were then displayed at the Graduate Inquiries Exhibition alongside a plaque encouraging viewers to take a booklet with them when they left. About eighty copies were taken over the course of a couple hours, and multiple people approached me to share their excitement to try one or another of the techniques described in the booklet. +ough demonstrated on a small scale, the overwhelmingly posi- tive response speaks to the desire that many feel to step outside of their typical—e(cient—design habits and take the extra time to foster a deeper curiosity and creativity in the design process. Iteration and healthy, inspiring design go hand in hand, and prioritizing pause in day to day design practice is crucial if it is to remain a relevant and genuine form of human communication. 1 Katie Ito, “Onion Fatigue,” Watercolor and Linocut Print, Indianapolis, Indiana, Accessed December 5, 2024. 2 Máiréad Ní Chróinín, “Slow Down (You Move Too Fast): Designing Mechanics to Encourage Practices of ‘Ecological Perception’ through Mobile Digital Performances,” International Journal of Performance Arts & Digital Media 18, no. 2 (July 2022): 232–48, doi:10.1080/1 4794713.2022.2101285. 3 Jessica Stillman, “Research Suggests We’re All Getting Less Creative and Scientists Think They Know Why,” Inc.Com, July 6, 2021, https:// www.inc.com/jessica-stillman/creativity-crisis-torrance-test.html.

author: Kaelen Rodgers
thesis_title: of graphic design
paragraph: Few people would take me seriously if I said that graphic design is a demanding job. Sure, it entails the usual wrist, shoulder, back, and eye strains of any desk job, but it’s not a physically demanding job by any comparison. Graphic design strains the heart. It is an opportunity for genuine human connection. It’s a kind of art, in more ways than one. Perhaps I’m revealing my naivety, or just plain old inexperience by saying so, but I still think there’s truth to it. No one goes into graphic design for the money. So it must be something else that con- tinues to draw people to the ,eld. Hurry Culture has had its way with much of design practice today, from rushed drafts and frantic critique sessions to underdeveloped brand systems and prematurely published marketing campaigns. Designers older than me often caution that one has to truly know and love their craft, and even then, it’s easy to get burnt out in the fast paced, high demand environment. +at’s usually said speci,cally of advertising, but it’s increasingly true of the rest of the graphic design industry. +e introduction of digital tools, and now arti,cial intelligence, into the design process has brought real bene,ts, but it has also contributed to the hurried mindset and furthered the e(- ciency driven tendency to choose from options provided by the in- ternet. In this rapidly changing landscape, designers need to return to some of the oldest tools in our toolbox, ones of character in addi- tion to technical skill. One has to have a thick skin to be a designer, to stand up at critique and have all of your flaws—no, I mean your work’s flaws— pointed out for all to see. To risk starting a conversation only to be rejected. To iterate again and again. And again. But a good designer needs to have a tender spot too, the willingness to o)er work daily, to start conversations, to keep iterating because human connection is deeply valuable—beautiful. A responsible designer needs a willingness to learn from the past in order to move forward. Graphic design comes from a rich tradition of beauty and innovation and connecting to this legacy of design is an opportunity to learn from the graphic design process on a scale larger than any single project. As is commonly said, those who don’t know history are doomed to repeat its mistakes. Being a designer that creates thoughtful visual solutions requires vulnerability and humility. +e best design solutions come from the work of a team, which sometimes means working with people you don’t naturally like and being willing to accept critique even on a bad day. Both vulnerability and humility are prerequisites to empathy, because real empathy requires closeness to others and a willingness to listen. +e iterative process is a journey of humility as it takes a recognition that the ,rst solution won’t be the best one and that ,nding creative solutions requires making bad work in the process. A poster or brochure is a relatively simple project, but small dif- ferences in color, typography, and layout can change the way the in- formation is perceived. Quality design is a result of empathy for the audience, taking time to pause, and iteration after iteration. Much like any design project, being a designer is itself a process that requires time, trial and error, and determination. +ough the cultural land- scape, tools of the trade, and even aspects of the design process may have changed drastically in the last century, what it means to be a designer has not: “the hand can never execute anything higher than the character can inspire.”!

author: Mengting Zhu
thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
paragraph: Abstract In an era where rapid urbanization and economic priorities often overshadow human-land relationships, this thesis explores the emotional and cultural dimensions of land and their role in fostering a sense of responsibility. Rooted in personal experiences and cross-cultural perspectives, this research investigates how memory and storytelling can bridge the gap between sentimental attachment and sustainable stewardship. Through a culture probe workshop, interviews, and data visualization, this study examines how individuals connect with land and how their memories can be transformed into actionable care. By employing slow, analog data collection methods, narrative-driven visualizations, this work challenges traditional data representation and emphasizes the power of emotional engagement. Findings suggest that visual storytelling and shared memory can serve as catalysts for ecological awareness and land stewardship, inspiring communities to integrate sustainability into personal and collective practices. This thesis ultimately argues for a reconceptualization of land beyond economic value—toward an ethical, emotional, and cultural commitment to preservation. Keywords: land stewardship, memory, data visualization, cultural narratives, environmental responsibility

author: Mengting Zhu
thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
paragraph: Introduction In an era where rapid urbanization and consumer-driven economies dominate, the connection between humans and the land they inhabit is often obscured, reduced to a transactional relationship devoid of depth and meaning. Yet, for many, land represents more than a resource; it is a vessel of memory, a site of identity, and a symbol of cultural continuity. This thesis, The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data, explores how land transcends mere economic value and invites us to rethink our relationship with it as one of interdependence and respect. Zhu, Mengting 2 My inspiration stems from my father’s land, a place woven with memories and rooted in personal history. I followed my father into the fields from a young age, working alongside him. Together, we would head out to tend to the crops. Most of the time, I would watch my father and the workers laboring. I often sat on the ground as a child, wondering why they worked so hard. To me, the toil of the fields was always linked with hardship. It wasn't until later that I understood that each season brought a different harvest of fruits and vegetables and that the food on our table came from our land.

author: Mengting Zhu
thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
paragraph: What struck me the most was the proud expression on my father's face as he introduced each harvest. As a child, I may not have grasped his pride, but now I understand. My father always told me to have trust in the power of the land, assuring me it would never disappoint. This experience and my father's teachings inspired me to explore the relationship between humanity, nature, and the land, sparking a deep interest in the interconnectedness within ecosystems. We live together, grow together, and thrive together. In my heart, the land has always symbolized abundance, prosperity, and vitality. Just like my home, built on the land, with a garden at the doorstep where vegetables, flowers, and trees grow. I come from China, a country with a rich agricultural history that spans thousands of years. From a young age, I was taught to revere and conserve food and the land, a tradition rooted in Chinese culture with its age-old teaching that every grain is hard-earned. To me, our connection to nature starts with the very ground beneath our feet. I see a mirror of collective human experiences in this landscape—where the soil holds fragments of stories, traditions, and values. Such spaces remind us of the richness and wisdom inherent in land, yet also reveal the urgent need for a renewed perspective on land ownership and stewardship. As we move further into urbanized and industrialized ways of living, the bonds that tie us to these physical spaces risk fraying, potentially eroding our collective responsibility to nurture and preserve them. This research examines a pressing problem: How can individuals and societies rediscover and uphold a sense of care and responsibility for the land within a framework that often prioritizes profit over preservation? What cultural and emotional frameworks might inspire a more balanced, sustainable relationship? Building on these questions, this thesis introduces data visualization as a novel tool to bridge the gap between memory and responsibility. Inspired by the idea that data collection can serve personal well-being rather than merely analytical ends, I propose a multi-layered approach: first, gathering data through intimate interviews where participants describe their most cherished lands using sensory details, personal objects, Zhu, Mengting 3 and emotional associations; second, organizing these responses by identifying recurring themes, symbols, and spatial connections; and finally, translating this data into visual narratives—hand-drawn maps, abstract forms, and layered compositions that reflect both personal sentiment and collective memory. By reframing data as a medium for storytelling, this method transforms subjective experiences into a shared visual language, fostering empathy and awareness. Through an exploration of personal memories, cultural narratives, and visual representation, this thesis argues for a renewed commitment to land as both a communal heritage and a vital part of our ecosystem. Such a commitment is not only essential for environmental sustainability but also for maintaining cultural identity in a world that increasingly treats land as a disposable asset. By engaging with data as a storytelling medium—one that preserves subjective experiences, visualizes emotional landscapes, and fosters collective reflection—this thesis proposes a new way of understanding and sustaining our connection to land through both memory and responsibility.

author: Mengting Zhu
thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
paragraph: Defining "Land": Beyond Geography to Memory and Identity The term "land" carries multiple meanings across different cultural, social, and personal contexts. In its most conventional sense, land refers to the physical environment—natural landscapes such as forests, mountains, rivers, and agricultural fields. However, for many, land is not just a geographical entity but a space imbued with memory, identity, and emotional significance. For those with rural backgrounds, land may represent ancestral fields, family farms, or places of generational labor and sustenance. For Indigenous communities, land is often seen as a living entity, deeply connected to spirituality, culture, and traditions of stewardship. In urban environments, where direct access to nature may be limited, the meaning of land shifts—becoming parks, neighborhood courtyards, community gardens, or even constructed spaces like basketball courts or local swimming pools. These places, though man-made, serve as essential touchpoints for personal and collective histories, offering spaces of belonging, gathering, and continuity. Beyond location and function, land is also defined by its material composition. In rural areas, it may be soil rich with the scent of earth, stones that mark boundaries, or streams that nourish the landscape. In urban spaces, land often takes form through concrete, steel, and asphalt—materials that, though artificial, carry their own stories of human interaction and transformation. Whether it is the texture of moss-covered bricks Zhu, Mengting 4 in an old city square or the worn wood of a park bench, these materials hold layers of memory and meaning, shaping how people connect with the spaces around them. This thesis adopts a broad and inclusive definition of land, acknowledging that people’s connections to place are deeply personal and shaped by their lived experiences. Whether through a childhood garden, an ancestral village, or a city square, land holds the capacity to anchor memories, shape identities, and inspire a sense of responsibility. Recognizing this diversity ensures that discussions around land stewardship and sustainability remain relevant across different landscapes—natural, urban, and in-between. By framing land not only as a physical space but also as a site of memory, material presence, and emotional connection, this thesis seeks to bridge diverse perspectives and expand the discourse on land responsibility beyond conservation efforts alone. Instead, it highlights how storytelling, data visualization, and personal narratives can reframe our understanding of land—encouraging both reflection and action in the process.

author: Mengting Zhu
thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
paragraph: Ephemeral Nature of Memory vs. Durability of Responsibility Memories of land are deeply personal and often tied to experiences that shape our identity and emotional well-being. One participant in my interviews recalled a grove of trees near their childhood home. This grove wasn’t vast or particularly remarkable—it was a patch of green tucked between the edges of a suburban neighborhood and an old railway line. For the participant, however, it was a world of its own: a secret place where they spent countless afternoons building forts with fallen branches, reading under the shade of an ancient oak, and listening to the symphony of birdsong mingled with the distant hum of passing trains. As time passed, the grove was cleared to make way for the expansion of a nearby road. The participant, now an adult, spoke of their profound sense of loss. The grove had been a sanctuary—a place that felt alive with its own stories and rhythms. Yet, as a child, they had no idea how fragile it was or how to advocate for its preservation. They lamented the disconnect between their cherished memories and the realization that they had no tools, no understanding, to protect them from being transformed into asphalt and pavement. This story highlights a universal dilemma: memories evoke strong emotional resonance, but without frameworks to translate that resonance into action, they often remain passive, unfulfilled connections to the past. It underscores the need for intentional Zhu, Mengting 5 strategies—whether educational, artistic, or communal—that bridge the gap between emotional memory and enduring responsibility for the land.

author: Mengting Zhu
thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
paragraph: Land Ownership and Responsibility: A Cultural Comparison In China, land ownership is collective, reflecting deeply rooted cultural and historical traditions. Urban land is state-owned, while rural land is collectively managed, where communities share usage rights. This system, which originated in the post-revolution era, was significantly reformed in the 1980s with the introduction of the Household Responsibility System. This reform redistributed land use rights to individual households, dramatically increasing agricultural productivity and fostering a sense of shared prosperity. As highlighted in China's Economy: Rural Reform and Agricultural Zhenglai1, Development by Deng this policy marked a critical turning point for rural economies, alleviating food scarcity and reinforcing the communal approach to land use. However, while this system motivated short-term productivity, the inability to own land outright has limited long-term investment in ecological sustainability, as temporary leases often prioritize immediate economic returns. A personal example of this can be seen in my father’s land. While my family worked diligently to cultivate and sustain it, the land itself was never fully ours to own. The temporary nature of land rights meant that any long-term ecological improvements—such as soil restoration or biodiversity conservation—were often deprioritized in favor of immediate yields. My father, like many others, had to navigate this system, making careful decisions that balanced productivity with the uncertainty of land tenure. This reality reflects a broader challenge in China's land management policies, where the absence of permanent ownership can discourage deep ecological commitments. By contrast, Western land ownership systems, particularly in the United States, emphasize private property, where land is treated as a commodity to be bought, sold, and developed. This model fosters a strong sense of personal responsibility for privately owned land but also enables exploitation. With owners often prioritizing profit over preservation, the commodification of land has long shaped ecological relationships. William Cronon’s Changes in the Land: Indians, Colonists, and the Ecology of New (1983)2 England examines how colonial land-use practices—such as deforestation and Zhu, Mengting 6 the introduction of monoculture farming—transformed ecological relationships and reshaped the land, and the privatization of common lands—disrupted the ecological balance maintained by Indigenous communities. For example, the clearing of forests to create farmland not only reduced biodiversity but also altered soil composition and water systems. Similarly, the introduction of private property led to over-extraction of resources, as landowners maximized short-term economic gains. These practices transformed the land from a shared, sustainable resource into a commodity, fundamentally reshaping both the environment and human relationships with it. Additionally, Aldo Leopold's A Sand County Almanac (1949) introduces the concept of a 3a "land ethic," visionary framework that remains relevant in contemporary discussions about environmental responsibility. Leopold argues for viewing land as a community to which humans belong, rather than as a commodity to exploit. A contrasting perspective can be seen in Indigenous cultures, such as those of Australia, where land represents a spiritual and cultural anchor rather than a resource. Deborah Bird Rose’s Dingo Makes Us Human: Life and Land in an Australian Aboriginal Culture (1992) explores how Indigenous Australians see land as a sacred part of their identity, governed by "Dreamtime" stories that imbue landscapes with meaning and moral responsibility. This perspective contrasts sharply with both the Chinese collective model and Western privatization, offering a holistic view where land care is inseparable from cultural and spiritual life.

author: Mengting Zhu
thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
paragraph: Culture Probe Workshop: Insights into Emotional and Cultural Connections to Land To explore how individuals form emotional and cultural connections to land, I conducted a culture probe workshop that invited 10 participants to engage with their personal land memories. They were asked to document these connections through photographs, drawings, and symbolic objects, capturing their emotions and associations with significant places. The feedback revealed three key insights: 1. Diverse Definitions of Land: Participants’ chosen lands ranged from natural landscapes to urban spaces, such as a bookstore in Beijing, a high school park in China, and the vast deserts of Arizona. This diversity highlights that “land” is Zhu, Mengting 7 not solely defined by physical geography but by personal and cultural significance. 2. Objects as Memory Anchors: Physical items like books, license plates, and perfumes served as tangible representations of participants’ connections to land. These objects acted as bridges, grounding abstract memories in the material world. 3. Barriers to Action: While participants shared vivid memories and strong emotional bonds with their chosen lands, many expressed feelings of helplessness in protecting or preserving these spaces, often due to physical distance, societal changes, or lack of resources.

author: Mengting Zhu
thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
paragraph: Figure 1. Workshop on Pratt campus, November 2024 Zhu, Mengting 8

author: Mengting Zhu
thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
paragraph: From Data Collection to Visualization: Bridging Emotional Landscapes and Action Transforming emotional connections to land into meaningful action begins with how we collect and represent those connections. By gathering data through slow, analog methods—such as interviews, handwritten notes, and symbolic objects—and visualizing it with warmth and storytelling, we can create a bridge between memory and responsibility. Here, “warm” visualization refers to an approach that prioritizes emotional resonance, sensory detail, and narrative depth, as opposed to the detached neutrality often associated with traditional, “cold” data. This process is as much about honoring individual narratives as it is about inspiring collective care for the land. 1. Data Collection: Capturing Fragile and Subjective Memories The journey started with intimate, hands-on methods of data collection. Participants were invited to recall significant lands in their lives, describing not just their memories but also the shapes, objects, and emotions tied to these places. To preserve the depth and intimacy of these connections, I used analog and manual techniques: Zhu, Mengting 9 ● Interviews: Open-ended prompts encouraged participants to reflect on their land memories at their own pace. Questions like “What land has left the deepest impression on you?” and “What objects or shapes remind you of this place?” helped surface vivid details. ● Sketches and Drawings: Participants created rough visuals of their land memories, from childhood gardens to urban sanctuaries, each capturing a unique perspective. ● Objects: Personal items such as books, pressed flowers, or even a license plate became tangible anchors for their stories. ● Handwritten Notes: Participants documented their reflections in their own handwriting, a deliberate choice to preserve the rawness and authenticity of their emotions. Every piece of data—a sketch, an object, a note—felt like a fragment of personal history. Together, they formed a mosaic of emotional landscapes, rich with meaning yet fragile in their subjectivity.

author: Mengting Zhu
thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
paragraph: Figure 4. From workshop interviews. 2. Reconstructing Emotional Landscapes Through Visualization The next step was to transform these fragments into visual stories that could evoke the same emotions and connections they represented. Data visualization became the tool to bridge the gap between memory and action. 1. Warm vs. Cold Data Traditional data often feels detached, prioritizing neutrality and analysis over emotion. Here, the goal was different: to create "warm" data that spoke to the senses and told a story. Each visual element—whether a drawn shape or a photograph—was designed to capture the human essence of the memories. 2. Design Approach Zhu, Mengting 10 The creators translated the land Honoring Emotional Memory: ○ shapes and objects shared by participants into abstract visual forms, combining organic textures, soft colors, and layered compositions. These visuals were designed to reflect not only the physical characteristics of the land as described by participants but also the emotions and memories deeply tied to these places. ○ Storytelling Through Layers: The visuals were layered with elements like handwritten notes and sketches, allowing viewers to sense the personal depth behind each piece. For example, the outline of a garden from a participant’s childhood might be paired with a pressed flower or a handwritten description of its scent.

author: Mengting Zhu
thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
paragraph: Figure 5. Data Visualization Poster 1 3. Audience Engagement The visualizations were not just static displays but invitations for reflection. By immersing viewers in these emotional landscapes, the goal was to spark their own memories and connections to land. This approach encouraged a dialogue, transforming passive observation into active engagement. Zhu, Mengting 11 3. From Memory to Responsibility By combining the personal intimacy of data collection with the evocative power of visualization, this process bridges emotional resonance with actionable care. The result is not just a representation of individual memories but a collective narrative that inspires reflection and responsibility. For some participants, these visual narratives became a starting point for action. A student who shared a memory of their grandmother’s village—now partially demolished—was motivated to reconnect with their relatives and document the remaining landscapes through photography. Another participant, who had spoken about the loss of a beloved urban green space in their hometown, later reached out to a community-led conservation group to learn more about advocacy efforts. While these actions may not have reversed land loss, they helped participants engage with their landscapes in new ways—whether through personal archiving, activism, or simply a renewed awareness of their role in preserving these places. These actions did not change policies or reclaim lost land, but they created new connections at both personal and community levels. Documenting a lost village means preserving its story for future generations; contacting a conservation group means using one's voice to contribute to broader ecological efforts. These small but meaningful steps demonstrate that even in the face of obstacles, individuals can find ways to care for and reconnect with the land. However, not all barriers to action were easily overcome. Physical distance remained a major challenge for many, particularly international students who could not return to their childhood lands. One participant, reflecting on their childhood home in China, noted that while they could not physically intervene in its rapid urban transformation, they found meaning in sharing its story and incorporating elements of its landscape into their artistic practice. Similarly, for those without financial or legal means to influence land use, the act of remembering—through storytelling, photography, or creative expression—became an alternative form of care. Each visual story becomes a call to action, not in the sense of immediate activism, but in its ability to shift perception. It reminds us of the land's role not only in our personal histories but also in our shared future. By engaging with these narratives, participants cultivated a renewed sense of responsibility—not necessarily through direct intervention, but through an ongoing process of recognition, documentation, and advocacy in whatever ways were accessible to them. Zhu, Mengting 12

author: Mengting Zhu
thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
paragraph: Sustainable Strategies for Cultiva Fostering a sustainable relationship with land requires strategies that connect personal memories, data visualization, and artistic expression to actionable outcomes. By engaging communities, integrating education, and advocating for systemic change, we can transform emotional connections A key approach lies in building community networks that use data visualization to spark collective dialogue. Visualizing shared memories of land creates opportunities for people to see their stories as part of a larger narrative, fostering a sense of shared purpose. William Cronon’s Changes in the Land: Indians, Colonists, and the Ecology of New England (1983) illustrates how land use practices are shaped by cultural relationships.5 narratives, showing that collective dialogue can reshape ecological For instance, a community exhibition featuring personal land visualizations can encourage conversations about collective responsibility. If these visualizations were displayed in public spaces—such as post offices, town halls, or community centers—they could serve as living records of the land’s evolving identity. Imagine a layered map of a neighborhood, composed of handwritten memories and hand-drawn elements representing personal and historical land use. This type of installation could invite intergenerational engagement, prompting discussions about land preservation and inspiring tangible community action, such as reforestation projects or local conservation efforts. This echoes the powerful impact of the “Blue Marble” photograph—the first image of Earth taken from space in 1972. That singular visual representation reframed how humanity saw the planet: not as a limitless resource, but as a small, fragile sphere floating in space. It became a catalyst for the modern environmental movement, proving how visual imagery can shift collective perception and inspire global responsibility. Similarly, community-based visualizations of land memory can reshape local relationships with the environment, turning individual stories into collective momentum for change. Beyond exhibitions, workshops and interactive installations can ensure that these visualizations continue to evolve. Community members could contribute their own land narratives over time, turning static data into an active dialogue. Digital platforms could also extend this interaction, allowing former residents or dispersed communities to reconnect with their land stories virtually. By embedding these visualizations in both Zhu, Mengting 13 physical and digital spaces, they become more than artistic representations—they become tools for advocacy and collective memory. Education also plays a vital role in embedding land responsibility into everyday consciousness. Robin Wall Kimmerer’s Braiding Sweetgrass (2013) emphasizes the value of teaching reciprocity with the land, drawing from Indigenous wisdom.6 Introducing visual and experiential learning methods in schools and communities allows people to connect with environmental issues on a personal level. Students might map their own land memories or engage in workshops that combine storytelling with hands-on activities like planting or restoring local ecosystems. When land-based narratives are integrated into education, they provide a foundation for fostering long-term ecological care. For sustainable impact, these personal and collective stories must tie into broader environmental efforts. Visualizing land memories alongside data on climate change or habitat loss can make abstract issues more relatable and urgent. For instance, illustrating how a beloved local park has diminished over time might galvanize action to protect similar spaces. Deborah Bird Rose’s Dingo Makes Us Human: Life and Land in an Australian Aboriginal Culture (2000) highlights how cultural stories tied to land create a deep sense of belonging and responsibility, which can inspire broader ecological advocacy.7 These emotionally charged narratives can also support policy efforts, where compelling visuals and stories influence decision-makers to enact protective legislation. Campaigns that highlight the emotional and cultural significance of land have the power to inspire changes in zoning laws, conservation funding, or public land management. Sustainability also benefits from aligning local initiatives with global frameworks like the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Efforts to preserve and restore land at the community level—whether through workshops, public art, or policy advocacy—can contribute to broader ecological and social outcomes. Donella Meadows’ Thinking in Systems (2008) explains how small-scale actions, when change.8 connected to larger systems, can create significant, lasting By embedding emotional connections into these actions, we can ensure that land responsibility extends beyond individual memory, becoming a shared commitment to future generations. Zhu, Mengting 14 By situating land memories within community spaces and allowing them to evolve through collective participation, we create pathways for sustained engagement. These strategies transform fleeting emotions into enduring care, ensuring that land responsibility does not remain an abstract concept but becomes an integrated, lived experience—woven into both physical landscapes and the cultural fabric of a place. Through shared stories, education, and systemic advocacy, this approach fosters a dynamic relationship between people and the land, inspiring long-term stewardship across generations.

author: Mengting Zhu
thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
paragraph: Conclusion This thesis has explored the intersection of memory, cultural perspectives, and data visualization in fostering a deeper sense of responsibility toward the land. By weaving personal stories into visual and emotional narratives, it demonstrates how memory can evolve from an ephemeral connection into a sustained and collective commitment to stewardship. Data visualization emerged as a pivotal tool, not only as an artistic medium but also as a bridge between emotional resonance and tangible action. Unlike traditional "cold" data, the visualizations in this study are warm, sensory, and narrative-driven—designed to evoke reflection and ignite meaningful dialogues about human relationships with the land. These visual stories are not merely representations of memories but invitations to action, encouraging individuals and communities to reconnect with land, honor its significance, and consider their role in its preservation. However, it is essential to recognize the limitations of this approach. While visual storytelling can be a compelling means of engagement, it remains inherently selective, shaped by the experiences and perspectives of those who contribute. The subjective nature of memory means that certain narratives may be emphasized while others remain overlooked, leading to gaps in representation. Moreover, emotional engagement alone does not always translate into direct environmental action—systemic barriers such as economic constraints, urban development policies, and land ownership structures continue to shape how people can—or cannot—exercise agency in land stewardship. Further research could explore ways to bridge the gap between personal land narratives and structural change. Longitudinal studies could assess whether exposure to these visualizations leads to sustained behavioral shifts or policy interventions. Additionally, Zhu, Mengting 15 expanding digital platforms for storytelling and community engagement may allow individuals who are geographically distant from their meaningful landscapes to remain connected and involved in conservation efforts. Interdisciplinary collaborations between artists, urban planners, environmentalists, and policymakers could also enhance the effectiveness of data-driven narratives in influencing public discourse and land management policies. It is also crucial to define land in an inclusive way. While some associate land with vast rural landscapes or agricultural fields, for others—especially those raised in urban environments—it may manifest as a neighborhood park, a basketball court, or a communal gathering space. Recognizing this diversity ensures that the discourse on land stewardship extends beyond traditional conservation models to include the everyday spaces that shape people’s lived experiences. Future studies could investigate how urban populations engage with land differently from rural communities and how these interactions inform broader ecological and cultural responsibilities. By acknowledging these complexities, this thesis moves beyond an idealized vision of human-land connection toward a more grounded understanding—one that recognizes both the transformative power and the inherent constraints of storytelling, data, and visualization. While visual narratives can evoke awareness and spark conversations, their long-term impact depends on how they are integrated into broader community engagement, policy advocacy, and education. Ultimately, this work offers a framework for reimagining land not just as a physical entity but as a shared cultural and emotional landscape—one that requires both memory and responsibility to sustain. Memory as a Call to Action The stories in this research show that remembering passive—it does not have to be can be the first step toward action. Some participants reconnected with their childhood landscapes through documentation, others reached out to local conservation efforts, and some simply took the time to reflect on their own role in shaping the land. None of these actions required permission or authority; they came from the simple yet profound realization that land, memory, and responsibility are intertwined. You, too, have a landscape that shaped you. You, too, carry memories that connect you to the land. And from those memories, you, too, can act. Whether by revisiting a lost place, engaging in local environmental efforts, or simply sharing your story, you become part of a larger movement—one that sees land not as a passive background but as a living archive of who we are and who we choose to be. Zhu, Mengting 16

thesis_title: The Visual Narrative of Land: Bridging Emotion and Responsibility Through Data
