author: Minjia Luo
thesis_title: Other “Me” --Exploring Self-Expression Through Original Characters.
paragraph: ● What has been the evolution of female characters in video games since the advent of social media? ● Why use original character design in daily life? Normally used in games, social media, or online communities? ● What are the psychological reasons behind the behaviors that make one own OC and use an OC for online social actions?

author: Minjia Luo
thesis_title: Other “Me” --Exploring Self-Expression Through Original Characters.
paragraph: This article describes the differences between the design of female game characters in Valorant and other games and the changes the design team wants to make for the female game characters and players. In contrast to many video games that choose hypersexualized or stereotypically “feminine” images, Valorant takes a more progressive and inclusive approach, allowing women to have a more diverse experience and display. The psychological reasons behind the application and creation of OC characters and scenarios are also covered. As a way of exploring identity and expressing emotions, the creation of original characters is to project an ideal self, explore other identities or cope with unmet psychological needs. Simultaneously, through simple experiments during the research process, more people can experience that self-exploration and discovery are not complicated. The results obtained after visualizing the fragmented self clearly show that everyone is different. The different image arrangement is similar to the description of the Myers-Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI) experimental test, but the images are more intuitive and simpler.

author: Minjia Luo
thesis_title: Other “Me” --Exploring Self-Expression Through Original Characters.
paragraph: Women like me sometimes would like to hide their real identity when playing games to avoid a lot of “trouble,” such as sexism or questioning their understanding of the game. Players in the same games will be attacked or questioned because of their game ID or female image. The attacker will assume that the skill and strength of female players behind the screen at the moment are not enough to appear in the same game as the attacker. This is undoubtedly unreasonable for gamers. Although they appear related to game performance, the attacks are mostly unjustified. Therefore, more and more people use virtual images online or in gaming social circles. OC (Original Character) is the vehicle for this approach, avoiding attacks to a certain extent while also showing players’ style. In addition to online social networking, OC is the one many people dream of being and can do things that are impossible to accomplish in reality. For example, the OC will defend themselves in the OC’s reality if someone is attacked in a game session, but is not good at defending in real life. Throughout the whole thesis process, I collected dress-up (or outfits) from daily life and used them as the basis for my research to construct my character image. The process of character creation is also a form of self-exploration, as we need to consider every design detail and why I choose this character’s characteristics. I kept asking myself why I chose these elements and the symbolic meanings behind them. Character design is not just about shaping an image, but also interpreting the story of this “person”. Since I thought “Valorant” was unique among competitive games, I initially used it as a sample for my exploration. I will go into detail about this game in the first chapter and explain the differences for female characters in the game. The second part will explore the process of character creation. This process is not complicated, but the designer’s background and creative philosophy will also influence the direction of design thinking invisibly. The psychological manifestation of the “individual subconscious” is also reflected in the construction of the character’s backstory. Differences in the subconscious are also reflected in personal experiences. Although we occasionally suppress many subconscious thoughts when confronted with reality, this does not negate their existence. The last section will explore the situations in which OC is used as well as the psychological causes of it. OC frequently appears in virtual social scenarios. Some people do not want to appear in virtual social scenarios with real identity information, and some people get spiritual healing through OC. This study will start from these two obvious directions and analyze the underlying psychological reasons.

author: Minjia Luo
thesis_title: Other “Me” --Exploring Self-Expression Through Original Characters.
paragraph: In many electronic games, the appearance or clothing design of female characters often makes some female players uncomfortable. Taking the early versions of ‘LEAGUE OF LEGENDS’ and ‘Tomb Raiding’ as examples, the character promotional posters prefer to highlight the body curves of female characters. Even though female players tend to choose same-sex characters for gaming, they are often at a competitive disadvantage due to the different positioning of the characters. When the game “Valorant” was released, its special features captured my attention. Unlike other female gaming characters, the female characters in Valorant are unique. They take on the important role of a duelist in every game. The duelist is required to seize the fighter jet to open up the game for the team, so this position is highly offensive and usually needs to be the first to break the enemy’s defensive area. However, the designers have assigned the role of requiring high explosive power to females, breaking the traditional “rules” of female characters.

author: Minjia Luo
thesis_title: Other “Me” --Exploring Self-Expression Through Original Characters.
paragraph: The so-called “rules” often manifest as female characters being positioned as team assistants or limited to an offensive role that is softer. Explosive roles such as duelist are usually played by male game characters. In addition, each female character in the game has her own unique backstory, personality, and skill design. These female characters come from different countries with distinctive personalities, so their skill designs are closely tailored to their respective character traits. For example, Jett is a white-haired girl from South Korea. She is confident and a little arrogant, and her skills revolve around speed and agility. Viper is a short-haired girl from the United States. She is cold and full of mystery, good at using chemical toxic mists, and her skills are about using poison.

author: Minjia Luo
thesis_title: Other “Me” --Exploring Self-Expression Through Original Characters.
paragraph: The visual design also breaks with tradition, focusing on diversity and inclusiveness. The character’s origins and birthplace are reflected in the costumes and accessories’ designs. Sage, for example, who is from Shaanxi, China, wears a Chinese-style martial arts costume. In contrast to the long robes of the past, her martial arts costume is also a modified short martial arts costume. Except for sex appeal, the costume design also focuses on functionality. Reyna’s costume design is both fashionable and tactical. Although she wears a tight-fitting outfit, it rarely makes people focus on her sexy figure. Therefore, the female figure is not a selling point. Players are more concerned about whether the clothing design conforms to the character’s identity setting rather than the presentation of body curves. This is a key to changing the inequality of women in games. I pay more attention to the content of the game than to the appearance of the characters when experiencing this game. Similarly, some male players I have asked also stated that they were initially attracted to Valorant because of the content design of the game, the skins and special effects of the guns, and the coordination of various skills. The figure and appearance of the female characters did not attract the expected number of players. In the past, the important selling points that attracted male players have become the least competitive part of this game. My research on Valorant stems from a genuine love for these game characters—the anticipation remains constant every time I log in to the game. Female characters are no longer equated with “weakness”, and players who play supportive female characters are not just female. I feel like I can play this game more comfortably because there are not many boundaries between players, even though unfair treatment still exists within competitive games. The designer aims to make players experience different styles. To designers, women can be agile and explosive; they also have more distinctive options in addition to the team positioning of supporting characters. Not only are Jett, Neon, Reyna, and Raze duelists, but there are also more assisting characters like Sage, Fade, and Killjoy. “Valorant” is committed to building a diverse and gender-neutral character system to attract different players and give female players more choices, rather than passively following. I think Valorant's change in the design and positioning of female characters is advanced and innovative in the gaming industry, which has made the industry consider more about the diversity of women. This game's female character reminded me of my design character, and I hope she is independent and capable of handling a variety of situations. Furthermore, she might need to have confidence. The way or style Riot shapes female characters has given me a new characteristics？ perspective—why do we want characters to be endowed with so many What is the reason behind the change in female character design between the past and now? How can the OC refer to that change?

author: Minjia Luo
thesis_title: Other “Me” --Exploring Self-Expression Through Original Characters.
paragraph: As I previously mentioned, OC (the original character) is a fictional character that has been entirely created by an author or artist. Each of them has a unique backstory, distinct personality, iconic appearance, and distinctive qualities that are easy to remember at a glance. Typically, when a creator creates a character, the figure may display the author’s ideas or style. As most people create OC, they often shape them into their ideal type—whether it’s appearance, occupation, personality, or skills, they will approach their ideal “perfect image.”

author: Minjia Luo
thesis_title: Other “Me” --Exploring Self-Expression Through Original Characters.
paragraph: I thought a lot when creating my own character, and it was also a process of finding my own position. Every detail and piece of backstory reflects my re-exploration of self-awareness. My creation of a brand-new character is more than a journey of self-discovery. Those settings may seem to belong to the “virtual world,” but they reflect the deepest choices and insights in one’s heart. The process of refining the character characteristics is a pursuit of my ideal self. As I keep asking, “What do I need?” I face my most genuine desires—I want the character to be confident, magnificent, and able to travel through time. These characteristics that I have endowed to the OC are actually what I desire, but they are currently unattainable, so I hope they can be achieved instead of me. Sometimes, I take the opposite approach—for example, if I am not good at handling emotions, I would give my character a high level of empathy. This is an outlet for me to vent my emotions or it is a world where those seemingly “ridiculous” fantasies can be placed. The setting of the background story also reflects the creator’s viewpoint. The creator’s perspective will affect the characters’ responses each time the storyline’s narrative is reenacted. However, since similar things might occur in reality, it is more of a reflection of the creator’s ideas than an influence. Creating a story plot is essentially a reproduction and simulation of real life and then reacting to it. It can be said to be a good “drill” or “relief”. Although my life is not as free as I would like it to be, I hope the character is. Thus, during such moments, the character’s experiences serve as a way for me to “escape from reality” and a kind of psychological release. There are also some psychological aspects involved. Carl Gustav Jung mentioned “personal unconscious” in The Archetypes and the Collective Unconscious. “The personal unconscious contains forgotten memories, repressed material, subliminal perceptions, and a variety of conscious contents that are temporarily out of awareness.” This personal unconscious can surface in the form of dreams, slips of the tongue, mood swings, and subconscious experiences.1 reactions. Its content is differentiated according to each individual's This also explains why the re-enactment of the OC's backstory is regarded as a means of “escaping reality”. It is a psychological condition that occurs frequently. The content of real life may drive us to bury our desires deep within our souls, but it doesn’t mean they haven’t appeared. Competence is a concept mentioned by Richard M. Ryan. Humans inherently desire to be “competent” in what they do, to be able to control challenges and leverage their strengths. 2In the process of creating OC, creators feel a sense of “I am capable of creating a world” through designing characters with powerful abilities or setting up complex plots—something that is difficult to achieve in reality but can be reinforced in a fantasy world. The sense of control and planning is also fully satisfied in this process, thereby providing psychological comfort. Considering that the details of daily experiences are easily forgotten, in the early parts of the thesis, I conducted a one-month daily dress code record. At first, it wasn’t successful since I kept using the same action in the same scene, which is indeed a very common way of using social media nowadays. However, there was no way to break through the pretty distressing situation. Later, I started to try to record in different scenes, even including scenes where my character may or may not appear at all. It was challenging to take the first step, but I finally overcame the obstacles, and everything went quite well. The appearance of the characters made me feel like I had companions to go to places where I would not set foot alone. These creations are a kind of spiritual comfort for me; perhaps this is the meaning of OC. They have constantly experienced “boundary breakthroughs.” Similar to the beginning, I will set a “relatively familiar image” for the character so that she overlaps with some of her qualities, such as “appearance” or “a harmonious approach to things.” As I delve deeper into my creative process, I will try to break through this “familiar boundary” and let the character live out another possibility for me, such as boldly challenging rules or stepping into faraway places I have never been before. It helps me to get acclimated to such “boundary breakthroughs.” I will gradually try new ways of dealing with things and accept the imperfect parts of myself. We can also observe that content that cannot be accomplished in reality will be presented in various ways to achieve a balance point in mental state or physical comfort. The act of “letting off steam” can also be regarded as a kind of “treatment” method. This is a continuous cycle of generating problems, discovering problems, and solving problems.

author: Minjia Luo
thesis_title: Other “Me” --Exploring Self-Expression Through Original Characters.
paragraph: The notion that “my character will heal me” is often mentioned in some threads about OC design. This is because they can accomplish things we can’t in the real world or wish they had. Some of the psychological theories involved are fantasy and compensation.

author: Minjia Luo
thesis_title: Other “Me” --Exploring Self-Expression Through Original Characters.
paragraph: From this, it can be seen that the essence of OCs has surpassed simple creativity and plays a dual role as a carrier of psychological healing and a medium of self-expression. Creators inject their inner desires, fears, and aspirations into OC, using the fictional worlds to digest emotions that are difficult to resolve in reality and overcome real-world limitations This is highly consistent with the psychological concepts of self-expansion and wish fulfillment, where individuals engage with activities or creations that allow them to transcend their immediate realities. OC creation gives creators a sense of agency, compensating for the suppressed need for autonomy in reality. For example, OC often carries the traits that creators aspire to, such as extraordinary courage, exceptional talent, or stunning beauty, forming an idealized image that is both inspiring and comforting. She is the projection of my aspirations. Moreover, the idea that “my character will heal me” resonates with the power of narrative therapy. By designing challenges and triumphs for their characters, creators often simulate personal growth and emotional resilience. Research in narrative therapy supports this idea. It emphasizes that individuals can reframe negative experiences and build a more positive self-image by constructing meaningful story frameworks, whether purely fictional or others. Thus, OCs are more than fictional personas. They evolved into mirrors, mentors, and even guardians for their creators, reflecting their ideal selves and offering a path to personal healing and growth. Substitutive satisfaction is a psychological phenomenon in which an individual turns to alternative behaviors or experiences to gain a sense of satisfaction when they are unable to achieve their real-life goals.4 Unlike wish fulfillment, substitutive satisfaction is not always related to subconscious desires but rather is more like a positive form of self-regulation. More precisely, this is a “mental compensation,” but does not necessarily presuppose negative emotions. It is like the goal that has not yet been achieved in reality but can be achieved in the world of OC. Baumann and Ruch divide satisfaction into several dimensions, including “satisfaction in activity,” “satisfaction in role,” and “satisfaction in overall life.” OC is related to “role satisfaction”, and this alternative experience can effectively enhance “satisfaction” at the 左岸文化, psychological level. The dimensions of “Unfolded Self and Life” and “Positive Impact and Legacy” mentioned in the study, where people think about “unfulfilled desires or potential”, coincide with the theory of “alternative satisfaction” that “unachieved goals can be compensated for through other activities (such as OC)”. “Fantasy and compensation” comes from the theory of defense mechanisms in psychology. When encountering frustration, depression, or dissatisfaction in reality, people may relieve these pressures through fantasy and psychological compensation. Fantasy is essentially a dynamic process of psychological self-adjustment, while compensation is reflected in the psychological situations.5 compensation for the lack of reality through substitute achievements in virtual A typical example is the creation of OC or its stories, which is an action that relieves mental pressure in reality. Although fantasy, compensation, and substitutive satisfaction have a common point in that OC is a compensation for reality, the difference is that substitutive satisfaction is a way to get positive emotional feedback, while fantasy and compensation are ways to regulate negative emotions. The difference between the two is not particularly significant: one describes the way to obtain emotions, and the other overviews this behavior. Whatever the theory, the goal of OC is to achieve a better self-beneficial mental state or social approach. In virtual social scenarios, we usually exhibit what we want to show to others, creating a distinct “personality” and enabling us to behave in the “desired” way according to our own settings. Virtual social scenarios essentially construct a dual shielding space: not only a display of the OC but also a safe zone. In the safe zone, everything is acceptable, and there is also space to accept the imperfections in reality. There is nothing wrong with being your true self in this situation. The OC can appear in various fictional art forms like paintings and novels, and even games specifically designed for these characters. All of this is to improve oneself. I also made a doll for my thesis. I would dress her up in lovely clothes and take her out for pictures. The whole process was enjoyable to me, though. During the photo shoots, the real scenes and the unreal people combined with each other, which was very similar to using an OC in a virtual scene. Despite being a simulated environment, it had genuine feelings. I also created a simple activity for people who don’t know the concept during the research process. The activity is to describe yourself through the most basic shapes in the outline of a blank human body. People are well aware of the features of the basic shapes, and there is a basic consensus on the meaning or character traits they symbolize. This also makes it easier for people who are unfamiliar with the concept of OC to uncover their own characteristics and then realize that this is also similar to the process of creating an OC. It is clear to show individual differences from the results obtained after visualizing the fragmented selves. The images convey the idea that “everyone is an individual” in a more intuitive way. The character on the right comes from a group with little interaction with related art fields. Her major is engineering, which is more inclined towards science and technology. Therefore, her arrangements are more regular and rarely circular or wavy. The one on the left comes from my own drawings. My ideas are more erratic and numerous, and my emotions are more abundant and soft. People can quickly remember these traits that may need consideration by visualizing them through the most basic graphical features. We live in different environments and pursue different careers, but that doesn't mean that one is superior to the other. It simply indicates that our methods and perspectives are different. Understanding yourself may be the first step towards change. Perhaps the design of OC might even be different from the test results. However, everyone has their own lifestyle or logic for maintaining their mental state. Finding a method that works for oneself is crucial. Our appearance is just one way of expressing ourselves. The design of OC is essentially a process of expressing the inner self through external manifestations, and the companionship experience provided by its image shaping can offer psychological comfort to people.

author: Minjia Luo
thesis_title: Other “Me” --Exploring Self-Expression Through Original Characters.
paragraph: Contemporary people face multiple life pressures, and OC creation is becoming an effective way for some people to maintain their mental health. Substitutional fulfillment, fantasy, and compensation are the psychological reasons for it. While they create the character used in that “treatment,” some people will not understand. Therefore, this project will introduce how to create the OC and the reasons or factors that make the creator develop a character.

author: Minjia Luo
thesis_title: Other “Me” --Exploring Self-Expression Through Original Characters.
paragraph: Additionally, we constantly struggle with the difference between our real and ideal selves. (Designed by: Shawn Perkins) https://www.leafly.com/news/lifestyle/420-community-nfts Abstract My thesis explores the multifaceted nature of cannabis, examining its historical, cultural, and scientific significance. Through a blend of personal narrative and academic research, this work delves into the evolving landscape of cannabis legalization and its impact on society. By examining the plant's potential benefits and risks, as well as its role in various cultures and subcultures, this thesis aims to foster a more nuanced understanding of cannabis. The first part of this thesis traces the historical journey of cannabis, from its ancient origins to its modern-day renaissance. It explores the plant's diverse applications, from medicinal and industrial uses to its role in spiritual and cultural practices. Additionally, the thesis examines the social and political factors that have shaped cannabis policy, including the impact of prohibition, the rise of the counterculture movement, and the ongoing legalization efforts. The second part delves into the personal experiences of cannabis use, highlighting the potential benefits and risks. It explores the subjective experiences of cannabis consumption, including its effects on mood, cognition, and sensory perception. Furthermore, the thesis examines the potential therapeutic applications of cannabis, such as pain relief, anxiety reduction, and improved sleep quality. It also addresses the importance of responsible use and the potential risks associated with high-potency cannabis products. By combining personal narrative, historical analysis, and scientific research, this thesis offers a comprehensive exploration of cannabis. It challenges societal stigmas, promotes responsible use, and advocates for evidence-based policies. Ultimately, this work aims to contribute to a more informed and compassionate understanding of cannabis, paving the way for a future where the plant is recognized for its full potential. Introduction Cannabis, a plant with a rich history and diverse cultural significance, has captivated human imagination for millennia. From its ancient origins as a sacred herb to its modern-day renaissance, cannabis has played a significant role in shaping human civilization. This thesis explores the multifaceted nature of cannabis, delving into its historical, cultural, and scientific dimensions. My personal journey with cannabis began during my early college years. A chance encounter with the plant sparked a lifelong fascination with its potential to heal, inspire, and transform. Through personal experiences and extensive research, I have gained a deep appreciation for the complex and often misunderstood nature of cannabis. In the United States, the legalization of cannabis has ushered in a new era of opportunities and challenges, reshaping the landscape of social justice, economic development, and personal growth. While the potential benefits are significant, it's essential to acknowledge the ongoing concerns surrounding public health, safety, and equity. Historically, cannabis policies and enforcement have disproportionately affected marginalized communities, perpetuating systemic inequalities. The long-term health effects of cannabis use remain a subject of ongoing research, necessitating evidence-based public health messaging. Additionally, the increasing availability of cannabis products raises concerns about youth access and potential negative consequences. However, cannabis also offers significant potential for positive social and economic impact. The legalization of cannabis can create new jobs, generate tax revenue, and stimulate economic growth. Furthermore, it can lead to reduced crime rates and a more equitable criminal justice system. Beyond its economic benefits, cannabis has the potential to promote individual well-being and cultural innovation. Many individuals report using cannabis to alleviate stress, anxiety, and chronic pain. Additionally, cannabis has inspired creativity and innovation in various fields, from art and music to science and technology. While the benefits of cannabis are numerous, it is important to address the potential risks associated with its use. These include impaired driving, addiction, and negative effects on mental health. To mitigate these risks, it is essential to implement evidence-based public health policies and education campaigns. Cannabis has a rich history, dating back thousands of years. Ancient civilizations in Asia and Africa recognized the plant's medicinal and spiritual properties, incorporating it into their cultural and religious practices. The ancient Egyptians used cannabis in religious rituals, while Hindus revered it as a sacred Introduction plant associated with the god Shiva. These cultures understood the plant's ability to induce altered states of consciousness and facilitate spiritual experiences. In the 19th century, cannabis gained popularity in Western medicine as a treatment for various ailments, including pain, anxiety, and insomnia. However, as the 20th century progressed, negative stereotypes and moral panic surrounding cannabis led to its criminalization. The Marihuana Tax Act of 1937 effectively outlawed cannabis in the United States, marking the beginning of a long period of prohibition. Despite the prohibition, cannabis continued to be used and celebrated in various cultural contexts. The counterculture movement of the 1960s and 1970s embraced cannabis as a symbol of rebellion and self-expression. Artists, musicians, and writers often incorporated cannabis into their work, exploring its potential to inspire creativity and challenge societal norms.

author: Paula Wang
thesis_title: Cannabis Equity
paragraph: Cannabis is a complex plant with a diverse range of cannabinoids, terpenes, and other compounds. The two primary psychoactive compounds in cannabis are tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) and cannabidiol (CBD). THC is responsible for the psychoactive effects of cannabis, while CBD is non-psychoactive and been1 has shown to have various therapeutic benefits. The endocannabinoid system, a network of receptors and neurotransmitters found throughout the body, plays a crucial role in regulating various physiological processes, including pain, mood, appetite, and sleep. Cannabis interacts with this system, producing a wide range of effects, from relaxation and pain relief to altered perception and creativity. The entourage effect, a concept that describes the synergistic interaction between different cannabinoids and terpenes, is believed to contribute to the complex effects of cannabis. By consuming whole-plant cannabis products, individuals can benefit from the full spectrum of compounds and their potential therapeutic benefits. However, it's important to acknowledge the potential risks associated with cannabis use, particularly for young people and individuals with underlying mental health conditions. Excessive or irresponsible use of cannabis can lead to negative consequences, including impaired cognitive function, addiction, and psychosis. Introduction My personal journey with cannabis has been transformative. I've experienced firsthand the plant's ability to enhance creativity, alleviate stress, and promote relaxation. Cannabis has allowed me to connect with nature, explore my inner self, and appreciate the beauty of the world around me. However, it's important to approach cannabis consumption with mindfulness and moderation. By understanding the potential benefits and risks, individuals can make informed choices about their cannabis use. As we continue to explore the complexities of cannabis, it's essential to approach the plant with an open mind and a commitment to evidence-based research. By promoting responsible use, advocating for sensible regulations, and challenging harmful stereotypes, we can unlock the full potential of cannabis and create a more informed and compassionate society. author: Paula Wang
thesis_title: Cannabis Equity
paragraph: Partannabis and Culture Chapter 1 : The History of Cannabis As a longtime cannabis enthusiast, I have witnessed firsthand the plant's potential to heal, inspire, and transform. I am passionate about advocating for cannabis legalization and responsible use, particularly in light of its historical and cultural significance. For too long, cannabis has been unfairly stigmatized, hindering its potential to address various social and environmental issues. I believe that cannabis can play a crucial role in creating a more sustainable and equitable future. By promoting sustainable cultivation practices and supporting local economies, the cannabis industry can contribute to a greener planet. Additionally, cannabis has the potential to alleviate suffering and improve quality of life for millions of people. My first encounter with cannabis was during my early college years. A friend invited me to join them for a smoke session, painting a picture of relaxation, creativity, and a deeper connection with oneself. Intrigued, I decided to give it a try. The first time I smoked, I remember feeling a mix of excitement and nervousness. As I took my first hit, I was immediately enveloped in a wave of relaxation. The world around me seemed to slow down, and my worries began to fade away. It was as if a weight had been lifted off my shoulders. I felt a sense of peace and contentment that I had never experienced before. I experienced a sense of derealization, not as a visual distortion, but as a mental distance, a sense of observing myself and my thoughts from a slight remove. This feeling of peace and derealization became something I sought in subsequent experiences, I still search for that bliss within each of my elevated experiences. This personal journey with cannabis has unfolded against the backdrop of its complex cultural and historical context. As I explored the plant further, I became fascinated by its rich history and diverse applications. My understanding of cannabis is rooted in its conceptual significance, its role in shaping human civilization and its potential to address contemporary challenges. As an Asian American woman, I recognize that my relationship with cannabis is through rose-tinted lenses. Unlike many marginalized communities who have been disproportionately targeted by drug laws, my experience has been largely free of fear and systemic oppression. My privilege is not based on a visual narrative, but on the tangible realities of my socio-economic background, my lighter skin tone, and the cultural context of my upbringing. These factors have allowed me to engage with cannabis more freely, without the constant fear of police intervention or the weight of historical trauma. While I'm aware of the potential consequences of public cannabis use, I feel a certain level of privilege that allows me to engage with the plant more freely. I've often enjoyed smoking a joint in a park or taking a leisurely puff outside a coffee shop without fear of repercussions. I recognize the importance of amplifying the voices of marginalized communities who have been disproportionately impacted by cannabis prohibition. By learning from their experiences and advocating for their rights, I hope to contribute to a more equitable and just cannabis culture. The model minority myth can obscure the struggles within the Asian American community, and the cultural stigma surrounding cannabis, while less overt, still exists. Therefore, it is crucial to amplify the voices of those who have been disproportionately impacted by cannabis prohibition. By understanding their stories and advocating for their rights, I hope to contribute to a more equitable and just cannabis culture, one that acknowledges the historical injustices and works towards a future where cannabis is accessible and used responsibly by all. author: Paula Wang
thesis_title: Cannabis Equity
paragraph: In the United States, Cannabis has a lengthy and intricate history that reflects the shifting political, social, and cultural climate. Cannabis has had a major impact on American culture since its early use as a medical herb and through its criminalization and eventual legalization. Cannabis has a rich history in North America, way before the 17th century when European colonists introduced the plant. Initially cultivated for its industrial properties, European colonists cultivated hemp to produce textiles, rope, and paper.1 However, the plant's spiritual and medicinal significance predates colonial influence. Indigenous peoples of the Americas have used cannabis, an entheogen, for spiritual and medicinal purposes, utilizing its ability to induce altered states of consciousness and facilitate a deeper connection to the divine or the self. Early cultivation of cannabis by European colonists was largely driven by economic interests and the self-sufficiency.2 desire for While Indigenous cultures revered cannabis for its spiritual and medicinal properties, European colonization brought a shift in its perception. Cannabis was then widely used for medicinal and recreational purposes in the 19th century. Pharmacies commonly stocked cannabis extracts and preparations for treating various ailments, including pain, anxiety, and insomnia. Notable figures like the Irish physician William Brooke O'Shaughnessy, who studied cannabis use in India, medicine.3 introduced it to Western Additionally, cannabis was used recreationally, particularly among the working class. In the 19th century, Hashish, a concentrated form of cannabis, was readily available in "hashish parlors" in major U.S cities. Hashish parlors, often frequented by artists, writers, and intellectuals, offered a space for experimentation and social interaction. These establishments provided a unique cultural experience, where individuals could gather to consume hashish and engage in stimulating conversations. However, the growing concerns about the potential dangers of cannabis, coupled with shifting social attitudes, led to increased restrictions on its use. The once-thriving hashish parlors were eventually shut down, marking the beginning of a period of prohibition. Despite the growing concerns surrounding cannabis, the plant continued to play a significant role in various industries, particularly during periods of economic and political upheaval in the United States. During the Civil War (1861-1865), hemp became a crucial industrial crop, playing a vital role in supporting the war effort for both the Union and the Confederacy. Its strong, durable fibers were used to produce a variety of essential goods, including rope, sails, uniforms, and paper. The federal government, recognizing the strategic importance of hemp, encouraged its cultivation through various initiatives and subsidies. This period marked a significant moment in the history of hemp within the United States, highlighting its versatility as a resource. And in select states within the Confederacy, like Pennsylvania, Virginia, and Maryland; hemp was exchanged as an alternative to legal tender, demonstrating its critical economic role during a crisis.4 time of national As the 19th century progressed, negative stereotypes about cannabis began to emerge, fueled by racial prejudice and moral panic. The demonization of cannabis was often linked to its association with marginalized groups, particularly newly arriving immigrants from Mexico and (Sayin, 2014) (Shaughnessy, 1843) 4 (USDA - Unites States Department of Agriculture, 2020) Body

author: Paula Wang
thesis_title: Cannabis Equity
paragraph: other Latin American countries. Several laws were enacted to restrict its use, laying the groundwork for the prohibition era that would follow. The demonization of cannabis, rooted in racial prejudice and moral panic, began to take shape in the early 20th century. This period saw a rise in anti-immigrant sentiment, particularly directed at Mexican immigrants who were associated with cannabis use due to its prevalence in Mexican culture. From its introduction by Spaniards in the 16th century, cannabis in Mexico served a variety of purpose. In Mexican folk medicine, cannabis was used to treat a variety of ailments, including pain, inflammation, and respiratory problems. It was often prepared as a tea or poultice.5 Cannabis was also used recreationally, particularly among the working class. It was often smoked in cigarettes or pipes. The demonization of cannabis during this period was not based on a widespread or problematic use within Mexican communities. Rather, it was a tool used to further marginalize and criminalize Mexican immigrants. The exaggerated claims about cannabis-induced violence and insanity were rooted in racist stereotypes and had little basis in reality. The Marihuana Tax Act of 1937, heavily influenced by racist propaganda like "Reefer Madness," criminalized cannabis and disproportionately targeted marginalized communities, particularly Mexican Americans, and those of Mexican descent. This act was not an isolated event but part of a broader trend of discriminatory legislation targeting immigrant communities. Additionally, cannabis possession was deemed a felony in 1956, under the Federal Narcotics Control Act. These legislations, along with the subsequent Controlled Substances Act of 1970, which classified cannabis as a Schedule I drug, laid the foundation for decades of prohibition and mass incarceration. This policy stated that there was no acceptable medicinal use application for cannabis and it was a high potential drug for misuse. The gradual shift towards harsher penalties, including felony charges, stemmed from a combination of factors: growing anti-drug sentiment, the association of cannabis with 'undesirable' groups, and the increasing influence of law enforcement agencies advocating for stricter drug control. This growing stigma against cannabis culminated in the 1930s with the release of the infamous propaganda film, "Reefer Madness." Released in 1936, "Reefer Madness" was a sensationalized exploitation film, falsely portraying cannabis as a 'killer weed' that drove users to insanity and violent crime. It depicted young people descending into madness after smoking marijuana, engaging in acts of murder, suicide, and sexual depravity. The film painted a terrifying picture of cannabis, portraying it as a gateway to madness, violence, and moral decay. This sensationalized film, with its exaggerated depictions of drug-induced psychosis and criminal behavior, stoked public fears and fueled a moral panic. The film portrayed Mexican immigrants as drug-crazed criminals, contributing to the racist association of cannabis with this marginalized group. This era also marked the emergence of the derogatory term "marijuana," which was used to further stigmatize the plant. The film's lasting impact is undeniable, shaping public perception of cannabis for generations to come. It laid the groundwork for the Marihuana Tax Act of 1937, which effectively criminalized cannabis in the United States. The Marihuana Tax Act of 1937, a product of racial prejudice and moral panic, effectively criminalized cannabis and ushered in a dark era of prohibition. This draconian law targeted marginalized communities, particularly Mexican immigrants, as evidenced by the work of Galliher and Walker (1977). 5 (Clarke and Merlin) Body

author: Paula Wang
thesis_title: Cannabis Equity
paragraph: The Controlled Substances Act of 1970 marked a significant turning point in the federal government's approach to drug policy. This legislation classified cannabis as a Schedule I drug, placing it in the same category as heroin and LSD. This classification, which implies a high potential for abuse and no currently accepted medical use, has had a profound impact on cannabis policy and criminal justice in the United States. The Act's rigid categorization has contributed to the mass incarceration of millions of individuals, disproportionately affecting marginalized communities and perpetuating systemic inequalities. Despite these restrictive policies, the cultural and social landscape began to shift in the mid-20th century. However, the seeds of rebellion were sown. The counterculture movement of the 1960s embraced cannabis as a symbol of freedom and self-expression. Young people, disillusioned with the rigid social norms of the 1950s, turned to cannabis as a means of escape and spiritual exploration. The drug's association with rock music, psychedelic art, and countercultural ideals solidified its place in popular culture. However, the War on Drugs, launched by President Richard Nixon in the 1970s, further entrenched cannabis prohibition and its associated harms. Ex-President Richard Nixon's War on Drugs, launched in the 1970s, targeted cannabis and other illicit substances. Nixon and his administration associated drug use with countercultural movements and social unrest, framing it as a threat to American values. This punitive approach led to mass incarceration, particularly affecting marginalized communities and people of color. The War on Drugs had a profound impact on cannabis policy, criminal justice, and public perception of the plant. The War on Drugs had a profound and disproportionate impact on communities of color, particularly Black and Brown communities. Cannabis, once a widely accepted plant with medicinal and industrial uses, was criminalized, leading to mass incarceration and the erosion of civil liberties.6 Despite similar rates of cannabis use, Black adults in the United States are arrested for cannabis offenses at significantly higher rates than white adults. This racial disparity is documented in research by Philbin et al. (2021), who found a lower lifetime prevalence of adults.7 cannabis use among Black adults compared to white However, Black adults were still more likely to be arrested for cannabis possession. This troubling trend points to racial bias within the criminal justice system, potentially due to factors like implicit bias in policing practices. As a result of “Reefer Madness” and the harsh enforcement of marijuana laws since the early 1980s has led to the arrest of millions, disproportionately impacting marginalized communities. Mandatory minimum sentencing has resulted in excessive prison terms, often exceeding those given to violent offenders. The cannabis arrest rate per 100,000 residents skyrocketed in the early 1990’s and continued to grow exponentially throughout the early 2000s. In recent decades, opinions regarding cannabis have shifted, with a growing push to legalize the herb for medical and recreational use. Several states legalized medical marijuana in the 1990s, followed by a wave of legalization for recreational use in the 2010s. However, cannabis remains illegal under federal law, creating continued legal and regulatory hurdles. Cannabis has a long and complex history in the United States, marked by periods of acceptance and prohibition. Cannabis legalization has had a tremendous cultural and societal impact. It has disrupted old beliefs and traditions, resulting in growing acceptance and (Singer, 2017) Body

author: Paula Wang
thesis_title: Cannabis Equity
paragraph: Chapter 2: Cannabis Legalization and Policy As a cannabis enthusiast, I've witnessed firsthand the transformative power of this plant. It's been a tool for relaxation, creativity, and personal growth. Seeing the legalization movement gain momentum has been both exciting and inspiring. It's a step towards acknowledging the plant's potential and addressing the injustices of the past. This shift in public perception and policy has paved the way for a more nuanced understanding of cannabis and its potential benefits. The legalization of cannabis in the United States has had significant societal implications. While federal law maintains its prohibition, a growing number of states have legalized cannabis for medical or recreational use. This shift is driven by factors such as changing public attitudes, recognition of potential medical benefits, and economic opportunities. Still, challenges remain, including potential public health concerns and ensuring equitable access to the industry, especially for marginalized communities. Legalization offers the potential to address historical injustices and promote social equity by reforming criminal justice practices and investing in disadvantaged communities. Since the 1990s, there has been a significant shift in public opinion and policy regarding cannabis, leading to a surge in state-level legalization efforts. As of 2023, within the continental United States, 38 states have legalized cannabis for medical use, while 24 states have legalized use.9 it for recreational This rapid expansion of legalization reflects a growing recognition of cannabis's potential therapeutic benefits and its role in promoting social justice and economic development. This surge in legalization has led to the development of a complex regulatory framework, ensuring public safety and consumer protection. States that have legalized cannabis have established comprehensive regulatory frameworks to oversee the industry. These regulations cover various aspects of the cannabis supply chain, including cultivation, processing, sales.10 manufacturing, distribution, and retail To ensure product quality and safety, states often implement strict quality control standards, such as testing for potency, contaminants, and pesticides. Additionally, age verification and other security measures are in place to prevent underage access to cannabis products. The growing body of scientific research into cannabis's therapeutic potential has played a pivotal role in driving legalization efforts. States like California, Colorado, and Oregon have been at the forefront of this movement, pioneering medical and recreational cannabis programs. This trend has been fueled by the recognition of cannabis's potential to alleviate a wide range of medical conditions, including chronic pain, anxiety, epilepsy, and even certain types of cancer. Moreover, the legalization of cannabis has unlocked significant economic opportunities, creating jobs and stimulating local economies. From cultivation and processing to retail sales and ancillary industries, the cannabis industry has the potential to generate substantial revenue and contribute to new economic opportunities, creating jobs in cultivation, processing, distribution, and retail.11 (The University of Vermont et al., 2023) 10(USC Schaeffer, Pacula, PhD et al., 2022) 11 (PMC PubMed Central et al., 2016) Body

author: Paula Wang
thesis_title: Cannabis Equity
paragraph: This shift in public opinion has paved the way for a more nuanced understanding of cannabis and its potential benefits. Public opinion on cannabis legalization has undergone a dramatic shift in recent decades. In the 1960s and 1970s, support for legalization began to rise, but it wasn't until the late 20th and early 21st centuries that a significant change occurred. According to a 1969 Gallup poll, only 12% of Americans favored legalizing marijuana. This number steadily climbed throughout the following decades, reaching a record high of 70% in a 2023 Gallup poll.12 This dramatic increase in support reflects a growing awareness of cannabis's potential medical benefits, a recognition of the failures of prohibition, and a general shift in social attitudes towards the drug. This growing support for legalization has opened the door to a more equitable and just approach to cannabis policy. The legalization of cannabis offers an opportunity to address these historical injustices and promote social justice. By implementing policies such as expungement of criminal records, supporting minority-owned businesses, investing in impacted communities, and educating the public, we can work towards a more equitable future. This includes addressing the disproportionate impact of cannabis prohibition on marginalized communities, particularly communities of color. By investing in these communities and providing opportunities for economic empowerment, we can begin to repair the damage caused by decades of criminalization. Cannabis legalization has gained significant momentum in recent years, with Canada and Mexico emerging as pioneers in this global movement. In 2018, Canada became the first G7 country to legalize recreational cannabis, paving the way for a regulated industry and a more nuanced approach to cannabis policy. The Group of Seven (G7) is an informal bloc of the world's seven most advanced economies: Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, the United Kingdom, and the United States, along with the European Union. Mexico followed suit in 2021, legalizing medical and recreational cannabis. Examining the policies and practices of these neighboring countries can provide valuable insights for the United States. By learning from their experiences, the US can implement effective regulatory frameworks, address potential challenges, and maximize the benefits of cannabis legalization. For instance, Canada's approach to cannabis legalization, including its focus on public health and safety, can inform US policy development. Similarly, Mexico's experience with cannabis legalization, particularly in terms of addressing social and economic disparities, can offer valuable lessons for the US. The path toward a more equitable future is not without its challenges. Growing up, I was always fascinated by the world of cannabis. It seemed like a plant shrouded in mystery, with a history as old as time. As a young adult, I got a firsthand look at the underground cannabis market. I started selling to friends and acquaintances, drawn to the idea of sharing something I believed in. It was a risky business, but the thrill of it all, and the knowledge that I was providing a valuable product, kept me going. I felt comfortable because I was selling products that were sourced from my friend's legal farm. As a seasoned cannabis user, I had a keen eye for quality and knew what to look for in a good product. I would often indulge in the very products I was selling, gaining a deep appreciation for their quality and effects. This firsthand experience allowed me to connect with customers on a personal level, sharing my knowledge and enthusiasm for cannabis. (Saad,2023) 13 (Hathaway and McCann, 2022) Body

author: Paula Wang
thesis_title: Cannabis Equity
paragraph: Today, the cannabis scene is as diverse as its strains. I enjoy indulging in a variety of cannabis products and appreciate the convenience of purchasing them both in-person and online. The dispensaries I frequent are legal establishments that sell a variety of shelf-legal THC, Delta, and CBD products. My notable favorites offer a luxurious and refined experience. As a consumer, I deeply appreciate the company’s attention to detail in curating the most enjoyable customer service experience. It felt like the world finally saw what I saw. However, to truly understand the impact of cannabis legalization, it's essential to consider perspectives from those outside the cannabis community. It's easy to get caught up in the excitement and normalization within the cannabis world, but it's important to acknowledge the viewpoints of those with less exposure. This is where my partner, Giovanny, comes in. Giovanny and I met through a popular dating app last year. Initially, our conversations rarely touched upon cannabis. While I was an enthusiastic consumer, Gio had limited knowledge and a somewhat neutral stance on the subject. As our relationship deepened, I became more open about my cannabis use, and he, in turn, became more curious about it. His initial curiosity eventually led to a willingness to experience and learn more about the plant in a legal and safe environment. Giovanny, a 26-year-old New Yorker and non-cannabis user, offered a unique perspective on the cannabis industry. Having limited exposure to the plant, his insights provided a valuable contrast to my own experiences. His first visit to a cannabis dispensary, Gotham NYC, a popular licensed dispensary located in the heart of Williamsburg, offered a glimpse into the evolving landscape of cannabis retail. "Walking into Gotham NYC was like stepping into a high-end boutique. The sleek design, knowledgeable staff, and curated selection of products were a far cry from the stereotypical image of a dispensary. It was an elevated experience, and I was impressed by the professionalism and care put into every aspect of the store." - Giovanny M. (2024) Giovanny described his first visit to Gotham NYC as entering a high-end luxury boutique, akin to Dior or Apple. He was impressed by the attentive staff, who frequently checked in to ensure a pleasant shopping experience. The store's curated selection and inviting atmosphere created a sense of exclusivity and sophistication. Gio was particularly fascinated by the diverse range of cannabis and cannabis-inspired lifestyle products available at Gotham NYC. From high-quality flowers to innovative CBD-infused skincare, the store offered a curated selection of products to suit every taste and preference. It's important to note that while dispensaries like Gotham NYC represent a shift towards a more refined cannabis experience, they are not the only face of the industry in New York. Since legalization, a variety of establishments have emerged, reflecting the diverse and evolving nature of cannabis culture. Some dispensaries embrace a more traditional "head shop" aesthetic, with vibrant colors, psychedelic art, and a focus on counter cultural imagery. These establishments often cater to long-time cannabis enthusiasts and those seeking a more casual and familiar environment. They might feature a wider array of smoking accessories, glassware, and less emphasis on a luxury experience. Body

author: Paula Wang
thesis_title: Cannabis Equity
paragraph: Conversely, some unlicensed pop-up shops still operate in a gray area, often existing in a temporary or semi-permanent state. These establishments sometimes perpetuate older stereotypes of cannabis use, both in their aesthetic and their approach to sales. Visually, these shops often stand in stark contrast to the sleek, modern design of licensed dispensaries. Loud, attention-grabbing colors like neon green, hot pink, or fluorescent orange dominate, creating a visually chaotic and sometimes overwhelming environment. Cartoonish figures, often with exaggerated features or stereotypical "stoner" traits (e.g., bloodshot eyes, goofy grins), are frequently used in their branding and decor. These characters can range from generic "weed mascots" to blatant rip-offs of popular cartoon characters, often in a way that could appeal to children. Some products, particularly edibles, might be packaged in a way that mimics candy or snack food, with bright colors, playful fonts, and imagery that seems targeted towards a younger audience. This raises concerns about accidental ingestion by children and perpetuates a lack of seriousness around cannabis consumption. This diversity highlights the ongoing evolution of cannabis culture. While some establishments are striving for a high-end, normalized experience, others maintain a connection to the plant's countercultural roots. This variety caters to different consumer preferences and reflects the complex journey of cannabis from the underground to the mainstream. The atmosphere of these unlicensed shops can be less professional and more reminiscent of the pre-legalization era, with a focus on secrecy and a more casual approach to transactions, fostering a more “underground” market feel. The language used, the products sold, and the overall atmosphere might embrace and even exaggerate stereotypical images of cannabis users as lazy, unproductive, or solely focused on getting "high." This can be alienating to those unfamiliar with cannabis or those seeking to integrate it into their lives in a responsible and wellness-oriented way. This hands-on experience not only fueled my passion for cannabis but also provided me with valuable insights into the industry. As I witnessed the growing acceptance of cannabis, a sense of vindication washed over me. As I see state after state legalize, I can’t help but feel a sense of pride and success. It feels as if the world is finally catching up. After years of stigma and criminalization, cannabis is finally being recognized for its potential benefits. It was a victory for personal freedom, social justice, and the power of individual choice. Body

author: Paula Wang
thesis_title: Cannabis Equity
paragraph: Part II: Cannabis and Consciousness Chapter 3: The Science of Cannabis Cannabis, a complex plant with a rich history, has been used for centuries for its medicinal and recreational properties. Its effects are primarily attributed to two main cannabinoids: tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) and cannabidiol (CBD). These compounds interact with the body's endocannabinoid system, a complex network of receptors and neurotransmitters that regulates various physiological processes. The endocannabinoid system is a complex network of receptors and neurotransmitters that regulates various physiological processes, including pain, mood, appetite, and sleep. Cannabis interacts with this system through its cannabinoid compounds. THC, the primary psychoactive compound, binds to cannabinoid receptors in the brain, producing a range of effects, including euphoria, relaxation, altered perception, and increased appetite. A non-psychoactive compound, CBD interacts with the endocannabinoid system to promote relaxation, reduce anxiety, and alleviate pain. It may also have neuroprotective disorders.14 properties and can be helpful in managing conditions like epilepsy and anxiety Personally, I've found a harmonious balance between THC and CBD. While THC offers a euphoric and mind-altering experience, CBD provides a calming and grounding effect. I often enjoy the synergistic benefits of full-spectrum products, which contain both THC and CBD. This balanced approach allows me to experience the best of both worlds, without overwhelming my system. I've explored various methods of cannabis consumption, from the classic joint to the modern convenience of edibles. Each method offers a unique experience, catering to different preferences and desired effects. Whether I'm enjoying a leisurely smoke session with friends or indulging in a potent edible, cannabis continues to enrich my life in countless ways. I typically start my night with a 10mg edible, allowing its calming effects to set in before diving into my work and nightly routine. Later in the evening, I often enjoy a joint or bowl to unwind and de-stress. This combination of methods allows me to experience the full spectrum of cannabis's benefits, from focused productivity to deep relaxation. Cannabis can be effective in managing chronic pain conditions, such as arthritis and neuropathic pain. CBD, in particular, has shown promise in reducing anxiety and promoting relaxation.15 Cannabis can also help alleviate insomnia and improve sleep quality. Cannabis can stimulate appetite, particularly in individuals with conditions like anorexia or cancer. Cannabis has been shown to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may be beneficial for conditions such as arthritis and inflammatory bowel disease. Preliminary studies suggest that cannabis may have neuroprotective properties, helping to protect the brain from damage. Alzheimer's disease (AD) is characterized by the accumulation of amyloid plaques and neuroinflammation. Studies have shown that cannabinoids may offer neuroprotection by reducing inflammation and oxidative stress. However, AD patients often exhibit decreased cannabinoid receptor expression, potentially limiting the therapeutic potential of cannabis. (Division et al., 2017) (National Library of Medicine et al., 2018) 16 (Maroon and Bost, 2018) Body

author: Paula Wang
thesis_title: Cannabis Equity
paragraph: While the individual effects of THC and CBD have been extensively studied, it's increasingly recognized that the full potential of cannabis lies in the synergistic interaction of its various components. This concept is known as the Entourage effect. The entourage effect refers to the synergistic interaction between various cannabinoids, terpenes, and other compounds found in cannabis. This complex interplay results in a more potent and nuanced effect than that of individual compounds alone. When consumed, these compounds work together to modulate the body's endocannabinoid system, a network of receptors and neurotransmitters that regulates a variety of physiological processes. By interacting with this system, cannabis can produce a wide range of effects, including pain relief, anxiety reduction, and improved sleep. By combining different strains and consumption methods, individuals can tailor their experience to specific needs and preferences. The specific effects of cannabis can vary depending on the combination of cannabinoids and terpenes present in the plant. For example, certain terpenes may enhance the pain-relieving properties of THC, while others may amplify its euphoric effects. Understanding the entourage effect is essential for optimizing the therapeutic potential of cannabis. By consuming whole-plant cannabis products, rather than isolated cannabinoids, individuals can benefit from the full spectrum of compounds and their synergistic interactions. While cannabis offers numerous potential benefits, including pain relief, anxiety reduction, improved sleep, and appetite stimulation, it's crucial to acknowledge the potential risks. These risks can include impaired cognitive function, respiratory problems, and, in rare cases, psychosis. THC can disrupt the delicate balance of the endocannabinoid system, particularly during adolescence, potentially leading to long-term cognitive and behavioral consequences. The potency of modern cannabis products has significantly increased. Today's marijuana strains often contain much higher levels of THC, the primary psychoactive compound, compared to those available in the past. This increased potency, particularly in concentrated forms like wax, shatter, and dabs, can lead to more intense and potentially risky experiences, especially for inexperienced users. While cannabis can be a pleasurable and beneficial experience, it's important to consume responsibly. Consuming too much THC can lead to uncomfortable side effects, including anxiety, paranoia, and impaired cognitive function. In extreme cases, it may even trigger psychotic episodes in individuals with underlying mental health conditions. It's crucial to start with low doses, especially for those new to cannabis, and gradually increase intake as needed. When discussing the potency of cannabis, the percentage of THC is a primary indicator. This percentage represents the amount of tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) – the main psychoactive compound in cannabis – present in a product. In cannabis bud, for example, a THC percentage of 20% means that 20% of the bud's weight is THC. Vape products also use THC percentages to denote the concentration of THC within the oil. However, it's crucial to understand that THC percentage doesn't always directly correlate with the user's experience of intoxication. A 2020 study by CU Boulder researchers published in JAMA Psychiatry found that while smoking high-potency marijuana concentrates significantly boosted blood levels of THC (more than twice as much as smoking conventional weed), it didn't necessarily get users higher. 17As lead author Cinnamon Bidwell noted, 'Surprisingly, we found that potency did not track with intoxication levels. While we saw striking differences in blood 17( L. Cinnamon Bidwell et al., “Association of Naturalistic Administration of Cannabis Flower and Concentrates with Intoxication and Impairment,” JAMA Psychiatry 77, no. 8 (August 1, 2020): 787) Body

paragraph: levels between the two groups, they were similarly impaired.' This research highlights the complex relationship between THC content and its effects, suggesting that factors like tolerance and individual metabolism play a significant role. I once made the mistake of underestimating the potency of an edible, a lesson etched vividly in my memory. The experience quickly transformed into a whirlwind of intense sensations, both exhilarating and terrifying. My perception of reality began to warp: everything seemed to vibrate with an unnatural intensity, sounds distorted into eerie echoes, and time stretched and compressed unpredictably. It was as if the world around me was disintegrating and reforming in a mind-warping dance. As the effects peaked, I found myself spiraling into a state of anxiety and paranoia. Every noise, every shadow seemed to hold a threat, amplified by a racing heart and a sense of impending doom. It felt as though I was slipping into a never-ending pit of despair, my mind conjuring up worst-case scenarios with relentless ferocity. The line between reality and hallucination blurred, and I struggled to anchor myself in the present moment. It was one of those moments where I felt - this is it. My time has come. The line between reality and hallucination blurred, and I struggled to anchor myself in the present moment. The entire period of horror lasted for maybe 4 hours or so. I spent most of that time laying on the floor and on my couch, not knowing where I was even though I was in my own apartment. To make matters worse, my body began to rebel against the me. Waves of nausea washed over me, culminating in hours spent grappling with vomiting, a physical manifestation of the internal turmoil. The physical distress only heightened the psychological distress, creating a feedback loop of discomfort and fear. I vomited my own bodyweight in despair. It was a stark reminder that even within the realm of cannabis, experiences can quickly become overwhelming and deeply unpleasant. Ever since that experience, I have learned my tolerance for cannabis and enjoy cautiously - even as a seasoned user. Consequently, animal studies have demonstrated that adolescent exposure to THC can have significant long-term consequences for brain development. Research suggests that THC can disrupt the normal maturation of the prefrontal cortex, a brain region crucial for decision-making, impulse control, and emotional regulation. This disruption can lead to impaired cognitive function and behavioral problems. Similarly, human studies have shown that adolescent cannabis use is associated with accelerated thinning of the prefrontal cortex, a process that may hinder cognitive development. These findings highlight the importance of protecting young people from the potential harms of early cannabis use. A recent study published in JAMA Network Open found that adolescents who used cannabis, even without meeting the criteria for substance use disorder, were twice as likely to experience depression or suicidal thoughts compared to non-users. To maximize the benefits and minimize the risks, it's essential to approach cannabis consumption responsibly and be aware of individual sensitivities. As research continues to evolve, we gain a deeper understanding of the complex relationship between cannabis and the human body. Since THC, the primary psychoactive compound in cannabis, doesn't interact with the brain in the same way as substances like heroin or cocaine, which can lead to physical dependence and withdrawal symptoms, it's important to recognize that cannabis can still be 18 (Sultan et al., 2023) Body

author: Paula Wang

paragraph: Chapter 4: Altered States of Consciousness through History and Culture For millennia, humans have sought altered states of consciousness, often turning to plants and substances to facilitate these experiences. Cannabis, with its psychoactive properties, has been particularly prominent in various cultures. Ancient civilizations in Asia and Africa incorporated cannabis into their spiritual and religious practices, using it to induce altered states of consciousness and connect with the divine. For example, the ancient Egyptians used cannabis in religious rituals, while Hindus revered it as a sacred plant associated with the god Shiva. These cultures recognized the plant's ability to induce profound shifts in perception, thought, and emotion. Cannabis, through its interaction with the endocannabinoid system, can induce a range of altered states of consciousness. THC, the primary psychoactive compound in cannabis, can alter perception, mood, and cognition. The subjective experiences associated with cannabis use can vary widely, depending on factors such as the specific strain, dosage, and individual sensitivity. It can produce feelings of euphoria, relaxation, and altered time perception. CBD, on the other hand, can promote calmness and reduce anxiety, without inducing significant psychoactive effects. Personally, I've found that cannabis can be a powerful tool for enhancing sensory experiences. When I'm under the influence, the world transforms into a vibrant tapestry of colors, senses, and textures. Food tastes more flavorful, music becomes more soulful, and touch is more intimate. The simple act of running my fingers through my hair or feeling the warmth of the sun on my skin can be a profoundly satisfying experience. Cannabis allows me to perceive the world with a newfound sense of wonder and appreciation. Cannabis can enhance physical sensations, making intimate experiences more pleasurable. It can heighten sensitivity to touch, leading to more intense and satisfying encounters. For some, cannabis can also reduce anxiety and inhibitions, allowing for greater openness and vulnerability. The human brain is capable of producing a wide range of altered states, from the dreamlike experiences of sleep to the transcendent states of meditation. These states are often associated with changes in brainwave patterns, neurotransmitter activity, and sensory perception. However, it is important to note that the effects of cannabis can also vary depending on the set and setting, or the individual's mindset and environment. I've found that cannabis can elevate intimate experiences, making them more sensual and enjoyable. The world seems to slow down, allowing for a deeper connection and heightened awareness of physical sensations. The senses are amplified, making every touch, every caress, more meaningful. This of course, includes powerful climaxes that stop time. Each pulsing movement invites our bodies to move with grace and delicacy. The invigorating nature of hot air envelopes the user with ecstasy. Each movement invites the next. Unlike drunk sex, while high intimacy feels luxurious and adventurous. The potential for cannabis to enhance intimacy is only one facet of its complex relationship with human experience. While historically, cannabis played a more utilitarian role, its diverse fields of application varied greatly based on culture. In Hindu culture, cannabis use dates back thousands of years. Sacred Hindu texts, such as the Vedas, revered cannabis as one of five enlightenment.20 sacred plants, associating it with divine qualities and spiritual The Hindu God Body
