author: Qi Lei
thesis_title: where we go?
paragraph: Taking Breaks from Screens can Stimulate Creativity and Wellbeing The wide application of social media creates an immersive environment in modern day that can distort self-perception by incessantly showcasing the idealized lives of others, which often leads to detrimental social comparisons. Purposefully reducing the use of digital devices and social media can mitigate these negative impacts. And then individuals can refocus attention on personal achievements and the immediate physical environment, thereby promoting mindfulness and reducing feelings of envy and inadequacy. Empirical evidence supports the psychological benefits of this approach. According to research made by Hunt et al., participants who engaged in periods of reduced social media usage feel less loneliness and depression than before.24 They also have an improved sense of personal wellbeing due to the shift from self-contentment.25 focusing on external validation towards inner values and For people working in creative fields, taking a break from constant digital input can help boost creativity. Stepping away from screens gives the mind quietness that makes it easier to think deeply and come up with fresh ideas—something that’s really important for design and artistic work. Taking a break can help creative workers rethink how they use technology. It can lead to more thoughtful and intentional use of digital tools, which can make their work better and more meaningful. Also as mentioned in the article “Why Your Brain Needs More Downtime” by Ferris Jabr, taking breaks from screens and letting the mind rest is helpful for not only attention, memory, creativity and productivity, but also for overall daily performances. Jabr explains that a good rest “replenishes the brain’s stores of attention and motivation, encourages productivity and creativity,”26 and helps people perform at their best. As Jabr quotes from essayist Tim Kreider, “The space and quiet that idleness provides is a necessary condition for standing back from life and seeing it whole, for making unexpected connections and waiting inspiration”27. for the wild summer lightning strikes of It becomes easier for people to reflect on their thoughts, process inner emotions, and form new connections when the mind has space to breathe and is not focused on anything in particular, especially not on digital devices, where information is often overwhelming and dense. For people who move around a lot, or live in new environments like international students or remote workers, taking time to pause becomes even more important. Simple things like going for a walk, sitting quietly, or just letting thoughts wander can help feel refreshed and more creative. From my own experience, some of my most effective brainstorming ideas often come during screen-free moments like when I’m taking a bath, resting in a quiet place, or spending time Melissa G. Nostalgia Helps Emotional Anchoring and Identity Reinforcement Nostalgia is an effective emotional mechanism that reinforces personal identity and emotional stability by connecting past experiences to the present. author: Qi Lei
thesis_title: where we go?
paragraph: My thesis mainly focuses on questioning: How can we create a sense of ‘home’ that feels spiritually fulfilling and remains with us no matter where we go? This direction derives from my personal identity as an international graduate student, and I spent all my undergraduate and graduate school years in another country far away from home. Similar to my academic journey, my grandparents’ narratives of navigating through different parts of the same country imbued me with a profound curiosity about the emotional and spiritual dimensions of navigation. These experiences prompted me to think about what "home" means to different individuals and how those definitions may develop based on all influences from geographies, cultures, and personal growth. The Intersection of Navigation, Identity, and Belonging The concept of “home” is deeply influenced by personal identity, yet it is also highly fluid, shaped by experiences, relationships, and environments. Growing up, my identity was grounded in the place where I was born, a foundation that sharply contrasted with my grandparents’ identification of “home,” which was deeply rooted in their birthplace and their journeys across the country. As I moved across countries and immersed myself in different cultures, my understanding of “home” was reshaped to something more of a collective feeling of emotion and spirits that's no longer being restricted by physical borders or a specific location or culture. Ongoing transitions are important in both my academic path and personal experiences. I clearly understand the importance of adaptability if I want to maintain a sense of belonging through such experiences. With each move, I found myself renegotiating my identity—trying to hold on to familiar parts of myself while also adjusting to new environments. That constant push and pull between stability and change revealed how fluid the idea of “home” really is. It's not just a physical space, but a mix of relationships, memories, and experiences that help us feel grounded, wherever we are. This ongoing movement—both physical and emotional—has made me rethink what it means to “settle down.” For some, that might mean staying in one place and putting down roots. For others, it’s more about carrying a sense of inner stability and peace, even when everything around them is in flux. These personal reflections have shaped my research, especially in understanding how people create homes that are not only emotionally and spiritually fulfilling, but also portable and flexible. The difference between living in my home country with my family around and staying in the places I’ve studied on my own has also made me aware of the wide range of living environments and cultural practices. While cities might look similar on the surface, the ways people live in them can be strikingly different. Adapting to the changes like living habits, language usage, and tiny differences that may not be observed that obviously meant adjustments on not just my habits, but also how I see myself and connect with others. That process, though often difficult, offered me a chance to reflect more deeply on what “home” really means—and how our surroundings influence our sense of identity and belonging. I started exploring my thesis topic by interviewing my family members, especially my grandparents, about how they see themselves and understand the idea of home, trying to find similarities and differences among all the photographs I took based on the categories I organized those and comparing the emotions I review the place with the feeling I was first in the place based on the conversation in communication apps. Materiality, Memory, and the Design Process The material exploration in my design process plays a critical role in articulating the concept of “home” as a portable and spiritually fulfilling experience. As navigation is an immersive, multi-sensory process, I began by experimenting with materials that could evoke sensory and emotional connections to the idea of home. These explorations aimed to capture the complex interplay between memory, identity, and environment. Three primary materials—digital platforms, fabrics, and burnt paper—formed the foundation of my initial investigations. Digital technology is widely used to help us find our way in today's world due to the ease of communicating and building connections no matter the distance. For those separated from family and friends, digital platforms often become a vital thread that keeps emotional closeness alive despite physical distance. In my work, I’ve incorporated digital elements to reflect how technology helps maintain a sense of “home,” even when we are far from it. Fabrics, by contrast, evoke tactile and emotional associations with safety, comfort, and nostalgia. The application of fabrics made the media a powerful medium for exploring the emotional dimensions of “home” are deeply connected to childhood memories like toys, family homes, and cherished objects. Burnt paper, with its aged and textured aesthetic, symbolizes the fragmentation and abstraction of memory. The imperfect quality of layered burnt paper mirrors the ways in which memories of home can feel distant or incomplete, especially for those navigating unfamiliar spaces. As my exploration progressed, I drew from my personal travel experiences to identify the natural elements and sensory cues that were essential to my sense of belonging. This made me come up with the idea of making an installation that centered on the metaphor of luggage—a symbol of travel, transition, and the tension between permanence and impermanence. Using cardboard strips, I constructed the framework of two interlocking boxes, mimicking the form of a suitcase. This structure served as a canvas for integrating elements that represented my personal connections to nature, memory, and navigation. The two boxes were intentionally misaligned, assembled in a way that suggested an open suitcase—allowing viewers to see what lies inside. The upper half stood vertically, while the lower half lay flat on the ground, tilted at an angle. This distortion was intentional, aiming to break away from the traditional suitcase shape and express the energy of life within. The idea was to show that the contents inside the luggage possess a kind of inner force—capable of pushing open the top, reaching upward, and seeking new space to explore. To imbue the project with a sense of organic continuity, I intertwined folded strips of burnt paper, creating vine-like patterns that concealed the underlying structure. These patterns are visualizations of growth and resilience that highlights the vitality characters of the natural world, which can be used as an easy-to-get tool that helps recall the sense of “home.” As a recurring motif in my work, ginkgo leaves were incorporated as a tangible reminder of continuity amidst change. This plant is significant to me because they were not only commonly seen near my childhood home, but also in many places I’ve visited. I used this plant in my project as a bridge between the familiar and the new, reinforcing the idea of home as a dynamic and portable construct. The design process was iterative, evolving through multiple stages to balance abstraction and representation. Abstract forms constructed with transparent effect in the early iterations carried a sense of ethereality that was then turned into the usage of transparent materials in later developments. This visual representation allowed the design to show a dynamic interplay of energy, memory, and emotion. Elements like airplane tickets and luggage tags were included as large-scale prints that had strong visual influences compared to the actual size, which further emphasized themes of movement and connection. I was seeking ways to create a design that resonates with more individuals by capturing the complexities of navigating identity, belonging, and spirituality. By integrating my personal memory, materials, and feeling of textures of the material usage, the project explores how individuals can create portable anchors to their sense of home—anchors that sustain them through transitions and changes.

author: Qi Lei
thesis_title: where we go?
paragraph: This thesis reconceptualized “home” as an emotional and spiritual construct that can adapt to the unique needs of individuals who often have to transit from place to place. The idea of “home” already moved beyond just being a physical place in today’s interconnected world. Instead, it can be understood as a psychological anchor that is shaped by the relationships, experiences, and values that give us a sense of stability and connection. This more personal and internal sense of home helps people hold on to their identity, especially when they’re constantly moving or living in transition. More research can be made in the future to explore how to apply these ideas to urban planning and social policies that better support a growing diversity of mobile populations. As cities and communities continue to evolve, there is an opportunity to create spaces that fulfill not only physical needs but also foster emotional and psychological wellbeing. This could include the development of adaptable communal spaces that digital nomads, expatriates, third culture kids, remote workers, and freelancers who increasingly populate a fluid workforce can all feel fit in. With changes in job structures and societal norms driven by technology, urban spaces must evolve to cultivate a sense of belonging and community through flexible housing solutions, shared cultural spaces, and infrastructure that supports both in-person and virtual interactions. As technology continues developing, and work culture becomes more fluid, urban life is also undergoing significant change. Looking ahead, city living will likely place greater value on flexibility and mobility. To keep pace with these shifts, urban planners and policymakers have an opportunity to design environments that reflect and support the more dynamic, mobile ways people are choosing to live in a globalized world. Such environments would address not only the practical needs of mobility but also the psychological and emotional challenges faced by those who regularly move across cultural and geographic boundaries. Building on the recognition that a stable sense of “home” is essential to the wellbeing of transient populations, these future efforts could contribute to more inclusive and resilient communities. With intentional design and a clear visionary, the idea of home can be widely accepted as a unity that is formed by both a shared and deeply personal experience, which adapts to the changing realities and challenges of 21st-century life.

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: ● What is "Loss" in the Translation Process? ● How Can We Reinterpret the Value of "Loss" in Translation? ● How Can Design Transform the Translation Process into a Generative Act?

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: I have yet to find an alternative Japanese translation for "I love you." The literal translation would "私はあなたを愛している (Watashi wa anata o aishite iru)," be but this feels too direct and unfamiliar in daily conversation, making it awkward somehow. The Japanese literary master, "月が綺麗ですね Natsume Soseki, is said to have translated "I love you" as (The moon is beautiful, isn't it?)"—a choice that, according to legend, captures the Japanese tendency to avoid expressing emotions too directly. However, while "The moon is beautiful, isn't it?" has been someone like me who lives regarded as a poetic reflection of Japanese sensibility in translation, to in a modern and globalized world, it feels a bit too roundabout and distant from the directness of "I love you." Over a century has passed since Natsume Soseki's translation of "I love you" as "The moon is beautiful, isn't it?" took place in the Meiji era, when English education was not yet widespread in Japan. Today, AI and deep learning-powered translation services have become profoundly integrated into our daily lives. When you type "I love you" into Google Translate, it instantly "愛してます translates to (Aishitemasu)." In this era, we can quickly interpret various languages with just a few clicks, easily overcoming language barriers and gaining access to information and ideas. Even when traveling to a place with a language we don't know, we can use our smartphones to read menus in the local language and have basic communication with native speakers.

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: Yet, I wonder if we have become too dependent on this convenience. Is it really enough to simply "愛してます"? translate "I love you" as In Japanese, there are many ways to translate this short "あなたを愛している "わたしは君を愛してる sentence, such as (Anata wo Aishiteiru)" or (Watashi Aishiteru)."1 "愛してます" wa Kimi wo Is it really okay to just end with and not consider the subtle 1"あなた(Anata)" "愛している(Aishiteiru)" "君(Kimi)" "あなた." "わたしは(Watashi "愛してる" "愛している," "あなたを愛している" "わたしは君を愛してる" differences in meaning that these other expressions convey? Soseki's translation is undeniably an interpretive rendering, straying far from a direct translation. It's unrealistic to expect a translation service to produce this result—nor should it. However, the cultural and linguistic nuances embedded in this translation, along with the uniquely Japanese sensibility it conveys, communicate too much for me to simply overlook. As a native Japanese speaker who learned English later in life, I often feel that translators can't fully convey my sense of humor. For example, unique expressions of politeness, subtle nuances, word choice suited to the situation, humor and metaphors often result in a "loss" in translation, given that all of my Japanese language choices are challenging for machine translators. This is not an uncommon realization for me, to notice such "loss" elements in daily translation, and it is not just with translation tools. I once exchanged an emotional message with an English-speaking acquaintance. Typically, when sorting my thoughts or dealing with complex logic, I write in Japanese first. On this occasion, I tried to write down my thoughts and feelings as accurately as possible in Japanese, while avoiding direct expressions because I did not want to confuse the other person. I once attempted to translate it into English myself, but there were parts that didn't seem to be fully translated, so I ran the text through a translation tool. As a result, although the translation was accurate, it turned out to be vague English that made it unclear what I was trying to say. I suspect this is because I used a uniquely Japanese way of avoiding direct expressions. From such experiences, I realized that when conveying something in English, even the ambiguous emotions or expressions that are acceptable in Japanese need to be rendered more concretely. Moreover, the English text produced through that process often felt like it was not my own words, but rather, someone else's. Accumulating these experiences led me to reflect more deeply on the limitations and challenges of using machine translation not merely as a tool, but as an object of critical consideration. For me, the sensation I experience when doing translation in English—often leading to words that sound like someone else's rather than my own—is both frustrating and fascinating. It feels as though I have lost some of my most cherished emotions and sensibilities, yet simultaneously discovered a new facet of myself. Perhaps it is precisely within the "lost" elements of the translation process that my identity and character are encapsulated. Moreover, although the mechanical translations often convey the original meaning of a speaker or writer accurately, I feel there's an overlooked problem in which our identities and the richness of our expressions can be lost in the translation. Through this research, I aim to explore how the "lost" elements in the translation process relate to our identity. Specifically, I seek to clarify what exactly is being "lost" in translations between Japanese and English. By focusing on these overlooked aspects, I intend to highlight the challenges of blindly relying on machine translation. At the same time, rather than viewing these omissions as mere losses, I will investigate how design can transform them into opportunities for self-discovery and deeper understanding of others. How would you, or I, translate "I love you" in our own languages? Furthermore, what might that choice reveal about our identities?

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: What is "Language"? Linguistic diversity has long been a subject of attention in linguistics and cognitive science for its profound influence on human perception and thought. There are currently over 7,000 languages spoken around the world, each with its unique sounds, vocabulary and structure. In addition, it is said that each language has its own expression and worldview. Charlemagne, the ruler of the Holy Roman Empire, is often quoted as saying, "To have a second language is to possess a 2024).2 second soul" (A Quote by Charlemagne Moreover, philosopher and linguist Wilhelm von Humboldt considered language not merely as a tool for communication but as a framework that shapes culture, thought and the worldview of individuals. "The difference between languages is not only in sounds and signs," he stated, "but in worldview. Herein is found the reason and 1820).3 ultimate goal of all the study of language" (Humboldt His ideas, named as "Sprachgeist," continue to influence contemporary fields significantly such as linguistics and cognitive science. These words and remarks suggest that language is not merely a tool for communication but something that profoundly shapes our thinking and identity. The Linguistic Relativity Hypothesis, developed by Edward Sapir and Benjamin Whorf, is another key theory asserting that language influences human thought and perception. According to their hypothesis, people who speak different languages experience reality in unique ways. For example, how colors are categorized, or how time and directions are conceptualized can vary 1956).4 based on one's language (Sapir and Whorf That is, the structure, vocabulary and syntax of a language play a significant role in shaping the thought patterns, values and worldviews of its speakers. Similarly, Ferdinand de Saussure's structuralist language theory, which forms a foundation of modern linguistics, views language as a system of signs, with each language reflecting the structure of its society and culture. According to Saussure, language functions as a framework for understanding society and shapes the very fabric of the social structure. In Saussure's view,

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: 2A 3Humboldt, 4Sapir, language is not just an assortment of words but a system that, within its cultural and social 1959)5. contexts, interrelates to shape the speaker's thought and identity (Saussure, Taken together, these theories show us how each language inherently reinforces or encapsulates a distinct worldview, shaping how its speakers perceive and interact with their environment and situations, often without conscious awareness. Both the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis and de Saussure's theory of linguistic relativity suggest that language fundamentally shapes how individuals perceive and interpret the world around them. These theories illustrate that linguistic structures influence cognitive frameworks, guiding thoughts and interactions in specific cultural and contextual ways. Vice versa, Saussure argues in the theory of linguistic relativity that by analyzing a language, we can understand the worldview of the society that uses it. This difference in worldviews is evident when we compare the linguistic structures of Japanese and English, which reflect the differences in perceptions of reality. Generally, it is said that Japanese uses more passive expressions, while English favors active sentences (Ikegami 1993).6 Yoshihiko and Seisaku Kawakami Due to the linguistic structure of English, which often requires a clear subject, there is an emphasis on clarifying the actor and actions, leading to more active sentence constructions. In contrast, Japanese does not necessarily require a subject, allowing for ambiguity regarding the actor and often avoiding clearly assigned responsibility. Imagine suddenly getting caught in the rain and ending up wet. In English, one might say, "I got wet in the rain," where the person "I" becomes impacted by the natural phenomenon, the rain, "雨に濡れた and this is expressed actively. In Japanese, however, it would be expressed as (ame ni nureta)", which translates to "(I) was rained on" in a passive construction—where the subject "I" is typically omitted. In Western thought, nature is often seen as something to be controlled, tamed and even dominated. This is said to have originated from the ancient Greek era, where civilization was built through the management and domination of nature. Since then, through the Industrial Revolution and up to the modern era, this way of thinking has been further reinforced alongside technological progress. In contrast, the Japanese view of nature is generally one of acceptance 2024).7 and reverence, recognizing nature as something beyond human control (Nagai This is believed to be influenced by the frequent experience of natural disasters as well as Shinto beliefs. The worldview of "coexistence with and reverence for nature" is deeply rooted in Japanese language, with expressions like "I was rained on" implicitly suggesting that humans are 5Saussure, 6Ikegami, Tokyo: Kinokuniya Shoten. 7Nagai not the masters in the face of nature's power but rather subordinate to it. Thus, the presence of many passive constructions in Japanese often reflects a sense of victimhood. From the above example, we can see that the structures of English, which emphasizes the actor and actions and Japanese, which emphasizes the passivity of the subject – both encode profoundly different worldviews and belief systems related to the natural world. In other words, in a situation such as "being rained on," English speakers and Japanese speakers perceive different realities. As we can see, the reality that there are over 7,000 languages in the world means that there are over 7,000 encoded ways of perceiving the world. The diversity of worldviews and perspectives embedded in each language should be increasingly valued in today's interconnected world, where globalization and internationalization continue to expand. As cultures and languages interact more frequently, the importance of linguistic diversity becomes clear—not only as a means of communication but also as a reservoir of unique cultural identities and perspectives. What is "Translation"? Assuming that each language encodes its own culture's unique expressions and worldview, the act of translation can be seen not merely as an equivalent replacement between languages but as a more complex and creative process of reconstructing reality in a way that aligns with another culture's worldview. That is, translators don't just translate words; they translate culture. Anthony Burgess, an English writer and composer, once described the difficulty of translation as "not a matter of words only: it is a matter of making intelligible a whole culture" (Burgess Anthony 2024).8 This presents the challenge translators face in respecting the original author's expression while adapting it to a different culture and language. Translation, then, is not simply about converting words but is a creative task requiring deep understanding and skill. Translators generally need to acquire specific skills to carry out this intricate work. They strive to capture the intent and background of the original text as closely as possible, while also recreating the author's intended message within a new language—and consequently, a new worldview. Skopos theory, with skopos as one of the concepts in translation theory that originates from a Greek word meaning "purpose" or "intention", asserts that the method of translation should 2000).9 change according to its purpose (Vermeer, Hans J According to this theory, translators must consider how to reconstruct the "reality" of the original text based on the translation's purpose and its target audience, rather than simply replacing linguistic elements. In other words, a translator must be both a linguist and a cultural mediator, as well as a creative writer. Thus, a 8Burgess, 9Vermeer, good translation is not merely one that is linguistically accurate but one that effectively recreates the original text's intent and impact within a different cultural context. However, if we assume that language shapes reality, achieving complete "equivalence" may be inherently impossible. In the process of translation, there is an effort to achieve objectivity by reproducing the original words and structures, striving for precise "equivalence" between languages. At the same time, there are almost always elements that cannot be fully reproduced within the linguistic framework of the target language. Cultural nuances, emotional undertones, or the unique resonance of the original language—these distinctive aspects are often irreproducible. In the case of machine translation, this tendency is likely to be particularly prominent. Many of the instant translation services currently available adopt two major technologies. The first is called Statistical Machine Translation (SMT), which uses large bilingual corpora to statistically generate the most likely translation. The second is Neural Machine Translation (NMT), which, by using deep learning, converts input phrases into the target language rather than translating word by word. This approach is characterized by the ability to produce more natural and fluent translations 2024).10 (Open AI Globally popular services like Google Translate attempt translations by combining these technologies. Although machine translation technology is advancing rapidly, it can still be said that fully understanding the complex concept that language shapes reality and reproducing equivalence based on that understanding remains a significant challenge. This brings us to Jonas Eltes' work, which explores the limitations of machine translation and communication. Through his installations, Eltes delves into the "lack of ability" inherent in computational systems, inviting us to reflect on the complexities of meaning lost in translation.

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: 10OpenAI. Figure 1: LOST IN COMPUTATION by Jonas Eltes 2017)11 Jonas Eltes' LOST IN COMPUTATION (Eltes, is an art piece that explores the relationship between humans and the digital world. In Elte's work, computers engage in a conversation with each other–one programmed to respond in Swedish, the other in Italian. However, as the computers converse, the conversation often fails to align, becoming more and more disconnected. For instance, in response to the statement "No, I have summer vacation," a reply like "No, start the eighth," questions and answers may not correspond meaningfully, leading to fragmented and incoherent dialogue. The artwork examines the cultural impacts of abstraction, distortion and fragmentation brought about by translation technologies using machine learning. The fragmented dialogue displayed across the two screens highlights how computational systems such as machine translation fail to capture the complexity and context-specific nuances of human language, reducing languages to standardized patterns. This also aligns with broader concerns in translation studies about the loss of human intentionality and cultural depth when meaning is processed algorithmically. The text displayed on each screen is linguistically "correct." However, it fails to grasp meanings unique to certain languages or continue a conversation by interpreting the intent behind a 11Jonas question. This reflects the limitations of machine translation: while machines can pursue linguistic "correctness," they fall short in achieving true equivalence, particularly in cultural, contextual and emotional dimensions—a critique effectively conveyed through this work. As mentioned earlier, languages embody the worldviews and cultures of their speakers, shaping their thoughts and identities. Translation, therefore, is not merely the substitution of words but an attempt to reconstruct the worldview expressed in the source language within the framework of the target language. Given that a perfect equivalence of worldviews is impossible, something is inevitably "lost" in the process of translation. In an era where machine translation has become commonplace and is routinely used, isn't it highly risky to simply transform one's words into another language with a single click using translation services? Could we be over-relying on the translated content, merely feeling as though we understand the original meaning, while neglecting the effort to truly comprehend it? Are we too oblivious to the "loss" — the things that "fall through the cracks" of translation? I believe that within this "loss" lies something profoundly important. In the following chapters, I will delve into what exactly is being "lost" and explore the significance contained within this "loss." I will use Japanese-English translation as a case study to examine what is "lost" in translation and explore the profound significance of this linguistic transformation. By analyzing the unique worldviews inherent in Japanese that lack direct English equivalents, I aim to illuminate the rich cultural nuances, emotional resonances and contextual subtleties that often become irretrievable during the translation process.

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: So, what exactly is lost in the process of translation? In this chapter, I will explore the different aspects of "loss" in linguistic translation. Drawing on examples between my native language, Japanese, and my second language, English, I will examine the elements that are lost when translating from Japanese to English. The Loss of Relationship Between Speakers and Listener Japanese is said to be the language with the most first-person pronouns in the world, and the first-person pronoun changes based on the speaker's social status, age, gender and relationship "私 "僕 "俺 "わたくし with the listener. For example, pronouns like (watashi)," (boku)," (ore)" and (watakushi)" reflect the speaker's position and relationship with the listener, and these nuanced distinctions are characteristic of the Japanese language. In contrast, English typically uses the single pronoun "I," which makes it difficult to convey these differences in translation. "わたくし" "私"; For example, the formal is not simply a first-person pronoun like it is an expression that conveys humility while showing respect for the listener. The nuance of respect embodied in " わたくし" cannot be captured by the English pronoun "I." Similarly, in everyday Japanese, the speaker adjusts the first-person pronoun based on the relationship with the listener, thus "俺 embedding more information within the pronoun. For instance, (ore)" is used by men in casual "私 settings among friends, while (watashi)" is used to show respect when addressing superiors. These subtle differences influence self-awareness and the perception of relationships with others are condensed into a single pronoun "I," in English. As a result, the impressions created by the speaker's choice of first-person pronoun, along with crucial information about the relationships between characters, their perceptions of one another and their attitudes, are inevitably lost in the translation process. The Japanese language contains expressions that reflect its unique cultural values and customs. One of the most striking examples is the use of first-person pronouns, which vary depending on context, gender and social relationships. As such, cultural values and psychological nuances embedded in language are often lost when translated into another language. In the following discussion, I will continue to explore these elements and analyze the complex layers of meaning that disappear in the process of translation.

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: Loss of Nuance in Descriptive Imagery and Resonance Another significant challenge in translation arises with Japanese-specific emotional expressions onomatopoeia.12 and Japanese is known to have over 4,500 onomatopoeic words, which is a substantial number compared to other languages. We use onomatopoeia daily, but conveying the same sense in English can be challenging. "さらさら" "ぽかぽか" For example, onomatopoeic words like (sarasara) or (pokapoka) evoke "さらさら specific sensory experiences or emotions, but there is no direct English equivalent. (sarasara)" describes the gentle flowing of water in a shallow stream, and while it is often translated as "smoothly" in English, the lightness or flowing imagery implied by the sound of the "ぽかぽか word is not fully conveyed. Similarly, (pokapoka)" refers to a warm, pleasant feeling, often used to describe a cozy warmth but when translated as simply "warm," the nuance of softness and gentleness carried by the original word is lost. In Japanese, there are many onomatopoeic words related to nature, and those related to rain are "ぽつぽつ"(potsupotsu), "ぱらぱら"(parapara), "しとしと frequently used. Some examples include "ざあざあ"(zaazaa) "どしゃどしゃ"(dosha "ぽつぽつ" "ぱらぱら" "(shitoshito), and dosha). and are "ぱらぱら" "ぽつぽつ. used when the rain starts, with suggesting larger raindrops compared to " しとしと" "ざあざあ" refers to a quiet rain that lasts for a long time, while describes the sound of "どしゃどしゃ" heavy rain. represents intense, heavy rain. When these phrases are put through machine translation (Google Translate), the results are: "ぽつぽつ雨が降る" ● = a light rain is falling "ぱらぱら雨が降る" ● = a light rain is falling "しとしと雨が降る" ● = a drizzle is falling "ざあざあ雨が降る" ● = a heavy rain is falling "どしゃどしゃ雨が降る" ● = a heavy downpour is falling "ぽつぽつ" "ぱらぱら," In this way, the nuance between and as well as the image of rain beginning "しとしと" to fall, is lost. The idea of as a long-lasting rain is also lost, and at the same time, the "しとしと" interpretation of "drizzle" in English adds a meaning that is different from the image of light, misty rain. In literature and poetry, the subtle nuances of descriptive imagery are especially important, and through translation, the intricacy and depth of situational or psychological portrayal are often lost. Loss of Cultural Worldview in Metaphors Metaphors in Japanese often reflect an animistic worldview. Animism is the belief that things have souls or spirits, and in Japanese culture, many expressions anthropomorphize objects or nature from this perspective, endowing them with pseudo-consciousness. For example, in Japanese language and culture, natural objects like wind, trees and rivers are often personified, 12Onomatopoeia being attributed with "feelings" or "will" as if they were living entities. This practice of animating the natural world is prevalent in Japanese literature and everyday conversation, reflecting a distinctive worldview that blurs the boundary between animate and inanimate. Expressions like "The wind whispers" or "The tree quietly nods" are not just depictions of natural phenomena but are based on a unique Japanese worldview that emphasizes the connection between humans and nature by attributing emotions or consciousness to these natural objects. However, translating such expressions directly into English is challenging, and they often come to be seen simply as natural phenomena. While "The wind whispers" or "The tree nods quietly" are grammatically correct in English, they fail to convey the animistic "soulful sensation" or "will of things" inherent in the original expression, and the cultural, spiritual elements and emotional depth behind them are lost through the translation process. Furthermore, an expression such as "mushi no koe" (the voice of insects) in Japanese refers to the sounds of insects heard on summer nights, particularly the sounds of crickets and other insects, symbolizing harmony with nature and the feeling of the season. However, it is incredibly difficult to express "mushi no koe" in other languages and convey the same meaning or sentiment. In English, terms like "the sound of insects" or "the chirping of crickets" might be used, but these merely convey the idea of "sound" and fail to capture the deeper cultural significance. In fact, research suggests that while the sound of insects is typically processed by the right hemisphere of the brain (sensory brain) in other cultures, in Japan, influenced by its linguistic and (角田 cultural framework, the sound of insects is processed by the left hemisphere (logical brain) 1978).13 This is a perfect example of how language is constructed cognitively in the brain and how it is perceived in reality, and how conveying a reality perceived through one language framework into the structure of another language is inherently challenging. Loss of Empathy Due to Differences in Alphabets 漢字(Kanji), The Japanese language has three different writing systems: which is derived from ひらがな(Hiragana) カタカナ(Katakana). Chinese characters, the native Japanese and Historically, Kanji and Katakana have been culturally associated with masculine aesthetic and communicative styles, while Hiragana is seen as feminine. After the invention of Hiragana during the Heian period, Kanji was referred to as "otokode" (masculine script) and Hiragana as "onnade" (feminine script). Male diaries were often written in classical Chinese (Kanji), while female diaries were 2021).14 written in Hiragana (MIKAMI Additionally, Katakana, which was created for reading Kanji, was primarily considered a masculine script, as men were more closely associated with Kanji and (歴史探求社 2017).15 classical Chinese On the other hand, the English language only has one form

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: 13角田忠信. 日本人の脳 脳の働きと東西の文化. 大修館書店. 歴史と文化への好奇心をひらく. "ジェンダー史研究事始（三上 喜孝）." 14MIKAMI Note（ノート）. 15歴史探求社. "昔はカタカナの方が使われてた。ひらがなと使用頻度が逆転した訳." まぐまぐニュース！. of the alphabet , making it difficult to convey the same nuanced gender-based impressions when translating between the two languages. Beyond gender implications, the three Japanese scripts also influence the level of empathy or connection that is felt. This is because each script carries its own emotional texture and communicative intent: Kanji can convey formality and intellectual distance, Hiragana often suggests intimacy and emotional softness, while Katakana can create a sense of external or foreign perspective. The choice of script subtly signals the writer's relationship to the content and the intended emotional resonance with the reader. For example, when a word is written in Hiragana, it tends to evoke a higher sense of empathy, while Katakana tends to evoke a lower level of empathy. For instance, the word for "mountain" in Japanese is read as "yama" (the native reading) or "san" (the Chinese-based reading). In Japan, mountains whose names are read with the native reading are often smaller, rounded hills loved by local communities, such as "Arashiyama" in Kyoto. In contrast, mountains with Chinese-based readings, like "Fujisan"(Mount (牧野 2018).16 Fuji), tend to be tall, steep, and awe-inspiring The sense of empathy is also lost when translating into English. Loss of Subtle Psychological Nuances Due to Postpositional Particles like「から」 and「ので」 In Japanese, postpositional particles (kara) (node) are used to indicate reasons or causes and are often translated as "because" or "so" in English, but their nuanced differences can be subtle and difficult to translate directly into English. Both can express cause 「から」 「ので」 and effect, but tends to feel more straightforward or objective, while conveys a 「から」 softer, often more personal or indirect tone. For instance, (kara) could be used in a direct 「ので」 context, such as "I couldn't go because I was busy," while (node) might be employed when the speaker wants to soften the reason, as in "I couldn't go because I was busy, so I apologize." This difference in nuance can be difficult to capture in English. The subtleties of these 「ので」 particles—where reflects a bit more politeness or empathy—can be lost in translation, as 「から」 「ので」 English lacks particles that precisely mirror this distinction. Therefore, while and both translate to "because" in many contexts, the underlying tone and cultural implications are often lost in translation. Translator Makino provides a specific example in his book. "I've finished knitting a sweater for (牧野) you, so I'll send it to you. Pink is great, isn't it? Mom, just ride in a wheelchair elegantly!" This passage is an excerpt from a letter written by a daughter to her mother in Japanese. In the "ので" English translation, the phrase translated as "so" corresponds to in the original Japanese. "から" "ので" Although could also be used to explain the relation of cause and effect, the use of (牧野 reflects the gentle feelings the daughter has for her mother in this case 2018). Loss of Formal Aesthetic and Shared Imagery in Poetry 16牧野成一. 日本語を翻訳するということ 失われるもの、残るもの. 中公新書. The formal aesthetics and structure of Japanese literature and poetry are also elements that are lost in translation. For example, the syllabic structure of haiku (5-7-5) or tanka (5-7-5-7-7) plays a significant role in the aesthetic experience of the poem. Reproducing this structure in English is "古池や蛙飛び込む水の extremely challenging. For instance, the famous haiku by Matsuo Bashō, 音" ("An old pond, and the sound of a frog jumping in"), when translated by various translators, (DMM英 illustrates the challenge of maintaining the original form and impact in another language 会話ブログ編集部 2015).17 Old pond / Frogs jumped in / Sound of water. ー小泉八雲(Lafcadio Hearn) The old pond, Aye! / And the sound of a frog / leaping into the water. ーBasil Hall Chamberlain The ancient pond / A frog leaps in / The sound of the water. ーDonald Keene The old pond. / A frog jumps in / Plop! ーReginald Horace Blyth An old silent pond... / A frog jumps into the pond, / splash! Silence again. ーHarry Behn It becomes clear when you actually try to pronounce it that not only is the original rhythm and resonance of the poem lost, but also, the poem's succinctness and aftertaste tend to be lost, significantly altering the impression it gives. Additionally, the unique linguistic structure of Japanese contributes to the differing shared imagery in this poem. In Japanese, there is no distinction between singular and plural nouns, so the number is often not explicitly considered depending on the context. This feature reflects a culturally significant aspect of the language. For example, when you say "kaeru" (frog) in Japanese, it can refer to a specific individual or to frogs in general. The nuance of this distinction is determined by context, and conversations often proceed without the explicit awareness of singular or plural forms. In contrast, English always distinguishes between singular and plural nouns, and this difference often poses a challenge in translation. In the translations above, many render "kaeru" as "a frog" in singular form, whereas Yakumo Koizumi uniquely translates it as "frogs" in the plural. While it is unclear whether Matsuo Basho was depicting a single frog or multiple ones, these differences create a significant shift in imagery for those viewing the translation. 17DMM英会話ブログ編集部. "俳句を英語で楽しもう！素晴らしきHAIKUの世界へ While these examples are only a few, they illustrate that achieving complete equivalence between Japanese and English in translation is inherently impossible, resulting in significant losses: the nuanced emotions and imagery, the subtle dynamics of relationships between speakers and readers and different impressions from different alphabets. As discussed earlier, in "から(kara)" "ので(node)", subtle nuances like the difference between and the expression of relationships through the choice of first-person pronouns and the psychological distance created by the use of Hiragana versus Kanji—all of these reflect culturally specific aspects of the language and delicate emotional subtleties of the speaker. Language inherently carries the speaker's choices and their intentions toward the listener; what words to choose, what metaphors to employ, the decision to opt for an unconventional phrasing and what tone to convey to the recipient. When reconsidering what "loss" truly entails, it becomes evident that it is not just a loss of linguistic meaning. A speaker's cultural background, identity, intentions, emotions and their careful consideration for the listener are deeply embedded in the language they use. When these aspects are erased in translation, we not only lose parts of the message but also let go of fragments of the self and identity that are constructed and communicated through that language. This realization brings an important insight to me. It might be a great waste to view the loss in translation merely as something that disappears. While it may be impossible to fully recover what is lost, perhaps the true value lies in understanding the senses and meanings embedded or hidden within it and in focusing on the process itself. Hence, I would like to consider the role that design can play. How can design not only recognize these linguistic and cultural losses but also reframe them as opportunities for discovering new perspectives and fostering alternative forms of communication? Can design, particularly through speculative investigation, create a model for innovation that goes beyond merely preserving original meaning, enabling the reconstruction or reinterpretation of fragments of identity and emotion that are lost in translation?

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: In the previous chapter, I discussed how loss is inevitable in translation, in the context of Japanese-to-English translation, and that such losses encompass elements like the speaker's identity: their cultural background, intentions, emotions and subtle consideration toward the other person. That is, a translation is not just about the loss of meaning—it can also evoke a sense of losing my identity, as if my own words have been transformed into someone else's. This loss is typically viewed as a challenge, something to be minimized or overcome through the translation process. However, what does this "loss" truly signify? If the things that are lost are an important part of our identity, wouldn't it be crucial to reflect on what we lose and what we gain in the process of translation? I believe that design holds the potential not only to recognize the linguistic and cultural losses inherent in translation but also to reframe these losses as opportunities to uncover new perspectives. Beyond merely preserving original meanings, design can interrogate the significance of loss in translation, enabling the reconstruction and reinterpretation of identity and emotion. This chapter explores how design can transform translation from a reductive process into a generative one, reimagining the act of translation as a creative and dynamic exchange.

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: The Significance of Loss: Subjectivity in Translation When we encounter loss in the process of translation, it is often not merely the omission of meaning or nuance but also the loss of identity. However, when certain words or expressions cannot be perfectly rendered into another language, focusing on what is "lost" can compel the translator to engage with a deeper understanding that goes beyond the surface meaning of the original text. This process necessitates unique interpretations of the parts that have been lost. Often, within these lost elements lie unnoticed layers of significant meaning, cultural context, or emotion. In this way, the "loss" in translation unintentionally fosters interpretive creativity, guiding the translator toward a profound reflection on the original content. Here, I will explore how this "loss" can serve as a starting point for new interpretations, providing us with deeper insights into others and ourselves through the act of translation. Figure 2: Take Care of Yourself by Sophie Calle 2007)18 Sophie Calle's Take Care of Yourself (Calle is an installation that offers a compelling exploration of subjectivity in translation, particularly within the context of emotions and interpersonal communication. The work involves Calle sharing a breakup email she received, with 107 women from various professional backgrounds—writers, editors, lawyers, dancers—and asking them to interpret it through the lens of their respective disciplines. The work relates to the theme of "interpretive creativity" by demonstrating how interpretation is deeply influenced by personal, cultural and professional contexts. The original email is a simple rejection message, yet it is "translated" into various manifestations , such as legal analysis, choreographed dance and literary criticism. These reinterpretations reveal that translation is not a neutral process but a performative act in which the translator's experiences, biases and expertise are embedded in the result. This installation highlights the fragmentation of meaning inherent in translation. Each expert's response reflects not only the influence of cultural and contextual factors on linguistic translation, but also their personal lens. Calle's work underscores that the notion of complete objectivity in translation is an illusion, and that meaning changes and evolves depending on the framework of the translator. This work illustrates that the act of translation is not merely a transfer of meaning, but a deeply subjective process in which the translator's identity, experiences and background significantly 18Calle, influence the understanding and recreation of the original text. This highlights the creative potential that arises from navigating these contextual influences, emphasizing how the translator's subjectivity contributes to generating new interpretations. In this way, I argue that complete objectivity in translation does not exist. When a word lacks an equivalent in another language—when the loss inherent in translation occurs—the act of translation becomes an opportunity to engage deeply with the original text and uncover the emotions and layers hidden behind its surface meaning. Translation is an introspective act and a process that deepens understanding of both oneself and others.

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: The Significance of Loss: Creating New Interpretations Next, I introduce a work that questions the multilayered emotions and nuances encapsulated within a single word.

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: 言葉を覗く Figure 3: by Chiaki Hoshina (言葉を覗く)" (保科 2023).19 This is Chiaki Hoshina's "Looking into the words 19保科千晶 "言葉を覗く" 武蔵野美術大学 卒業制作優秀作品集. やばい(yabai)? "What kind of word is In daily life, it's not always easy to instantly articulate your feelings in words. In such moments, the word yabai slips out. Perhaps yabai serves as a kind of placeholder for emotions. What becomes visible through this object are the feelings that, at certain moments, couldn't be properly expressed and were instead substituted with yabai. What kind of yabai can we discern from this chaotic world of (保科 emotions and words encapsulated in this object?" 2023) As she describes in her artist's statement, Hoshina focuses on the multifaceted emotions encapsulated within the Japanese word yabai; an adjective denoting very bad or very good, depending on the context of the speaker. She classifies its subtle nuances into clusters of other words (such as lonely, nostalgic, bittersweet) and embeds them within a transparent resin block, sculpted into an asymmetrical polyhedron. Each facet of the object is inscribed with words representing various emotions, with other feelings faintly visible on the reverse side through the transparent material. This work expresses the delicate emotional nuances that yabai tends to aggregate and obscure. Hoshina's work reveals the process of uncovering the subtle nuances and emotional layers embedded within a single word leads to a range of interpretative possibilities. When this idea is applied to translation, it becomes clear that deeply considering the emotions and sentiments encapsulated in a word is vital when converting it into another language. The "loss" inherent in translation can serve as an opportunity to explore the feelings and thoughts behind the words. Continuing on this path to examine how loss in translation can generate new interpretations and "自分の感受 introspection, I conducted two experiments. The first involved subjecting the poem 性くらい (At Least My Own Sensitivity)" by Japanese poet Noriko Ibaragi to Google Translate and then carefully analyzing the results with myself and two other Japanese-English speakers. We identified specific points of loss in the translation process and compared our interpretations of the missing elements and original meaning. In the following sections, I will detail the nuanced differences in interpretation and insights gained through the comments I made, as well as those provided by two other bilingual Japanese-English speakers, whom I will refer to as Participant A and Participant B.

paragraph: (自分の感受性くらい)", Regarding the title's translation, "At least your own sensitivity all participants felt a loss of the original meaning, with particular concerns raised about translating "感受性" the Japanese term as "sensitivity." Both I and Participant A felt that the English word "sensitivity" primarily signifies a physical or psychological reactiveness, failing to capture the core "感受性" "ability" of the word itself to mean deeply perceive and analyze.. I specifically noted that "感受性" the Japanese term carries nuances closer to "vulnerability," encompassing elements of weakness and delicacy. Participant A, meanwhile, observed that the translation "sensitivity" evokes a negative impression, inadvertently erasing the original term's positive undertones of "richness of heart." These perspectives collectively illuminate the profound linguistic challenge of "感受性" translating - a term for which no direct English equivalent exists. More importantly, they provide a rich opportunity for introspection, revealing how individuals conceptualize and define this complex emotional and perceptual capacity. Participant B's observations further highlighted the delicate nature of textual interpretation. While "くらい" semantically and grammatically accurate, Participant B pointed out that translating as "at least" risked losing the original's subtle "encouragement" nuance in title itself. Similarly, "ぱさぱさに乾 Participant B indicated the literal translation "Your heart growing dry and brittle" for いていく心" was deemed too direct. Participant B interpreted the original's essence as closer to "dying" than merely "drying," suggesting that the heart's dryness carried a more profound sense of emotional extinction. These observations suggest new interpretive possibilities for the original text, reinforcing the idea that translation is not merely a substitution of words but a process where faithfully reproducing nuanced expressions is crucial.

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: The Significance of Loss: Rediscovering Identity These insights led me to consider how they might apply to my own writing. For a second experiment, I selected entries from my personal journal and translated them into English using Google Translate. I then carefully analyzed the translated output to gain deeper insights into my thoughts and emotions. "だけどこの先今の生き方を続けていくことの絶望に比べたらいくらか明るく ワクワクできるのだ。" "But compared to the despair of continuing to live my current way, it feels a little brighter and more exciting." In this passage, I felt that the machine translation "a little brighter and more exciting" "いくらか明るくワクワクできるのだ." oversimplified the nuance of the original phrase While the "ワクワク," meaning is correct, the delicate nuance of Japanese onomatopoeia which conveys a subtle stirring of excitement, is lost. Additionally, the word "bright" seems overly optimistic, deviating from the intended subtle sense of "a small remaining hope." "ワクワク." Reflecting on this translation, I reconsidered why I chose the word While I could have "ワクワク" used "excited," better expressed a sense of hopeful anticipation or emotions or feelings I can't quite put into words yet. What I was feeling was different from mere "excitement"; it was a delicate feeling of hope or prayer directed toward an uncertain future. こぼれ落ちた"私"たち Figure 8-9: (The Fragments of Me Slipping Away) by Reiko Sakugawa Thus, I put the full contents of a diary of a certain day into a translation machine. As part of an attempt to identify losses in translation and reflect on the hidden aspects of myself within those losses, I created the work "The Fragments of Me Slipping Away". Beyond the faint sense of ワクワク, anticipation concealed behind the word I found traces of my determination, ideals, desires and humor within the words I had unconsciously chosen at the time—elements of myself that had been lost in translation. In this work, these rediscovered fragments of myself, brought to light through loss, become tangible and are collected into several transparent boxes. What began as a process of identifying loss ultimately transformed, through creation, into an act of reexamining and reclaiming my identity. Through this experiment, I realized that the words I subconsciously choose when writing reflect my emotions and ideas. The process of translation revealed how everyday language expresses my feelings and thoughts, and in this process, I discovered new emotions and perspectives I hadn't recognized before. Language is a powerful medium for individuals to express complex emotions, thoughts and identity. Encountering expressions that cannot be easily translated—"loss" in translation—offers an opportunity to reflect on why certain words were chosen. This is a moment to re-evaluate one's thoughts and emotions. It shows that "loss" is not simply a flaw but an entry point for self-discovery, a path to rediscovering one's identity. As seen in these examples, I believe that design can demonstrate the process of translation itself offers an opportunity to reconsider what the original text truly aims to convey and what we, as recipients, wish to receive. This chapter demonstrates how "loss," through the act of translation, encourages a deeper reflection on the original text, providing opportunities for new interpretations and a deeper understanding of oneself and others. Translation is not just about bridging linguistic or cultural gaps; it also offers a chance to view "loss" as a space for creativity and reinterpretation. Design transforms "loss" from a mere loss of meaning into a generative act, deepening our understanding and turning translation into a tool for enrichment. Through translation experiments and design, I came to realize that the elements I had felt were "lost" through translation into English were, in fact, valuable opportunities to enrich my identity.

author: Reiko Sakugawa
thesis_title: Where I Lost and Found Myself: The Reinvention of Identity in Translation
paragraph: This study explored the concept of "loss" in translation, emphasizing that translation is a complex and interpretative act beyond mere word exchange. These "losses" do not have to be understood as failures or deficiencies but rather can be seen as opportunities to gain deeper insight into the original content and the translator's identity. In other words, focusing on "loss" allows translators to rediscover their own voices as well as the voices of others, fostering a deeper sense of mutual understanding. Translation, while borrowing another language, ultimately becomes an act of rediscovering one's own identity. This paper has primarily examined the concept of "loss" in the translation of Japanese into English and how such loss leads to the discovery of cultural and personal identity. At the same time, I am in the process of absorbing English as a second language, gradually integrating the identity embedded within the language into my own. As Charlemagne once said, "To have a second language is to possess a second soul." In this sense, I may be gaining a new soul through my engagement with English. When speaking English, I sometimes feel that I am not entirely the same person as when I speak Japanese. Through this process, I also encounter a new kind of "loss" when attempting to express thoughts and ideas that first emerged in English and then in Japanese. Yet, through this loss, I am able to discover new aspects of myself. In today's world, where technological advancements have made translation increasingly efficient, translation services are widely used in daily life. I am one such user myself. However, I make a conscious effort not to blindly accept the output of translation services, but rather, to verify and engage with it as much as possible. This is particularly important when dealing with personal or intimate content, where I do not want to overlook the nuances of identity, such as the care, emotions and subtle presentation of persona within myself and others. By engaging with translation as a process and confronting the "loss" it entails, we may be able to connect more deeply with both ourselves and others. Furthermore, this study moves beyond the notion of "loss" in translation to prompt a broader reconsideration of issues such as the absence of individuality and identity in AI and generative art, as well as the growing disconnection from human sensibility and the detachment from human nature. While the sources AI learns from are created by humans, I feel that as AI processes them merely as data and transforms them into collective knowledge, human experiences and emotions are stripped away in the act. As generative AI, trained through algorithms, produces language and visuals, how are the human experiences and emotions that should have been embedded within them being treated? How can we reconsider the "something" that is lost in what machines create? What opportunities might arise from examining the concept of "loss" in AI-generated expressions? Once I have grown into a new soul shaped by English, how then will I translate the words “I love you”?

author: Roshni Kumaravel
thesis_title: Is a Swedish Fish a Real Fish: Notes on Hyperreality
paragraph: When you or I hear the word ‘fish,’ we might first think of an actual, live fish—like a tuna, shark, or minnow. However, the word ‘fish’ also refers to other objects: plastic wall decorations shaped like fish, fried chicken nuggets shaped like fish, or even candy shaped like fish. Although a Swedish Fish candy may not be a ‘real’ live fish, it is a kind of fish that is real in its own way. At the same time, a Swedish Fish does not live the life a real fish does. It doesn’t swim around in water, but the grocery store. It does not grow. It does not lay eggs. It does not get eaten by bigger fish—although it does get eaten. It’s candy. It’s a lump of sugar and Red 40 dye mixed together and molded into the shape of a fish. Its reality is fundamentally different from that of an actual live fish. The case of the Swedish Fish suggests that every time we use symbols like words or images to represent objects or ideas, we lose some amount of information in that process. But what do we gain in this process? In his 1981 essay !"#$%&’(&)&*+)!"#$%&,"-*., French theorist Jean Baudrillard suggested that reality had been replaced entirely by signs and symbols, and that the idea of ‘real life’ was a sim- ulated construct. He described contemporary society as ‘hyperreal,’ and that hyperreality was more real than the idea of reality. He described a process through which reality slowly distances itself from its original meaning, eventually to be replaced by a simulacrum. means
