author: Roshni Kumaravel
thesis_title: Is a Swedish Fish a Real Fish: Notes on Hyperreality
paragraph: There are a lot of candies out there.1 Gummy bears, jelly beans, Sour Patch Kids and Swedish Fish are all exam- ples of chewy candy that can be easily found in the candy aisle in an American supermarket. If you take away the “bears” from the gummy bears, the “kids” from the Sour Patch Kids, the “beans” from the jelly beans, and the “fish” from the Swedish Fish, the candies all start looking very similar. They’re mostly lumps of sugar, preservatives, fla- vouring, food colouring, and a thickening agent to give it a chewy texture.234 Reduced down to their basic ingredients, they’re pretty much the same. Shaping them into bears, kids, beans and fish allows the candy manufacturers to separate one product from another and give the lump of sugar some character and personality. In this context then, the fact that a Swedish Fish is the shape of a fish becomes pretty important— it’s what separates one lump of sugar from another. It is arguably a fish that is valid in its own right. At the same time, however, a Swedish Fish does not live the life of a real fish. It does not swim around in water. It does not grow. It does not lay eggs. It does not get eaten by bigger fishes. It’s candy. It’s a lump of sugar. Its material reality is fundamentally different from that of a ‘real’ fish. Does this mean that a Swedish Fish candy is not a ‘real’ fish? Maybe. Maybe not. This calls into question what counts as a ‘real’ fish. Everytime we represent an object, we lose something in that process. If I wanted to explain to someone what a fish is, I could take a photograph of a fish and print it out and show them what a fish looks like. However, this would not be an adequate substitute for actually seeing a real fish swim- ming in water. While the photograph, as a representation of a fish, allows me to communicate the idea of a fish to other people, it cannot paint a complete picture of the real- ity of a fish. It cannot communicate how a fish moves, what it might smell like, or how the texture of its scales might feel. Some amount of information would be lost in the pro- cess of representation. When we represent something based on a rep- resentation of a representation of a representation of an object, in every stage of this process we lose some of the reality of the original object. In his 1981 text !"#$%&’(&)&*+) !"#$%&,"-*, Jean Baudrillard described a process in which every subsequent representation gets distorted to the point where the representation becomes a simulacrum of the original object: Whereas representation attempts to absorb simulation by interpreting it as a false representation, simulation envel- ops the whole edifice of representation itself as a simula- crum. Such would be the successive phases of the image: it is the reflection of a profound reality; it masks and denatures a profound reality; it masks the absence of a profound reality; it has no relation to any reality whatsoever; it is its own pure simulacrum. In the first case, the image is a good appearance: the rep- resentation is of the order of sacrament. In the second, it is an evil appearance: of the order of malefice. In the third, it plays at being an appearance: it is of the order of sorcery. In the fourth, it is no longer in the order of appearance at 5 all, but of simulation. —Jean Baudrillard.5 Baudrillard’s text takes a strong moral stance against sim- ulacra—he uses the words ‘good,’ ‘evil,’ ‘sacramental,’ ‘malef- icence’ to describe different stages of the process, clearly indicating his negative opinion of simulacra and hyperre- ality. This aligns with my personal biases, even if I wouldn’t use language this harsh to describe the simulacra and images I encounter on a daily basis. Baudrillard sets up his position by arguing that God isn’t real. God is merely a symbol for organised religion, organised religion itself being a front for politics, power and control: “God himself has only ever been his own simulacrum.”6 The text refers to religion in a Western context, and his arguments may not necessarily be transferable to other religions in the world, practised currently and historically. Baudrillard’s choice to use the example of religion here is intentionally provocative. To explore his assertions, I will begin with a less pro- vocative entity than God, to study the representations with- out the aim of assigning value judgements. I simply wish to observe how an object is transformed when it is turned into an image. In my research online, I came across this meme (and variations of it) on the internet, explaining the stages of simulacra. In the above image (original source unknown), the first stage of simulation is the image of a fish. It is not an actual fish, but it conveys the idea of what a fish looks like. The sec- ond stage shows a wall ornament shaped like a fish. It isn’t just meant to represent a fish, it is also meant to be a deco- rative element on a wall, a function that an actual ‘real’ fish would not serve. The image of the fish itself is stylised and has fewer details. The third stage of simulation is fried food shaped like fish, but may or may not actually be fish itself. In any case, it is no longer the real, live fish initially being represented, but a simplified silhouette of a fish, being fried in a pan. Even there there’s a layer of representation. These aren’t fishes that are in the process of being fried, or fish that have just finished frying - these are pre-fried fish carefully and neatly placed in an orderly manner in a pan with some oil in it, to suggest the idea of fish being fried. In the fourth stage, we see packaging for Swedish Fish candy. Although the idea of fishes being eaten has remained throughout the representations, the Swedish Fish candy isn’t pretending to be a ‘real’ fish—it’s obvious about the fact that it’s candy. It’s bite-sized, it’s sugary, and has the name of the brand embossed on its scales. It is now a simulacrum of a fish. Ironically, this meme over simplifies Baudrillard’s ideas and very verbose descriptions and condenses down a 164-page book to a table with four images and the stages of simulacra next to it, losing all nuance. I launched a visual investigation into Jean Baudrillard’s out- line/overview/explanation of the process in which an image evolves/devolves into a simulacrum of the original object.

author: Roshni Kumaravel
thesis_title: Is a Swedish Fish a Real Fish: Notes on Hyperreality
paragraph: Image of a fish represented on a screen, photographed through a scanner. Image of a fish represented on a screen, photographed through a scanner. The fingerprints and dust on the scanner bed add texture. Image of a fish represented on a screen, photographed In this image, the composition is affected by the limitations through a scanner. of having to scan an image displayed on the same laptop that is connected to the scanner as it is scanning the image. Similar to the previous two images, fingerprints and dust fish. The fishes in this image are not the same size as the add texture to the scanned image. Additionally, scanning original Swedish Fish candy being scanned. the candy and cropping the images changes the scale of the Image of a single Swedish Fish candy photographed through a scanner. Image of a single Swedish Fish candy photographed through a scanner, laser printed on newsprint and scanned again. In this image, I wanted to make a ‘fish bowl’—a bowl that is a deviation from reality, as a real fish probably wouldn’t is in the shape of a fish. In order to get the bowl to stand be able to stand upright on its tail and fins. Documenting upright, the scale of the fins and tails of the fish had to be them through a scanner, the fish had to be laid down on modified to act as a base/stand to support the ‘bowl’. This its side anyway. In this image, I melted a single Swedish Fish candy. This here too, the medium affects the image—the fact that it is lump of sugar was once a fish. Is it still a fish if it’s melted melted candy means that it has no structure, and has to be down and no longer has the shape of the fish? Regardless, spread on to a paper towel. A fish made out of Swedish Fish candy, held together using glue and steel wire. Image of a fish made out of Swedish Fish candy, photo- graphed through a scanner, laser printed on newsprint and scanned again. A fish made using beads woven together. Studying these representations of fishes, my biggest take- aways were 1. Any material that is arranged into the general shape of what is recognised as a fish, immediately appro- priates the meaning, identity and personality of a generic fish. I wasn’t trying to represent a specific species of fish. I just made representations of what I intuitively know as a fish. 2. The material used to represent the fish also adds personality and character to the fish. The fish made out of Swedish Fish candy, smells like Swedish Fish candy. Because it is held together by glue and steel wire, it is sturdy enough to be lifted and held up by hand, but is also delicate at the same time. In the beaded tapestry with the image of a fish, the image itself is two dimensional—it is a matrix of differ- ently coloured beads arranged in the shape of a fish, it has an x-axis and a y-axis, but no z-axis. The image of the fish has two dimensions. But because I wove beads together, and beads are three dimen- sional objects, the woven piece ends up being a three dimensional object that flows almost like a piece of fabric. The image of the fish is abstracted in all of these representa- tions, but as the presence of the fish fades into the back- ground, the media used to represent the fish swims to the surface. A representation is necessarily a mediated process. For instance, if I were to represent a fish using a photograph printed on paper, the representation of the fish would not exist without the photograph or the paper or the ink with which the photograph was printed. On one hand, some amount of the image/likeness/verisimilitude of the fish is distorted. But this representation is also something else. It’s a piece of paper with ink layered on top of it—this is its physical reality. It has to have the texture of paper, and the printed image can only have the colours of the ink used, and the image can only show what was within the frame at the exact moment it was shot (from the distance it was shot). My point here is that the representation isn’t just the im- age of the fish, it’s a negotiation between the image of the fish and the physical reality of the media used in the pro- cess of representation. The representation of the fish is flat- tened to accommodate the materiality of the paper it is print- ed on, and the paper in turn is permanently transformed into a vessel holding the image of a fish. Everytime we represent something, we gain something in that process. Although we lose some of the reality of the object or idea being represented, we also gain some of the reality of the medium used to represent it. Although an image of a fish printed on paper cannot accurately portray the en- tire reality of the fish, it can stand in for a real fish and al- low us to explain the idea of a real fish even when a real fish isn’t present. The piece of paper can be scanned and reprinted, or it can be bound in a book, or be preserved as evidence of the fish having existed. This image can be ap- propriated and manipulated for our own purposes (benign or malignant).

author: Roshni Kumaravel
thesis_title: Is a Swedish Fish a Real Fish: Notes on Hyperreality
paragraph: As an exercise, I tried to identify examples of hyperreality and see which stage of simulacrum it belonged to. Then, I tried to work backwards and see if it was indeed a simu- lacrum, what it was a simulacrum of and what the other stages look like.

author: Roshni Kumaravel
thesis_title: Is a Swedish Fish a Real Fish: Notes on Hyperreality
paragraph: If a person has never seen a real fish before, how would they be able to tell the ways in which a Swedish Fish candy is similar or dissimilar to a real fish? If they’ve never seen a real fish before, would they think that all ‘real’ fish are the same size as a Swedish Fish? Or that all fishes smell like candy? Or that they could just bite into a fish and it would taste sweet? Would they be able to recognise that a Swedish Fish is a distant representation of a fish and isn’t actually a ‘real’ fish? A simulacrum isn’t simply a lower quality representa- tion of an object, it eventually entirely loses the reality of the original object, and the simulacrum is real in its own right, or ‘hyperreal’. In 1981, Baudrillard suggested that contem- porary society was filled with simulacra and hyperreal- ity to the point where hyperreality felt more real than the idea of reality. Today, more than four decades later, hyperreality has scaled significantly. Most of us use smartphones or per- sonal computers that are used for communication. All com- munication, whether through words or images, are rep- resentations of objects or events or ideas. We are constantly learning about the world we live in through representa- tions of it. The way we understand and process events hap- pening across the world isn’t based on objective reality. When we learn about an object/event/idea through social media, there are several layers of representation, from the way that it is documented and the language used to describe it, to the way that the individual piece of media is curated and selected by the platform’s algorithms for us to see.7 At each stage of representation, we must assume we are moving further and further away from the reality of the object/event/idea being represented. If we haven’t actually witnessed the original object/ event/idea ourselves, how would we be able to tell the ways in which their representation is similar or dissimilar to the original object/event/idea? Would we even be able to recognise that the representation is not the same as the original object/event/idea? Consider two modes of storing information—books, and the internet. Both contain pages. A ‘page’ is a site within a book. A ‘page’ is also a site on the internet. Both types of ‘pages’ contain information. The difference between them is the space they occupy. A book is measurable. It’s a tangible object that holds a record of its life. Cracks on the spine, dog ears, notes on the margin and scratches on the cover are all permanent wrinkles that show a book’s age and the life it has lived. No two books—even if they are copies of the same edition— are the same. A book occupies a definite volume of space. Its pages can be counted. It is a physical container that is finite, and can only contain printed words and images. A page within a book can only contain what it can physically contain within that given space. The size of the page is fixed, and dictates the amount of information it can hold. Calling a website a ‘page’ feels deceptive, but it’s a type of ‘page’ that has become valid in its own right. Digital ‘pages’ are stored in servers most of us never see. What we see is only what is displayed on a screen in front of us. There is no limitation to the amount of content it can hold. The page is an endless plane that keeps expanding to accom- modate more content as it is added. While a book usually has a beginning, middle and end, pages on the internet are non-linear, infinite and indefinite. On the internet, the size of the page isn’t a fixed parameter, it is dictated by the amount of content it contains. This prioritisation of content directs any focus away from the physicality of the medium, to the information held within. But we forget that the physicality of a medium can contain information too. A Mark Rothko painting seen through a glossy 14-inch screen isn’t the same as stand- ing in front of a colour-field the size of a wall. Up close, a painting reveals brushstrokes layered on top of each other. From further away, a painting is seen from the same dis- tance the artist might have seen it as they decided the paint- ing was finished. From even further away, a painting is a blur of colours, and the context it is placed in takes prece- dence—a quiet gallery as it’s closing, or a busy living room in the morning just before leaving for work, or an office lobby where people are streaming in and out all day. On the internet, this is lost. A page on the internet doesn’t age the way a page in a book does. There may not be a trace of people who have visited a page before us, and we may not leave behind a trace of us for the people who visit after us to know we were there. We don’t see people come and go, it’s just us and the page in front of us. Two people could be staring at the same spot of the same page at the same time and never cross paths with each other. In his 1935 essay ./0)1-(2)-3)4(,)"*),/0)450)-3)60’/&*"’&%)708(-9 +$’,"-*, Walter Benjamin makes the distinction between two ways a work of art can have value. Artwork that is meant to be revered for its uniqueness derives its value from its authenticity.8 When this type of art is photographed and reproduced, it loses its authenticity, and the reproduction does not have the same value as the original. Viewing a painting by Rothko through a glossy 14-inch laptop screen, for instance, is not the same as experiencing it in person.9 On the other hand, Benjamin argues that some art- works derive their value from their ability to be reproduced mechanically. He uses the case of photography to argue that a photograph gets its value from being reproduced as a print (which lacks authenticity, as the photograph can be printed multiple times), and not for the photographic negative (which does have authenticity, as a unique object with a unique position in time and space). The point of mass media (Benjamin uses the example of film) is its ability to be mechanically reproduced. And when it is reproduced, it is likely to be seen by the masses, allowing it to be an effective tool for the dissemination of propaganda. “...the instant the criterion of authenticity ceases to be applicable to artistic production, the total function of art is reversed. Instead of being based on ritual, it begins to be based on another practice—politics.”10 The internet is a paradoxical medium. It took the privilege of physical interaction and turned it into a barrier to be eliminated. Alienation isn’t a side effect of digital spaces, it’s the entire point. Communication over the internet, because it is a public, decentralised medium that’s meant to be seen by people and reproduced on screens repeatedly, maybe also inherently lacks some amount of authenticity. And without authenticity, maybe that makes the human connection over the internet less meaningful, less ‘real’. Although there's a lack of authenticity and a loss of individual human connection, this doesn’t make the medium necessarily bad. Although the medium can still be used for political control through propaganda, as Benjamin warned, the decentralised nature of the internet makes it a tool that is easily reclaimed by anyone, for any purpose.

author: Roshni Kumaravel
thesis_title: Is a Swedish Fish a Real Fish: Notes on Hyperreality
paragraph: I made a photobook of “perfect moments,” where the defi- nition of a perfect moment was generated through an artificial intelligence (AI) chatbot. I used generic stock images to illustrate this AI generated definition of a ‘perfect moment’. I consider generative AI to be inauthentic. A gener- ative AI chatbot doesn’t ‘think’ in any meaningful sense. It isn’t sentient. It does not experience joy, pain, growth or grief. It cannot reflect on lived human experiences. I consider generic stock images to be inauthentic as well. The point of stock images, such as the ones in adver- tisements, is to be sold. Often they are used to sell other products. They don’t offer any insight into who the person is that made them. Is a ‘perfect moment’ described by inauthentic media a ‘real’ ‘perfect moment’?

author: Roshni Kumaravel
thesis_title: Is a Swedish Fish a Real Fish: Notes on Hyperreality
paragraph: The Situationist International was a radical movement in the 1960s in Europe, which was highly influential. The Situ- ationists were a group of artists and political theorists who were deeply unsatisfied with contemporary society, and particularly bored11 with consumerism and the rapid com- modification of every aspect of life. They defied any singu- lar, static ideology—“Static ideologies, however true they may be, tend, like everything else in capitalist society, to rigidify and become fetishised, just one more thing to pas- sively consume.”12 They saw the working class as oppressed not by force but by the promise of meaningless consumer goods—consumer goods that got their value from their 563);3=-%$,"-*)-/)8=3(9+&9) glorified representation in mass media. The first stage of the economy’s domination of social life brought about an evident degradation of being into hav- ing — human fulfillment was no longer equated with what one was, but with what one possessed. The present stage, in which social life has become completely dominated by the accumulated productions of the economy, is bring- ing about a general shift from having to appearing — all “having” must now derive its immediate prestige and its ultimate purpose from appearances. At the same time all individual reality has become social, in the sense that it is shaped by social forces and is directly dependent on them. Individual reality is allowed to appear only if it is not actually real. —Thesis 17, ./0)!-’"0,:)-3)./0)!80’,&’%013 In ./0)!-’"0,:)-3)./0)!80’,&’%0, Guy Debord (a founding mem- ber of the Situationist International) argued that it wasn’t simply that advertisements and marketing were encour- aging people to buy more consumer goods, people were also socially motivated by the prestige associated with the appearance of owning material goods. “Everything that was directly lived has receded into a representation.” (Thesis 1, ./0)!-’"0,:)-3)./0)!80’,&’%0)14 But the social validation of the appearance of owning mate- rial goods is a superficial connection, and cannot stand in for meaningful and authentic human relationships. The Situationists were concerned by this social alienation, and attempted to create situations in which people would interact with each other without any mediation through commodities. The movement, however, was short-lived. Since Debord published ./0)!-’"0,:)-3)./0)!80’,&’%0, the nature of images have changed. Images and mass com- munication don’t simply advertise a lifestyle built around meaningless commodities—images themselves have become commodities to consume. Everywhere we go, in our pockets we carry an endless stream of images. Consider the photograph as a medium. When it was first introduced, a single photograph would have been captured using a shoebox-sized camera, exposed for hours. The pro- cess was simple—light sensitive chemicals exposed to light, and a mechanism that facilitated framing and composition. Today, a far more complex camera takes up far less space and is far more ubiquitous. The camera has become a staple feature on mobile phones. Photographs aren’t just tools for news and advertisements to communicate to the masses. Photographs aren’t just a tool for documenting important moments in time. Photographs are as much a tool for com- munication as talking to someone on the phone. The photo archive on my phone is a sinkhole of digital ephemera. Screenshots for reference. Memes that made me laugh. Pictures of my friends and family. Pic- tures that have no context, and I no longer remember why I took them. Pictures that are meaningful. There are over 10,000 images and videos saved to my phone. The earliest of this was taken in 2014, when I bought my first smartphone. These pictures do indeed take up space, they are saved in a server somewhere, but I can’t actually see how much space they take up. In my life, they take up no space at all. The images on my phone are representations of information stored elsewhere. They don’t ‘exist’ as a photograph in a meaningful sense. Old family photo albums serve a purpose. They serve as a record of people having existed, of events hav- ing happened. They are undeniable. They are meant to take a fleeting moment and preserve it forever. Of course, this isn’t possible—photographs rip, fade, or get lost—but we try our best to preserve them for as long as we can. Every time we look at them, they serve their purpose. They are meant to be seen. The 10,000+ images on my phone were meant to be seen too, but the truth is I never look at them. They end up never serving their purpose. I post them on my social media, and then forget about them and never look at them again. These digital images achieve the goal that the phys- ical photographs aspire to—they don’t lose quality over time, they don’t rip, fade or get lost. They do indeed pre- serve these moments forever. But if no one ever looks at them, then what’s the point? Social media is a subset of the internet that allows peo- ple (although here we are ‘users’ rather than people) to create and share ‘content’. ‘Content’ is a broad term that covers a wide variety of media. ‘Content’ can be anything from a bootlegged movie on Youtube, to 8-hour Twitch live streams of a person playing a video game, to a picture of Swedish Fish candy posted on Instagram. Hypothetically, if the internet were a physical place, then social media platforms would be the town squares and the movie thea- tres and the auditoriums and amphitheaters and the sta- diums. It’s a place of gathering, except we don’t actually feel each others’ presence, since we’re only seeing flattened representations of people online. These spaces aren’t neutral. Some of the biggest social media platforms are run by the wealthiest billion- aires on the planet. The goal of these platforms is to be prof- itable, and the algorithms that curate content will favour the pieces that will keep us on the platform long enough to spend money. Linguist Adam Aleksic (@etymologynerd on Insta- gram) who creates content relating to linguistics, details the way the meaning of the word ‘content’ has shifted in the context of advertising on social media.15 ‘Content’ inher- ently refers to something contained within a larger frame or a container. A user on a social media platform might think of an image/video/tweet as a container that holds ‘content’. In Aleksic’s case, ‘content’ would be information relating to linguistics, held within the container of a video. But from the perspective of the social media platforms, the platform itself is the container holding the image/video/ tweet, and the image is the ‘content’. It doesn’t matter what content is held within the image, as long as it’s posted to the platform. A platform might present itself as a place for com- munication, but pay no attention to the quality of the ‘con- tent’ within the ‘conversation’, because from the platform’s perspective, the conversation itself is the point.

author: Roshni Kumaravel
thesis_title: Is a Swedish Fish a Real Fish: Notes on Hyperreality
paragraph: Using the internet, we can communicate with someone on the other side of the world in real time. We’re doing it all the time when we publish or consume information on social media. Communication over the internet is faster than ever. Music, film and literature from other countries and cultures are within reach. The world is more connected than ever. But the need to meet another person in real life is lower than ever. There is a decreased need to travel to another city to visit friends and family when we can keep up with every moment of their lives on social media. There is no need to walk to the restaurant, when the food can be picked up and dropped off at our doorsteps (we don’t even have to call and talk to another person to place an order, we do it through an app). There is no need to go to the local movie theater and watch a movie along with several other people when we can watch it from our own home. We can access unlimited amounts of information without hav- ing to go to the library. We don’t even have to leave the couch. There isn’t even a need to go to work, if work can be completed remotely. The world is more disconnected than ever. Simulacra of human connection have replaced authentic interactions and relationships leading to this dissonance of connection and disconnection at the same time. A ‘real’ fish swimming around in water has authen- ticity. It’s valuable for the individual life it has lived. Rep- resentations of this fish would inevitably lose this authen- ticity. Whether a Swedish Fish is a real fish or not, it lacks the authenticity of a ‘real’ fish. It is a mass produced piece of candy, and shaped into a fish to give the candy char- acter. The value of this simulacrum of fish is its ability to sell more candy. From the perspective of the candy manufacturer, the fish is simply ‘content’.

author: Sara Marsh
thesis_title: Flick Chicks: Reimagining Female Trauma Through Modern Cinema
paragraph: Trauma, a concept that until the 20th century was grossly misunderstood, has become deeply embedded in our collective consciousness. Advances in mental health awareness have greatly expanded our understanding of the transformative experiences that many are subjected to during their lives. The trauma experienced by women – a historically marginalized group – has for centuries remained frequently misunderstood, misrepresented, and even fetishized. Today, women’s responses to trauma, particularly anger, continue to face social stigmatization, and despite these feelings being crucial to physical and mental health, women are often pressured to suppress it to avoid being labeled “unfeminine” or “unpleasant.” Anger is an inherent part of the human experience – and, in a world where it can acutely feel as if progress is being unraveled, many are angry. Although awareness of women’s trauma has indeed advanced, today’s patriarchal society at-large remains desensitized to the literal and figurative attacks women endure. Historically, women have conveyed their trauma through art, often in coded or indirect forms. Artists like Frida Kahlo, for example, used self-portraiture to articulate pain that might otherwise be silent. Similarly, film has emerged as a powerful medium for processing and depicting trauma, providing a uniquely visceral experience to viewers, who are more often than not male. As more female writers and directors join the traditionally male-dominated industry, the way in which women’s trauma is portrayed and engaged with by filmgoers has shifted significantly. These portrayals are often raw, uncomfortable, dark, disturbing, and even gory – but also empowering, hopeful, and deeply resonant with the feminine experience. In an increasingly complex world, film offers a space to confront and reimagine trauma. It is true that historically, women have been greatly exploited by the film industry as their stories were for years told by men. However, today, as the viewer is more and more often put behind the eyes of both a female character – and almost more importantly, a female-guided camera – trauma is understood and represented in a different way. Women have found a space within film that uses fantasy and fiction to explore collective trauma. This thesis expands upon the following questions, using texts, artworks, and feature-length films as research materials to explore the aforementioned themes. 1. How do audiences perceive and emotionally engage with women’s trauma when it is portrayed in film through fiction or fantasy? 2. How do the screen and the camera’s eye act as containers and conduits for exploring female trauma? 3. How do world-building and fantasy construct filmic spaces where women’s experiences can be safely reimagined and reexamined?

author: Sara Marsh
thesis_title: Flick Chicks: Reimagining Female Trauma Through Modern Cinema
paragraph: Does film mirror culture, or does culture mirror film? Both, says Tom Sherak, former film director and president of the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences. “Film is a reflection of society, both present and past. Movies are a form of communication and that communication, those stories, come from societies – not just where society is presently and what it’s doing now, but where society has been.”1 He’s right – the societal impact of film is overwhelmingly significant. The entire film library to date is a chronicle of history, representing the fears, wants, and needs of society. During WW2, the film industry aligned closely with the US government’s wartime goals, resulting in often-comedic propaganda-esque motion pictures that boasted “patriotic, morale-boosting themes and messages about the ‘American way of life,’ the nature of the enemy and the allies, civilian responsibility on the home front, and the fighting forces themselves.”2 In Blondie for Victory (1942), housewives, caught up in patriotic fervor, ditch their housework for war efforts (to the dismay of their husbands). As the war dragged on with increased hostility, movies too got more serious. Man Hunt (1941), Hangmen Also Die! (1943), and The Purple Heart (1944) were released in succession, all of which engage with the dark sides of war and echo the fears and anxieties of the American population at the time. Similar trends can be seen across cultures throughout the ups and downs of history. Movies serve different purposes for different people. They can be cathartic, educational, escapist, comforting, mindless. But they haven’t always represented the experiences of everyone. Film, like history as a whole, has been largely authored and controlled by those in power – namely white men. The experiences of women, people of color, the LGBTQ+ community, people with disabilities, and other marginalized groups have been exploited, fetishized, or ignored entirely. This narrow authorship has shaped the lens through which so-called ”'universal” themes are portrayed, limiting the scope of representation and chronicling only the most dominant perspectives. With this framework in place, harmful depictions of these groups too easily become societal norms. Feminist film theory blossomed in the 1970s as a critical response to the portrayal of women in film and the reproduction of gendered myths. During the rise of the Second Wave Feminist Movement, women in Hollywood were overwhelmingly cast as sex objects or confined to fixed archetypes – think the caretaker, the mother, or the whore.3 Or, as renowned American film critic and author Molly Haskell puts it in her book From Reverence to Rape: The Treatment of Women in the Movies, “the tension between the spirited single girl and the whimpering bride, between stereotype”4 the star and the was palpable, and often not to empower, but to chastise and warn. Patriarchal ideology at the time defined women solely in relation to men, creating a system that left little room for the female spectator, even as more substantial roles for women were injected into the industry. Since then, evolving perspectives on femininity and womanhood, along with the increasing presence of women in key roles as writers, producers, directors, and actors, have begun to shift the cinematic narrative. These creators are subverting traditional tropes, portraying women in ways that are unsexy, unfeminine, and unspeakable – bringing a new level of complexity to both the screen and the minds of the movie-going public. Both fantasy and fiction are used as narrative devices to drive these shifts, embracing the filmic liminal space – one that exists both within and outside reality – to confront trauma in all its raw complexity. Through world-building, female filmmakers have begun creating symbolic, surreal, and speculative spaces where trauma can be reimagined and recontextualized. Fantasy becomes an essential tool for reclaiming agency, breaking from patriarchal logic, and creating a cinematic space where marginalized experiences can be processed in safety. With a woman in the director’s chair, both the frame (the screen) and the gaze (the eye of the camera) are reoriented to center the feminine experience of trauma, offering the viewer not just spectacle, but the opportunity for deeper emotional engagement. Building on the work of feminist film theorists like Laura Mulvey and Carol J. Clover, this thesis examines explorations of female trauma in film and how they relate to today’s environment as well as to larger historical trends. It explores how, in an increasingly chaotic and sinister world, women are subverting film tropes and using fantasy to create spaces for the safe exploration of trauma — in all its raw, messy, unvarnished forms. As depictions of female trauma in film become more prominent, so does the opportunity for deeper understanding through art and storytelling in places where preconceived notions and societal expectations may seem impossible to penetrate.

author: Sara Marsh
thesis_title: Flick Chicks: Reimagining Female Trauma Through Modern Cinema
paragraph: Going to the movies – or, for a while, popping a tape into the VCR – was a highlight of my childhood and a cornerstone of my emotional and cultural development. I wore out DVDs like well-loved books, and the characters on-screen became my friends. A storyteller from a young age, I would imagine their lives beyond the confines of the screen, crafting new trials and triumphs that mirrored my own. A free-spirited, impassioned girl from the start, I found inspiration in the complex women I watched on screen. They challenged me to be bolder, braver, and better – teaching lessons that perhaps could only be learned through the visceral experience of a good film. Later, I felt compelled to continue telling stories myself, using film as my own mechanism for expression. This thesis serves as a continuation of that journey – an opportunity to champion female narratives in film and better understand how cultural phenomena and shifts are driven, reflected, and reimagined through media.

author: Sara Marsh
thesis_title: Flick Chicks: Reimagining Female Trauma Through Modern Cinema
paragraph: It is important to expand upon a few key terms that will be repeated throughout this thesis and what they mean in context. The term WOMAN pertains to anyone who lives and identifies as a woman, regardless of sex assigned at birth. However, this thesis focuses specifically on the experiences of cisgender women and their portrayals in film. The term TRAUMA is used in the collective sense, referring to larger cultural phenomena that impact not just individuals, but entire groups. Rooted in systemic oppression and discrimination, this kind of trauma is perpetuated by dominant groups and ingrained in societal structures. It is often reinforced through media and pop culture, where harmful tropes and stereotypes shape and sustain preconceived notions about femininity and the female experience. In this way, collective trauma becomes cyclical, woven into the quilt of cultural narratives that dictate how women’s stories are told, perceived, and internalized. Collective and individual trauma frequently intersect, influencing both personal identity and broader societal understandings of womanhood. The term FILM, unless otherwise specified, refers to English-language films born out of the Western world. Themes discussed in this thesis can be found in cinema throughout culture, and just a small portion of the multitudes of trauma-focused films are investigated.

author: Sara Marsh
thesis_title: Flick Chicks: Reimagining Female Trauma Through Modern Cinema
paragraph: As children, we discover our role in the world in part through the resources that surround us – for me, that meant sneaking peeks at issues of Us Weekly and People on the magazine stand at the grocery store, collecting whispers from cousins just a few years older than myself (and more frequently than not imparting knowledge that was utterly incorrect), and, of course, watching PG-13-rated movies. This early consumption of cultural narratives quietly shaped my understanding of what it meant to be a woman, from the good, to the beautiful, to the bad, to the even worse. Reflecting on her own maturation and childhood celebrity idolizations in From Reverence to Rape, Molly Haskell notes that “One of the definitions of the loss of innocence is perhaps the fragmenting of [the] unified self – a split that is different, and emblematic, not only for each sex, but also for each era. My own split, between the way I saw myself (as a free agent) and the way I was expected to behave (as a lady, deferential to authority) was reflected, as such things are, in the movies and in the parallel split in movie heroines. It was a split that whore.”5 brought up to date the age-old dualism between body and soul, virgin and The fragmented sense of self that Haskell examines is one that many young women encounter as they grow out of their youthful free-spiritedness and take on – or, at times, are made to take on – more submissive, traditional roles. “Oh Scottie, don't let me go.” – A Closer Look at Hitchcock Nowhere is this shift more clearly seen than in film, where women’s roles have historically been reduced to a binary. The duality between the pure “virgin” and the sexually deviant “whore” is one of the oldest cinematic tropes, encouraging girls like the young Heskell to choose the virtuous path under threat that “no man would marry a woman who was not a virgin.” This trope is the basis for actress Kim Novak’s role in Vertigo (1958), lauded as one of director Alfred Hitchcock’s best films and ranked among the greatest movies ever made (to the extent that greatness can be objectively evaluated, of course). In the movie, Novak plays both blonde Madeleine, the sacrificial “virgin”, and brunette Judy, the “whore”, transforming on-screen from one to the other with a simple hair color swap. While Vertigo is noted as one of the most “sympathetic” of Hitchcock’s films when it comes to empathy for his female victims, Madeleine and Judy serve as little more than plot devices for Scottie, the protagonist (played by James Stewart), and his own psychological development. We view Madeleine / Judy as an object in the her.6 same way that Scottie sees This narrow, male-centric view of the film’s female characters is presented almost immediately and persists throughout the story, as Scottie – and the camera – follows the action across the backdrop of San Francisco. He first glimpses Madeleine, his colleague Gavin Elster’s wife, across the room at Ernie’s Restaurant, where she sits, glowing, among a smartly dressed crowd. Her back is to him, draped in a green satin shawl, her exposed skin stark white against a sea of dark suits and evening wear. Madeleine does not see Scottie, and he turns his head to avoid her gaze as she leaves the restaurant – for Scottie has been tasked with following her by her husband. From the fortification of his car, Scottie watches her leave her apartment to go about her day. He spies on her through a cracked doorway as she purchases flowers, her face mere inches from his, unaware of his looming presence. He observes her admiring the Portrait of Carlotta at the Legion of Honor art museum. He watches her again as she places flowers on a tombstone in a lush cemetery. His voyeuristic tour ends when a seemingly possessed Madeleine throws herself into San Francisco Bay, and Scottie rescues her – setting in motion a whirlwind romance between the two. Throughout this sequence, we view Madeleine entirely through Scottie’s eyes as he silently stalks her, his need to protect and possess her growing with each glance. His quiet obsession forms in the shadows, presenting an entitlement to this detached, distant form of a woman – one that seeps into the audience’s understanding, too. Films like Vertigo echo a broader trend in cinema at the time, in which women exist primarily to advance men’s emotional and psychological journeys, while their own complexities remain untouched or nonexistent. Here, that journey is Scottie’s battle with debilitating vertigo and obsessive desire. The film exemplifies the cinematic fragmentation of women into one-dimensional tropes: bodies to be gazed upon, manipulated, and consumed by actors on screen and audiences in theaters. These archetypal, copy / paste characters are little more than projections of the fantasies and psyches of the men — including Hitchcock — they exist to serve, both on and off screen. As film critic Roger Ebert wrote in his 1996 review, “The female characters in [Hitchcock’s] films reflected the same qualities over and over again: They were blond. They were icy and remote. They were imprisoned in costumes that subtly combined fashion with fetishism. They mesmerized the men, who often had physical or psychological handicaps. Sooner or later, every Hitchcock woman was humiliated.” It is as if Hitchcock’s own personal fetishes are shamelessly repurposed, again and again, for the screen. The harmful dynamics presented in Hitchcock’s movies were not always entirely fictional, and in fact, often eerily mimicked the reality on-set. Hitchcock, hailed by many as one of the best directors in film history, was also known in the business as infamously intense and even sadistic, especially toward his film’s actresses. Tippi Hedren, known for her hair-raising performance of Melanie Daniels in The Birds (1963) – including a brutal on-camera assault by a real, live flock of crows – wrote in her 2016 tell-all Tippi: A Memoir that Hitchcock sexually harassed her repeatedly on-set and retaliated when she rebuffed his advances. Hedren, who also plays the lead in Marnie (1964), detailed Hitchcock’s physical and mental abuse, which included numerous examples of forced physical interaction and inappropriate practices. Hedren details how Hitchcock swapped mechanical birds for real birds on the set of the famous horror film, resulting in days of live birds being thrown at her and attached to her body. She also claims that Marnie, which features a troubled female protagonist who is raped by her husband, was in part representative of Hitchcock’s own personal fantasy.7

author: Sara Marsh
thesis_title: Flick Chicks: Reimagining Female Trauma Through Modern Cinema
paragraph: Allegations like these are awful, but not shocking – especially when considered alongside the reality of being a woman in the 1950s and ’60s. Women of the time were expected to fit into a strict, gendered box and to obey the men in their lives without question. During the early stages of the Cold War, American propaganda promoted the nuclear family as a symbol of national superiority – an ideal that set Americans apart from the Communists. The ideal American woman was portrayed as feminine, delicate, and a happy homemaker, relishing in capitalism and freedom. Women went to college not necessarily to learn, but to earn the “Mrs. Degree” – a husband. Being single was undesirable; being single and pregnant was worse. Troublemaking girls.8 girls were sent away, either to live with relatives or to homes for wayward In this vein, movies both reflect and reinforce the social conventions of their time. And for decades, film history was authored almost entirely by white male patriarchal forces—those in power. There was little room for women on set, and creative roles like director, producer, writer, and lead actor were typically reserved for men. This still holds true to a significant extent today, as film remains an industry where women, people of color, and other marginalized groups remain underrepresented. A narrative shift began in the early 1970s, when feminist film theory emerged as a way to critically examine cinema as a cultural practice that reproduces stereotypes about women and femininity. Drawing from psychoanalysis, scholars introduced the now-famous concept of the structures.9 male gaze and began to explore how visual culture encodes gendered power In one of feminist film theory’s most famous texts, Visual Pleasure and Narrative Cinema, Laura Mulvey expands upon the idea of scopophilia, or the pleasure of looking, as an explanation for the narcissistic, voyeuristic fascination of Hollywood. Here, self-identification and the pleasure of spying on another intertwine, two processes that are highly gendered. Mulvey examines these contradictory forces, writing that “In a world ordered by sexual imbalance, pleasure in looking has been split between active / male and passive / female. The determining male gaze projects its phantasy onto the female figure which is styled accordingly. Women displayed as sexual object is the leit-motiff of erotic spectacle: from pin-ups to strip-tease, from Ziegfeld to Busby Berkeley, she holds the look, plays to and signifies male desire. Mainstream film neatly combined spectacle and narrative. The presence of woman is an indispensable element of spectacle in normal narrative film, yet her visual presence tends to work against the contemplation.”10 development of a story line, to freeze the flow of action in moments of erotic In other words, the conventional filmic structure of a Hollywood film was set up in a manner that inherently objectifies women as mere spectacles in larger storylines.

author: Sara Marsh
thesis_title: Flick Chicks: Reimagining Female Trauma Through Modern Cinema
paragraph: Some love them, some despise them. Horror films may be polarizing, but they undoubtedly offer one of the richest frameworks for analyzing the relationship between viewer, camera, and gender. Researcher and author Carol J. Clover examines gender in the horror genre in-depth in her book Men, Women, and Chainsaws: Gender in the Modern Horror Film, published in the wake of cult classics like The Shining (1980), Poltergeist (1982), and A Nightmare on Elm Street (1984). Horror, rife with gore, assault, and degradation – and particularly exploitative in its treatment of women – may not immediately seem like a feminist art form. Yet Clover uncovers a compelling dynamic between hero, monster, and audience that, while deeply gendered, creates new avenues for viewer identification. Horror is notoriously formulaic, with a cast of recognizable tropes and narrative arcs that fans anticipate long before the theater lights begin to dim. “In fact,” Clover writes, ”Horror’s system of sympathies transcends and preexists any given example. Patrons of a slasher film or rape-revenge film know more or less what to expect well before the film rolls, and at least one horror director has suggested that their emotional engagement with the movie begins while they are standing in line – a proposition that acknowledges the profoundly formulaic nature of the enterprise.”11 Audience members begin emotionally engaging with the story while still in line at the theater. That anticipation soon turns to empathy, particularly with the film’s protagonist – often, a woman facing imminent peril. “She is the one who encounters the mutilated bodies of her friends and perceives the full extent of the preceding horror and of her own peril; who is chased, cornered, wounded; whom we see scream, stagger, fall, rise, and scream again. She is abject terror personified. If her friends knew they were about to die only seconds before the event, the Final Girl lives with the knowledge for long minutes or hours. She alone looks death in the face, but she alone also finds the strength B).”12 either to stay the killer long enough to be rescued (ending A) or to kill him herself (ending These traits define the Final Girl – a term coined by Clover – or the last woman standing at the end of the film, the one who kills the monster after an extended battle. She is Nancy Thompson from A Nightmare on Elm Street (1984), Ellen Ripley from Alien (1979), and Laurie Strode from Halloween (1978). And she is often more rational and courageous – even after being chased, attacked, bludgeoned, and mutilated – than her cowering male counterparts. Though she appears “feminine,” she survives not because of traditional gender norms but because she adopts qualities coded as masculine – strength, survival, and self-preservation. Gender, Clover argues, is more a state of mind than body, and characters “switch” back and forth between roles; weapons wielded by female protagonists are often phallic, male killers fail to fit into traditional molds of masculinity. Clover notes that “The Final Girl is boyish, in a word. She is not fully feminine not, in any case, feminine in the ways of her friends. Her smartness, gravity, competence in mechanical and other practical matters, and sexual reluctance set her apart from the other girls and ally her, ironically, with the very boys she fears or rejects, not to speak of the killer himself. Lest we miss the point, it is spelled out in her name: Stevie, Marti, Terry, Laurie, Stretch, Will, Joey, Max.”13 Though she defeats evil herself, it is the “male qualities” she adopts – strength, smarts, survival instincts – that ultimately define her ability to survive. Final Girls quickly became fixtures of the slasher films of the 1970s and 80s. Interestingly, early versions of the Final Girl often relied on male intervention, while later films allowed them to be more self-sufficient. This is perfectly juxtaposed in The Texas Chain Saw Massacre (1974) and its 1986 sequel. The two films end similarly, with protagonists Sally and Stretch narrowly escaping their sadistic pursuers. “In Part One the Final Girl,” says Clover, “for all her survivor pluck, is, like Red Riding Hood, saved through male agency. In Part Two, however, there is no male agency; the figure so designated proves so utterly ineffectual that he cannot save himself, much less the girl.”14 The former film closes after a blood-covered Sally, who is seen running down the road being chased by Leatherface, is pulled into the bed of a truck by a random traveler, narrowly escaping death. She laughs maniacally as Leatherface angrily waves his chainsaw, disappearing into the distance. The latter ends after Stretch, alone on top of a stone structure in an amusement park, grabs a chain saw and sends Chop Top to his death, then triumphantly mimics Leatherface’s angry “dance” from the first film. In this moment, she is defined by her self-sufficiency, her ability to survive on her own. There is no shortage of excellent horror films. It’s a genre that continues to grow in popularity with time. Horror’s unique structural elements – predictability, tropes, and symbolic excess – allow for audiences of all kinds to connect deeply with the characters and themes. They create a space to confront monstrosity, trauma, and survival. Yet films like A Nightmare on Elm Street (1984), Alien (1979), Halloween (1978), and most horror movies of the time were directed by men (I’d be remiss, though, to not give a shout out to pioneers like Kathryn Bigelow, Mary Lambert, and Jackie Kong!). Thus, they present female trauma, fear, and despair, through a lens that is clouded by bias and fetishization. Their cameras leer, stalk, and fetishize their female protagonists – even as those characters demonstrate strength. Female trauma becomes entertainment, repackaged through male fantasies of both power and vulnerability. The 2000s and 2010s saw a wave of new female directors arrive on the horror scene who reclaimed and reimagined the genre. American Psycho (2000), directed by Mary Harron, quickly consumerism.15 became a cult favorite, skewering toxic masculinity and cultural Karyn Kusama’s Jennifer’s Body (2009), which flopped on initial release, is now hailed as a feminist masterpiece.16 More recent films like The Babadook (2014, dir. Jennifer Kent), A Girl Walks Home Alone at Night (2014, dir. Ana Lily Amirpour), and Revenge (2017, dir. Coralie Fargeat) use the horror format to explore trauma, rage, and transformation from a distinctly female perspective. With women directing the narrative, sexist tropes and antiquated motifs become powerful tools to explore human nature and bring female stories to a vast audience.

author: Sara Marsh
thesis_title: Flick Chicks: Reimagining Female Trauma Through Modern Cinema
paragraph: “Have you ever dreamt of a better version of yourself? Younger, more beautiful, more perfect?” This is the question posed at the beginning of The Substance (2024). Though directed at the film’s protagonist, it’s also aimed at us, the audience. After all, who hasn’t longed to be better? More beautiful? More perfect? The Substance, directed by Coralie Fargeat, premiered in 2024 at the Cannes Film Festival and has since been met with a mix of praise and revulsion. The movie tells the story of Elizabeth Sparkle, a washed-up actress played masterfully (and somewhat self-referentially) by Demi Moore. Elizabeth takes drastic measures when, after turning fifty, she is deemed irrelevant by her boorish manager: too old, too faded, yesterday’s news. When an inconspicuous box containing syringes and a neon-green treatment called The Substance arrives at her door, Elizabeth – desperate to realize its claims that once taken, “a better version of yourself” will Lauren Coates, “Amanda Seyfried Says ‘Jennifer’s Body’ Was Ruined by Marketing,” Variety, March 2025. emerge – injects herself. Moments later, while writhing on the cold, hard tiles of her bathroom, she gives “birth” to a perky, bubbly version of her younger self – played by Margaret Qualley – simply named Sue. The two women begin splitting their lives in alternating seven-day shifts—living the same life, but experiencing it through radically different lenses. As expected, The Substance is not all it promises. Elizabeth is soon forced to reckon with her age, her fading fame, and the literal manifestation of her former self, in the most absurd, gory, and monstrous way possible. A visceral example of body horror, The Substance is not for the squeamish. It’s also not subtle. But that’s the point. Fargeat leans into metaphor and excess to critique what today’s culture tells us a body should be. The film takes place in a liminal, hyper-stylized version of Los Angeles: inconspicuous yet heightened, neon-colored, and shimmery. Long, empty hallways and cold, sanitized living spaces feel both uncanny and familiar. We learn little about characters’ backstories beyond their names (if even that). The movie relies on historical cultural references, like the 80s aerobic dance craze and smartphones, without placing the story in any specific time frame. All of these choices allow us to situate ourselves in an obviously fictional world that at the same time feels oddly close to our own. From the film’s opening, Elizabeth is haunted by warped reflections of herself: in door knobs, mirrors, and a sultry portrait looming over her living room. Once Sue becomes the face of her former TV slot, Elizabeth’s torment takes a new shape, in the form of a towering billboard of a leotard-clad Sue, smiling down at her, omnipresent and larger-than-life. The film isn't just about aging. It’s a blistering commentary on self-surveillance and the way women are conditioned to scrutinize their bodies. In a world where cosmetic procedures and “get fit quick” solutions are more accessible than ever, The Substance takes this quest for perfection to its grotesque extreme. The film culminates in a grotesque, gory, jaw-dropping, satirical display that is as funny as it is shocking. Through exaggeration and absurdity, Fargeat skewers the impossible expectations placed on women. She recasts the male gaze, putting Sue’s body on display in a way that is deliberately exaggerated and deeply uncomfortable. And in doing so, she forces the viewer to question their complicity. The Substance turns the camera back on us, suggesting that patriarchy aside, we are often our own worst critics, internalizing the very ideals that harm us.

author: Sara Marsh
thesis_title: Flick Chicks: Reimagining Female Trauma Through Modern Cinema
paragraph: Film is a bright, brilliant illustration of history. It is a portal through which we can viscerally experience past events, see the world through the eyes of others. With the establishment of more and more female leaders on the film set, the portrayal of women’s trauma has evolved drastically, leading to rich, nuanced explorations of women’s lived experiences – not just in art films, but in top-grossing blockbusters. While movies about difficult or taboo topics – mental illness, aging, abuse, rape – that feature female protagonists are historically exploitative, ranging from caricatures of the female experience to the utter desecration for pure malice, women in the film industry are turning stereotypical tropes on their head, creating spaces for audiences of all backgrounds to confront the raw complexities of trauma. The screen, once a narrow lens controlled by those in power, has become a liminal space through which fantastical worlds and creative characters can decipher the questions that are sometimes too complicated or painful to confront in the real world. This evolution is especially urgent at a time when the world is grappling with how to understand one another’s lived experiences. In an era marked by division, multimedia storytelling mediums like film offer a bridge – a way to relate, to empathize, and to confront the events that shape our shared humanity. As much as history shapes culture, film shapes history, offering a subversion of entrenched norms and shifts in the way audiences perceive the world. The film industry – still very white, very straight, very cis, and very male – has a long way to go. In 2022, the percentage of female characters with speaking roles in movies was just 34.6%, and role.17 70 of the top 100 films of the same year featured no women of color in any The need for new perspectives behind the camera is great, and creative storytellers have a responsibility not only to protect and promote vulnerable stories, but to tell them in ways that cultivate empathy and understanding.
