Studies have found that having a variety of social bonds may help reduce stress and heart-related risks. Strong social bonds are even linked to a longer life. On the other hand, loneliness and social isolation are linked to poorer health, depression, and increased risk of early death. (5) During the World Cup, the sense of global unity becomes palpable. The excitement and passion for the game bring people together across continents, narrowing the distance between fans and cultures worldwide. On the field, the match is more than just a display of players’ skills; it showcases each na- tion’s culture, identity, and pride. Fans experience not only the thrill of the sport but also shared experiences, flags, colors, and chants that transform each match into a celebration of diversity. Whether cheering for a national team or sup- porting a favorite club in league matches, soccer fans form an expansive, interconnected community united by their shared love for the game. This global fan base crosses language barriers, time zones, and borders, with millions staying up late or waking early to watch games, feeling the highs and lows of every match together. During the 2022 World Cup, I joined a group of German team fans at a sports bar to watch a crucial match. If Germany won, they would advance to the top 16. At first, the team’s lead electrified the crowd; fans waved German flags, danced, and even sang the year’s theme song. In the meantime, one fan bought beer for everyone present, amplifying the joyful atmosphere. However, the mood shifted dra- matically when Germany fell behind in the second half and ultimately lost. Some fans even shed tears, and the collective disappointment was palpable. Despite the loss, there was a comforting sense of unity among us. We shared the team’s sorrow and hope for their future comeback. This experience re- flects the unique emotional bond in soccer fandom—a connection where shared passion and collective resilience amplify the sense of belonging and inspire unwavering support. The World Cup intensifies this bond as fans around the world cheer, celebrate, and mourn as one, demonstrating the unparal- leled ability of soccer to foster solidarity and togetherness across nations. High levels of sports team identification correspond to positive levels of social well-being. For instance, team identification correlates with higher levels of social self-esteem and well-being. Higher levels of vigor (i.e., energy) and more frequent positive emotion, lower levels of loneliness, depression, alien- ation, and negative emotion experiences, and higher levels of openness, conscientiousness, and ex- troversion. (6) 4. Conclusion In this chapter, we explored the nature of social bonds, emphasizing their formation through shared ex- periences, mutual participation, and aligned goals. Sports fan communities exemplify this as they create environments where individuals feel connected to a larger collective, with each member playing a vital role. These bonds foster a supportive, encouraging culture, reinforcing emotional safety and shared identity. Finally, social bonds fulfill the innate human need for connection, making them essential for individuals and our well-being.

author: Sports Culture
thesis_title: Chapter 3: Design Inspired
paragraph: In what ways does sports fan culture foster a sense of belonging and emotional well-being among global fans? What might we learn from this environment and apply to design? Today, enthusiasm for sports transcends language, geography, and societal divisions. In 2022, the FIFA World Cup Qatar set many new records, with 5 billion people engaged.(7) Every year, 1.86 million people from diverse backgrounds—spanning race, gender, and age—unite under their shared love for football. By aligning themselves closely with a specific team, sports fans experience a profound sense of belonging and identity with the team athletes and others who share the same loyalty. Engaging in rituals and practices like passionately following the highs and lows of one’s favorite team, consistently wearing one’s team’s colors, and memorizing the names and achievements of every player all create a sense of tangible connection and belonging. These synchronized, symbolic gestures unify diverse groups through shared movement and energy, reinforcing a sense of belonging and fan-com- munity bonds. In this chapter, I will examine how fans cultivate a unique and vibrant culture through shared practices and rituals, focusing on soccer. Today, football fan culture connects a vast global community with a shared passion for the sport. What aspects of soccer fandom contribute to forming strong social bonds? 1. The Global Soccer Fan Community: Drawing massive viewership during events like the FIFA World Cup, soccer—as it’s known in much of the world–holds the title of the most-watched sport globally. With a fanbase exceeding 3.5 billion, it’s one of the most popular sports worldwide. (1) In the next section, I will explore specific elements of soccer fandom, such as team gestures, theme songs, and team colors, and analyze their role in deepening connections and reinforcing the power of unity. Through this lens, we’ll discover how these practices can inform design approaches to strengthen social bonds in everyday life. 2.0 Rituals and Traditions: Fan culture is filled with unique elements that strengthen the bond between supporters, including fan chants, team colors, and symbolic rituals. These synchronized, symbolic gestures unify diverse groups through shared movement and energy, reinforcing a sense of belonging and fan-community bonds. 2.1 South African: Vuvuzela South African soccer fans, for example, are famously known for their use of the vuvuzela, a trumpet-like horn that produces a loud, buzzing sound. During matches, fans blow the vuvuzela in unison to cheer for their teams, creating an electrifying atmosphere unique to South African football culture. This ritual energizes the crowd and is a distinctive way to express collective enthusiasm and pride. 2.2 Iceland: Viking Clap A gesture ritual is a sequence of specific body movements or hand gestures performed as part of a ritual practice. In soccer fan culture, gesture rituals are a particular body language. Fans perform actions in unison to convey a message. During the World Cup, the Icelandic national team’s famous “Viking clap”–where fans and players clap together in unison–stands out as particularly powerful. The ritual typically starts slowly, with fans raising their arms above their heads in sync with the clap. Gradually increasing the tempo while chant- ing a deep “Huh!” to a drum beat creates a memorable and electric atmosphere. Iceland’s fans became known for using the Viking Thunder Clap chant in the mid-2010s. (2) Witness- ing this scene when I was young was deeply moving; the fierce rhythm and unified clapping felt like witnessing an ancient and unbreakable display of Iceland’s spirit and pride. The iconic “Viking clap” is more than just encouragement; it’s a shared language—a tradition that connects Icelanders across gen- erations. Win or lose, Icelandic fans continue to show their unwavering support through their powerful gestures and chants. This unique, timeless form of communication captivates all who watch. 2.3: Manchester: The Poznan: A similarly powerful example of a gesture ritual is the “Poznan,” currently practiced by fans from Man- chester City to celebrate a goal. Turning their back to the pitch and linking arms, City fans jump up and down as one. (3) In 2010, UEFA, Manchester City fans filled the stadium in their iconic light blue jerseys, forming a wave in an awe-inspiring display of unity. The scene was electrifying, energizing the fans and players, who drew strength from the crowd’s vibrant energy and support. This unforgettable moment demonstrated the powerful impact of fan enthusiasm on the game’s intensity and spirit. 3.0 Theme song: A theme song in soccer fandom is a song that is associated with a specific team or event. Like ges- ture rituals, theme songs are powerful cultural elements passed down from generation to generation, strengthening the emotional ties and collective memory of sports communities. Theme songs are often released to coincide with major events like the World Cup. Like national anthems that unify citizens and hold a unique place in human civilization, theme songs have a unique place in sports fan culture. For instance, the FIFA World Cup theme songs encapsulate shared ideals through melodies and up- lifting lyrics. By celebrating unity, perseverance, and joy, these songs go beyond entertainment; they become cultural artifacts that reinforce the communal experience of soccer fandom. 3.1: Liverpool FC’s famous fans’ theme song: You’ll Never Walk Alone The song “You’ll Never Walk Alone” has been a source of resilience for Liverpool fans, inspiring hope during difficult times on and off the field. Its power lies in the lyrics and melody (“At the end of a storm. There’s a golden sky. “) and in the fact that fans often sing it in unison. It reflects death, hard times, faith, and human defiance and has become a symbol of hope and solidarity for the club, its fans, and the city of Liverpool. During the 2005 UEFA Champions League Final in Istanbul, Liverpool was down 3-0 against AC Milan. (4) As fans began singing “You’ll Never Walk Alone” in defiance of defeat, their unwavering support lifted the players’ spirits. This surge of collective energy and belief from the fans is said to have reignited the team’s determination. Against the odds, Liverpool fought back, ultimately equalizing and winning the match in a dramatic penalty shootout to claim the European Cup. 3.2: “Olé, Olé, Olé The other famous theme song for soccer fans is “Olé, Olé, Olé.” Originating in the late 1980s, this soccer Caption: Liverpool fans sang anthem has become a unifying chant for fans worldwide, its popularity crossing national boundaries. “You’ll Never Walk Alone” to face Wolves on Championship Sunday 2022. (5) Known for its simple yet powerful refrain, the song allows thousands of fans to join in unison. Cel- ebrated for its simple, memorable chant that’s easy to pronounce, its catchy “Olé, Olé, Olé” refrain al- lows fans of any nationality to join in instantly, rallying fans and players alike to display shared passion Lyrics: (Pic. 7) that transcends language barriers. When you walk through a storm Caption: Irish fans sang Olé, Olé, Olé to celebrate. Lyrics: (Pic. 8) Hold your head up high The name of the game And don’t be afraid of the dark (football) At the end of a storm The name of the game There’s a golden sky (football) And the sweet silver song of a lark The name of the game Walk on through the wind (football) Walk on through the rain The name of the game For your dreams be tossed and blown Ole ole ole ole Walk on, walk on We are the champions With hope in your heart We are the champions And you’ll never walk alone Ole ole ole ole You’ll never walk alone We are the champions Walk on, walk on We are the champions With hope in your heart Ole ole ole ole And you’ll never walk alone We are the champions~ You’ll never walk alone Theme songs like “You’ll Never Walk Alone” and “Ole Ole Ole Ole” demonstrate how universal, shared 4.0 Team color: rituals and languages can create emotional resonance and unity among sports fans. These songs en- capsulate collective memories, inspire pride, and reinforce bonds within fan communities. By singing Color is one of the most powerful channels we have for communication. Today, all major sports teams these anthems, fans transcend individual identities, becoming part of something bigger: a global are identified by the color of their uniforms, which their fans also adopt in their attire and accessories. community rooted in shared passion and belonging. As far back as I can remember, certain team colors have always stood out to me, for example, Man- chester City’s iconic light blue. Manchester City cleverly references this color in a memorable chant: “We are the boys in blue, coming after you.”(14) Although I’m not a fan of the team, I can’t help but be impressed by the strong visual identity they’ve created through this color. It’s an aspect of their identity that unites supporters and reinforces team pride. The power of visual language in soccer fan culture isn’t just about representation; it’s about fostering a sense of belonging that transcends the game, al- lowing fans to participate and be “part of the team”. Team colors have long been a part of fans’ identities, regardless of a specific game (Tamir, 2021). (6) In soccer, team colors hold immense historical and cultural significance and symbolize identity and unity. As a designer, understanding the impact of color is crucial in crafting designs. This section will delve into the unique influence of team colors, exploring how they evoke pride, reinforce loyalty, and contrib- ute to the collective spirit of soccer fan culture. Colors reflect historical and cultural significance while fostering fans’ deep sense of belonging, identity, and unity. 4.1: Bayern Munich (Red): Bayern Munich’s iconic red jerseys, symbolizing energy and passion, trace back to the club’s early days when red was chosen as the primary color. In the Münchner Sport Club in 1906, the team started wear- ing white jerseys and red shorts, earning them the nickname Die Roten (The Reds). This decision reflect- ed the Bavarian region’s cultural vibrancy. (8) Fans often refer to the team as Die Roten (The Reds), a nickname that underscores their identity and loyalty. The color unites fans in stadiums and across the globe, creating a visual and emotional bond through shared representation. 4.2: Netherlands National Team (Orange): The Netherlands’ flag may be red, white, and blue, but the national colour is orange - that comes from the Dutch Royal Family, the House of Orange-Nassau, which dates back almost 500 years to William of Orange. Like the colour, the national team’s badge featuring a lion takes its root from Dutch royalty, and Lopez wanted to find a new way of portraying the “power, glow, and regalness” of the lion and its mane within the jersey. (9) Whether playing sport or celebrating King’s Day, wearing orange is a huge source of pride. During matches, seas of orange-clad fans turn stadiums and streets into vibrant displays of collective enthusiasm. In Esport soccer, a video soccer game, the official Dutch fan club even named the game “E-Orange.” (19) 4.3 Croatia (Red and White Checkered): The Croatian national team is instantly recognizable by its red-and-white checkered pattern, inspired by the nation’s flag and coat of arms. This pattern connects deeply to Croatia’s history and cultural heri- tage (10) and becomes a source of national pride. Fans proudly wear the checkered jerseys, visually and emotionally linking themselves to the team’s identity. Such a gesture illustrates how design can anchor belonging through cultural significance. The examples of team colors from Bayern Munich, Croatia, and the Dutch national team showcase visu- al identity’s profound role in fostering community. Team colors serve as immediate symbols of recogni- tion, connecting fans to their teams and offering a unifying visual language. Bayern Munich’s red jersey embodies dominance and passion, Croatia’s checkered red-and-white design signifies cultural heri- tage, and the Netherlands’ vibrant orange reflects national pride tied to historical associations with the House of Orange. These colors evoke potent emotions, symbolizing shared values. Furthermore, their global recognition demonstrates their ability to transcend borders and create cultural touchstones, uniting fans worldwide through shared visual cues. These examples highlight how thoughtfully designed elements amplify a collective sense of identity and belonging. Understanding sports fandom through elements like team colors informs how we de- sign for social connection and belonging. As a designer, transforming the abstract sense of belong- ing into physical visual elements involves creating symbols that resonate emotionally and univer- sally. Using emotionally charged elements, like impactful colors, patterns, or iconography, designers communicate meanings that transcend traditional language barriers. These visual tools foster a sense of connection and enable individuals to align themselves with a larger community. 5. Conclusion The exploration of gestures, rituals, theme songs, and team colors in sports fandom highlights their role in shaping collective identity and fostering emotional connections. These elements strengthen the sense of belonging by creating shared experiences, from synchronized movements like the Viking Clap to universally recognized anthems like You’ll Never Walk Alone. Team colors serve as powerful symbols of pride, reinforcing unity among fans. For designers, these insights reveal how visual, auditory, and physical elements can create immersive experiences that encourage social bonding. Designers can craft environments that inspire connection and collaboration by integrating symbolic gestures, coordinated participation, and impactful design choices. Applying these principles to design—whether through public spaces, events, or interactive experiences—ensures that people feel part of something greater, reinforcing human connection in an increasingly digital world.

author: Sports Culture
thesis_title: Chapter 3: Design Inspired
paragraph: How can we foster connection, identity, and a sense of belonging in design practice? As exemplified in Chapter 2, participation in fan groups can create emotional bonds, unite diverse groups of people, and foster a shared identity through rituals and tactics like theme songs and team colors. For designers, understanding how these elements cultivate community and reinforce real-world social connections provides a roadmap for creating designs that are not only visually engaging but also deep- ly meaningful, encouraging real-life interaction and a sense of belonging. This chapter will explore examples demonstrating how artists and designers use physical interaction and intentional gatherings to create experiences where people can connect, interact, and feel a sense of belonging to something greater than themselves. These approaches highlight the value of shared ex- periences and the unique power of collective engagement in fostering meaningful social connections. I will also discuss some of my design experiments in which I’ve explored how design can improve healthy connections between us. 1. Rirkrit Tiravanija: Tomorrow is the Question (1) Artist Rikrit Tiravanija is interested in the visitor experience and how people relate to and interact with art. He employs interaction as one way to experience the exhibition. In Rirkrit Tiravanija’s exhibition, “ Tomorrow is the Question”, on view at MoMA PS1 in 2019, several table tennis tables are set up, each bearing the exhibition’s title, “Tomorrow is the Question.” Alongside the tables, billiard balls and rackets are provided. Visitors are invited to engage in spontaneous table tennis games with fellow attendees. This interactive setup transforms the exhibition space into a dynamic social environment where the viewers are part of the work. Ping-pong is a unique activity that inherently requires two participants, emphasizing the importance of collaboration and connection. It’s not a game that can be played alone; instead, it thrives on the interaction and rhythm shared between players. Rirkrit Tiravanija’s creative practice centers around fostering community and shared experiences, aligning with themes of belonging and collective participation. His works often blur the boundaries between art and life, using social interaction as a medium. For example, his iconic installation, untitled 1992-1995 (free/still) (3), invited gallery visitors to share a communal meal of Thai curry, transforming the exhibition space into a gathering place for dialogue and connection. By removing traditional hier- archies between artist and audience, Tiravanija emphasizes the relational aspects of art, encouraging participants to engage with each other in meaningful ways. This approach resonates with the themes of sports fandom and shared rituals, as both involve creating environments where individuals can connect, participate, and feel part of something larger than them- selves. As designers, Tiravanija’s practice teaches us to prioritize human interaction and commu- nal experience, creating designs that foster unity and inclusivity. Although the exhibition’s theme is “Tomorrow is the question,” playing ping-pong with another person shifts our focus entirely to the present moment. In that instant, all concerns and distractions fade, leaving only the pure joy of the game—the ball, the paddle, and the dynamic exchange. This act of shared focus and physical interaction demonstrates how engaging in a mutual activity fos- ters connection and builds relationships rooted in collaboration. By immersing ourselves in the present, ping-pong becomes more than just a game; it becomes a medium for cultivating emotional well-being. Tiravanija’s practice demonstrates the transformative power of shared experiences, reminding us of the value of human connection and its ability to enhance our overall well-being. 2. Olafur Eliasson: The Weather Project (2) Olafur Eliasson’s broader artistic approach focuses on creating immersive environments that encour- age interaction, reflection, and collective experience. Known for his use of natural elements like light, water, and mist, Eliasson breaks boundaries between art, nature, and science, transforming spaces into sites of sensory engagement. In 2003, Eliasson’s “The Weather Project” (2) featured a massive artificial sun and mist-filled atmo- sphere installed in the Turbine Hall of the Tate Modern in London. The artificial sun emitted a warm orange glow and invited visitors to interact with the environment. This often led to collective actions like lying on the floor, forming patterns with their bodies, or simply gathering to share the immersive experience. What makes this exhibition particularly captivating is its ability to transform the traditional art-view- ing experience into a collective, participatory experience. Unlike ordinary exhibitions where viewers passively stand and observe,people are invited to sit, recline, or even lie down, gazing at the immense, glowing sun overhead. This relaxed, unstructured positioning naturally leads individuals to lean on one another. The installation provides ample space for visitors to gather, fostering a welcoming en- vironment where collective engagement is both possible and naturally encouraged. The gathering becomes part of the artwork, symbolizing the beauty of human connection. Strangers, friends, and families inadvertently come together under the shared warmth of the artificial sun– a universal symbol of life, energy, and triumph. Eliasson’s sun is a metaphorical anchor—a shared goal that draws people together regardless of their backgrounds. This environment makes the visitors feel they’re part of something bigger than themselves by breaking traditional barriers, allowing participants to connect through a shared sensory experience. The art- work highlights the importance of collective effort and reminds us of our innate human desire to come together. 3. My experiments I am deeply energized by sports fan culture’s ability to foster a sense of belonging and encourage phys- ical interaction. This inspiration drives my interest in designing interactive experiences that connect people and promote healthy social bonds. In my HAVE SUN FUN project, (4) In recent years, the number of Taiwanese youths suffering from depression and not enough sunlight has gradually increased. One out of every five college students suffers from depression; I sought to encourage teenagers to spend more time outdoors, exposing them- selves to sunlight while building connections with one another. To achieve this, I designed a set of pho- tosensitive tattoo stickers and sun exposure kits. These stickers, featuring designs of the 12 zodiac con- stellations, change from transparent to vibrant colors when exposed to sufficient sunlight. Observing participants during testing, I saw how engaging with the stickers sparked smiles, laughter, and social interaction. Sharing and exchanging these tattoos became a joyful, interactive experience, encouraging teens to go out together and connect. This project highlighted the power of tactile, shared experiences in creating meaningful connections and promoting mental health. Building on this insight, I began experimenting further, exploring how games and play could enhance communal connections. I brought puzzles, UNO, and building blocks to campus, inviting random par- ticipants to join in. I discovered that team-based activities quickly broke down barriers and helped foster collaboration and shared joy. Inspired by this, I envisioned evolving simple games into team-ver- sus-team formats to amplify the experience. The shared goals and cooperation inherent in these team dynamics echoed the unity and excitement often seen in sports fandom. These games’ collective effort and sense of belonging felt akin to the communal spirit embodied in Rirkrit Tiravanija’s “Tomorrow is the Question” exhibition. These experiments affirm design’s transformative role in building stronger, healthier social connec- tions. By focusing on physical interaction and shared goals, we can craft experiences that transcend mere entertainment, offering participants a chance to feel part of something larger, fostering unity, excitement, and a deeper sense of community. 4. Conclusion These diverse examples reveal how powerful shared activities can be in cultivating community and fostering real-world connections. Whether it’s the unity of sports fan culture, the emotional resonance of collaborative campaigns, or the unexpected communal experiences in exhibitions, these instances demonstrate how design can invite viewers to interact with others and share experiences physically. They highlight a universal truth: Bonds formed through shared goals and physical presence play a cru- cial role in fostering a sense of belonging. As demonstrated in the previous examples, meaningful inter- actions—whether through collective participation, rituals, or team-based activities—strengthen social connections. These shared experiences create a deep sense of unity, allowing individuals to feel part of something larger than themselves. Thoughtful design approaches, such as enabling people to engage with each other through the five senses and allowing the audience to immerse themselves in a unique experience, can amplify the el- ements of shared rituals, common goals, and collective experiences, reinforcing the connections that bring people together. Whether in different platforms, public spaces, or campaigns, designers must strive to be more than just engaging. We should foster environments that encourage real-life interaction, nurture inclusivity, and look for ways to help participants foster meaningful social bonds. This approach enriches the hu- man experience and addresses broader societal challenges like isolation, offering a path toward more connected, cohesive communities. In doing so, designers play a pivotal role in shaping a world where belonging and unity are celebrated and actively sustained, making all the social bonds meaningful.

author: Sports Culture
thesis_title: Chapter 3: Design Inspired
paragraph: As a designer, and building on the discussions in the previous chapters, I am inspired by sports fandom culture. I aim to use my skills and experiences to create interactive designs that encourage people to reflect on the significance of physical interactions. Drawing from the collective energy, shared rituals, and emotional connections in fan communities, I seek to design experiences that strengthen social bonds and highlight the value of real-world human connection. 1. The purpose of the game The block-stacking game is designed to immerse participants in a shared physical activity that high- lights the power of social bonds. By engaging in teamwork, collaboration, and communication under the pressure of a time limit, participants are encouraged to experience the emotional and psycholog- ical effects of belonging to a group working toward a common goal. This design reflects the core ideas of the thesis by illustrating how physical interaction and teamwork foster social bonds. Like sports fan culture, the game promotes community and belonging through cooperative play, allowing participants to feel how collaboration shapes identity, builds trust, and en- hances well-being. It serves as a hands-on exploration of the unifying principles central to the thesis. 2. Rules Overview: You have 30 seconds to stack the blocks, and the highest one wins. All the blocks are different; each shape only has one. First grab, first use. 1.Choose a teammate; at least two people are in one team. One person grabs the blocks while the other stacks the blocks. 2. 3. Grab only one block each turn. No talk during the game; however, you have 20 seconds to discuss with 4. teammates how to divide before the competition and 30 seconds to stack the blocks silently. 3. The Process In the fall of 2024, I began testing my stacking blocks game with graduate and undergraduate students from Pratt Institute. I invited willing participants, many of whom were strangers to me and each other. These test sessions allowed me to reflect on the opportunities and challenges of the game and further evolve the rules of play. For instance, after several sessions, I introduced a new parameter—the rule of silence. Removing verbal communication transformed the experience, making teamwork and nonver- bal cooperation essential. This simple change heightened the challenge and strengthened the sense of collective effort. At the start of each session, I introduced my thesis research questions to the players, encouraging them to reflect on these ideas throughout the experience. After the game, I facilitated discussions where participants shared their thoughts, connecting their gameplay experience to broader themes of social interaction and belonging. This structured approach allowed me to observe how players engaged with the game and provided valuable insights into how interactive design can foster real-world connections. The game structure involved four players divided into two teams. I set a timer for 50 seconds: the first 20 seconds were dedicated to strategy discussions, and the remaining 30 seconds were for competi- tive block stacking. Despite the enforced silence during gameplay, participants often couldn’t help but laugh as they rushed to grab the largest blocks to create a stable foundation. The game was met with overwhelmingly positive feedback—players found it surprisingly fun yet more challenging than expect- ed. Many commented that it was a simple yet effective way to connect with new people. The most re- markable outcome was witnessing strangers who had never met chatting and bonding after the game. However, the game also presented logistical challenges. Finding four players available at the same time was tricky, and accommodating five people—including myself as the facilitator—required a larger space. This limitation made it challenging to conduct sessions indoors. Despite these obstacles, the experience reinforced the power of physical interaction in fostering social connections, proving that even a simple game can bring people together in meaningful ways. 4. Conclusion The Stacking Blocks Game is a hands-on representation of the thesis’s core ideas, transforming ab- stract concepts into tangible experiences. Through embodied, playful participation with color, shape, form, and the laws of gravity, the game creates a space for individuals to come together, connect, and foster a sense of belonging. Working together toward a common goal encourages meaningful relation- ships, even among strangers. The experience evokes joy, unity, and accomplishment, reinforcing the power of social bonds. Participants leave with a deeper appreciation for how collective efforts and physical interactions contribute to building a strong, connected community.

author: Sports Culture
thesis_title: Chapter 3: Design Inspired
paragraph: Being a soccer fan has become integral to my life, shaping my social connections, self-confidence, and worldview. The power of a strong sense of belonging extends beyond the shared experience of watch- ing matches—it fosters friendships, mutual support, and a positive outlook on life. This thesis asks: As designers, how can we draw from sports fan culture to create designs that bring people closer together and cultivate meaningful social bonds? This work explores how the rituals, gestures, team colors, theme songs, and collective experiences found in soccer fandom exemplify humanity’s profound need for connection and belonging. These el- ements unite fans and nurture deep emotional ties rooted in identity and tradition. The thesis demon- strates how shared passion and collective identity transcend individual differences, forging vibrant communities worldwide. By incorporating shared goals, physical interaction, and visual storytelling principles, designers can craft experiences that transcend traditional boundaries, fostering inclusivity and belonging in diverse contexts. These insights serve as a call to action for designers to leverage the unifying power of fan culture, creating designs that address the challenges of loneliness and isolation. Let us work together to design a world with fewer walls and more shared passions, where unity and collective energy inspire healthier and more connected communities. Much like the camaraderie of sports fans, our collective creativity can spark a brighter, more inclusive future—one in which everyone feels they are part of something larger than themselves. author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: elements to tell a personal experience with a clear plot through the use of memes. From a symbolic point of view, memes are virtual symbols on which the production and distribution of meme videos relies greatly, such as popular animals and characters “happy cat” and “doge” and other easily recognizable visual elements. These symbols are used repeatedly in meme videos to express different emotions or attitudes. With the widespread use of the Internet, meme videos have gradually formed a shared “secret language”, enabling people to gain instant emotional understanding and empathy in the videos. As a result, meme videos are not only a tool for entertainment, but they have also become a unique cultural phenomenon with deep symbolic significance. People living in society are like actors, performing various roles on the “stage” of life in order to adapt to different social situations. Meme videos are an extension of this theory in the digital world. They allow people’s “role-playing” to extend from our reality to cyberspace, and it can even be said that the Internet itself has become an infinitely expandable stage. In this infinitely expanding online stage, creators and viewers of meme videos are not just passive participants receiving information, but are actively engaged in this cultural production by selecting, sharing, and recreating meme videos. This form of interaction allows individuals to make a SELF-PRESENTATION in a broader public space.

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: In modern society, recording and sharing have become part of our daily life. Whether it's uploading a video of everyday moments on TikTok or sharing a casual shot of yourself on Instagram, these behaviors are not only a form of personal expression but also a means of interacting with the world. Among these forms, the vlog used to be the most representative medium of personal expression: telling one's life story directly to the viewer through the camera, showing the individual's emotions and experiences from a first- person perspective, and establishing a strong sense of authenticity and intimacy. However, with the continuous development of internet culture, meme videos are gradually becoming a new medium. Ashley is a well-known video blogger who used to be active on YouTube, and her videos are about her daily life.1 outfits and Short videos with author appearances uploaded on TikTok are also one of the variants of vlogging. In contrast to vlogs, meme videos just require various meme elements accompanied by simple text and music to convey their message delivery, reducing the dependence on authenticity and cohesion in traditional video formats. Vlogs primarily depend on the creator’s verbal communication and personal actions, while meme videos utilize virtual symbols for content expression. Its low-threshold production method not only reduces the cost of creation, but also possesses the features of imitation and re-creation, which promotes the widespread use and large-scale dissemination of meme videos.

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: However, the spread of memes is not entirely dependent on mechanical replication. Dawkins points out that, “we have the power to turn against our creators. We, alone on earth, can rebel against the tyranny of the selfish replicators.” (Richard Dawkins, “The Selfish Gene. 40th anniversary edition,” 462) What makes memes “survive” is their “infective power” to attract acceptance, imitation, and diffusion. This infective power depends not only on the characteristics of the meme itself (simplicity |or recognizability), it is also closely related to the subjective will and creativity of human beings. The “God” as one of the most “infective power” cultural memes Human beings are not only the bearers of memes but also active participants in their recreation and dissemination. This ability to resist is what is at stake in cultural transmission - by creatively accepting, transforming, or rejecting memes, human beings are able to transcend the role of mere reproduction and play a dynamic role in cultural evolution, allowing memes to form unique modes of expression and transmission.

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: I have asked four participants who have been active on social media to utilize a culture probe to record the memes they like to use in their daily life. Cute animals and cartoon images dominated their daily use, and cat memes were more common in meme videos due to their “infective power”. First, cats are popular pets whose owners share their lives on social media platforms.

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: of each other; and (c) were circulated, imitated, and/or transformed users.1 via the Internet by many Shifman’s analysis transforms the meme from Dawkins’s biological analogy into a universal cyber-symbolic language. The dissemination of the meme is highlighted as a process of deconstruction, adaptation and re-creation, leading to an open and dynamic virtual semiotic system. Unlike vlogs that purely express personal experiences, meme videos are collaborative in nature, through which they combine the dual characteristics of individual expression and collective creation. In our era of accelerated in- dividualization, people are expected to fashion a unique identity and image and by doing so actively construct their “selves”. (Limor Sh ifman, “Memes in Digital Culture,” 33) Shifman criticizes the idea of memes as viruses, emphasizing that the nature of meme communication is closer to the “ritual view of communication” proposed by James Carey. Carey calls the “ritual” model that defines communication not as the act of imparting information but as the construction and representation of shared beliefs. It highlights the sharedness of values, symbols, and cultural sensibilities that embody what people see as their communities. According to this view, the “message” in communication is not a unit whose reach and effect are easily traceable, but an ongoing process in which identities and senses of belonging are continually constructed. Memes serve not only as tools for individual expression but also as a way for people to engage with their community and form their

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: resonates both individually and collectively: the creators redefine “themselves” through the re-creation of the meme and construct a unique identity connected to the anime subculture, and the viewers feel a sense of belonging to the group through this familiar symbol. This cultural “ritual” reinforces the group’s identity, transforming the original animation scene into a “secret language” within the community. Comparing meme videos with traditional personal expression videos, such as vlogs, reveals that meme videos not only have a uniqueness in terms of their format and distribution but also demonstrate intricate cultural functions: vlog content provides a more passive viewing experience, whereas meme videos encourage viewers to engage in the interpretation and re-creation of meaning through their interactive and adaptive nature. However, such an expression is not without its drawbacks. meme videos, in shaping identity, can either make an individual’s self- expression more efficient and enriching, or they may simplify or even distort the deeper issues behind identity. To delve deeper into this topic, the next chapter will analyze specific meme videos to demonstrate how the virtual symbols within them can impact or even alter an individual’s understanding of identity.

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: Among the numerous meme videos, “cat meme” has become one of the most common visual symbols. They are diverse and adorable, but when this virtual symbol is combined with deeply controversial social themes, the emotional contrasts and identity conflicts are even more thought-provoking. In a cat meme video with a call girl (sex worker) as the main narrative subject, the creator shows the daily experience of call girls and stimulating viewers' discussions about marginalized occupations and social prejudices. Through this video, memes are not only an entertaining tool of expression but also a complex medium for constructing and interpreting identity. Combining Erving Goffman's theory of Dramaturgy with a representative cat meme video as a case study, this chapter will explore how virtual symbols shape and potentially distort the identity of the call girl through dramatic representation and the viewer's multiple perspectives in symbolic interpretation. video.1 The timeline on the right side shows the basic content of the

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: This video shows the professional day-to-day life of a call girl through cat memes and dialogue. The video describes her work experience in several scenes: the office where she receives a new appointment, the taxi ride to the client's location, and the hotel where she interacts with the client. In the hotel, she is confronted with various requests from the client, including flirtatious inquiries, being asked to do work beyond the range of services, and even mansplaining by the client. She responds by displaying a sense of humor and subservience, yet expressing irony and self-deprecation at the client's behavior through her dialogue. In addition, in private, she shows exhaustion, fear, and boredom, as in “It's finally over!” and “scared to death.” The whole video skillfully combines the “cute cat meme” with the social role of “sex worker”. Contextual visualization charts were developed based on Goffman’s dramaturgy theory to examine how virtual symbols influence and alter the call girl's identity in the meme video.

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: It will be convenient to label as ‘front’ that part of the individual’s performance which regularly functions in a general and fixed fashion to define the situation for those who observe the performance. Front, then, is the expressive equipment of a standard kind intentionally or unwittingly employed by the individual during his performance. ……it is to be noted that a given social front tends to become institutionalized in terms of the abstract stereotyped expectations to which it gives rise, and tends to take on a meaning and stability apart from the specific tasks which happen at the time name.1 to be performed in its Goffman defines “front” as the part of an individual’s performance that responds to the observer’s stereotypical expectations through the “equipment” of a standard. In the video, the call girl’s “front performance” reflects this concept. In front of her clients, she expresses herself as a servant role with a more adorable and cheerful cat meme, paired with polite, flirtatious and even humorous dialogue in response to their requests and queries. paragraph: Goffman points out that the dramatized reality constructed by the front is fragile and can collapse due to “destructive information.” He explains, “……if attention is drawn to them during the performance, it will discredit, disrupt, or make useless the impression that the performance fosters. ” (Erving Goffman, “The Presentation of Self in Everyday Life,” 147.) In the video, the “cute” and “submissive” image of the cat meme maintains the context of professional service between the call girl and the customer. If only I were a woman, I could make money doing such an easy job.

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: However, in the Mansplaining scene of the video, when cat meme suddenly breaks this situation by saying “go died” with an innocent and cute face (49 sec.), this “destructive message” instantly disintegrates the superficial harmony constructed in the performance and exposes the inequality of power in the society behind the story. Through this destructive message, the viewer gains a new understanding of the video’s situational definition: the apparent obedience and harmony are not real, but the result of distorted social power relations. The video thus transcends simple entertainment expressions and becomes a critical narrative. Although the “destructive message” breaks the consistency of the context, the overall style and outward appearance of the meme

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: This idealized representation often has a “conspiracy”, which makes some viewers believe in the character and intensifies the rejection from other viewers. Some commenters felt sympathy for the character, believing that she was forced to repeat labor in a poor environment and had difficulty escaping from her situation. Therefore, they left comments such as “I feel sorry for you” and “I admire you for working so hard.” Another part of commenters is critical of the “conspiracy plot,” believing that the call girl is using the image of cats to play the role of a pitiful person who does not deserve sympathy, and even reinforces negative stereotypes of professional identity. You’re making big bucks Just watching it is so stop whining and focus on the customer. exhausting, I seriously This isn’t some crappy admire you. minimum-wage job! ......

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: This disagreement suggests that the meme gives the call girl a higher level of social acceptance or self-satisfaction through idealization, but at the same time, it may also lead the viewer to doubt authenticity, thus ignoring issues such as the real social power inequality behind the video. In shaping and distorting the call girl’s identity, the virtual symbol has both the potential to empower freedom of expression and the risk of simplification and prejudice. This dual role both makes the virtual symbol a powerful tool for identity construction and exposes its limitations in the process of communication and cognition. In this context, the upcoming chapter will investigate how the virtual symbols and stories found in meme videos can be translated

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: While the previous chapters focused on how meme videos construct identity, this chapter explores how interactive media can reinterpret this process. Although meme videos rely on symbols and linear narratives to present identities to the viewer, games can embed these same symbols and narrative elements into the player's choices, conversations, and explorations, moving from passive viewing to active participation, granting the given player autonomy in shaping identity narratives. Fly Me to the Moon is a HD2D escape room RPG that combines AVG and free-exploration. HD2D is a visual style that mixes pixel art characters with 3D environments, creating a nostalgic and modern visual feeling. AVG is a narrative-driven game genre focused on dialogue, choices, and story rather than action elements. Players will take on the role of a psychiatrist who drops into the mental world of a patient with a paranoia disorder. In this closed fantasy space, players need to collect hints, solve puzzles and find a way to escape by conversing with objects and animals. In the exploration process, the player gradually reveals the patient's psychological state of indulging in memories and closing off the self.

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: The following content analyzes various visual assets in the game, including

paragraph: characters, environments, and symbolic objects

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: A closed island with a moon surface, floating in the cosmos. The patient indulges in the fantasy world of his childhood, the myths of ancient literature. He is unable to accept that he has grown up. It is static and closed compared to the changing real world.

paragraph: Retro Soviet Futurism AND Cold War space exploration. The patient imagines himself as

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: A Piece of graffiti, the first thing the player gets in space, is also the key to the space station.The graffiti is the first way for the patient to give meaning to the world. Play button: automatic playback of memories; Incomplete “b”: an unfinished childhood; The man with open arms: the patient’s childhood as a positive gesture; Theta : unknowns, the patient’s inability to reconcile with time and self.

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: A reflection in the lake, is lifted by the player with a fishing rod. It symbolizes both the hope of escape and the futility of hope, and the irony of “catching the rocket” emphasizes that the world is nothing more than an illusion. In terms of structure, the game tends to be free-exploration, keeping with the fragmented narrative style of meme videos.As the player explores the world, they are encouraged to collect bits and pieces of clues, interact with other characters, and gradually learn the truth about the virtual world from the experience. In the early stages of the game, the player instinctively seeks escape. However, only after completing a puzzle does the player see their own face in the frame, representing a return to rational “outsider” identity, and the player finally has the opportunity to obtain the “rocket's reflection” (It represents both the hope of escape and the futility of hope). This shift from confusion to acceptance mirrors how the meme video combines fragmented symbols to reveal a more complete identity. Although the game's presentation is very different from the meme video, they are similar at their core: the operation of virtual symbols, fragmented narratives, and the establishment of new identities and secret languages. The game did not offer a clear program on how to treat the patient, but simply allowed the player to move repeatedly through the virtual and real worlds. As in the meme video, the boundaries between reality and self-perception are never fixed, but are always being acted out, guided and reinterpreted.

author: Tongyu Xiang
thesis_title: Virtual Echoes: Meme Videos as Practice for Social Interaction and Identity Construction
paragraph: In the age of digital culture, meme videos have become a popular medium in the online world. Why do people choose these seemingly absurd and humorous forms to convey their emotions and experiences? This thesis suggests that meme video is a form of self-presentation that combines symbolic language with personal expression, allowing creators to selectively exaggerate or hide themselves to seek understanding and recognition. The construction of digital identities is not fixed but instead created and reproduced in a fragmented, symbolic way in a constantly changing communication environment. Meme videos provide creators with a recognizable, transmittable, and re-created symbolic system that allows complex emotions and personal experiences to be simplified and shared. Especially for marginalized groups (like sex workers), this type of media provides a form of expression that is both protective and expressive, which on the one hand, enhances the possibility of social understanding, but on the other hand, may also lead to questions and misunderstandings about their authenticity. The practical part of the thesis, the interactive narrative game Fly Me to the Moon, draws on the meme video’s structure and presentation mechanism, transforming the player from a passive viewer to an active participant. Through exploration and decoding, players can personally engage in the narrative of identities and experience their true selves hidden behind virtual symbols. This process realizes the transformation from “viewing” to “interpreting”, and verifies that game design as a research method can provide new interactive perspectives on how identities are constructed, misinterpreted, and perceived. Overall, meme video plays a dual role in contemporary digital culture: it is both a medium for individual expression and a collectively shared cultural symbol.
