The size and material of the black book not only simulate the screen on a physical level but also construct a symbolic metaphor: it is a “closed screen”. Even if there is no obvious electronic information, it still exists as a concrete physical entity, carrying and transmitting information. The screen is not just a container for content, it is part of the information itself. Whether it is a black book or a closed screen, they both indicate that the existence of information is not limited to the explicit content, but permeates the materiality of the medium. When the screen is lit, we focus on the image it presents; when it is turned off, we will truly realize its status as a physical object. Turning pages, touching, and viewing the black book are already part of the flow of information. It makes “turning off the screen” no longer mean the absence of information, but another form of information. Photos of the flipping process When the book slowly turns from the first page to the last page, and then from the last page back to the first page, this experience is extremely specific and clear in reality. Every time you turn a page, the edge of the paper will slide over and bring a slight resistance and touch. As the pages turn, the pages on the left-hand side gradually accumulate from less to more, and the weight slowly increases in the hand. On the other hand, the pages held in the right hand change from thick to thin, until only a thin page is left. This process becomes three-dimensional and concrete. This is not only the unfolding of a physical action, but also an experience of the fluidity of time, matter, and perception. When I used my phone to shoot this page-turning process, I converted the physical movement of the pages into 80 static images. The originally coherent and vivid tactile experience was broken, and the sliding, accumulation, and thickness change of each page were cut into single-frame image fragments. The fluidity of page turning is compressed in the image, and the traces of time are folded into a series of static pictures. When these images are re-watched, the process of turning the book is no longer obvious. Between pages 5 to 35, and between pages 45 to 75, it is difficult to see the difference without careful observation. This transformation reveals a phenomenon - when the real world is compressed into electronic media, many textures that were originally clear and perceptible will disappear in the process. The level of detail will be reduced, and the immediacy of touch will be delayed or erased. However, this loss is not just a loss, it also brings new information: when the fluidity of reality is rewoven into static fragments, our way of viewing also changes. This slow and subtle change forces us to pay more attention when watching and re-perceiving those details that are easily overlooked in the fast-paced information flow. This process shows that the threshold is a process of change that is difficult to detect, and more attention and thinking about the whole process can be perceived. When the action of turning pages is solidified into an image, each image is not only an archive of a moment in the past but also a slice of time that needs to be reinterpreted. The texture of the book is smooth, and new information appears. The information on the screen is not just a reproduction of reality, but a transformed reality. It is both a compression of real experience and a reshaping of perception. When we focus on the process itself, it is like putting a spotlight on the action of “flipping the switch”, making us more aware of the changes and impacts brought about by this transformation. Thereby stimulating a deeper awareness of how information is transmitted, perceived, and transformed. [Fig.37]-[Fig.76] The flipping process of the slide projector Simple image changes are often difficult to perceive intuitively, especially when the changes are slow and the differences are small; they are easily ignored or regarded as static. However, through the old-fashioned slide projector, these photos are given a new texture, making the originally imperceptible change process more physically present. The working method of the projector is particularly critical in this process; the image gradually emerges under the action of light, and when the photos are switched, the short one or two seconds of blankness create intermittent breaks, and the “click” mechanical sound of the slide when the slide is switched, emphasizes this sense of rhythm. Light penetrates the image printed on the film and projects the picture on the surface of the blank black page. However, these images are not simply reproductions - they have already produced tiny pixel particles during the processing of the printer, and when the projector enlarges these images, these particles are further enlarged, making the images appear rough and grainy. The black pages are no longer just a surface for projection, but a redefined medium that absorbs light and displays information in the form of projection. The blank black book is fixed on the mobile phone holder and can no longer be turned, but the image of the projector “turns” the pages in another way. Here, the action of turning the pages has been transferred - originally physical, now optical, the physical turning process is transferred from the book to the projector. Although the physical pages of the book are still, the switching process of the projector is constantly simulating the dynamics of turning the pages, which makes the whole device like a switch that is “turned on”. This process is more like a switch being turned to the on side, the screen lights up. Although the pages cannot be turned, the projector is turning them one by one. The projection not only falls on the blank black pages of the book but also extends to the wall behind the book. Compared with the image on the page, the projection on the wall becomes more blurred and diffused, while the book’s occlusion leaves part of the light on the surface of the book, casting a blank shadow on the wall. This contrast forms a metaphor: when the image is clearly presented on the page, our attention is naturally focused on it, while the blurred image on the wall and the blank area not covered by the projection are often ignored. This phenomenon reflects our attention pattern when watching the screen - people tend to over-focus on the content on the screen and ignore the real environment outside the screen. At the same time, the information on the screen does not mean a complete panorama, it is just a certain cut or selection of reality, and those parts that are not illuminated by the projection - the blank on the wall, symbolize the world we have not noticed. This projection process reminds us that under the occupation of digital information, our perceptual habits are being reshaped, and those “blanks” that are blocked by the screen and not in the field of vision are also part of the information and deserve to be re-recognized. Black book with contents I used my phone to shoot the projection process again and made the newly generated 80 photos into the second book. This process means that reality has undergone another translation and turned into a new form. In this shoot, the photos no longer record the flipping of physical books, but the images of page flipping projected on black pages and walls - the projected images themselves become new objects of capture. This translation is not only a reproduction, but also a transformation. Each layer of media intervention will change the way information is presented. I made these 80 new photos into a second book in the form of “black printed on black”, and disassembled the whole process into four parts: in front of the screen, on the screen, behind the screen, and off the screen. Through this disassembly, I tried to shift the viewer’s attention from the surface of the screen to the overall environment in front and behind the screen. I chose the method of “black printed on black” precisely to contrast with the excessive clarity and direct readability of the screen content, making the reading process slower and more concrete. Readers need to actively adjust the angle and light to recognize the text and images. The texture of paper and ink is magnified in this process. The acquisition of information is no longer instantaneous, but becomes a perceptual behavior that requires time and patience. This “uncertainty of readability” contrasts with the high contrast and instant feedback of the screen, making us rethink the visibility of information, the way media intervenes, and the perceptual experience of reading in different media. [Fig.120]-[Fig.122] Coda The unlit screen and the blank pages are not really blank, but my metaphor for the material world - it symbolizes the process of slowing down and carefully interpreting. Absence and blankness here do not mean “nothing”, but an open space, which not only becomes the carrier of projection, but also becomes the content itself, accommodating the projection of the real world, and at the same time carrying the viewer’s interpretation and imagination of the image. This blankness does not point to a final answer, but a continuously generated problem. It represents a temporary loss of focus, and also means a possibility of a more comprehensive understanding of the complexity of the world. Faced with a multi-layered reality, we ◾ should realize that the complex world cannot be simply summarized, and there is no single universal solution. In my project, I regard the whole process as a metaphorical study of the nature of the screen. I do not try to draw a clear conclusion, but through the process of “from book to photo, then to projection, and finally back to book”, I show the complexity of the screen as a threshold. The screen is not only a display device, but also a process of continuous transformation - its meaning can be constantly rewritten and reshaped. Through the transformation of media such as page turning, projection, shooting, printing, etc., the content flows between different forms. In this process, it is compressed, distorted, expanded, and ◾ enriched. Are we expanding our experience of reality through the screen, or are we compressing reality into the frame of the digital screen? If the answer is somewhere in between - in the gray area of the threshold - then what information is filtered and what content is added in this process? Is the screen reshaping our understanding of authenticity? How should we respond to such a reality? Is this process of compression and expansion not only ◾ a limitation, but also a lens to reveal the complexity of reality? For my thesis question, I did not try to give a clear answer, but was more concerned with how it all happened. In a way, I even refused to give an answer. The whole process became my proof of concept. I paid more attention to the process of flipping the switch, the threshold moment, than the ending. The complexity of the screen as a media tool makes it impossible for me to simply criticize its drawbacks or praise the convenience it brings. I feel that when I simply draw a conclusion, it is a denial of some potential possibility. The pursuit of simplified answers is also the domestication of us by the screen society. What I pursue is to build a broader and more detailed perspective to understand the screen from different angles. When we look at the overall process with enough attention, can we promote more discussion and new understanding? In the process of continuous flow and transformation of information, how do we find a balance between compression and expansion? Perhaps the key is not what the screen gives us, but how we actively watch, think, and define the meaning of this threshold.

author: Yazhe Gigi Luan
thesis_title: Threshold: The Chronology of Screens
paragraph: When I wrote the conclusion, I felt that although my thesis was coming to an end at this moment, I also felt something new was growing out of it. I don’t know what it is, but I guess I am about to move on. When I look at my life from a longer perspective, perhaps this moment is the starting point of a new journey. To be honest, I am not sure whether I still agree with or am interested in some part of my thesis, but it is always better to say it out and write it down. Even if I want to move on, it is not a bad thing to know how I came here or why I changed. This book can also be proof of what I am thinking at ◾ this moment in 2024 and 2025. I suspect that my own thesis and works will step into or make people feel the limitations of relativism, but I guess what I want to present is not that everything is fine; the vicious circle of everything or everyone having their own reasons, but that we are still trying to find a consensus, an answer, but I think this answer should not be too easy to find or say. Because the world itself is complex, people are complex, and materials are complex, everything is destined to be multifaceted, and it is difficult to easily conclude. Therefore, we should pay more attention to this complexity, try to consider more widely the aspects that have not been explored and known by us, rather than ignore the minor aspects, so as to try to come up with a more loving and inclusive answer. I know this is not a simple process. Even if this is the theme of my thesis, in my daily life, I always can’t help but eagerly try to find the answer to a certain problem. But usually, this eagerness will only make everything worse, deform my actions, and make me ignore my original intention. When I focus on the present, focus on the process of development, and do every step well, I will find that the answer emerges inadvertently. From this perspective, whether it is seeing a psychologist or writing a thesis, everything that happened this year, I tried to analyze my emotions, feelings, and thoughts in my mind. When I try to treat all this as a process rather than an end, I seem to be able to see the dynamics of my life more clearly, and those things I think are precious. Although my thesis is about change, it also makes me more firm and solid. In the end, thank you to my best, best, best instructors—Michael Dyer, Gerardo Ismael Madera, Tyler Paige, and Scott Vander Zee. I always feel so ◾ lucky to be in your classes. I wouldn’t be who I am today without you guys. Also, thank you to all my friends for your support, love, and late-night ◾ studio hangs. I’m so proud of us. Love you all—Ting, Catherine, Shamika, Yazz, Frieja, Roshni... Sorry, I can’t list everyone! To my family, thank you for your financial support. Mom, Dad, Grandma, and Grandpa—without you, I wouldn’t even be standing here. Thank you for supporting me in doing ◾ whatever I love, even if you don’t fully understand what I’m chasing. Last but not least, thanks to myself—Yazhe Gigi. Proud of you for the whole ◾ year’s work, and keep pushing yourself and never feel satisfied (for better or worse). Now, let’s get started on the new journey..

author: Yifan Xu
thesis_title: Monster-making As a Mirror: Reflecting Ourselves and The Future
paragraph: Ever since I was a child, monsters have fascinated me. They seemed to be more than just petrifiing creatures; they had stories to tell. These mythical beings, strange and often terrifying, hinted at something deeper, something intriguing. The more I learned about them, the more I could see monsters in daily life and in the corners of my dreams. This growing fascination made me wonder about the true meaning of monsters. I couldn’t help but to ask: What do monsters mean to us? I realized that creating monsters, or thinking about them, doesn'tjustpertaintonightmaresforchildren. Rather, it's a way to dive deep into our own thoughts and subconscious. Every monster imagined became a path to understand people’s minds, helping them comprehend their fears and curiosities. In a way, monsters became a key to unlocking parts of ourselves we didn't know existed. As technology evolves, so does our relationship with it. As we create new technologies, technology also reshapes patterns in our thinking and behaviors. This fluid interaction is highlighted by Sarah Hendren's assertion, "All technology is assistive technology" (Hendren, 5). The article underscores how technology not only enhances human capabilities but also challenges us to reevaluate conventional views of normality and disability. In this era of rapid technological change, we grapple daily with the uncertainties of the future and the brisk pace of technological advancements. New concerns arise around biotechnology, genetic engineering, control issues of AI, and ecological damage, yet we don't know how to deal with this anxiety of technology in the Anthropocene, where humans have become a dominant force shaping the Earth’s systems and processes. Monsters are manifestations of our fears. The human action of creating monsters has been historically significant throughout the Anthropocene. In this context, the Anthropocene can be understood as the geologic time where human civilization developed and flourished. For Humans, monsters are an exploration of our own existence. For nature, monsters represent the irreversible ecological crisis caused by human impact. For art and design, monsters are a rebellion that challenges prevailing cultural narratives and disrupts hierarchies. As society and technology progress, so too do the monsters we create in our collective psyche. These future monsters, born from current anxieties and uncertainties, serve as cautionary tales, reminding us of the consequences of unfettered progress and the responsibilities of wielding immense power. Visual communication designers can create functional fiction through future monsters that help us explore what technological world we wish to live in in order to better accommodate ourselves into this unfamiliar world. Future technological advancements may blur human distinctions, prompting ethical, AI, and ecosystem impact questions. This interdisciplinary approach deepens our understanding of the human experience and shared responsibility for addressing environmental damage, decolonizing design, and emerging technology ethics. Studying monsters uncovers societal fears and desires, providing insights for adapting to our complex world and upcoming technological frontiers. These monsters serve as mirrors, reflecting the possibilities of what might be and begging the question: Is this the world we are thriving for?

author: Yifan Xu
thesis_title: Monster-making As a Mirror: Reflecting Ourselves and The Future
paragraph: In this section, a taxonomy of monsters is provided in order to illuminate monstrous characteristics and their metaphoric meanings by analyzing adjectives used to describe monsters historically. The taxonomy of monsters and the analysis of the adjectives used to describe them can offer profound insights into cultural fears, values, and the embodiment of the "other" or the unknown in various societies. This section explores different categories of monsters, looking into their appearances and the metaphoric meanings attached to them through historical descriptions Thus, the audiences could gain an understanding of how languages are related to various monstrous features. 1. Dread: Feared greatly; hence, to be feared; dreadful, terrible. (Oxford English Dictionary) Gorgons The Gorgons, as described by Hesiod, embody the archetypal fear of a cursed visage, compelling in its terror. Medusa, the quintessential Gorgon, is often depicted with a writhing mass of serpents for hair, her face a paragon of petrifying horror. Hesiod's "Theogony" is one of the earliest sources that mention Medusa. In "Theogony," Hesiod describes Medusa as one of the three Gorgons. Unlike her immortal sisters, Medusa was mortal. Hesiod's description is more genealogical and doesn't delve deeply into her appearance or the narrative of her demise(Hesiod). Later In Ovid’s Metamorphoses, the word “dread” was used to describe the appearances of Medusa in Perseus's quest to Gorgons: “Nevertheless, he looked at the dread form of Medusa reflected in a circular shield of polished bronze that he carried on his left arm.”(Ovid) The transformation of Medusa from a beautiful lady into a snake-haired monster was also depicted in Metamorphoses: "She was once most beautiful... Of all her beauties, none was more admired than her hair... They say that Neptune... violated her in the temple of Minerva... So that it might not go unpunished, she changed the Gorgon’s hair to foul snakes"(Ovid). The transformation from a beautiful maiden into a monstrous figure with snake-infested hair, such representation serves as an amalgamation of natural aversions, primarily ophidiophobia—the fear of snakes—and the deep-seated human fear of being rendered powerless, frozen in place forever. The poet Ann Stanford, as noted by scholar Alicia Ostriker, interprets Medusa's story as encapsulating the psychological effects of her rape. Medusa is seen as being 'imprisoned' in self-dividing anger and a will to revenge, from which she cannot escape, despite yearning to do so(Ostriker, 77). Contrasting with her common portrayal as a monstrous being, Medusa can also be perceived as a “tragic figure”, driven by a desire for vengeance. This perspective shifts the focus from her monstrosity to the tragic elements of her story. antefix with head of Gorgona. Fasos island, 4 c. BC. Pushkin museum

Uffizi Gallery, Florence. 2. Prodigious - Of great size, extent, amount, etc.; enormous, immense; extreme; prolific. (Oxford English Dictionary) Cyclopes The Cyclopes, as characterized in "The Odyssey," represent a colossal force of nature, unrestrained by the laws of men or gods. “"We then saw a monster, the last of the sons of Earth, a prodigious giant. His single eye in the middle of his forehead shone like the sun. He was taller than a tree" (Homer, Book IX). Their singular eye may symbolize a myopic view of the world, lacking depth and understanding. In Homer’s description, these Cyclopes lead a primitive existence, lacking societal structure and agricultural knowledge, which presents them as uncivilized and barbaric. This depiction is more aligned with primal strength and a lawless, solitary lifestyle, emphasizing their monstrous and fearsome nature. Another interpretation, as explored in an article from The Symbolic World, suggests the Cyclopes represent a mental departure from the norm, embodying the idea of a creature fixated on a singular perspective or idea. This symbolic representation ties into the concept of the Cyclops being out of touch with reality, focusing solely on intellectual or abstract concepts to the detriment of practical considerations. This idea is exemplified in the tale of Thales, the pre-Socratic sage, who is likened to a Cyclops for his lofty but detached intellectual pursuits (Rhomer). A first century AD head of a Cyclops from the Roman Colosseum

author: Yifan Xu
thesis_title: Monster-making As a Mirror: Reflecting Ourselves and The Future
paragraph: 3. Hybrid - Produced by the inter-breeding of two different species or varieties of animals or plants; mongrel, cross-bred, half-bred (Oxford English Dictionary). Minotaur The Minotauros (Minotaur) was a bull-headed monster born to Queen Pasiphae of Krete after she coupled with a bull. The creature resided in the twisting maze of the labyrinth where it was offered a regular sacrifice of youths and maidens to satisfy its cannibalistic hunger. The beast was eventually slain by the hero Theseus. Minotauros, as Euripides says, was ‘A mingled form and hybrid birth of monstrous shape’, and that ‘Two different natures, man and bull, were joined in him’. The Minotaur's chimeric form, with the head of a bull and the body of a man, as described by Plutarch, visually symbolizes a duality of brute force and human intellect, entwined in a discordant amalgamation. This monstrous figure suggests a perversion of natural order, a corporeal manifestation of the inner beast that lurks beneath the veneer of civilization (Plutarch, Life of Theseus). According to Ovid’s record in Metamorphoses, Minotaur was also seen as a stigma as a hybrid creature of the queen’s affair with an animal. “His [Minos'] dynasty's disgrace has grown; the monstrous beast [Minotauros] hybrid beast declared the queen's obscene adultery.” (Ovid, Metamorphoses 8. 130 ff) Statue of the Minotaur. Pentelic marble. Found in Athens, near St. Demetrios Katephoris in Plaka. Roman copy of an early Classical statue by Myron. Accession Number: 1664. National Archaeological Museum of Athens. Athens, Greece. Pasiphaë and the Minotaur, Attic red-figure kylix found at Etruscan Vulci in Italy. Now exhibited at Cabinet des Médailles, Paris

paragraph: 4. Saturnine - In regard to a person's temperament, mood, or manner: gloomy, melancholy, dejected, downcast, grim; not easily enlivened, enthused, or cheered; (in early use) ill-tempered, angry. (Oxford English Dictionary) Vampire The vampire, popularized by Bram Stoker, is often visualized as a creature of aristocratic bearing, with an insidious charm. Dracula was one of the most popular vampires in the history of literature. Dracula's skin is often described as being unusually pale. This pallor is a notable characteristic that adds to his ghostly appearance. “Somehow his words and his look did not seem to accord, or else it was that his cast of face made his smile look malignant and saturnine ” (Bram Stoker, Chapter2). Similar to Dracula, most vampires share a melancholy and moody atmosphere, accompanied with highly humanized lookings, making them both terrifying and charming. The vampire's immortality and thirst for blood mirror human anxieties about death and the parasitic nature of some human relationships, with the vampire's bite representing an irreversible corruption or transformation The Vampire, by Philip Burne-Jones, 1897. author: Yifan Xu
thesis_title: Monster-making As a Mirror: Reflecting Ourselves and The Future
paragraph: 5. Shape-shifting -To undergo a transformation in appearance or form, esp. through the exercise of supernatural or magical powers. Also more generally: to undergo change; to alter or transform (Oxford English Dictionary). Kitsune The Kitsune, a central figure in Japanese mythology, is depicted as a multifaceted and supernatural fox-like creature. According to various historical and literary sources, Kitsune are portrayed as magical, multi-tailed foxes capable of shapeshifting into humans, possessing people, and performing various other supernatural acts. They are distinguished from ordinary foxes by the number of their tails; the more tails a Kitsune has, the older and more powerful it is. These creatures are also known for their cunning, intelligence, and magical abilities. In Japanese folklore, Kitsune are divided into two types: Zenko (good foxes), who are benevolent servants of the kami Inari, and Yako (field foxes), which are weaker, self-serving, and mischievous or even evil (Zhelyazkov). This duality in their nature reflects the complexity of the Kitsune as both protectors and tricksters. The Kitsune are symbolically associated with intelligence, cunning, trickery, and sometimes, malice. They can be seen as both guardian spirits and malevolent entities, depending on their actions and whom they serve or help(Japanese legends, dates vary but tales have ancient origins). A nine-tailed fox spirit (kyūbi no kitsune) scaring Prince Hanzoku; print by Utagawa Kuniyoshi, Edo period, 19th century

author: Yifan Xu
thesis_title: Monster-making As a Mirror: Reflecting Ourselves and The Future
paragraph: The perception and role of monsters in human culture has evolved significantly over time, influenced by changes in society, technology, and media. In the classical era, monsters were deeply rooted in mythology and folklore, serving as symbols of fear, the unknown, and moral lessons. As time progressed, the depiction and role of monsters shifted, particularly with the advancement of science and technology. For instance, Mary Shelley's "Frankenstein," represents a pivotal moment in the evolution of the monster archetype, blending elements of science and horror. The novel raises questions about the ethics of scientific advancement and the consequences of playing God, portraying the monster as a product of scientific experimentation gone awry. This shift reflects a broader cultural engagement with the implications of scientific progress and its potential to create new forms of monstrosity . In children's literature, the representation of monsters has also evolved, showing a range from frightening to friendly characters. Stories like Maurice Sendak's "Where the Wild Things Are" present monsters as part of a child's imagination and adventure, offering a way to explore fear. In contrast, other works use monsters as metaphors for real-life issues, such as the messiness depicted in Chae Strathie’s "Jumblebum." These literary monsters can serve as tools for children to confront and understand their fears and anxieties, fostering emotional and imaginative development . In contemporary culture, the concept of monsters has increasingly been influenced by consumerism and the commercialization of media. Modern monsters are not only shaped by societal fears and technological advancements but also by their commercial viability and appeal in popular culture. The commercialization of monsters can be seen in the way they are used in movies, television shows, video games, and other media. These monsters are often designed to appeal to a wide audience, making them more marketable and profitable. This commercial aspect influences not only the appearance and characteristics of these monsters but also the narratives they are a part of. This commercialization of monsters also reflects a broader trend in society where traditional folklore and mythology are adapted for contemporary audiences, often losing some of their original meanings and gaining new ones that resonate with modern consumers. The transformation of these creatures into commodities is indicative of the changing landscape of media and consumer culture, where the line between cultural symbols and marketable products is increasingly blurred.

author: Yifan Xu
thesis_title: Monster-making As a Mirror: Reflecting Ourselves and The Future
paragraph: Inanagewheretheboundariesbetweenthemythicalandthetechnologicalblur,thequestionof whatconstitutesamonsterbecomesevermorecomplex.Domonstershavespines?Legs?If so,howmany?Thesequestionsmightseemtrivialatfirstglance,buttheytapintoadeeper curiosityaboutthenatureofmonstersandtheirplaceinourimaginationandreality.Arethese creaturesfeared,desired,orperhapsmisunderstood?Anddothey,intheirownrealmof existence,recognizethemselvesasthe'monsters'webelievethemtobe?Theanswersarenot clear,suggestingthattheessenceofmonstrosityresidesasmuchwithinusasinthecreatures ofourimagination.Thisambiguitypointstoaprofoundconnectionbetweenourperceptionof monstersandthewayweseeourselvesandourrapidlyevolvingworld,particularlyinthe contextofemergingtechnologies. Toexplorethisconnection,Iembarkedonaseriesofinterviewswithindividualsdeeplyengaged intherealmsofemergingtechnologies.Theseconversationsaimedtounraveltheintricate relationshipbetweenourlongstandingmythsofmonstersandthemodern-daytitansof technology.Intotal,Iinterviewed4individuals:Damien,Shuli,Shawn,andLydia.Allofthemare currentlystudyingatPrattandhaveparticularinterestsandfearsofAI. Thisinvestigationisnotmerelyaboutidentifyingtheparallelsbetweenancientfearsand contemporaryanxietiesbutratheraboutunderstandinghowtechnologicaladvancementshave fundamentallytransformedourconceptionofwhatmonstersare.Bydelvingintopersonal reflectionsandstories,thisresearchseekstoshedlightonthebroaderculturalandexistential dilemmaswefaceattheintersectionofmythology,technology,andhumancreativity.Through thislens,webegintoseehowthedigitalagehasnotonlyredefinedourmonstersbuthasalso offerednewinsightsintoourcollectivepsyche,illuminatingthecomplexinterplaybetweenfear, desire,andtheunknownforindividualsinthe21stcentury. Tothoroughlyexaminetheintricaterelationshipbetweentheconceptsofmonstersand personalinterpretationsofmonstersfromindividuals,Idrewupondetaileddefinitionsfromthe OxfordEnglishDictionary(OED)alongsideinsightsfromaseriesofinterviewsconductedwith studentsatPrattInstitute.Thiscomprehensiveapproachaimstouncoverhowtheseconcepts intersectandinfluenceourperceptionoftheworldintheeraofrapidtechnological advancement. Monsters: An Evolving Concept The OED traces the definition of "monster" back to 1375, initially describing mythical creatures that are part animal, part human, or a combination of animal forms, often of great size and ferocious appearance. Over time, the term expanded to include anything extraordinary or unnatural, such as a prodigy or marvel (1384-1710), and later, to refer to malformed animals or plants, and even individuals with significant congenital malformations (from around 1400). By the 15th century, monsters were also defined as creatures of huge size, and by the early 16th century, the term evolved to describe individuals exhibiting extreme cruelty or wickedness, appearing inhuman. This historical progression highlights how the concept of monsters has broadened from mythical beings to encompass a wide range of unnatural or extraordinary phenomena. Damien's portrayal of monsters as beings striving for acceptance yet shunned reflects our own struggles with the alienating effects of technology. Shuli's and Lydia's descriptions of monsters—ranging from the grotesquely physical to the deeply psychological—underscore the multifaceted nature of our fears, where technology both fascinates and terrifies. Shawn's recollection of monsters from childhood stories defeating heroes mirrors our apprehensions about being overpowered by our own creations. Technology: A Catalyst for Change The definition of "technology" according to the OED has also evolved significantly over time. Initially, in 1612, it referred to a discourse or treatise on an art or arts, especially concerning practical arts or crafts. By 1787, the term shifted to denote the branch of knowledge dealing with the mechanical arts and applied sciences. In contemporary times, technology encompasses not only the practical application of scientific knowledge but also the digital revolution that has transformed every aspect of society. This evolution reflects the expanding role of technology from practical tools to a fundamental force shaping human experience and interaction. The interviewees' reflections on technology reveal a nuanced perspective. Damien associates technology with elements both innovative and ominous, hinting at the dual potential for advancement and catastrophe. Shuli and Lydia express ambivalence, recognizing technology's utility while fearing its potential to dehumanize or lead to unforeseen consequences. Shaun's concerns about rapid, uncontrollable growth and the loss of what is cherished echo a widespread unease about technology's impact on society and the individual. Anxiety: The Shadow of Progress Anxiety, as defined by the OED, ranges from a worry over the future or about something with an uncertain outcome to a pathological state characterized by excessive apprehension or fear. This broad definition encapsulates the multifaceted nature of anxiety, from everyday worries to clinically significant conditions. The digital age, with its rapid technological advancements and associated societal changes, has introduced new sources of anxiety, reflecting fears about the unknown and the potential consequences of unchecked technological growth. The discussions on anxiety highlight how technology can exacerbate fears of the unknown. Shuli's mention of nuclear weapons and mass hysteria, Lydia's concern over AI usurping human uniqueness, and Shaun's feeling of being overwhelmed by the pace of development reflect a collective apprehension about the potential dangers that technological progress could unleash. Dreams: Windows to the Subconscious The concept of "dreams" encompasses a series of images, thoughts, and emotions generated by mental activity during sleep, with historical definitions highlighting their story-like quality and potential prophetic or supernatural nature. Dreams have been regarded as both reflections of inner desires and fears and as mediums for supernatural visions. In the context of this essay, dreams are explored as expressions of the subconscious, particularly in relation to the themes of monsters, technology, and anxiety, revealing the deep psychological impact of these concepts on individuals. Dreams serve as a window into the subconscious, revealing the internalization of societal anxieties related to technology. Damien's fear of sea creatures, likely a metaphor for the unknown depths of technological advancement, and Lydia's and Shawn's imagined monsters, demonstrate how technology and its potential repercussions occupy a significant space in our psyche, shaping our fears and aspirations even in our most private moments. Insights The interviews conducted with Pratt Institute students offer contemporary perspectives on these definitions. Participants shared their views on monsters as entities that challenge our understanding of the natural and the normal, reflecting societal fears and anxieties, especially in relation to technological advancements. Their insights into technology highlighted both its potential for innovation and its capacity to evoke fear and anxiety, particularly regarding the future of human society. Discussions of anxiety revealed a complex relationship with technology, where the rapid pace of change and the unknown implications of digital advancements contribute to a sense of unease. Finally, reflections on dreams serve to underscore the deeply personal ways in which individuals process their fears, desires, and the influence of technology on their subconscious. By weaving together historical definitions and contemporary insights, this essay explores the dynamic interplay between monsters, technology, anxiety, and dreams in the digital age. It reveals how these concepts, while distinct, are interconnected, reflecting and shaping our collective and individual responses to the challenges and opportunities presented by technological advancement. This analysis not only deepens our understanding of each concept but also illuminates the broader cultural and psychological landscape of the 21st century, marked by rapid change and the perpetual negotiation between progress and the primal fears that lurk within the human psyche.

author: Yifan Xu
thesis_title: Monster-making As a Mirror: Reflecting Ourselves and The Future
paragraph: In order to gain a better understanding of the relationship between art and monster, I looked into pre-existing artworks in history that embody monsters as themes or subjects in them. “The Garden of Earthly Delights”, a religious triptych created in the late 15th or early 16th century by Hieronymus Bosch, caught my attention. Viewed by today’s audiences, although painted hundreds of years ago, it maintains a contemporary resonance. "The Garden of Earthly Delights" is celebrated for its detailed and imaginative depiction of fantastical scenes that blend elements of paradise, life's pleasures, and hellish horrors. The painting features a rich tapestry of fantastical creatures and scenes, showcasing Bosch’s creativity and reflecting the complex symbolism. The chaotic and surrealistic style makes the painting an “Ai looking artwork”, which demonstrates boundless diversity and strong adaptability in style and can sometimes exhibit inconsistencies in style, showcasing a unique, cross-genre blend of creative expression. The monsters in this triptych are integral to Bosch's narrative, serving as physical representations of human fears, desires, and moral failings, and possibly reflecting on the chaotic nature of the world and the mysterious aspects of creation. The leftmost panel, depicting the Garden of Eden, has the fewest monstrous creatures, symbolizing divine creation in its purest form. Bosch's imagination is evident in the creation of hybrid creatures that hint at deeper complexities. The central panel, showcasing a celebration of life, reaches the zenith of Bosch's inventiveness with a profusion of bizarre, hybrid creatures amid human figures, suggesting a blurring of natural and supernatural lines, and the intertwining of innocence and sin. In contrast, the right panel’s dystopian landscape of Hell features sinister monsters, embodying fears of the unknown and the consequences of sin, reflecting medieval conceptions of afterlife torments. These monsters contrast with the human figures, perhaps to highlight human frailty and sinfulness. They serve as mirrors to human vice, embodying excess, gluttony, lust, and violence, and illustrating the consequences of humanity's fall from grace. Bosch’s style, characterized by striking, fantastical imagery and complex symbolism, offered a critical perspective on societal and religious norms. Professor Peter Plum interprets "The Garden of Earthly Delights" as an allegory of The Last Judgment rather than a celebration of worldly pleasures offers a thought-provoking perspective on how art can reflect contemporary societal concerns. In his essay, Plum suggests that Bosch's work is a mirror to the eschatological fears of his era, a period rife with religious scrutiny and apocalyptic anticipation (Glum, 54). Drawing parallels between Bosch's allegorical representations and modern-day attitudes towards technology and the future, it's evident that similar eschatological concerns persist. Through my analysis of interview materials, it becomes apparent that people today—whether optimistic or pessimistic—harbor their own versions of apocalyptic anxiety. This sentiment echoes the cautionary tales depicted in Bosch's triptych, suggesting that while the nature of these fears may have evolved, the underlying human tendency to contemplate the end of times remains unchanged. “The painting is a mirror image of us” (Bosch, the Garden of Dreams). “The Garden of Earthly Delights” gives the viewer a dreamy landscape by portraying a strange upside down world where all creatures - humans, beasts, and monsters - work in harmony. When considering the intersection of AI and art, AI can be seen as an extension of human creativity, capable of visualizing personalized monsters that reflect contemporary societal fears and anxieties. Drawing from human knowledge, experiences, and emotions, AI could create a new array of monsters, each serving as a digital representation of our current anxieties. Bosch’s painting The Garden of Earthly Delights provides generative AI a pre-existing context to capture those generated monsters into one scenario, adding a contemporary layer to this famous painting. This process might reveal new patterns or associations by putting all those personalized monsters into one world, offering insights into our collective human experience in this speeding technological society.

author: Yifan Xu
thesis_title: Monster-making As a Mirror: Reflecting Ourselves and The Future
paragraph: Artificial intelligence is a frontier technology that is gradually penetrating our lives. Compared to some other technologies, AI is more akin to an entity comparable to human beings. It talks like us, behaves like us, acts like us, but it is not us. It is a newly born monster, powerful yet emotionless, filled with both fear and hope. It serves as a mirror, reflecting our collective psyche, beneath its unassuming surface, the deep currents of our personal fears, anxieties, and whispered narratives gently undulate, each ripple echoing a story untold, a dream unshared. In order to use AI to translate our personal narratives into monsters, I used 3 different AI tools to create visual, sound and movement for those monsters in the visualization part. Midjourney, Runway, and MyEdit are platforms that use advanced AI algorithms to create unique works in their respective fields. Midjourney focuses on converting text descriptions into images, employing sophisticated neural networks to interpret and visualize ideas. This process involves learning from extensive datasets to produce detailed and engaging visual content. Runway, on the other hand, specializes in video creation and editing. It uses AI trained on a wide array of video data, enabling it to perform tasks like style transfer and object detection. This technology allows users to easily apply complex effects and edits to their videos, enhancing their quality and appeal. MyEdit is geared towards the creation of sound effects. It likely utilizes AI algorithms trained on various audio samples to either create new sounds or modify existing ones according to user needs. This results in tailor-made audio effects, which can be particularly useful in a range of applications. Monsters are hybrid creatures, collages of associations. According to the synthesis of historical records, the syntax of creating monsters seems to include four steps of consideration: role, characteristics, symbolism and manifestation. ● Role: This involves brainstorming the idea of the monster, including its origins, purpose, and role in the narrative or cultural context. I collected some commonly used roles of monsters. Antagonist Guardians or Protectors Symbol of Fear Tragic Figures Moral Cautionary Figures Representations of the Other Metaphors for Personal or Societal Agents of Change or Destruction Issues Objects of Curiosity and Wonder Catalysts for Heroism Complex Characters Agents of Chaos Comic Relief Allegorical Figures ● Characteristics: The physical and behavioral traits of the monster are defined. This can include size, shape, abilities, weaknesses, and other descriptive characteristics. In this part, I included all the adjectives I collected from classics, media and interviews in my previous research as the various sources of databases for the AI monster generator. List1.Classics Dreadful Gruesome Hideous Nocturnal Lurking Eldtrich Ravenous Chimerical Ghastly Vile Insidious Ferocious Morbidly Unearthly Demonic … List2. Media Disruptive Powerful Desperate Dystopian Alien-seeming Manipulative Detached Dehumanizing Dominating World-ending Ubiquitous Intangible Terrifying … Pervasive Out-of-control List3. Interview Pathetic Playful Ambivalent Divisive Cute Unwelcome Immoral Unimaginable Charming Ancient Restrictive Dangerous Strange ... Inhumane Disgusting ● Symbolism: Deciding what the monster represents. Societal Fear Personal Demon ● Manifestation: Bringing the monster to life through the chosen medium, be it writing, film, art, sculpture, or other forms of expression. Historical Record Painting Mythology Literature Religious Text Children’s Book Illuminated Manuscripts Movie Theatre TV Series Animation Prompt template: create an image of a monster as [Role], with [Characteristics] characteristics that symbolizes [Symbolism], [Manifestation]. author: Yifan Xu
thesis_title: Monster-making As a Mirror: Reflecting Ourselves and The Future
paragraph: Based on the results of prompt testing, I decided to design a second round of cultural probes that allowed people to directly design their own monsters based on syntax. Due to the limitations and lack of emotions in the classification and word count of the prompt template, I asked participants to write an authentic description of the monster in their minds. I fed the collected responses back into AI, allowing the descriptive language of monsters to undergo transformations in form, sound, and motions, turning into a living digital creature, imprisoned in the new "The Garden of Earthly Delights". author: Yifan Xu
thesis_title: Monster-making As a Mirror: Reflecting Ourselves and The Future
paragraph: Design：The Website New Eden This website is an experimental narrative remixing, a symphony of chaos, and a celebration of our own monsters. It has two layers: the painting layer and the underpainting layer. The painting layer portrays captured AI monsters living in Bosch’s painting “The Garden of Earthly Delights”, vividly manifesting our personal desires and fears in this eschatological digital environment. The underpainting layer reveals the human element within the creative process, showing how these seemingly paradoxical AI monsters were crafted, as well as the characteristics they were given and the narratives written about them. They embody the emotions and souls imparted by their creators.

author: Yifan Xu
thesis_title: Monster-making As a Mirror: Reflecting Ourselves and The Future
paragraph: In the realm of art and technology, the synthesis of AI with the conceptualization of monsters heralds a new epoch in creative expression and cultural representation. These AI-integrated monsters, transcending their traditional roles, echo the shifting paradigms of our understanding of the monstrous and the fears it encapsulates. The transformation is profound, reflecting not only in the aesthetics but also in the underlying symbolism and societal implications. The incorporation of AI in reimagining monsters, especially those styled after Hieronymus Bosch's work, raises intriguing questions about the nature of fear and its representation in the digital age. Bosch's paintings, often rife with esoteric symbolism and nightmarish visions, have historically served as visual metaphors for the human psyche's darker recesses and moral allegories. When AI is commanded to recreate these images, the resulting creations are curiously devoid of the terror typically associated with Bosch’s work. This phenomenon prompts a reflection on the programming and objectives set for AI in artistic creation – are these digital entities coded to align with a more optimistic, human-centric perspective? This leads to a broader contemplation of how modern society perceives and interacts with the notion of the monstrous. Unlike the past, where monsters were embodiments of fears and moral warnings, contemporary interpretations often lean towards a nuanced representation. This is particularly evident in monsters designed for children's media, which, while retaining certain monstrous characteristics, are often infused with elements of charm and whimsy. These creatures are crafted not to alarm but to aid young audiences in navigating and overcoming their fears. This approach marks a significant departure from traditional representations, redefining the monstrous as a construct that can be both endearing and educational. In this context, AI assumes the role of a modern-day mythmaker, a creator of monsters that are as much guardians as they are manifestations of the unknown. The AI-generated monsters become metaphors for the protective shields we erect against our primal fears, similar to the way children watch monsters on screen, safely distanced from real danger. However, the inherent unpredictability and autonomy of AI introduces a paradox – the creator of protective myths can also become a source of new anxieties. As AI continues to evolve, its capability to generate narratives and entities that resonate with our deepest fears becomes increasingly potent. This evolving relationship between humanity and its technological creations mirrors the journey from childhood to maturity. Just as a child's perception of monsters evolves from fear to understanding, society’s engagement with AI-generated entities may transition from viewing them as benign creative tools to recognizing their potential for eliciting new forms of fear. The future of this relationship is as uncertain as it is fascinating – will AI continue to serve as a protective force, or will it eventually unleash new kinds of monsters that challenge our understanding of fear and safety? The intersection of AI and monster mythology in the arts is a microcosm of the broader human-technology interaction. It reflects our ongoing quest to understand and articulate the complexities of our nature and the world we inhabit – a world increasingly influenced by the algorithms and artificial minds we have created. The monsters of tomorrow may well be the ultimate test of our ability to confront and comprehend the unknown frontiers of technology and imagination.

author: Yu Gao
thesis_title: Possibility of Paths
paragraph: I walk to school every day. Surprisingly, even though it's only a ten-minute walk, I have more than five choices of which route to take. Sometimes I'll choose the one that passes by my friend Kaia’s house, hoping for a chance encounter with her. Sometimes I choose to visit the supermarket across the street to buy breakfast, and then enter the campus dormitory area from the supermarket’s back door. And some days I’ll take a detour to the park near the school to see the puppies playing there. No matter which way I choose to walk to school, I always think about my future, the next steps in my life. It seems like I don’t have many choices, especially living in New York City as a foreigner–my home is in southern China. Sometimes I just think that it would be better if my future path had as many options as ways I could go to school. Let’s consider the word “path.” In conventional terms, a path is predetermined, a route, track, or trail that is deliberately constructed by people or naturally formed through repetitive activities. Because a conventional path is predetermined, people often overlook its potential and flexibility, just like I would only take paths with gates and passages instead of going over walls into school. At the same time, we often use the word “path” as a metaphor to describe how we move through life in seemingly predetermined ways. But life is not predetermined. So why is this metaphor so popular? My perspective began to shift when I encountered a climbing wall. This structure wasn't a path in the traditional sense; it wasn't a fixed line, but rather an array of different points. It didn't dictate a specific direction; one could choose to ascend, move left, veer right, or even fall off. Yet, it was still a path. This realization prompted me to become more attuned to the paths that surround us in various forms. Perhaps a path could be a trace of movement that has already occurred or something that hasn't happened yet. It can be a narrative or evidence of the movement of people, animals, or things to different places, as well as a foreshadowing, a rule, or a guide for what might happen next. It could exist in, for example, a music score that is a map to a beautiful melody–melody as path. It can exist on the billiard table, first as an idea in the player's mind, and then as an executed break on the table. It could even exist in the sky, in the countless potential paths that birds might choose. We can use the concept of the path in visual communication design. The visual guide, typography, transformation between verbal or written narrative into a visual narrative, etc.–these are all paths. More specifically, every time we go to a park, museum, or hospital, we can see some arrow, text, and number to help us to find the place we want to go. That’s how a visual guide works. The reason I think it’s a kind of path is because before we arrive at that place, we will be very clear about the way we will go. This kind of visual guide creates a path in our mind instead of a physical route. Also, when we read a book, we can read it from the first page to the end, and we also can find the part we are interested in according to the table of contents. How the content is put in the book and how the table of contents and page number lead the reader to find the part they want, could be seen as two kinds of paths existing in the book. These are the core of my exploration. But this is not the only way to think about paths. Paths also exist in our minds. Logical thinking, scientific methodology, and even habits are all kinds of paths. Brain scientists talk about learning as creating neural pathways. In this context, paths in different people’s minds are all slightly different. Every day I wake up, I will brush my teeth first, then wash my face and drink a cup of warm water. While for my roommate, she will go to take a shower first and then go to school as soon as possible to finish her assignments every morning. We each have a different routine, a different morning path, which corresponds to neural pathways in our brains. Further, when we think of designing a book (like this one you are reading, perhaps…), the first step in my mind is thinking about the system of the book, while another designer might come up with many interesting ideas of typography details at first. The ways of managing time, thinking, and learning, doing and making–these will be different, even though we are studying at the same program and taking several courses with the same professors. Unique paths in people’s minds determine that everyone has a unique life. No two people's life paths are the same, and no one can copy other people’s life paths, even if they want to, even if they try. Sometimes, we can say that the path in the mind is the most flexible, hardest to predict, and most difficult to control. After eating breakfast, I might decide that I will go to a Korean restaurant to eat lunch. But maybe after school, I will change my mind and go eat a hamburger instead. Also, for my thesis, when I was still considering what I wanted to talk about and didn’t have a clear idea, my mind changed every day. There are a lot of things that influence my mind, such as news I read, conversations between me and my friend, advice from different professors, etc. My mind changes so fast that I don’t even have a chance to record most thoughts, and they pass away just as fast as they arrive. However, sometimes paths in our minds are hard to change. There is a concept in social science named Path Dependence, referring to the idea that once individuals or collectives get used it.1 to a mode or a rule, even though there are some inconveniences, they would not like to change What I didn't expect was that Path Dependence also happens in the design field. Adobe InDesign 1 Wikipedia Contributors, “Path Dependence,” Wikipedia (Wikimedia Foundation, February 25, 2019), https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Path_dependence. is a software that is recognized as a desktop publishing and layout application for creating books, images.2 magazines, etc. And Adobe Illustrator is especially good for creating vector But because there are many students who learn Adobe Illustrator earlier than Adobe InDesign, they will use Adobe Illustrator to design a booklet. Even though there are many more convenient functions in Adobe InDesign for book design, they don’t want to use it since they aren't familiar with it. They have become dependent on the path offered by their knowledge of Adobe Illustrator. As designers, if we stay in our comfort zone and just repeat the way we went before, our design will be very boring. So, discussing path’s different concepts can help designers to open our minds both in concept and design methods. In my thesis, I’m trying not to repeat routes and to explore new possibilities in communications design. Just as physical paths abound, the potential in our life's journey is boundless. The fact that no one in our immediate surroundings has ventured down a certain road doesn't render it inaccessible. The absence of precedent in our vicinity regarding a particular choice doesn't inherently make it a poor one for us. In my thesis, my aspiration is to convey the limitless possibilities of life's paths to the audience. I intend to utilize the concept of a path as a metaphor for the myriad choices people have in life, encouraging people to envision their unique life journeys. When confronted with challenges, we often feel trapped, believing there are limited options available to us. But this is not true. Our lives are vaster than the sky, and the paths we can choose from are even more numerous than those available to birds.

author: Yu Gao
thesis_title: Possibility of Paths
paragraph: Part 1: Visual Research What do you think a path is? A road that we pass every day? A highway that's hard to navigate, impossible to turn around? A railroad track that has been planned for a long time? A way to walk into the woods? As we have seen, the word “path” typically refers to a route, track, or trail that is meticulously constructed by people or naturally formed through repetitive activities. It could be a physical or metaphorical route that relates to the process of moving or traveling from a starting point to a destination. It may exist to fulfill a need (road or trail) or be left unintentionally, such as footprints in the snow, ripples on the water, and light and shadows created by a busy street with traffic in a time-lapse photo. It could be a trace of movement that has already occurred: words written with a pen or doodles drawn, the path a sewing machine takes on fabrics, paths in the wild due to repeated trampling by people, or vacancies of books borrowed from library shelves. Or it indicates something that hasn’t happened yet: blank checkerboards, rock-covered climbing walls, a labyrinth playground that's opening soon, articles that are waiting to be read, and sheet music waiting to be played. “There is no one-size-fits-all narrative; everyone's path winds in different ways.” – Sarah McBride3 Paths also appear in places other than on the ground or in the transportation industry, such as games, books, the sky, the human’s body, etc.

author: Yu Gao
thesis_title: Possibility of Paths
paragraph: 3 Sarah McBride, “The Real Me,” HuffPost, May 9, 2012, https://www.huffpost.com/entry/the-real-me_b_1504207. It can be guided: circuit diagrams in textbooks, navigation and menus in website design, train station schedules, rules of board games and goals for winning. Time is the longest distance between two places. — Tennessee Williams, The Glass Menagerie4 It can be implied: The direction and water force of a defined spout on a fountain, the game of connecting the dots drawn on paper, the experience of previous generations. Or it can be chosen differently by individuals: a strategy based on the position of the ball during a billiards game, a history showing the jumps between different web pages while surfing the Internet, a way to organize and find local files, and different daily itineraries for each person. For one person, organized files might be a crucial tool for creativity; another Rubin5 person finds inspiration in random juxtapositions. – Gretchen It can be formed in one step after another or be revealed at the end of the last step. In philosophy, it is not the attainment of the goal that matters, it is the things that are met Life6 — with by the way. Havelock Ellis, The Dance of Besides, when we think of paths, we often think of the shape of a line. However, during visual research, I noticed that the shape of a path is not just a line. It could be a table, an area, some scattered points that will be connected, or even something intangible. Through visual research, I want to use existing images to break people’s original imagination of path. I hope to inspire people to generate more visual associations with paths, exploring and expanding the definition of the concept of "paths" together. author: Yu Gao
thesis_title: Possibility of Paths
paragraph: —--------- Part 2 Case Study After conducting research on artworks related to paths, I selected ten pieces and curated an exhibition catalog for them. The pieces are meticulously arranged in ascending order, reflecting the scale of the paths they depict–from a piece of fabric and a wall to a room, a street, a city's border, an entire city, the world, and finally, the vast expanse of the sky. In this part, I will select a few of these projects to introduce and explain why they are related to the path concept and how they inspired the thesis. The first project I want to introduce is "The Embroidered Computer" by Irene Posch, a science,7 researcher and artist with a background in media and computer and Ebru Kurbak, an artist.8 internationally working “The Embroidered Computer” is what they call “an exploration into using historic gold embroidery materials and knowledge to craft a programmable 8-bit computer.”9 In this project, there are two more obvious paths in this work: the artist's embroidery on the cloth, and the circuit diagram drawn on the computer. In everyday life, handicrafts and electronics would seem to be two very different classifications. This work embodies the hidden relationship between handicrafts and electronics. In this work, the connection between these two media is also a kind of path. This shared logic serves as a bridge, illustrating how diverse practices can converge on a conceptual level, despite their differences in medium and application. In this way, path represents a transformation where the physical and aesthetic practices of embroidery are applied to the functional and logical realm of computing. This crossover not only challenges traditional classifications but also suggests a fluidity between domains that are often considered fixed and separate. It showcases how ideas and principles can transcend their original contexts to find new expressions and applications. Irene Posch, “About,” Irene Posch (Irene Posch, n.d.), https://ireneposch.net/about/. Ebru Kurbak, “About – Ebru Kurbak,” Ebru Kurbak, accessed March 8, 2024, https://ebrukurbak.net/about/. 9 Irene Posch, “The Embroidered Computer,” Irene Posch, accessed February 5, 2024, https://ireneposch.net/the-embroidered-computer/.

The entire project is presented in a documentary video, which records the process of
