This opened up my understanding of paths: a path is not necessarily a line. It can be invisible, it can exist only in the viewer's mind. And the presentation of a project is not necessarily the path itself, but can be presented in other ways.

author: Yu Gao
thesis_title: Possibility of Paths
paragraph: For the path in another dimension, "Cooking Potato Stories," made by Ana Núñez Rodríguez, and “A GUIDE THROUGH HUE,” created by Sibylle Eimermacher, give us a good explanation. Ana Núñez Rodríguez is a research-based photographer. Her work "Cooking Potato Stories," traces the trajectory of the potato across the world based on her personal experience moving between Latin America and Europe. Through potatoes' historical path, she wants to discuss “the tension between personal and social identity and the historical and cultural influences on its formation.”11 In “A GUIDE THROUGH HUE,” the artist, Sibylle Eimermacher, explored a rock’s disappearance.12 birth and She thinks these rocks not only could represent migratory, homeless change.”13 states of being, but also could be “a surrender to the universal condition of constant

author: Yu Gao
thesis_title: Possibility of Paths
paragraph: 11 Ana Núñez Rodríguez, “Cooking Potato Stories — Ana Núñez Rodríguez,” ananunezrodriguez.com, accessed February 5, 2024, https://ananunezrodriguez.com/Cooking- Potato-Stories. “Thing Books,” Thing Books, accessed February 5, 2024, https://thingbooks.shop/products/a- guide-through-hue. 13 “Sibylle Eimermacher - Sibylle Eimermacher: A Guide through Hue,” Printed Matter, accessed February 5, 2024, https://www.printedmatter.org/catalog/60060/. The two projects were started at the same time in my design book because I thought the paths in both projects would be of similar scale. Both artists trace the origin and development of objects/plants in different places. Interestingly, neither of the two study subjects, Potato and Stone, could walk or move on their own. This actually shows the historical path of studying potatoes and stones, as well as the historical path of human beings and human culture. There is no linear path in their work. Both works are presented through photography. But with their narratives, we can appreciate the development path of the research subjects, how they drifted from one place to another, and the changes that occurred in the research subjects during this process.

author: Yu Gao
thesis_title: Possibility of Paths
paragraph: In this book I edited and designed, each project commences sequentially based on the scale of its respective path. The initiation of the next project occurs only after the completion of the previous one. This sequencing may occasionally lead to simultaneous starts for two items sharing the same scale. Rather than conventional page numbers, the book measures the length of each page from the front cover to the back cover. The numerical markings on each page indicate the cumulative length reached at that point. The presentation of each project unfolds as a continuous line of text, spanning multiple pages until the completion of its content. However, every thousand millimeters, images will appear. The path of the text will be obstructed by the appearance of the images. This intentional layout design creates a distinctive effect, symbolizing key moments along the journey.

author: Yu Gao
thesis_title: Possibility of Paths
paragraph: In our daily walks, mobile navigation and the destination stand out as two crucial factors. However, these elements often lead us to overlook the route itself. We are either fixated on the route displayed on our phones or preoccupied with the thought of quickly reaching our destination. In this project, I wanted to divert the attention of walkers from the map on their phone or the over-eager desire to reach their destination, to the path they are walking on. I set a very simple rule. Within 15 minutes, people need to complete all turns in this order: turn right, turn left, turn right, turn left, turn right, turn right, turn left, turn left, and then turn right. (During my pre-testing, I came up with this arbitrary order. It was able to make the route complex enough yet easy enough to complete in 15 minutes.) Because of this rule, navigation and destinations lose their roles. Guided by this rule, I hope that people will be liberated from the constraints of navigation and destination and can freely explore the neighborhood in which they are located. As a presentation of the project, I used myself as the experimental subject and began to explore this rule. In the book from this project, I recorded three of my walks, respectively in Clinton Hill, Noho to the East Village, and Koreatown to Chelsea. As I walked, I took pictures of where I decided to turn every time, as well as what I saw in the sky when I looked up and the ground when I looked down. In this book, the photos and information about where they were taken (street, neighborhood, direction to turn) were placed based on the direction I was walking at the time. I hope that when people read this book, they can change the direction of the book according to the direction of the pictures and text, and complete these three walks with me. In this practice, the importance of ending points diminishes. The linear direction of navigation transforms into personal walking habits, decisions driven by intuition or confusing choices, the whisper of the wind, the rhythm of one's own breathing, and countless unforeseen details.

author: Yu Gao
thesis_title: Possibility of Paths
paragraph: After coming to the United States, instant noodle (ramen) has become an indispensable food in my life. I then realized that for many Chinese international students, instant noodles also occupy a very important place. The instant noodle available on the American market mainly comes from Asia, which made me curious: Is there a certain similarity between ramen and myself, someone who came to the United States from Asia? For example, when we come to the United States, our names will change from our own languages to English, but the original names in our languages do not disappear; they coexist and are used by peers who speak the same mother tongue. I collected all the instant noodles from my home and my friends’ homes and selected four of the more common types of instant noodles to start my research. During this process, I recorded the addresses of the noodle companies, the production locations of these noodles, the companies responsible for transportation, and the addresses of the ports in the United States where the goods were received. I then connected these addresses into four paths using the shapes of the noodles, instead of drawing lines. The paths were formed by the list of original ingredients for each type of noodle. Additionally, I used the noodle packaging, which includes the names, to form the shapes of continents on the map. We can see that in their places of origin, their names are in their original languages, but when they arrive in the United States, English translations are added to the packaging. In this process, I found that some instant noodles imported into the United States are not produced in the noodle companies' home countries; some are produced in other Asian countries, and some are directly produced in the United States. This exploration of instant noodles, a seemingly simple food item, unfolded into a profound reflection on identity, adaptation, and the interconnectedness of global cultures. Just like the noodles that traverse continents, changing in form and presentation yet retaining their essence, we, as individuals in a diaspora, adapt to new environments while preserving the core of who we are. The journey of these noodles mirrors our own—transcending geographical boundaries, adapting to new cultural contexts, and creating a unique blend of identities. It serves as a reminder that, in the vast global landscape, there are threads of familiarity and comfort that bind us to our roots, even as we embrace the new and unfamiliar. In the end, this research is not just about tracing the paths of instant noodles; it's about understanding our paths, as we weave through the tapestry of global migration and cultural exchange, continually shaping our identity in a dynamic world. Project Three: Path as Metaphor: The Journey of Thelma & Louise Road movies are a well-known genre in cinema. In these films, the relationships between the characters, and even their life paths, change as the journey progresses. This concept illustrates the meaning of 'path' that I mentioned earlier, which can signify both the road and a person's life path. Such films reveal a profound, reflective relationship between the journey of life and our physical travels. It suggests that the roads taken (in both a literal and symbolic sense) are not merely paths but reflections of life's choices, experiences, and transformations. After delving into research on road movies, I discovered a sub-genre known as female road movies. As we know, gender bias in driving has always existed. In this context, the stories of female road movies distinctly stand out from other types (family road movies, male road movies). 'Thelma & Louise,' the first road movie featuring all-female leads, showcases the contemporary struggles of women. The writer of Thelma & Louise, Callie Khouri, who won an Oscar in 1991, mentioned that in the previous road movie, women’s roles are always passive, “simply because wheel!”14 they are never at the The two female characters start their story with a planned short vacation, but end their life because of seeking freedom, which leaves a lasting impression on the hearts of the audience and became the icon of the feminist movement later. Beyond showcasing the mutual reflection of roads and life paths, in this project, I use film dialogue as a tool to narrate the entire movie story, interspersing it with the scenery the protagonists see along their journey. It demonstrates how dialogues, narrative storytelling, and these metaphorical paths reflect themes such as freedom, identity, and rebellion, thus providing a richer, more detailed understanding of the film's impact and significance. I also aimed to highlight the

author: Yu Gao
thesis_title: Possibility of Paths
paragraph: 14 “Thelma & Louise, a Feminist Road Movie,” Historic Route 66, accessed March 8, 2024, https://www.route66guide.com/thelma-louise.html. complexity and interconnectedness of character narratives, personal and physical journeys, such as the changes the two protagonists undergo in three phases of the movie, and how the film transitions from one storyline to two, eventually merging these storylines into a unified narrative.

author: Yu Gao
thesis_title: Possibility of Paths
paragraph: This is a collaborative project for my Thesis class. The theme of this project is March 3rd. Each participant is tasked with developing their work around a unique, personal theme derived from this date. My project is premised on the assumption that on March 3, 2024, my parents and friends will travel to New York to meet me, coming from various locations around the world. I have collected and analyzed the travel methods required for their journeys, devising the most efficient routes in terms of time, cost, and overall travel experience. The travel plans vary significantly; while some will fly directly, others may need to take a train, with some arriving in New York on March 3rd and others needing to embark on their journey two days prior. An intriguing aspect of this project is the ephemeral nature of travel arrangements—after March 3, it will become challenging to retrieve the flight and train schedules for that specific day. Hence, documenting this information in a tangible format holds special significance, especially given our project's focus on this particular date. In my project, I employed text and tables to depict the travel routes, getting inspiration from the train timetables I encountered during my visual research. The concept emphasizes that a physical map is not necessary to understand a journey; merely knowing the start and end points allows us to visualize the path in our minds. This approach is mirrored in my project, which identifies nine cities across three countries as the origins and New York City as the destination. It delves into the specifics of these routes: How were they selected? What are the nuances of each journey? The project addresses these questions through information organization and typography.

author: Yu Gao
thesis_title: Possibility of Paths
paragraph: This book has attempted to explore the possibility of “path” as a way to employ critical thinking to challenge conventional patterns. Besides a physical existence, a path can be a way of life, being, or thinking. Breaking with the conventional definition of path is important because exploring all the possibilities can help us break the mold of conventional thinking, and that break is what leads to innovative design. When we think we have nowhere to go, this mindset will help us find a new path. In some of my experiments, I set different strict rules in the beginning. no matter how rigid the rule was, there were always unexpected things happening. In the project “Take a Walk,” for example, I made a rule telling me how many times I needed to turn left and right, and what order to turn left and right. But paths recorded are different every time because they are also influenced by the weather, the starting point, the neighborhood I walked, and the most important thing — my mind. Even though the streets of the city have long been established, seeming unchangeable and unbreachable, my ideas add infinite variety to the routes I walk. Sometimes, I achieve a turnaround by making two right turns; sometimes, I cross streets where no one walks. After becoming familiar with the rules of directional order, I began to explore the possibilities within these rules, constantly seeking more interesting paths. In this way, I broke the habit of walking while just looking at my phone's map or only taking familiar routes, and I started to explore the city where I live. Similar things happened in another project “Paths in Paths.” While designing the book, I established a rule that I only use one text line to display the text content of each project, because each artwork included in this book is about path. While images of projects will appear in every thousand millimeters, which will interrupt the text's flow. Despite the strict rule, it caused an unforeseen and unique layout for each project in this book. At first, I thought this book might be a bit boring because the rules were simple and rigid. But later, I discovered that even I, the designer of this book, couldn't predict how many pages a project would occupy, or how thick the book would become during the design process. In the end, every project’s layout is different because they have different content. This example reminds me that we always have envisions in the beginning of a project to make sure that things are under control. But as designers, we should play with rules, to challenge the envision we have at first, opening the door to new opportunities within the project and allowing for a richer and more dynamic design. After these two projects, I tried to explore more meanings of path. The project about the movie Thelma & Louise was inspired from the idea of road movies, which actually shows how paths can be used both in a physical and metaphorical way. During this project, I continually found more and more paths in this movie: the road that two characters drive on, the life paths of the characters, the narrative way, the story, the transcripts, etc. In the last project, I explored things related to paths besides geographic location in the content, such transportation and people’s relationships. Also, I want to change path’s default images in our mind and use some other elements to represent it. For example, when we think about how a person flies from Beijing to New York, the flight routes in the airplane’s screen will appear in our mind. So for the project “Pathways to Reunion: The March 3rd Project,” I used text and timetables to replace the path in the map. For example, It’s a small project but it includes a bunch of information. During this project, I tried to make all the information clear enough and get rid of ornamentations. Life is rich with constraints, some trivial and others daunting. Likewise, we can’t predict the unexpected. This thesis illustrates possibilities of paths. Even amidst challenges and hurdles, we possess the agency to craft a unique life journey. It isn’t difficult. Once we break our conventional thinking and practice those possibilities in our mind bravely, we can always find new paths, new directions, new approaches, and new futures.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: In an average day, a typical smartphone user unlocks their device 150 times and performs over 2,600 touch operations, with approximately 90% of these actions being unconscious, habitual behaviors (Markowetz et al., 2015). These automated interactions extend beyond our digital devices—from typing on keyboards to turning pages in books —our daily routines have become so ingrained that they often bypass conscious thought entirely. This automation, while efficient, raises a critical question: In our pursuit of seamless interaction, have we inadvertently diminished our capacity for conscious engagement and creative exploration?

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: Recent neuroscience research by Davidson and McEwen (2012) demonstrates that repeated behaviors create strong neural pathways, making these actions increasingly automatic and resistant to change. While this neuroplasticity serves an evolutionary purpose in conserving cognitive resources, it also presents a challenge in an era where adaptability and creative thinking are increasingly crucial. A 2023 Microsoft Workplace Intelligence Report reveals that knowledge workers are interrupted by digital notifications every 33 minutes on average, leading to fragmented attention patterns and decreased capacity for deep engagement.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: In our current "attention economy," as termed by Herbert Simon and elaborated by Thomas Davenport (2001), we face a paradox: the tools designed to enhance our productivity and connectivity often lead to what Eli Pariser (2011) calls "filter bubbles" - algorithmic echo chambers that reinforce existing patterns of thought and behavior. Research by the Nielsen Norman Group (2022) indicates that the average time spent deeply engaging with online content has decreased by 88% since 2000, suggesting a shift toward increasingly superficial interaction patterns.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: The concept of defamiliarization, first introduced by Viktor Shklovsky in his 1917 essay "Art as Device," provides a theoretical framework for addressing these modern challenges. Shklovsky argued that art's purpose is to make the familiar strange, forcing longer and more deliberate perception. This century-old theory finds new relevance in contemporary cognitive science research:

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: 1. Neural Plasticity Studies: Work by Draganski et al. (2021) demonstrates that novel experiences trigger increased neural connectivity and enhanced learning capacity. Their longitudinal studies show that introducing unfamiliar elements to routine tasks can significantly improve cognitive flexibility.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: 2. Behavioral Economics: Kahneman and Tversky's research on cognitive biases (2019) reveals how "default option bias" influences decision-making, suggesting that intentional disruption of defaults can lead to more conscious choices.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: Practical applications of defamiliarization in design demonstrate its potential for enhancing engagement and learning:

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: 1. Isamu Noguchi's Playground Designs (1933-1965): His revolutionary approach to children's play spaces replaced standard equipment with abstract forms. Studies by the Noguchi Museum (2020) document how these "defamiliarized" environments significantly increased creative play and problem-solving behaviors among children.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: 2. Lex Pott's "True Time" Clock (2018): By reinventing the traditional clock face, this design challenges users' habitual perception of time. User studies conducted by Design Academy Eindhoven (2019) showed that participants demonstrated increased awareness of time passing and reported more mindful time management practices.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: This research investigates how intentional design obstacles can transform passive receivers into active explorers. Through carefully crafted experiments with everyday objects—from scrambled keyboard layouts to reorganized book structures—we examine how defamiliarization can:

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: 1. Facilitate "unlearning" and "relearning" of habitual behaviors. 2. Enhance critical thinking and creative problem-solving capabilities. 3. Counter the passive consumption patterns encouraged by algorithmic systems.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: 1. Develop a theoretical framework for implementing defamiliarization in design, specifically focusing on how it can transform passive interaction patterns into active exploration. 2. Demonstrate the potential of defamiliarization as a design intervention in the digital age, where algorithmic recommendations and automated behaviors increasingly dominate our daily interactions. 3. Investigate how intentionally designed obstacles can create meaningful engagement and foster independent thinking, particularly in contexts where efficiency and automation might otherwise limit conscious interaction.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: 7:30 AM, your iPhone alarm goes off. Before your mind fully awakens, your fingers instinctively swipe to unlock the screen. The first thing you see is a curated "For You" feed, algorithmically selected based on your viewing history from last night. These short videos seem to know your preferences perfectly—morning routines from your favorite fitness influencers, trendy Manhattan coffee spots, and the latest productivity hacks. Twenty minutes slip by unnoticed.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: 8:00 AM, you open Google Maps to prepare for your commute from Brooklyn to Manhattan. The app has already predicted the morning traffic on the BQE and suggests an alternate route through local streets. "Take Manhattan Bridge instead of Brooklyn Bridge to save 15 minutes," the notification pops up. Without hesitation, you accept the algorithm's suggestion as you do every morning. The robotic voice guides your every turn, eliminating any need to think about the route. According to studies, 76% of urban commuters rely entirely on navigation recommendations, with over 80% never questioning or exploring alternative paths (Urban Mobility Report, 2023).

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: Arriving at your office in Midtown at 8:30 a.m., you boot up your MacBook. Your fingers naturally find their way to the F and J keys with their small bumps, as they have thousands of times before. Research shows that an average office worker performs about 7,000 keystrokes per hour (Barrett, 2021), with 90% of these actions happening unconsciously. From Google Docs to Excel sheets, from Gmail replies to Slack messages, your fingers dance across the keyboard in a choreographed routine perfected through countless repetitions. During lunch break, you open Spotify. The "Made For You" playlist is ready with its algorithmic precision. As you grab your salad from Sweetgreen, you habitually scroll through Instagram. Your feed is precisely targeted: photos of new restaurants in the Lower East Side, news from your subscribed media, and discussions about topics you've recently engaged with. Studies indicate that modern urban professionals perform over 2,600 touchscreen operations daily, with 85% being completely automated repetitive actions (Digital Behavior Research, 2023).

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: In the afternoon meeting, you open your notebook to a fresh page. The pages, always ordered from left to right, 1 to 100, represent our deeply ingrained linear way of processing information. After the meeting, on your subway ride home, you put on your AirPods, letting a recommended podcast from Spotify fill your commute back to Brooklyn. Research shows that we're interrupted by an average of 110 push notifications daily (Tech Habits Survey, 2023), yet we've somehow normalized this constant bombardment of information.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: Evening finds you in bed, your phone's blue light illuminating the room. Social media algorithms know exactly what will keep your attention: what people in your demographic are doing, friends you might know in New York, and products you might like from that boutique in Williamsburg. Studies show users need to scroll an average of 60 times before encountering content completely unrelated to their current social bubble (Social Media Dynamics Study, 2023). We've become unwitting inhabitants of our own digital echo chambers.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: This is a day in modern urban life: filled with countless automated behaviors and algorithmic recommendations. Our lives have become extraordinarily efficient, yet increasingly passive. From keyboard layouts to page numbers, from unlock swipes to playlist selections, these familiar interfaces and interactions dictate our daily routines. They've become invisible scripts choreographing our every move. But what is the cost of this efficiency? As our behaviors become increasingly automated, are we losing something more profound—our capacity for deeper connection and independent thought? The QWERTY keyboard layout has existed for 140 years, but should we accept it as the optimal choice? Could rethinking these familiar interfaces spark new forms of creativity and engagement?

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: My journey toward defamiliarization began in an unexpected place. Initially, my research focused on multisensory design, questioning the overwhelming dominance of visual perception in design practice. As designers, we often find ourselves trapped in a visually-centric mindset, perhaps too comfortable with familiar ways of seeing and presenting information. This recognition led me to a fundamental question: How might we communicate and understand when deprived of our primary sense of sight? This early exploration manifested in an experiment I call "The Blind Box." Participants are presented with a box containing various objects and shapes, which they can only experience through touch. Each person is asked to draw what they perceived through their tactile exploration, before passing the box to the next participant. The final collection of drawings revealed fascinating variations in perception and interpretation, highlighting how different sensory experiences could lead to diverse understandings of the same object. What began as an investigation into multisensory design unexpectedly revealed something more profound: our reliance on familiar patterns of perception and interaction. When participants were asked to abandon their primary mode of understanding (vision) and rely on alternative senses, they became more conscious and engaged. Their interaction with objects transformed from passive recognition to active exploration .

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: While this initial research focused on how artists could expand their modes of expression through multisensory approaches, a crucial realization shifted my perspective. I began to question whether the phenomenon of limited perception extended beyond artists to affect audience engagement with art itself. This led to a fundamental question: In our current cultural institutions, are audiences truly engaging with art, or are they merely passive observers? To explore this question, I conducted an observational study at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA). For approximately one hour, I observed visitor behavior in front of "María's Great Expedition." The results were revealing: out of an estimated 150-200 visitors who passed by the artwork, only eight took the time to read the accompanying description. This stark statistic suggested that most visitors were engaging with art passively and superficially, missing opportunities for deeper connection and understanding. This observation catalyzed a new direction in my research: How might we transform audience members from passive viewers into active participants? In response, I developed an experimental piece influenced by conditional design principles. The experiment invited viewers to become co-creators through a series of simple yet engaging rules: 1. Draw one continuous line for 15 minutes 2. Keep the pen in constant contact with the paper 3. Never cross the line previously drawn This participatory approach represented a significant shift from traditional audience-artwork relationships. Instead of merely observing, participants were now actively involved in the creation process, challenging the conventional passive viewing experience common in museums and galleries

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: These early experiments - from the tactile exploration of the Blind Box to the participatory drawing exercise - revealed a consistent pattern: when familiar interactions are disrupted or challenged, people tend to engage more deeply and consciously with their experiences. The Blind Box forced participants to abandon their reliance on vision, while the conditional drawing exercise transformed passive viewers into active creators. Both experiments, though different in approach, pointed to a powerful insight: the potential of defamiliarization as a design strategy. This realization led me to question broader patterns of passive interaction in our daily lives. If disrupting familiar patterns could enhance engagement in art and sensory experiences, could similar principles be applied to our everyday interactions with digital interfaces and physical objects? How might we use intentional disruption to transform other forms of passive consumption into active engagement? These questions would eventually shape my current research focus: exploring how defamiliarization can be used as a deliberate design strategy to break automated behaviors and encourage more conscious, meaningful interactions in our daily lives. The journey from multisensory exploration to audience participation ultimately pointed toward a more fundamental investigation of how we might transform familiar experiences into opportunities for discovery and engagement.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: As we observed in Chapter 1, our daily interactions with technology have become increasingly automated. Our fingers dance across keyboards and screens in well-rehearsed patterns, often with minimal conscious awareness. These observations raise fundamental questions about how and why we develop such automated behaviors and, more importantly, how we might transform these passive interactions into more meaningful engagements. To understand these phenomena and develop effective interventions, we must first explore the theoretical foundations that explain both the formation of automated behaviors and the potential strategies for disrupting them.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: In 1971, decades before the dawn of smartphones and social media, Herbert Simon made a prescient observation that would become increasingly relevant in our modern world: "In an information-rich world, the wealth of information means a dearth of something else: a scarcity of what that information consumes—attention." This insight provides a crucial framework for understanding our current digital behavior patterns. Consider a typical morning in New York City: A designer sits at their desk, surrounded by the quiet hum of technology. Their laptop displays multiple open windows—email, Slack channels, design software, and countless browser tabs. Their smartphone periodically illuminates with notifications, each competing for immediate attention. This scene, replicated millions of times across offices and homes, illustrates the modern attention crisis. Research by Microsoft in 2023 reveals that knowledge workers now switch tasks approximately 373 times during their workday, while the average professional receives over 100 notifications daily. The consequences of this constant demand for attention manifest in what researchers call "attention residue"—the lingering cognitive effects of previous tasks impair our performance on current ones. Imagine trying to focus on a complex design problem while part of your mind still processes the urgent email you just read or the Slack message waiting for your response. This cognitive juggling act forces our brains to develop coping mechanisms, leading to increasingly automated behaviors.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: The way our brains respond to this attention crisis can be understood through Don Norman's three levels of processing, which provide a crucial framework for understanding human-technology interaction. Consider how we interact with a familiar interface, such as our smartphone's home screen. When we first encountered this interface, every action required conscious thought – which icon represented which app, how much pressure to apply for a successful tap, and the meaning of various gestures. This formative stage represents what Norman calls the "reflective level" of processing, characterized by conscious decision-making and active learning. However, through repeated use, these actions gradually shift to the "behavioral level," becoming automated routines requiring minimal conscious awareness. Research from the Digital Behavior Institute (2023) demonstrates this transformation: experienced smartphone users can locate and launch frequently used apps in under 0.3 seconds, often without consciously registering the icons they're selecting. At an even more basic level, the "visceral level" of processing governs our immediate, unconscious responses to interface elements—the satisfaction of a successful swipe, the frustration of an error message. This progression from conscious to automated behavior is not inherently problematic. It serves a crucial purpose in managing our limited cognitive resources. The challenge arises when this automation extends beyond basic mechanical operations to more complex interactions that benefit from thoughtful engagement. For instance, when we mindlessly scroll through social media feeds, automatically clicking 'like' on posts without truly processing their content, we operate primarily at the behavioral level, missing opportunities for deeper engagement and critical thinking.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: The concept of defamiliarization, first introduced by Viktor Shklovsky in 1917, offers a compelling strategy for addressing the challenges of automated behavior. While Shklovsky developed this concept for literary criticism, arguing that art should make the familiar strange to enhance perception, its principles resonate powerfully in our digital age. The parallel between automated reading habits that Shklovsky sought to disrupt and our modern automated digital behaviors is striking. Consider how this concept might apply to our daily digital interactions. When we type on a standard QWERTY keyboard, our fingers move automatically, finding keys without conscious thought. This efficiency comes at the cost of engagement – we're no longer aware of the physical interface we're using or the decisions we're making. However, something interesting happens when confronted with a deliberately scrambled keyboard layout. Our interaction suddenly shifts from the behavioral level back to the reflective level. Each keystroke requires conscious attention, forcing us to engage more deeply with the interface. Research in cognitive neuroscience supports the effectiveness of this approach. A 2023 study by the Neural Plasticity Institute found that novel interaction patterns trigger increased activity in the prefrontal cortex, the brain region associated with conscious decision-making and learning. When participants encountered unfamiliar interface layouts, they showed: ● 47% increase in neural activity related to attention ● 68% improvement in information retention ● Significantly higher levels of engagement compared to familiar interfaces

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: The relationship between technology and behavior forms a complex feedback loop that both shapes and is shaped by our interaction patterns. Modern interfaces are increasingly designed to minimize cognitive load, creating what UX designers call "frictionless experiences." While this approach has clear benefits, it may inadvertently contribute to what psychologist Daniel Kahneman describes as "System 1" thinking – fast, automatic, and often unconscious processing. Take, for example, the evolution of social media interfaces. Instagram's transition from a chronological feed to an algorithm-driven experience illustrates this trend: ● 2010: Users actively chose what to view in a chronological feed ● 2016: Algorithm-driven content required less active decision-making ● 2020: Auto-playing Reels further reduced user agency ● 2024: AI-curated content streams minimize conscious interaction This evolution reflects a broader trend in digital design, where interfaces increasingly anticipate and automatically fulfill user needs. While efficient, this approach may contribute to what sociologist Sherry Turkle calls "the flight from conversation" – a retreat from active engagement to passive consumption.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: The challenge is not to eliminate automated behaviors entirely—they serve valuable purposes in managing cognitive load—but to strategically disrupt them when deeper engagement would be beneficial. This is where the concept of "productive friction" becomes relevant. Consider the design of educational software: research shows that introducing controlled difficulties can actually enhance learning. A study by the Learning Sciences Institute (2023) found that students using interfaces with deliberate elements of friction showed: ● 34% better retention of material ● 42% increase in critical thinking scores ● Higher levels of reported engagement These findings suggest that defamiliarization aims not to create difficulty for its own sake but to introduce what cognitive scientists call "desirable difficulties"— challenges that promote more active engagement and deeper processing.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: Understanding these theoretical foundations – from Simon's attention economy to Norman's processing levels and Shklovsky's defamiliarization – provides a robust framework for addressing the challenges of passive digital interaction. The convergence of these theories suggests that strategic disruption of familiar patterns can lead to more meaningful engagement. With this theoretical groundwork established, we can now turn our attention to practical applications and experimental interventions that implement these principles. The following chapter examines how these theories might translate into concrete design strategies, exploring specific experiments and their outcomes in promoting active engagement in our digital interactions.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: Having established the theoretical framework for defamiliarization in the previous chapter, we now turn to practical applications and experimental interventions. These experiments aim to transform theoretical insights into tangible design solutions that challenge automated behaviors and encourage more conscious engagement with everyday objects and spaces.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: Our first experiment focused on one of the most fundamental and automated human behaviors: walking through a doorway. In typical situations, people pass through doorways with minimal conscious thought—the action is so ingrained that it operates entirely within Norman's behavioral level of processing. This automated behavior presented an ideal opportunity to implement defamiliarization principles. Experimental Design The intervention consisted of a simple yet effective modification: installing curtains across doorways using hooks mounted on both the door frame and adjacent wall. This intervention created an additional barrier for users to engage with before entering the space. The design deliberately introduced what we might call "productive friction" – a controlled disruption of automated movement patterns. Breaking Automated Patterns This intervention works on several levels: 1. It interrupts the expected doorway experience, forcing conscious engagement with the entrance 2. It creates a moment of pause in what is typically an unconscious transition 3. It transforms a straightforward passage into a more deliberate act Observations and Outcomes When encountering the curtained doorway, participants demonstrated notably different behaviors compared to their usual door interactions: ● Initial hesitation upon approaching the doorway ● More conscious and deliberate movement through the space ● Increased awareness of the transition between spaces The curtain intervention effectively transformed what Norman would describe as a behavioral-level interaction into a reflective-level engagement, albeit briefly. This shift aligns with our theoretical framework's prediction that disrupting familiar patterns can lead to more conscious engagement.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: Following our doorway intervention, our next experiment focused on another deeply ingrained daily behavior: putting on a T-shirt. This seemingly simple action is so automated that most people can perform it while half-asleep or engaged in other activities. By deliberately disrupting this familiar pattern, we aimed to transform an unconscious action into a moment of conscious problem-solving. Experimental Design The intervention involved a carefully modified T-shirt, the collar of which was subtly sewn shut using thread in a color similar to the fabric. At first glance, the shirt appears normal, maintaining the expected visual appearance of a standard T-shirt. However, when attempting to put it on in the conventional way—by inserting one's head through the collar— – users encounter an unexpected obstacle. Breaking Automated Patterns This design intervention works on multiple levels: 1. It disrupts a deeply ingrained motor pattern that most adults have performed thousands of times. 2. It forces users to reconsider the fundamental nature of how clothing is worn. 3. It transforms a mindless morning routine into a puzzle requiring active problem-solving. Cognitive Engagement The sealed collar creates what cognitive psychologists call a "pattern interruption"—a moment when automated behavior must give way to conscious problem-solving. This intervention aligns with our theoretical framework in several ways: ● It shifts interaction from Norman's behavioral level to the reflective level. ● It requires active attention rather than passive habit. ● It creates a moment of productive confusion that demands creative thinking. User Response and Observations When confronted with the sealed T-shirt, participants exhibited a range of responses: ● Initial confusion upon discovering the sealed collar; ● Moments of pause and reflection; ● Active exploration of alternative ways to put on the garment; ● Engagement in problem-solving behavior. Like the curtained doorway, this experiment demonstrates how subtle modifications to familiar objects can effectively disrupt automated behaviors and encourage more conscious engagement with everyday activities.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: Our next intervention tackles perhaps the most automated behavior in our digital age: typing. The QWERTY keyboard layout, established in the 1870s, has become so ingrained in users' muscle memory that most proficient typists can't consciously recall where specific keys are located. This deep automation presents an ideal opportunity for exploring defamiliarization through interactive design. Experimental Design The intervention consists of two components: 1. A physically randomized keyboard layout that disrupts the familiar QWERTY arrangement 2. An interactive gaming interface featuring: ○ A Snake game requiring navigation through the new key layout; ○ A typing game challenging users to adapt to the unfamiliar arrangement. This design combines challenge with play, transforming a frustrating experience into an engaging exploration. The intervention encourages users to approach the unfamiliar layout with curiosity rather than resistance by incorporating game elements. Cognitive Engagement and Learning This experiment is particularly significant because it addresses what cognitive scientists call "procedural memory" – the deeply embedded motor skills that typically operate below conscious awareness. When confronted with the randomized layout: ● Users must abandon ingrained typing patterns; ● Each keystroke requires conscious attention; ● New motor patterns develop through active learning. The gaming element adds an essential dimension to the defamiliarization process. Research shows that game-based learning can increase engagement and reduce resistance to challenging tasks. The Snake game, in particular, provides immediate feedback and rewards, helping users build familiarity with the new layout in a low-pressure context.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: Our final experiment challenges another fundamental assumption: the linear progression of book pages. By disrupting the expected page sequence (1,2,3...), this intervention forces readers to engage with text in new and more active ways. Experimental Design The intervention involves reorganizing a book's pages in non-sequential order. A page numbered '1' might be followed by page 99, then page 78, creating a reading experience that: ● Breaks the automatic flow of reading; ● Requires active decision-making about navigation; ● Challenges assumptions about narrative structure. Breaking Linear Cognition This intervention is exciting because it disrupts physical behavior and cognitive patterns of information processing. Traditional page numbering has trained readers to expect and rely on linear progression, often leading to passive content consumption. By breaking this pattern, the intervention: ● Forces readers to actively engage with the text's structure; ● Encourages new ways of understanding narrative flow; ● Creates opportunities for unexpected connections and insights. These two interventions represent more complex applications of defamiliarization principles, moving beyond physical disruption to challenge deeply ingrained cognitive patterns. They demonstrate how strategic disruption of familiar patterns can create opportunities for more engaged and conscious interaction with digital and physical interfaces.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: Through these series of experiments, what emerges most clearly is not just the effectiveness of defamiliarization as a design strategy but the subtle interplay between disruption and engagement. The success of these interventions hinges not on the degree of disruption they create but instead on their ability to transform moments of interruption into opportunities for discovery. Particularly revealing is how participants' initial reactions of confusion or hesitation often evolved into curiosity and exploration. This transformation suggests that when thoughtfully implemented, defamiliarization can do more than simply break habits creating new spaces for learning and engagement. For instance, the gaming elements in the keyboard experiment demonstrate how strategic design can help users navigate unfamiliarity not as an obstacle to be overcome but as an invitation to explore. These findings suggest that the real potential of defamiliarization lies not in its ability to make the familiar strange, but in its capacity to make the strange intriguing. As we continue to navigate an increasingly automated world, this insight offers valuable direction for designers seeking to create more meaningful interactions in both digital and physical spaces.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: At the outset of this research, we observed how deeply automated behaviors have become embedded in our daily interactions with both digital and physical interfaces. From the way we type on keyboards to how we navigate spaces and consume information, these automated patterns, while efficient, often lead to decreased engagement and passive consumption. This research explores how deliberately disrupting familiar patterns through defamiliarization might transform passive interactions into more conscious and meaningful engagements. Several key insights have emerged through theoretical investigation and practical experiments. First, the formation of automated behaviors in our digital age is not merely a matter of convenience but a necessary response to what Herbert Simon termed the "attention economy." As our digital environments become increasingly complex, these automated behaviors serve as crucial cognitive coping mechanisms. However, this efficiency often comes at the cost of meaningful engagement. Our experiments with defamiliarization—from the curtained doorway to the randomized keyboard—demonstrate that strategic disruption of familiar patterns can effectively shift interactions from automated to conscious engagement. Particularly significant was the discovery that successful defamiliarization depends not just on disrupting familiar patterns but on creating what we might call "productive uncertainty." Such moments of pause are productive precisely because they invite exploration rather than frustration. Perhaps most surprisingly, our research revealed that participants often found joy in these moments of disruption when well designed. For instance, the integration of gaming elements in the keyboard experiment transformed what could have been a frustrating experience into an engaging challenge. Such findings suggest that defamiliarization when properly implemented, can do more than just break habits—it creates new spaces for learning and discovery.

author: Yu Zhao
thesis_title: From Familiar to Unfamiliar From Passive to Active
paragraph: These findings have significant implications for how we preserve and promote independent thinking in an age of algorithmic recommendations and automated behaviors. As our digital environments increasingly predict and shape our choices, this research demonstrates how strategic defamiliarization can create moments of pause that encourage critical reflection and autonomous decision-making. This research offers designers and creators a framework for developing interfaces and experiences that challenge passive consumption patterns. The success of these experiments suggests that defamiliarization can be particularly powerful in: ● Breaking through algorithmic echo chambers by disrupting automated content consumption patterns; ● Encouraging users to question and explore beyond their habitual choices and behaviors; ● Creating spaces for independent discovery rather than relying on predictive recommendations; ● Promoting active engagement with content rather than passive acceptance of curated information. These applications are particularly crucial in our current digital landscape, where the convenience of automated recommendations often comes at the cost of independent exploration and critical thinking. By introducing strategic moments of unfamiliarity, we can help users step outside their algorithmic bubbles and rediscover the value of active, conscious engagement with both digital and physical environments. Future Directions While this research highlights the potential of defamiliarization as a design strategy, it also raises more profound questions: In a world saturated with familiar patterns, how can we seize opportunities to disrupt ingrained habits? As digital and physical interactions become increasingly optimized for "frictionless" experiences, how might we identify points of intervention to reawaken curiosity and encourage exploration within these seemingly seamless systems? Future studies could focus on how defamiliarization interventions in everyday objects and interactions can challenge deeply rooted behaviors. Such research implies the rethinking of commonly accepted structures and patterns in design. For instance, does a book always need to follow a linear sequence? Must the flow of a space always align with user expectations? Can a shopping experience disrupt conventional processes to offer fresh perspectives? Additionally, the long-term effects of defamiliarization warrant further investigation: How might breaking habitual routines influence behavior patterns and cognitive flexibility? Can defamiliarization design help users better adapt to rapidly changing environments and foster more creative thinking? Defamiliarization is not just a design strategy but a mindset that challenges the ordinary. Through intentional interventions and the redefinition of the familiar, we can revive engagement and reflection that is lost in the pursuit of efficiency. This methodology offers a gateway to deeper interactions and new possibilities for meaningful engagement in a world increasingly shaped by digital automation.

author: lesli
paragraph: “We are the women who were kidnapped and brought to this country as slaves. We are the women who were raped, are still being raped, and our bastard children snatched from our breasts and scattered to the winds to be lynched, castrated, de-egoed, robbed, burned, and deceived. We are the women whose strong and beautiful Black bodies were and are still being used as a cheap labor force for Miss Annie’s kitchen and Mr. Charlie’s bed, whose rich, black, and warm milk nurtured—and still nurtures—the heir to the racist and evil slavemaster… Black womanhood is outraged and humiliated. Black womanhood cries for dignity, restitution and salvation. Black womanhood wants and needs protection, and keeping, and holding. Who will assuage her indignation? Who will keep her precious and pure? Who will glorify and proclaim her beautiful image? To whom will she cry rape (Lincoln & Bombara & Cade, 1970, p.84)”? For generations throughout global history, the Black woman’s existence has been drowning in narratives that suffocate her reality. She persists in her fight against tropes of being a “firecracker”—otherwise known as the angry Black woman; she is silently accused of smelling like slavery; she is oversexualized and classified as barbaric.

author: lesli
paragraph: Yet, the imprint of the Black woman on society is undeniable. A steward of her land, she prides herself in farm-to-table practices as an indigenous trait, not a modern trend. A maternal fountain of nurture, protection, and nourishment, she overflows into children and families beyond her home. Her voice and vote are pivotal to every political movement. A palette of color and texture, her hair and physical form bear visible features that outsiders seek to emulate. Whether she is in her natural naked form or fully clothed, her head carries a crown as she glides through spaces with effortless elegance and ease.

author: lesli
paragraph: We are disintegrating the Black woman, the milk and honey of our culture. When she is gone, how will we survive without her? Picture this: a world without bees, a critical life force for the existence of all living beings. The Black woman is the pollinator of our society, carrying with her generations of ingenuity that bridge the past, present, and future.

author: lesli
paragraph: I hypothesize that a unique combination of discursive design, olfactic art, and the concept of the Afro Infinite can serve as a social disruptor by maneuvering the subconscious in the short term and re-coding DNA over an extended time, purifying systems—biological and beyond—for future generations. We are at a pivotal moment of dual existence. At one extreme, our climate crisis is more detrimental than ever. At the other end, more Black women are running for positions that will ultimately redefine what it means to have a “Black job”. Historically, Black women have driven solutions from the kitchen to grassroots movements and behind the scenes as support systems to their partners and family. Now is the time to unleash the colonial stronghold that stifles us and allow the Black woman to take center stage. She rejuvenates society by pollinating all she encounters— food systems, waterways, infrastructure, arts, athletics, and policy. I will thoroughly explore this topic by weaving freeform speculative writing that pushes boundaries to the infinite realm of possibility with self-portraits rendered in diverse mediums, including aluminum foil, video projection, and fragrance, with an overarching academic structure to support the formal, iterative placement this topic deserves.

author: lesli
paragraph: This probative question about care and how we invite outsiders into our definition of care will be the foundation for building related theories and frameworks. The concept of care is neutral yet holds a personal resonance with every person. Part of human evolution involves developing a sense of care for oneself, family, friends, and community. My perspective is supported by a theoretical framework introduced to us by Rael Jero Salley. He defines his framework of Black care as “a mode of relational, affective, and communal responsibility expressed through artistic practice. I concur with Salley’s approach and believe that art can be a healing disruptor of “dominant narratives (https://www.raelsalley.studio/)”. The first phase of the research process has culminated with the following probative inquiries that seek to shape the next phase of exploration:

author: lesli
paragraph: How might we define care in a way that tends to our innate healing and prosperity while holding space for others to join us on this multi-generational call to action? How might we activate what Resmaa Menakem has coined as the “technology of Blackness”? How might Black women reclaim our narrative through scent? Phase two of this work will introduce the process of speculative making, rooted in the above three core questions, to unearth unique concepts that can be actualized for public enrichment. I will spearhead this work to ensure the authenticity and accuracy of outputs, forging pathways for other Black women to join me on this journey. I find validation in knowing my approach is a shared sentiment amongst prolific Black female artists that

author: lesli
paragraph: I admire like Mickalene Thomas: "...that’s why it’s important for Black people to also continue to own and provide particular resources… because we have a little bit more control… we have control of the narrative, the conversations that we want to tell, so that way our people are engaged and informed (Washington Post, 2023)”.

author: lesli
paragraph: We can create a social impact design tool to recode cellular trauma. This shift in consciousness can perpetuate behavioral changes experienced in various capacities, from interpersonal to ballot initiatives, with a multi-generational influence. Research has shown us that the sustainability of the Black woman is solely dependent on herself and her innate desire to break free from a victim mentality to live in a sanctuary of her construction. In an interview with MUSEE Magazine, Renee Cox aptly describes the uphill battle we face as we carefully carve out spaces of belonging and understanding: “When you have Black people who express self-love, it becomes revolutionary for white society. They have perpetuated over the past 400 years that you, the African American, are a victim. You’re starving. You don’t know which way is up. You’re a mess, basically. I think once you have a Black person who steps up and says, “No, I’m not going to be your victim, I’m not interested in being a victim,” now suddenly it’s like, “Oh my God, this one is full of herself, how dare she think she can be a superhero or anything for that matter except some slave-like person.” We must not acquiesce when faced with adverse opinions of who we ought to be. Instead, we should continue to disrupt the status quo, moving from slave to superhero”. Although Black women spearhead the core of this work, it is imperative to its upward mobility that publications continue to platform diverse perspectives that acknowledge our impact while holding space for the condition of the Black woman. Samuel Formo, a contributing writer to the April 2025 issue of Brooklyn Rail, aptly depicts his deep appreciation for and understanding of the state of the Black woman and her unfaltering desire to craft narratives that reflect her true, innermost self: “I am a white, cis-gender gay male. Black women have been essential in shaping who I am today, integral to my growth, showing me how to be fearless, resolved, and to take no bullshit, as well as keeping me accountable and aware of the space I occupy as a person with privilege… I can be witness to their experience through their stories and existence and their demand for visibility and equity… I am invited in to share that beauty, to praise it...When I see or read their work, [it affects] me in ways that reach beyond the emotional and into the somatic, my core. I am able to have a moment in which the shared experience is mutual, is silently understood, but also respected as subject matter I can take no ownership of”. When Black women create for other Black women, we unknowingly create for the world. We are the pollinators, carrying the seeds of new ways of being that lead to fruitful futures for all.

author: lesli
paragraph: Fragrance permeates the consumer marketplace as a communication tool for non-visual identity narratives. Valued as a multi-billion-dollar economic sector, data supports consumer interest in its evolution, allowing for innovative advancements rooted in social change. Centered in co-design, a cohort of industry experts, including chemists and botanists, specialized physicians and philosophers, designers and educators, can bring any olfactory vision to life. Together, we can push boundaries, apply theories, and generate a new mode of communication rooted in socio-emotional evolution. According to a 2024 Nielsen Report, Black women spend more than $7 billion on self-care, yet less than 2.5% of beauty brands are owned by Black people. This stark statistic further underscores the importance of research and development in this sector to meet an underserved population’s needs.

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: by Zoe Bartholot We designers love to fashion ourselves as “problem solvers” but let’s face it: the line between solving problems and creating them is blurry at best. — Michael Rock, Executive Creative Director of 2x4 INTRODUCTION

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: When was the last time you interacted with a digital interface? Fifteen minutes ago? Ten? Perhaps right now, as you read this on your phone or computer? Digital systems have become so embedded in our lives that sometimes we forget to stop to think about their impact. Whether ordering groceries, managing finances, or scrolling through social media, these interfaces shape how we interact with technology, often in ways we don’t realize. Behind every tap, swipe, and click is the work of User Experience (UX) design, a silent yet powerful force that influences our emotions, decisions, and behaviors. To understand the significance of UX consider a simple object—a cup, for example. The way a cup is designed—its handle, weight, size, and radius—all determine how easily we use it. A well-designed cup feels intuitive, blending function with form to serve its purpose without effort. UX applies this thinking to digital spaces by crafting interfaces that guide users through tasks smoothly. But, unlike a physical object, the consequences of poorly designed or manipulative digital experiences are often hidden, manifesting as subtle frustrations, stress, or even unintentional actions. As digital systems mediate more aspects of daily life, these experiences become increasingly significant. As a young designer, I fell in love with UX because of its ability to improve people’s lives. At its core, UX is a human-centered discipline, rooted in understanding behavior and designing systems to meet users’ needs. However, my admiration for the field is tempered by concern. Over time, UX has drifted from its original purpose. What began as a craft aimed at helping users has, in many cases, shifted toward prioritizing corporate goals, often at the expense of the very people it was designed to serve. Deceptive design strategies that manipulate users into taking actions they don’t intend to (i.e. dark patterns) are a troubling example of this shift. My personal connection to this topic stems from my experiences during the COVID-19 pandemic, a time when digital interfaces became essential for nearly every aspect of life. Living in San Francisco, I witnessed how technology could simultaneously enable connection and create barriers. The realization that UX design could evoke such strong emotional and behavioral responses ranging from frustration to joy sparked my curiosity and concern about its ethical implications. I began to ask myself: How aware are people of the role UX plays in shaping their IINNTTRROODDUUCCTTIIOONN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: digital experiences? How can the field evolve to better serve its users? The research I present in this thesis is not intended to dismiss UX. Instead, I hope to reflect in detail on how it can be realigned with its ethical roots. I believe UX should be a tool for empowerment, fostering transparency and trust rather than manipulation. My goal is to illuminate the unseen ways UX influences our lives, advocate for more ethical practices, and encourage both designers and users to engage with technology more consciously. Two key issues shape this thesis. The first is a lack of public awareness about UX. In a survey I conducted, 39% of participants said they had never heard of UX and even those familiar with the term often struggled to define it. This knowledge gap leaves users vulnerable to the influence of design choices they don’t fully understand. The second issue is how UX has shifted away from its original purpose. It was once centered on usability and accessibility, ensuring systems served people. Now, however, the rise of dark patterns and engagement-driven design demonstrates how UX has been co-opted to prioritize profit over user empowerment. This thesis is my attempt to answer these questions by examining the origin and effects of UX’s silence. It explores how UX operates as a “silent force” by influencing users’ lives without their awareness and investigates the ethical challenges posed by this influence. By combining research methods such as surveys and interviews, I aim to bridge the knowledge gap between designers and users, revealing how UX can simultaneously empower and manipulate. Ultimately, this work is for both designers and non-designers. For designers, it offers a critical reflection on their craft, urging a return to ethical, user-centered practices. For non-designers, it invites them to look behind the curtain to understand the invisible forces shaping their interactions with technology. I want to make visible an aspect of design that has faded away from the commercial process, reminding the industry that awareness is a crucial aspect of the autonomy of the human being at the center of its operations. PART 01

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: My Journey With UX Design Living Through the Digital Shift During COVID-19 Shifts in the UX Design Industry: The Evolving Role of Designers UX as an Overlooked Force A Deeper Look Into Public (Mis)understandings of UX The Importance of Bringing Attention to UX Design

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: My journey with UX design started with a pivotal moment in high school when I shadowed employees on the design team at the company where my father worked. At the time, I had already figured out that math and science weren’t for me, but I wasn’t sure what path I wanted to pursue. All I knew was that I wanted to help people. That day was transformative—it was the day I fell in love with UX design. It struck me as the perfect intersection of art, design, and psychology, all wrapped up in technology. UX became the way that would allow me to connect human behavior with the digital world. There is a significant difference between doing design and being a designer. Doing design can feel mechanical, following patterns, principles, or guidelines to achieve a functional outcome. On the other hand, being a designer involves a deeper connection to the work; it’s about empathizing with users, predicting their needs, and considering how design affects their emotions. This distinction became clear as I started working on real-world projects and user interfaces where I saw firsthand how technology could evoke good and bad emotions. When technology works smoothly, it brings a sense of relief and satisfaction that I’ve experienced many times myself. For example, my undergraduate school required Medical Evacuation & Repatriation insurance for all international students, which I did not have at the time. I scrambled to book the insurance policy online with a company called Seven Corners and immediately felt a wave of relief when I began the process and realized how intuitive their website was. The smooth, easy-to- navigate design helped me understand what I was purchasing, reducing the stress that often comes with buying complex insurance policies. On the other hand, when technology fails the frustration can be immense. In 2022, for example, I needed to book an appointment with the U.S. government to get my Green Card. The website felt like a labyrinth of poorly designed forms, broken links, and unclear instructions. It was an incredibly stressful and complex process as a whole and the lack of good UX only added to my anxiety. Another frustrating example is something as simple as trying to find the customer service call button on Amazon’s website. That button is buried so deep in their site that it feels like they actively don’t want you to call—they’d rather you give up or figure it out yourself. These experiences for me are clear reminders of how bad design can lead to PART 01

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: unnecessary stress and irritation. Moments like these have helped me to see UX not just as a job or a career but as a way to make a tangible difference in people’s lives. My personal experience has shaped my drive to create designs that prioritize the user’s emotional journey whether it’s relief, satisfaction, or even joy. In recent years, however, these experiences also revealed something else: a lot of people are unaware of the power that UX design holds over them. This was later confirmed and underscored by research I conducted for this thesis. During my undergraduate studies, I attended a dinner party with my partner David, a physicist. As we introduced ourselves to a couple, I mentioned that I studied UX/UI design. The couple cocked their heads and said, “I’ve never heard of that, what is it?” That moment was eye-opening for me. While David’s field of physics was quickly recognized and respected, UX seemed invisible despite its constant presence in daily life. It’s something we all interact with but few people truly understand how much it shapes our experiences and decisions. That dinner party conversation planted a seed in my mind—I realized I wanted to shine a light on UX and give users the language to articulate their experiences with the digital tools they use every day. This personal reflection is not only a window into my journey as a UX designer, but it also reveals broader trends I’ve noticed in my work. Users often feel frustrated when interacting with technology but they don’t always understand why. They can’t put into words the exact flaws of a website or an app, yet they intuitively know when something feels “off.” These observations have fueled my commitment to creating better, more intuitive designs as well as to develop tools—like the educational card game in my thesis—to help users and designers alike understand the deeper mechanics that tie human behaviors and user experience design together. As Sam Aquillano, Executive Director and Founder of the Design Museum Foundation, stated in Design Miami in 2021:

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: The reflective nature of this journey is what makes UX such a powerful, silent force. It’s more than just aesthetics or usability; it’s about how technology makes us feel. Whether those feelings are positive or negative, they have a profound impact on how we interact with the world. THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: During the COVID-19 pandemic San Francisco, a city known for housing the tech-industry-fueling Silicon Valley, quickly became a testing ground for digital platforms. Like many others, I found myself relying on technology for even the most basic aspects of my daily life. Grocery shopping, medical appointments, social interactions, and school all transitioned into virtual spaces overnight. These rapid shifts not only transformed the way we now interact with the world but also brought into sharp focus the role UX design has in shaping our daily interactions with technology. One particularly memorable moment for me was when I had to book a medical appointment online for the first time. With physical offices closed and in-person consultations off the table, telehealth became the new norm. I remember logging onto the platform expecting this to be an easy process given the stakes of healthcare during a pandemic. Sadly, this was not the case. What could have and should have been a straightforward process turned into a very complex problem that I could not solve on my own. The design lacked clarity—navigation was convoluted and crucial features like appointment availability were buried under layers of menus. The overall experience was anything but user-friendly. I even wondered if, as with the Green Card obstacles, these design choices were meant to discourage patients from accessing care. As I struggled to book that appointment I wondered how people less tech-savvy than I, older, with no strong knowledge of design patents, could figure out how to advocate for themselves, especially in a time of crisis. People were not just booking movie tickets or ordering food—they were trying to access vital services in moments of uncertainty. As a UX designer, there are shortcuts and patterns I can decipher when looking at a website for the first time. I automatically visualize in my head the flow of things and layers of information which helps me navigate even the most frustrating user experiences. This pandemic-era experience, amongst many others, only reinforced my belief that UX design plays a vital role in people's lives. Not everything about the pandemic was negative—it also highlighted instances of great design. For example, I relied heavily on Zoom, the virtual meeting app that gained widespread popularity during this time. Founded by Eric Yuan in 2011, Zoom PART 01

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: was built on Yuan’s expertise as the former Corporate Vice President of Engineering at Cisco, where he led the development of collaboration software. The platform’s simplicity and ease of use stood out—clear buttons, intuitive navigation, and minimal barriers to entry. In a time when learning new systems felt overwhelming, Zoom’s thoughtful design allowed me to focus on communication rather than figuring out how to use the tool. In a particularly unique experience, my art classes (which included a drawing class) transitioned over to Zoom. As a design/art major, it was daunting to imagine how we would adapt such physical, hands-on classes to online platforms. Our professor, a lovely older gentleman in his 70s, initially struggled with the technology. Teaching something as tactile as drawing through a screen seemed impossible at first, but Zoom’s structure made the transition smoother than expected. We would sit on Zoom for four hours, drawing at our desks with our cameras on while the professor guided us through exercises. We’d mute ourselves, listen to his feedback, and unmute to ask questions or show our work. Surprisingly, the platform allowed us to recreate some of the communal feeling of being in a physical classroom. While not a perfect substitute, this experience was a testament to how digital tools, when designed well, can support even the most unlikely of activities. These experiences reinforced for me that UX design is more than just making things look nice and function adequately. It plays an invisible but profound role in guiding users through stressful and even life-altering decisions.
