Whether we are booking a doctor’s appointment, scheduling a Zoom call with family, or navigating new online grocery platforms, the usability of these tools impacts not just efficiency but also mental well-being.

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: of UX as a silent force. The rise of digital platforms as

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: Over the past two decades, UX design has expanded from a discipline centered on understanding and enhancing user experiences into a multifaceted and highly remunerative field shaped by complex market demands. Initially, UX designers focused on usability and user satisfaction, crafting interfaces that were intuitive, accessible, and responsive to people’s needs. Unfortunately, as the digital landscape has grown so has the competition among companies vying for user attention. This shift has fundamentally changed employers’ expectations of UX designers, often requiring them to take on roles beyond UX and UI such as content creation, market research, data analysis, and strategy. Rather than dedicating their efforts solely to creating user-centered designs, designers now face the pressure to align with business metrics like engagement and conversions. This evolution marks a subtle yet significant transformation in UX, introducing new responsibilities and challenges that redefine what it means to be a UX designer in today’s digital economy. Evolving Job Expectations and Titles Job listings in the design industry have evolved dramatically over the past few years. The traditional “UX/UI Designer” title, once the popular title across job posting websites, has splintered into various specialized roles such as separate UX, UI, and Product Design positions. These distinctions show a recognition of the specialized skill sets each role brings. But, instead of narrowing down on responsibilities the opposite occurred—the shift has led to broadened responsibilities for designers. Today, employers often expect a UX designer to possess a blend of skills that go beyond core design. They are increasingly required to handle content creation, conduct research, analyze data, and even engage in statistics-based decision- making. This multidisciplinary expectation demands that designers continuously adapt, learn, and juggle tasks that might fall outside their initial training or areas of passion. [1] This shift in expectations and industry standards presents both opportunities and challenges for designers. On one hand, it encourages a more comprehensive understanding of the product life cycle, allowing designers to become valuable contributors to broader business strategies. On the other hand, these expanding PART 01

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: roles can dilute the designer’s focus, spreading them thin across tasks that may not align with their core competencies or interests. For some, the pressure to meet business objectives may override the time and thought required to develop truly user-centered designs. Additionally, the new delineation of UX and UI roles (alongside Product Design) reflects how companies now view these fields as highly specialized areas rather than a cohesive whole. This evolution in the designer’s role has significant consequences for both the field of UX design and the designers themselves. With increasing responsibilities spanning content strategy, user research, and data analytics, designers are under ramping pressure to perform in roles that demand expertise across a wide range of areas. This “jack-of-all-trades” expectation may lead to fragmented design practices where individual roles focus narrowly on separate aspects at the expense of a cohesive and seamless user experience. For companies, this shift has led to a growing demand for rapid iterations and faster product releases to remain competitive. Sadly, the drive for speed in UX often comes at a cost. When UX teams prioritize fast turnarounds the time for thoughtful, iterative design is often reduced, leading to decisions that are less reflective of user needs and more geared toward hitting metrics. From a user perspective, this emphasis on rapid, data-driven design can lead to products that feel rushed or impersonal, where engagement tactics overshadow usability. The Shift Away from User-Centered Design In the early years of UX, these business pressures didn’t exist—e-commerce and websites that were once supplementary have now become primary. As digital platforms expanded and data-driven decision-making became central, UX began to intertwine with business objectives that sometimes conflicted with user-centered design principles. Market pressures have increasingly prioritized design elements that drive business goals such as prolonged engagement or conversions over those that solely support user well-being. For instance, social media platforms have been designed with features that encourage continuous scrolling, creating an addictive loop that keeps users engaged. While this benefits the company by increasing active user time, it may have unfavorable effects on users by contributing to digital fatigue and even addictive behaviors. THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: In this shifting landscape, designers play a pivotal role in keeping UX “silent” by working to create seamless, intuitive experiences that prioritize user needs and well-being, even in the face of market pressures. As designers are tasked with balancing user advocacy and corporate objectives, it is essential for them to push for solutions that align with the foundational principles of UX: empathy, usability, and accessibility. By doing so, designers can strive to maintain the integrity of UX’s original user-centered trajectory even as their roles and responsibilities continue to evolve. This balance between supporting users and fulfilling business goals is crucial in sustaining UX as a field dedicated to enhancing human experiences.

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: UX design has become an integral aspect of our digital landscape that shapes how we interact with technology. However, despite its growing importance UX remains an overlooked force, operating quietly behind the scenes. Through my research into the historical development of UX, it became apparent that while UX design plays a critical role in shaping user behavior and decision-making, the general public often remains unaware of its influences. For us to understand how UX came to occupy this invisible yet pivotal position, it is necessary to look back at its origins and the key milestones that have defined its rise. History of the Rise of UX While the concept of UX design has its roots in the mid-20th century, it only truly began to take shape when personal computers were first introduced in the 1980s. [2] One of the earliest and most influential figures in this field was Don Norman who coined the term “User Experience” in the early 1990s while working at Apple. [3] His book The Design of Everyday Things (1988) laid the groundwork for how designers approach the usability of products, focusing on how design should prioritize ease of use, user satisfaction, and human psychology. Norman’s work was instrumental in shifting design thinking from being product-centric to user-centric, emphasizing that design should facilitate intuitive and enjoyable interactions with technology. As technology progressed through the 1990s and into the early 2000s, the web became an essential medium for interaction and UX became increasingly critical. Jakob Nielsen, another pioneer in the field, advanced the importance of usability and PART 01

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: web design through his work on usability heuristics such as simplicity, consistency, and feedback. Nielsen’s contributions helped guide the principles of modern UX by aiming to reduce the cognitive load on users, directly influencing how people interact with digital interfaces. [4] Once UX design became a key component of digital interfaces, companies began to realize the power it held. This marked a turning point where the intention behind UX started to shift from solely enhancing user experience to increasing engagement and profit. [5] Corporations, especially in the social media and e-commerce sectors, began leveraging UX design to subtly influence user behavior. Techniques such as infinite scrolling, push notifications, and persuasive design patterns emerged, all designed to keep users engaged for longer periods. [6] These UX elements are now used strategically to drive user retention, clicks, and purchases, sometimes at the expense of the user’s well-being. Key Milestones in UX History 1. The Early Days: Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) – The field of UX design began to formalize in the 1970s and 1980s alongside the rise of human- computer interaction (HCI). [7] Researchers like Douglas Engelbart, the developer of the computer mouse, and Alan Kay, the pioneer of graphical user interfaces (GUIs), laid the groundwork for more intuitive interactions between humans and technology. [8] These developments aimed to make technology accessible to a broader audience, moving beyond professionals in the field and into the hands of everyday users. 2. The 1990s: The Emergence of UX as a Discipline – As personal computing became more prevalent, UX shifted from hardware to software design. Don Norman's work at Apple solidified the role of UX as a crucial element of product development. His emphasis on user-centered design led to the widespread adoption of usability testing and the creation of standards for digital interfaces that prioritize user needs. [9] 3. The 2000s: UX in the Age of the Internet – The rise of the Internet and web- based applications brought UX to the forefront of technological development. Companies like Google and Amazon recognized early on the importance of designing intuitive and frictionless user experiences. [10] UX became essential to product success as user satisfaction directly correlated with engagement, retention, and revenue. THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: 4. The 2010s: The Dark Side of UX – With the rise of mobile apps and social media, UX took a turn—one that wasn’t always in the user’s best interest. [11] Dark patterns, a term coined by Harry Brignull in 2010, refer to design elements that intentionally deceive users into taking actions they might not otherwise choose such as subscribing to services or providing personal information. [12] This marked a significant shift as companies increasingly used psychological principles embedded in UX design to manipulate user behavior for profit. Positive and Negative Influences of UX UX design has undeniable benefits. When it is done well it simplifies complex tasks, making digital experiences seamless and intuitive for everyone. Apps like Google Maps revolutionized navigation, giving users turn-by-turn directions and real-time updates. Online shopping platforms like Amazon have made purchasing items easier and faster than ever before, removing barriers to completing transactions with features such as one-click purchases and easy checkout processes. However, there is also a darker side to UX where design elements are used to manipulate rather than empower users. [13] Social media platforms, for instance, use UX to foster addiction by implementing infinite scrolling and variable reward systems that trigger dopamine releases in the brain, just like the psychology of gambling. These designs create habitual behavior, keeping users on platforms longer—sometimes to their detriment. Studies have shown that prolonged social media use can negatively impact mental health, particularly in young adults. [14] This dark side of UX exploits the research into psychology on which UX design was originally based. This exploitation often allows UX to obscure or hide information, guiding users toward decisions that benefit companies rather than users. For example, dark patterns in e-commerce websites often make it difficult to cancel subscriptions or find customer service contact information, leaving users frustrated and trapped in unwanted services. For instance, Amazon infamously buries its customer service contact information deep within the labyrinth that is its site navigation. Such manipulative designs ruin trust and highlight the ethical dilemmas that UX designers face when balancing business goals with user well-being. PART 01

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: The Need for Public Understanding This historical context underscores the urgency of raising public awareness about UX’s role in shaping behavior. As UX continues to evolve, it wields an invisible power over how users engage with technology. Public understanding of UX principles is vital because it empowers users to recognize when their behaviors are being influenced. When users understand that psychological principles such as cognitive biases and decision-making shortcuts are manipulating their digital experiences, they are better equipped to make informed decisions. This understanding would also put commercial pressure on companies to adopt more ethical UX practices, promoting transparency and prioritizing user well-being. As this thesis will explore in detail, UX design must balance its powerful influence with ethical considerations, ensuring that users are empowered and protected from exploitation. THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: In today’s digital age, UX design shapes nearly every digital interaction we have, silently impacting our decision-making process, emotions, and behaviors. Yet, for most users, the principles guiding these interactions remain hidden, unrecognized, and rarely questioned. My research examines this knowledge gap and aims to uncover how users perceive UX, their level of understanding of its impact, and how this hidden layer of design can lead to both enhanced engagement and unintended manipulation. Through a blend of qualitative and quantitative research—including surveys, cultural probes, and ethnographic observations—I sought to understand the public’s awareness of UX and its impact on their digital interactions. Findings and Insights: A Snapshot of Public Understanding To investigate the public’s perception of UX, I conducted a survey to gauge users’ familiarity with the concept. This survey, which I distributed to 59 participants, asked three core questions: “Have you ever heard the term UX design before?”, “Do you know what UX design is?”, and “Can you describe UX design in your own words?” The results revealed a significant awareness gap: 39 percent of respondents had never heard of UX design, 18.6 percent were unsure, and only 42.4 percent indicated some familiarity with the term. Responses to the third question—asking participants to define UX—further illuminated a limited or vague understanding, often focusing solely on “website design” or “ease of use” with few recognizing the deeper psychological impact that UX design can have. To further explore this lack of awareness, I conducted in-person interviews followed by a week-long take-home exercise called the “UX Notebook.” This exercise was designed to capture users’ personal experiences with technology, both from a technical and emotional perspective. Over the course of a week, participants documented their interactions with various digital interfaces, reflecting on their emotions, the decisions they made, and how those decisions were influenced. The findings were very clear: Participants demonstrated strong emotional responses to their digital interactions. Whether it was frustration with an app’s slow performance, satisfaction from a seamless checkout experience, or joy from a well-designed social media feed, these emotions deeply affected the user experience. What stood PART 01

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: out the most was how these emotional responses were often triggered without the user being aware of the UX design principles that were at play. Users described their emotions as driven by the app or platform itself, but when asked to elaborate, they couldn’t identify what specifically in the design had shaped those emotions. This demonstrates the “silent” aspect of UX and how it shapes user behavior without any conscious recognition of that process. A few critical patterns emerged: 1. Emotional Triggers and Subconscious Engagement: Many participants, especially when using social apps, admitted to turning to these platforms in moments of stress or boredom. This behavior aligns with my initial research which suggested that app designs such as Instagram’s infinite scroll feature frequently exploit emotional triggers to keep users engaged without their conscious realization. These emotional reactions to digital platforms powerfully indicated the “silent” role that UX plays in people’s lives. It’s not just that UX is invisible—it is emotionally charged and deeply connected to users’ psychological states. 2. Trust and First Impressions: Users highly value intuitive, visually appealing interfaces and place a strong emphasis on trust-building within the initial moments of interaction. Responses indicated that users often abandon apps or websites that feel overwhelming, unclear, or intrusive within seconds, reinforcing the critical role of UX in capturing and sustaining user engagement. However, users rarely recognize these elements as deliberate design choices; they simply “trust” or “distrust” an app based on initial interactions, often without understanding why. 3. Blurring of Technology Boundaries in Daily Life: Participants revealed an increasing overlap between technology and non-technology interactions, with many expressing difficulty distinguishing where one ends and the other begins. For example, a seemingly simple tool like a TV remote was often perceived as an extension of their digital ecosystem rather than a standalone device. This seamless integration of digital-related tools into daily routines illustrates how technology quietly weaves itself into even the most mundane activities, often without users consciously recognizing its role. THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: The Knowledge Gap: UX as a Silent Force Designers create user experiences to optimize user interactions, but without user understanding it functions as a powerful, invisible hand guiding their choices. This lack of understanding leaves users vulnerable to subtle design manipulations, particularly during moments of emotional vulnerability or habitual app usage, creating a potential for exploitation. Users are often not cognizant of how design choices are engineered to increase engagement, nor do they see how these elements tap into subconscious behaviors. This lack of recognition and understanding is what first inspired me to title my thesis The Silent Force of UX. The fact that such a substantial portion of users are unaware of the mechanisms shaping their online interactions speaks to how powerful yet invisible UX is. It’s everywhere, influencing decision- making, behavior, and emotions all while operating silently in the background. This phenomenon is crucial to understanding why UX is such an essential but under- appreciated aspect of modern digital design. Why This Research Matters The research outlined in this section is foundational to understanding why UX is so important, and why its influence is so dangerous when left unchecked. By acknowledging that UX is an invisible force, present in every digital interaction yet largely unnoticed, I aim to shine a light on its critical role in shaping our modern lives. This research isn’t just about uncovering how UX shapes decisions; it’s about empowering users to recognize and understand the silent mechanisms that drive their behavior. This thesis calls for a shift in the way designers approach their work. Designers must acknowledge and respect the “silent force” of UX. The power UX holds over emotional and behavioral decision-making is not inherently negative, but without transparency, awareness, and ethics it can easily become manipulative. The future of digital technology lies in how we understand and navigate these silent forces and it is the responsibility of both designers and users to ensure that this influence is exercised ethically. PART 01

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: It is impossible to overstate the invisible yet pervasive influence of UX design on our daily lives, yet it remains an area that receives so little attention from the general public despite being interacted with on a daily basis. My personal motivation to bring awareness to this stems from a desire to illuminate the hidden forces that shape our digital experiences. As someone who has worked in UX, I have seen firsthand how design choices can guide user behavior, emotions, and decisions, often without the user’s conscious awareness. This realization has driven me to advocate for a more informed and transparent understanding of UX, not only within the design community but also among the general public. Everyone deserves to know what is being done to them, even in the digital world. It became clear to me early on in my career that UX is often misunderstood or overlooked entirely, despite its critical role in shaping the technology that defines modern life. Whether it's helping someone find the information they need quickly or frustrating them with unclear navigation, UX has a profound impact on our digital lives. And yet, many people remain unaware of how their behaviors are being subtly influenced by design elements like layout, color, and feedback loops. This lack of understanding creates a gap between the users and the designers, one that I believe needs to be bridged. It is not enough for UX to just be functional—it needs to be understood. By helping users recognize how design affects their experiences, we can move toward a more transparent and user-centered digital world. As digital products grow ever more integrated into daily life, this lack of awareness around UX design becomes increasingly consequential. UX designers wield the power to shape user experiences for both positive and nefarious purposes. By shining a light on UX’s subconscious influence, I aim to advocate for greater user awareness and education. Just as we educate ourselves about media literacy or online privacy, I believe there is an ethical imperative to understand how UX shapes digital behavior. This research provides a foundation for this call to action, showing that UX operates as a silent force that can enhance, influence, or manipulate without user consent or awareness. This thesis will explore how we might bridge the knowledge gap between designers and users in order to foster a more transparent, ethical, and empowering approach to digital interaction design. PART 02

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: The Intersection of Psychology and UX Design The Evolving Role of Psychology in Shaping Digital Experiences Recognizing Influence and Intention in UX Design For Better or Worse?

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: Psychology forms the backbone of UX design. It helps designers understand how users think, feel, and behave. By embedding psychological principles into digital products, UX designers can create experiences that are not only functional but also resonate on an emotional and cognitive level with people. Foundational psychological concepts—such as cognitive load, decision-making biases, and emotional engagement—are essential for shaping how users interact with digital interfaces, influencing everything from task completion to overall satisfaction. Together, psychology and UX become a bridge between a product’s functional aspects and the user's needs, affecting usability, engagement, and perception of the product. In my experience, one of the most significant contributions of psychology to UX design is its ability to predict and guide user’s needs. Psychological principles help us designers foresee users’ reactions and needs, enabling us to create more intuitive and satisfying experiences. For instance, understanding cognitive load allows designers to reduce unnecessary mental effort for users by streamlining interactions and eliminating distractions. Knowledge of decision-making biases such as choice overload also helps designers guide users toward recommended options without overwhelming them. Emotional design, however, goes a step further by tapping into users’ feelings, helping foster a sense of connection and enjoyment with the product that builds loyalty and trust. By applying the insights of psychology, UX design has the power to remove all burdens from users by achieving a balance between meeting user needs and enhancing their digital experiences. But like all good things, using psychology in UX design has its downsides. After years of designers studying user to better understand their needs and how they think, some companies now produce experiences that prioritize their own profit margin rather than the well- being of their customers. Don Norman first coined the term “user experience” in 1993 to bring people’s needs into design decisions. So when and where did UX get derailed from its initial trajectory? Is it purely because of capitalism? If so, how can we, as designers, bring it back on the right track? PART 02

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: Significance of Psychology in UX Psychology’s role in UX today is indispensable because it directly influences the effectiveness and accessibility of design choices. When designers understand psychological principles they can create products that feel intuitive and empower users to accomplish their goals more efficiently, which in a world that now features such a prominent digital landscape is vital. This foundational understanding leads to more successful products as users seek out and return to experiences that feel seamless and familiar. A design that lacks psychological insight or uses them wrong risks creating confusion, frustration, and even unintended harm, particularly if it disregards the user’s cognitive limitations or exploits their vulnerabilities. [15] Platforms like TikTok and Instagram, for example, arguably cause more harm than good to young teenagers by using some of these principles to keep them logged on longer and become addicted. This use of psychology was first introduced to the field of UX design as a solution to help people better interact with everyday interfaces. By leveraging concepts such as cognitive ease and emotional resonance, designers have created products that not only capture user attention but also encourage longer interactions and return visits. One of the most influential voices in this area is Jon Yablonski, whose 2024 work in Laws of UX demonstrates how psychological laws can directly inform design practices to create seamless user experiences. [16] Yablonski’s principles include concepts like Hick’s Law, which posits that the time taken to make a decision increases with the number of available options. By understanding this simple fact of human nature designers can reduce user cognitive load by limiting choices, especially during critical moments in the user journey such as completing a purchase or signing up or making an appointment. Another principle Yablonski addresses is the Serial Position Effect, which suggests that people are more likely to remember items at the beginning and end of a list, which can be strategically applied to highlight important information. Yablonski’s work provides designers with a practical, psychology-informed framework that simplifies complex digital interactions, helping people get what they need faster and with less frustration. By integrating psychological principles, designers don’t just make interfaces look good, which is a common misconception— they create systems that align with human psychology, ensuring a design is both user-friendly and deeply attuned to user needs. Similarly, Aaron Walter, a prominent THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: A prominent UX designer in the field named Aaron Walter published a book in 2011 titled Designing for Emotions which underscores the importance of engaging users at a personal and emotional level. [17] Walter argues that by evoking positive emotions designers can create memorable and impactful user experiences that foster loyalty and long-term engagement. In his framework, he outlines four levels of user needs: functional, reliable, usable, and pleasurable. Walter emphasizes that while functionality and usability are fundamental, a pleasurable and emotionally engaging experience is what truly sets an exceptional design apart. He advocates for the use of elements like humor, storytelling, and relatable character design to create interfaces that resonate emotionally and elevate the user experience beyond mere utility. As a young designer in New York, I am constantly reminded that I inhabit a tech savvy and visually oriented demographic bubble. Aaron Walter’s work, however, serves as a personal reminder that design must go beyond this bubble—it has to be accessible and meaningful for everyone. While functionality and usability are undeniably important, they’re not always what resonates most with users; for many, an emotional connection to an interface can make all the difference, helping them feel engaged and supported in their journey. Psychology plays a vital role in this, allowing designers to understand and anticipate the diverse emotional needs of different demographic groups. For instance, when designing a website for a retirement home it is essential to create an experience that makes the adult children feel welcome and supported as they search for options for their elderly parents. Thoughtful design can make difficult decisions feel just a little easier by fostering a sense of warmth and reassurance, showcasing the powerful potential of UX to go beyond mere usability and truly connect with users. Key Psychological Theories in UX In order to understand how UX design can profoundly impact user behavior, it is essential to explore key psychological theories that underlie effective design practices. Each of the theories presented here—Cognitive Load Theory, Decision- Making Biases, and Emotional Design—serves a unique purpose in shaping user experiences by addressing different aspects of human behavior. I chose these three specific theories not only for their relevance in guiding user-friendly design but also for their potential to highlight the ethical considerations UX designers face: PART 02

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: • Cognitive Load Theory: Cognitive load theory suggests that humans have limited cognitive resources, and when an interface is cluttered or complex it demands more mental effort, reducing usability. UX designers who apply cognitive load theory aim to simplify interfaces by minimizing distractions and organizing content hierarchically. Examples of this in action include minimalistic designs with clear navigation and well-structured menus, which reduce cognitive demands and allow users to complete tasks more easily. [18] For instance, Google’s homepage, with its minimalist design, enables users to focus solely on the search task, enhancing usability by limiting cognitive load. • Decision-Making Biases: Psychological biases in decision-making such as choice overload and confirmation bias shape user behavior and are powerful tools for UX design. Confirmation bias, where users seek information that aligns with their existing beliefs, can influence how content is presented; designers might prioritize showing options that align with users' past interactions to reinforce positive engagement. [19] Choice overload is another significant bias, where too many options can overwhelm users and prevent them from making a choice. UX designers can address choice overload by limiting options or categorizing them to make decision-making easier. For example, streaming services like Netflix and Spotify use personalized recommendations to narrow down choices, aiding user satisfaction and encouraging exploration. • Emotional Design: Emotional design focuses on creating experiences that resonate on an emotional level, helping users connect with products in ways that go beyond functionality. Interfaces that tap into psychological needs like comfort, excitement, and satisfaction can evoke positive emotions, fostering both engagement and brand loyalty. UX designers achieve this through thoughtful visual and interactive elements—such as vibrant colors, friendly animations, or personalized notifications—that resonate with users and create a more enjoyable, memorable experience. [20] For example, Instagram’s use of visuals, heart icons, and encouraging notifications is designed to stimulate positive feelings, encouraging users to engage more frequently. This approach to design is rooted in the belief that products should not only be easy to use but should also delight and inspire. THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN PART 02

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: — Jon Yablonski THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: Examples of Theories in Action We can see one example of cognitive load theory in action by looking mobile banking apps. By applying this theory, designers place in the app’s main screen only essential options such as balance, recent transactions, and a “Quick Actions” button for common tasks like transferring money or paying bills. This simplifies the users’ point of entry on the interface, helping users focus on immediate needs without distraction. However, while this approach enhances usability it also raises concerns about whether users are fully aware of all features or if this streamlined design subtly limits their freedom of choice. In cases like these, psychology can become a double-edged sword—making tasks easier at a potential cost of transparency. Mobile banking apps also tend to utilize decision-making biases by suggesting commonly used payment amounts based on past behavior, making repetitive tasks feel quicker and more intuitive. While this feature reduces cognitive strain, it subtly nudges users to make consistent choices without questioning alternatives, which could unintentionally reinforce habitual patterns. This highlights a tension in psychology-driven UX: while it offers convenience it can also restrict critical engagement, potentially limiting user agency over time. Emotional design is also at play in mobile banking app design. Through calming colors and comforting messages, many of these apps aim to reduce user anxiety in financial tasks, creating a feeling of safety and satisfaction. While this promotes positive engagement, it introduces an ethical dilemma: is the design genuinely fostering well-being or is it creating an illusion of security to keep users engaged? Here lies the risk of potential psychological manipulation. In this example, psychology undeniably assists UX design by making it more user- centered and accessible. But, when psychological insights are used to subtly direct behavior there is potential for misuse and abuse.

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: The integration of psychology into UX design has undergone significant shifts over the past few years, transitioning from what originally was meant to be a primarily human-centered approach to a framework that increasingly incorporates commercial and, at times, manipulative tactics. When the term was first coined, UX aimed to enhance usability and empower users, driven by the belief that designs should serve human needs and improve interaction with digital interfaces. However, as companies recognized the potential in leveraging psychological insights for profit, the core values of UX started shifting. In this section we will explore the evolution of UX design, identifying both the benefits and flaws in its trajectory so far and questioning whether foundational aspects of UX left room for its current, sometimes exploitative, practices. From Human-Centered Design to Commercialized UX When Don Norman introduced human-centered design principles in the late 20th century his focus was clear: usability, accessibility, and user empowerment. Norman emphasized “invisible design,” an approach that would allow technology to fade into the background and seamlessly support users’ goals. This focus on intuitive, easy-to-navigate interfaces placed human needs and behaviors at the core, valuing simplicity and clarity over flashy or distracting features. Norman’s work created the foundation for UX as an empathetic and problem-solving discipline, built on the understanding that good design should quietly guide users. However, as UX evolved, companies in many markets began recognizing the potential for these same principles to drive user engagement in ways that prioritized business goals over user well-being. This shift marks a pivotal moment in UX history: while psychology in UX was initially intended to serve users, it became a tool for directing behavior toward profit-driven outcomes. The question then arises: was the framework itself flawed, leaving too much room for its own misuse? Or was the issue more a result of economic pressures that repurposed well-intentioned principles? Regardless, the trajectory of UX began bending away from user empowerment and towards user retention, engagement, and sometimes even manipulation. THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: Key Milestones in the Intersection of Psychology and UX Here are several major milestones that illustrate the changing face of psychology in UX, marking its transition from supportive to exploitative: 1. The Introduction of Human-Centered Design Principles: This foundational milestone, by Don Norman, brought psychology into UX design with the purpose of making digital interactions accessible, intuitive, and beneficial to users. Norman’s philosophy set UX on a path of empathy-driven design, an idea that would later face challenges as commercial interests reshaped the discipline. 2. The Digital Boom and Emergence of Dark Patterns: As digital platforms grew in the early 2000s, competition for user attention heightened. Companies discovered the power of psychology-based tactics, not only to enhance usability but to encourage prolonged engagement (such as social media platforms becoming addictive). [21] This shift in strategy led to the birth of dark patterns, which unlike early UX principles, put business goals ahead of user well-being. These patterns exploited cognitive biases like loss aversion and confirmation bias, turning UX into a tool for manipulating behavior rather than just guiding it. 3. The Normalization of Manipulative UX Tactics: Today, dark patterns are pervasive across many digital products, demonstrating how far UX has deviated from its initial values. The transition from human-centered design to commercially driven UX signals a shift in its trajectory, one that risks destroying user trust and compromising ethical standards. [22] As designers continue to leverage psychology in ways that prioritize engagement and profitability, UX is at risk of becoming a means for exerting control over user behavior rather than empowering users. Shift to Manipulative Tactics and Dark Patterns As the digital world expanded and competition for user attention grew, businesses sought out more aggressive methods to boost user engagement. While UX was originally rooted in improving usability and user satisfaction, the digital boom of the 2000s gave rise to a new, more manipulative set of tactics. This shift led to the emergence of dark patterns—design choices that exploit user psychology to push PART 02

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: individuals into decisions that serve business interests rather than supporting the user's well-being. The term “dark patterns” was initially coined by Harry Brignull in 2010, a UK-based UX designer who defined the term as “a user interface that has been carefully crafted to trick users into doing things, such as buying insurance with their purchase or signing up for recurring bills.” [23] Done very subtly, these tactics manipulate user behavior to prioritize product metrics, subscription retention, and data collection, frequently at the expense of users’ clarity, autonomy, and trust. What Are Dark Patterns? Dark patterns are design elements that intentionally mislead or coerce users into performing actions that they might not otherwise take if presented with clear, straightforward choices. Unlike user-centered design which focuses on guiding users toward informed decisions, dark patterns deliberately obscure options, disguise actions, and add friction to tasks to influence behavior in favor of business goals. These manipulative tactics tap into cognitive biases to ensure that users are more likely to remain subscribed, make purchases, or accept terms that benefit the company. Example: The Subscription Trap A simple example of dark patterns is subscription traps. Imagine a user signing up for a free trial of an online service, such as a streaming platform or fitness app (e.g. Audible by Amazon). Upon signing up, the user is asked to input payment details with the understanding that they can cancel the subscription anytime during the free trial period. But, when the free trial nears its end, and the user attempts to cancel the service they encounter a plethora of obstacles long and confusing navigation paths, and a request to call customer support, which may involve lengthy hold times. These steps intentionally complicate the cancellation process, making it difficult for users to exit the service easily by hiding the cancellation button under layers of different steps and paths. The goal here is clear: by complicating what should be a one-step process the user is far more likely to become frustrated enough to avoid the process, or even give up on unsubscribing altogether, causing them to continue to pay for service they no longer want or need. THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: What Makes a Dark Pattern “Dark”? The term “dark pattern” suggests an unethical nature, but it is important to note that the distinction between what constitutes a dark pattern and what does not is not always black and white. What some may deem a manipulative design, others might see as simply a clever business strategy. The subjectivity of dark patterns lies in how they are perceived by users. For some, the obscured cancellation process might be seen as a slight inconvenience, but for others it can feel like a deliberate attempt to exploit their time and attention. The essence of a dark pattern lies in its ability to exploit users’ cognitive biases in ways that go against their intentions. The “darkness” of a pattern is ultimately determined by the user’s experience, whether they feel deceived, misled, or coerced into making a decision they did not intend. More importantly, dark patterns rely on a deep understanding of human behavior and psychology. Cognitive biases such as loss aversion (where people are more motivated to avoid losing something than to gain something) are often leveraged in these scenarios. For example, a user who has already invested several weeks or months into an online service may feel reluctant to cancel a subscription because of the psychological weight of what they've already spent, even if they don't derive much value from the service anymore. The Ethical Implications of Dark Patterns Dark patterns represent a growing ethical dilemma in UX design. While the goal of any designer is to create user-friendly, accessible experiences, the rise of dark patterns forces designers to ask: At what point does guiding a user toward a goal cross over into manipulation? Unlike traditional UX principles that focus on understanding user needs, dark patterns are intentionally deceptive, using psychological tactics to guide users toward decisions that are not necessarily in their best interest. This shift underscores the tension between user needs and business goals: A tension that can result in ethical compromises. As the digital landscape continues to grow, and with it the reliance on user data and engagement, dark patterns will likely evolve and get darker. However, it is important to recognize the consequences of these practices not only for users but also for the long-term reputation of brands and platforms. Users who feel manipulated are less likely to trust a company again, and as public awareness of dark patterns grows, users may become more wary of interfaces designed to deceive them. PART 02

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: Reflecting on the Trajectory of UX The adoption of dark patterns represents a turning point in UX. The danger lies in how UX’s psychological foundation can be twisted, leading to a design culture where retention and profit overshadow the user's benefits.

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: As UX designers, it is crucial to understand the power and responsibility we hold in shaping the digital experience. UX design is not just about creating aesthetically pleasing or even functional products; it’s about guiding users through complex systems in ways that respect their autonomy and well-being. While it may be tempting to lean into dark patterns for short-term business gains, the long-term value of user trust and loyalty far outweighs any short-term profits gained from manipulation. Ultimately, the evolution of psychology in UX is a field that is standing at a crossroads. While UX was founded on human-centered ideals, the influence of psychology has made it susceptible to commercial misuse. The trajectory of UX has shown that the same principles meant to support users can also serve corporate interests, highlighting a fundamental flaw in UX’s framework: when profit drives design, psychology becomes a double-edged sword. Recognizing this shift is essential, not only to critique the current state of UX but also to advocate for a realignment back to its core purpose—serving the user’s needs and fostering genuine, transparent interactions in the digital landscape. THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: In UX design, every feature, button, or interactive element is typically embedded with a clear intent and purpose, stemming from an understanding of thinking and decision making. Designers meticulously design and build these elements to guide users smoothly through a digital experience, shape their interactions based on principles that enhance usability, and encourage them to make specific actions. This intentionality in design, while often being well-intentioned, is also strategically complex; every aspect of a design has been researched, iterated, and optimized to predict and direct users’ behavior. However, while designers create with a clear vision of these intended effects, users remain generally unaware of the psychological strategies shaping their engagements, making choices and decisions without an understanding of how their interactions are being influenced. This knowledge gap that I keep referring to—where the designer’s intentions are hidden from the user—creates ground for subtle (and sometimes not so subtle) manipulation. When users navigate an interface, they naturally make the assumption that the layout and features are there purely to enhance their experience and not to nudge them toward certain actions. This gap allows for situations where users’ decisions are carefully shaped but remain invisible to most, blurring the lines between encouragement and manipulation. A very basic example is a checkout process designed with a “one-click purchase” button which makes buying easier and faster (Amazon is known for using this method). This method aligns with the designer’s goal to streamline transactions and make them accessible for people who might be in a rush. However, users are not always aware that this design choice has the power to leverage impulse-buying tendencies, potentially leading to purchases they might not have otherwise considered if the process involved more steps. The longer a process is, the more time someone has to think about their choice and question whether they truly need it; switching the process to a one-click button creates an ease that removes this self-autonomy of choice users hold. The potential for psychological manipulation is heightened when talking about persuasive design elements, such as default options or scarcity messages on items, that very subtly press users toward making decisions without openly presenting other alternatives. While users might think they’re making independent decisions, PART 02

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: these interactions are partly a product of the designer’s intention to influence specific outcomes through design choices. Research in psychology and behavioral economics has shown that people more often than not default to pre-selected options or behave in ways that follow the path that requires the least effort and would cause the least frustration. When designers use these methods to create interfaces with the intention of creating an effortless experience they toe the line of manipulation, inadvertently leading users to outcomes they may not have chosen if they had fully understood the underlying intentions. This invisible influence can be especially impactful when paired with dark patterns—design methods that intentionally exploit cognitive biases to push users toward decisions that benefit the company more than the user. For instance, hiding an “unsubscribe” link or automatically adding items to a shopping cart uses user expectations and trust against them, taking advantage of this knowledge gap. The manipulation may be subtle, even invisible at times, but it exploits the user’s lack of understanding in regards to how design features shape and alter their decisions and behavior while interacting with specific products. This is especially problematic because users lack the tools and insights to critically analyze these interactions in real-time, leaving them vulnerable to design choices that feel natural but carry hidden agendas for companies to gain users and make more money off them. This gap between designers’ intent and users’ understanding raises ethical questions about the responsibility and power that designers hold in shaping user experiences. While guiding users to their desired outcomes can improve usability and accessibility, it also affects users’ autonomy and freedom of choice. The question now becomes: where should UX design draw the line between user- centered assistance and persuasion? This is a critical turning point in the trajectory of UX design as it not only affects the user experience but also brings up the broader impact of UX on users' autonomy and agency within the digital landscape. THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: Case Study: Donald Norman’s Living with Complexity Donald Norman’s Living with Complexity offers a compelling analysis of how designers manage complexity in technology and interfaces, often by simplifying or concealing certain processes to help lighten the load on users. In his book, Norman argues that “complexity is not a bad thing; it is confusion that is bad.” He emphasizes that when complex elements are hidden rather than explained, it can lead users to make decisions without fully understanding the technology they’re engaging with. [24] If users can’t see all the elements on the board, how can we expect them to make the right decisions for themselves? Norman argues that design should make complex systems manageable for everyone without masking anything, allowing users to appreciate the depth of the interfaces they’re using while still having full control over their choices. Norman’s research is especially relevant in today’s digital landscape as the push for “invisible” design often comes at the cost of user understanding. The concept of invisible design is used in the argument that to make user interfaces seamless, designs need to minimize confusion and streamline experiences. On one hand, streamlining of this kind is crucial in creating smooth and efficient user experiences. On the other hand, it can also hide critical information, leading users to engage with features or products they may not fully understand or choose of their own volition. For example, financial apps often simplify complex investment tools by turning them into processes that are only a few clicks long, making it easy for users to invest large sums of money without necessarily understanding the financial risks involved. Norman warns against this type of oversimplification saying, “If things are too easy, people don’t learn.” By making complex processes feel too easy and straightforward, designers may unintentionally encourage users to make decisions without fully grasping the potential outcomes. Such outcomes directly contradict the aims that Norman articulated for UX designers. As Norman puts it, “We need to confront the complexity, not hide from it.” His perspective calls on designers to seek a balance between offering clarity where it matters most and ensuring that users are empowered to engage with digital tools in ways that respect their understanding and agency. PART 02

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: The Growing Presence of UX in Modern Life The digital landscape today is larger and more integrated into our everyday lives than at any point in history and shows no signs of slowing down. The stakes are higher now than ever as the digital landscape has rapidly expanded to infiltrate nearly every aspect of our daily lives. At the center of this growing digital ecosystem stands UX design. It is UX that defines how smoothly we interact with these digital tools, how well these interactions align with our expectations, and ultimately how technology fits into our lives. These constant interactions with technology also mean that people are now engaging with UX design principles throughout their daily lives, often in ways they may not even recognize. This level of immersion is unprecedented and means that users—often subconsciously—experience the influence of UX design in nearly everything they do, from ordering groceries online to booking medical appointments to managing finances through mobile banking. Given the reach of UX in this ever-expanding digital environment, now is the time to critically examine the role of design. From smart homes to screens embedded in cars and wearable devices like Apple Watches, we are surrounded by interfaces and engaging with UX design. These interfaces may seem like conveniences, but they are the latest extensions of a digital world that is constantly shaping and reacting to our behaviors. As technology makes its way into every facet of our lives, UX has more power than ever to influence our actions and our mental health. Between the growth of the digital landscape and people’s increasing self-awareness of mental health, customers are primed to begin the process of demanding that the design community reclaim UX as a tool for genuine user empowerment. UX was born out of a desire to help people, to make their interactions with technology intuitive and accessible; it is imperative that the field returns to its original trajectory and resists the temptation to exploit psychological insights solely for profit. This expansion coincides with another critical development: an increase in mental health awareness. In recent years, particularly since the start of the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, conversations around mental health have broken into mainstream media with movements advocating for openness about well-being thriving on social media and in popular culture. People are becoming more mindful of how their interactions with technology affect their mental health, particularly of how certain design elements can either enhance or disrupt their lives. As more users begin to understand that UX design can play a crucial role in affecting their well-being, THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: they’re calling for technology to be an ally and not an enemy. This cultural shift represents a prime opportunity to reflect on the true purpose of UX design and to realign the field with its foundational principles of helping people. This is a pivotal moment for UX designers to balance innovation with responsibility. While leveraging psychology has enriched user experiences making them more intuitive and engaging, the same techniques can lead to what some call “behavioral lock-in,” where users are kept on platforms longer than they intend without realizing it. [25] For users to make informed decisions, transparency and ethical commitment are crucial in UX design, and it is up to designers to respect and protect user autonomy. As UX continues to evolve it is essential to emphasize mental health, transparency, and ethical practices so that the benefits of psychological insights serve people rather than exploit them. This balance is critical as users increasingly recognize the role that design plays in shaping their digital experiences and seek a more genuine connection between technology and their well-being.

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: Gestalt principles such as proximity, similarity, and continuity demonstrate how psychological principles can create order and clarity in visual experiences. These principles guide users’ attention and help them process information more efficiently. For instance, grouping related items together (proximity) or aligning elements in a continuous flow (continuity) enables users to intuitively understand the structure and meaning of a layout. These same principles can be overly relied upon, used merely to achieve visual appeal without sufficient regard for usability. When design becomes too visually driven, prioritizing aesthetics over functionality, users may find themselves lost or unable to complete basic tasks, resulting in the UX looking impressive but functioning poorly. The intersection of aesthetics and usability is one of the most complex and debated areas within UX design. On one hand, the use of design principles in design can greatly enhance user experiences by simplifying complex problems, improving comprehension, and forging an emotional connection with the product. However, there is also potential for these principles to be misapplied or misinterpreted, creating experiences that may appear visually pleasing but lack true functionality. The contrary can also happen where functional designs disregard aesthetic quality PART 02

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: and fail to engage users in the long term. In UX, achieving a “healthy” balance between aesthetics and usability is vital, but challenging, as sometimes designers lean heavily toward one at the expense of the other. While beautiful design can certainly attract users, to keep them it must also be usable. There is a longstanding cultural divide in UX between graphic design and UX design—as one focuses on creating visually compelling images and layouts, the other looks at users and functionality. This divide often manifests in product design, where the desire for aesthetics may come into conflict with the need for usability. For a product to be effective, it must be both visually engaging and functional, as aesthetics alone are not enough to deliver a smooth user experience. Like all fields in design, UX at times still gets dismissed by people as “just making things pretty,” a misconception that grossly oversimplifies the role of UX and ignores all the research, iterations, and tests that go into making digital products usable. This view reduces UX design to surface-level aesthetics, missing the point that is the silent force behind all our interactions that actually make products accessible and navigable. It is UX that subtly shapes users’ interactions, guiding them toward successful outcomes and minimizing frustration.

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: While some argue that aesthetics naturally enhances user satisfaction, it remains true that overly aesthetic designs, when not grounded in usability, can lead to user frustration and increased cognitive load. A design that is visually complex can mislead users, causing confusion or even mistrust if they cannot easily navigate or complete tasks. The misuse of both Gestalt’s and psychological principles ultimately works against the intended goal of UX, detracting from the user experience rather than enhancing it. Aesthetics and usability both serve the same purpose: to improve user interaction with a product. When aesthetics meet usability, a product not only appeals visually but also works intuitively, guiding users effortlessly through their journey. This balanced approach upholds the original mission of UX—to serve users by creating functional, intuitive, and ethically sound experiences that respect their autonomy and cognitive needs. No field in the design world is just about making things “pretty.” UX is what makes things work. THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: A Balanced Approach The evolution of UX design has shown how seamlessly psychology can enhance digital experiences, helping people interact with technology in more intuitive and satisfying ways. The convergence of psychology and design has allowed UX to develop interfaces that consider cognitive processes, habits, and user expectations, making it an invaluable tool in today’s digital landscape. But as this influence grows, so does the responsibility to use psychological insights thoughtfully. Popular designers in the field like Fabricio Teixeira emphasize that this responsibility extends beyond just making products more “usable”—it means making them more humane. [26] Teixeira’s advocacy for accessibility and inclusivity in UX encourages designers to view psychological principles as tools for supporting user well-being rather than controlling user behavior. This perspective urges designers to consider users’ mental and emotional needs and challenges them to create spaces where users feel empowered, not manipulated. Teixeira’s work aligns with a growing emphasis on mental health and digital well-being, drawing attention to the subtle but profound influence that UX exerts on our lives. His focus on accessibility connects deeply with user-centered design, suggesting that when UX is done ethically, it respects humans as being with agency and doesn’t just view them as users. This balanced approach aims to uplift and empower users, giving them control over their interactions and experiences rather than drawing them into dependency. As a designer cognizant of these industry developments, I advocate for a UX approach that integrates psychology thoughtfully and ethically, creating digital environments that view users as humans with autonomy and honor the field’s original intention: to improve people’s interactions with technology, not to manipulate them. This balanced integration of psychology and ethics sets the stage for the final chapter, where I will address the path forward for UX as it reclaims its focus on user-centered, ethical design practices in an era of increasing digital influence. PART 02

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: — Fabricio Teixeira & Caio Braga PART 03

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: From Road Signs to UX: Mapping How Design is Understood Developing an Educational Card Game as a Learning Tool Designing UX with Transparency and Empowering Knowledge

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: UX design does not exist in isolation. It is deeply rooted in historic design disciplines, borrowing principles from architecture, industrial design, and graphic design. These fields have long shaped how people interact with the built environment, objects, and visual information, prioritizing clarity, usability, and efficiency. Architecture, for instance, dictates the flow of movement through physical space by using layout and spatial hierarchy to guide people naturally from one point to another. Industrial design refines everyday objects, tools, appliances, furniture, etc., ensuring that they are intuitive to use, with affordances that signal how they should function. Graphic design influences perception through visual hierarchy, typography, and layout, determining how information is processed and understood. UX draws from all of these disciplines, applying their core principles to the digital realm. [27] To fully understand the importance of UX’s role, let’s compare it to another designed system that depends on human behavior: transportation. Navigating the internet is not so different from navigating a physical environment; the cursor on your screen acts like your feet on the ground. Consider driving, a practice where design and human cognition are tightly linked to our safety. Road signs, lane markings, and traffic lights were not randomly created; they are the result of careful planning to create a system that drivers can interpret at a glance. A stop sign, for example, does not require an explanation. Its octagonal shape, bold red color, and large, capitalized letters are universally recognized signals that prompt immediate action. These elements were deliberately designed to ensure that even at high speeds, a driver can instantly register and react to the information. The success of this system relies on two things: (1) the effectiveness of the design itself, and (2) the driver’s understanding of how to interpret it. Consider the car’s dashboard. Every element of its interface has been designed with user cognition in mind. Speedometers, fuel gauges, and warning lights all follow standard conventions so that drivers can quickly and instinctively interpret information. A red flashing light signals urgency, a fuel icon indicates low gas and, a green turn signal communicates direction. These choices are based on decades of refinement to ensure clarity, consistency, and ease of use. Drivers may not think about these elements in their daily commutes, but they trust the system because PART 03

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: they understand it. They know that if their check engine light turns on, it means something is wrong. Now compare this to a modern digital interface. The same principles apply to UX design. Just like road infrastructure, digital interfaces are created to guide users through a system, whether that be an app, a website, or a software platform. A well-designed interface ensures that users intuitively know where to click, what actions are available to them, and how to achieve their goals with minimal friction. Just as drivers do not consciously think about how they process road signs, digital users often do not realize how much their behavior is shaped by design decisions. In both cases, the design is most successful when it operates invisibly, when it feels natural, seamless, and frictionless. A user navigating an app or website might encounter a pop-up message, a notification badge, or an autoplay feature. On the surface, these elements seem just as intuitive as a car dashboard, they grab attention, signal an action, and guide the user. But the key difference lies in intent. In the case of a car dashboard, the design choices exist primarily to inform the user and support their decision-making. In the case of digital UX, the choices might serve another purpose such as encouraging longer screen time, nudging users toward a purchase, or collecting data. There is a crucial difference between the two. Road signs and traffic systems are publicly regulated, standardized, and built around safety and efficiency. There is an expectation that these design choices serve the public good. UX, on the other hand, operates in a largely commercial space where business goals often shape design decisions. This is where the silent force of UX becomes particularly important to examine. Unlike road signs, which aim only to inform and guide, UX can also be used to influence, persuade, or even manipulate. Because users are not always aware of the mechanisms guiding their behavior, they may unknowingly be steered toward specific actions—whether that be subscribing to a service, making a purchase, or spending more time on a platform than they intended. This distinction highlights a central tension within UX design: is its invisibility a strength or a vulnerability? When users understand designs, like traffic infrastructure, they retain autonomy. They know what a red light means, and what a one-way sign indicates, and they use that knowledge to make informed decisions. But when UX design operates silently, without user awareness, it raises an ethical question: should users be more conscious of how digital interfaces influence their choices? THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: Ultimately, UX is as embedded in modern life as road signs and transportation systems. Just as cities are designed to shape how people move through space, digital interfaces shape how people move through information, commerce, and communication. The difference is that while people recognize the structure of transportation systems, they rarely acknowledge UX for what it is. This lack of user awareness creates a conceptual gap. People recognize when they are learning a new skill like driving because they actively engage with the system’s mechanics. They understand traffic laws, the logic behind street signs, and the cause-and- effect relationships within the transportation infrastructure. But in the digital world, users are rarely encouraged to think critically about the mechanics of the systems they use every day. The design of a mobile app does not come with a “rulebook” explaining its intent, nor does a website explicitly state how its layout influences user decisions. As a result, users navigate these spaces with passive trust, rarely questioning the design choices that shape their behaviors. This knowledge gap is more than just an abstract concept, it has tangible consequences. When users are unaware of how design choices impact their decisions, they become more susceptible to influence. If UX is meant to facilitate smooth interactions, should that mean users never need to think about it? Or should there be a push for greater awareness, similar to how people are taught to understand road signs, safety features, and basic mechanics in transportation? This is not to suggest that every user needs to become a UX expert, just as not every driver needs to understand the engineering behind their car’s engine. Still, a fundamental level of awareness, knowing what UX is, recognizing its influence, and questioning its intent, could empower users to navigate digital spaces with more autonomy. The more users understand the mechanics of design, just as a driver comes to understand the mechanics of navigating the road, the more they can engage with digital spaces on their own terms rather than being unconsciously guided by them. Motive, Public Safety, and UX Design The idea that UX should operate invisibly has long been considered a strength. A well-designed interface guides users without friction, just as a well-planned city directs foot traffic without confusion. But the very success of this approach creates a vulnerability: when users do not recognize the mechanisms shaping their digital experiences they have little ability to question, critique, or push back against them. PART 03

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: This raises a critical question: When does UX guide and when does it manipulate? A road sign informs a driver, offering clear cues to support an independent decision. In contrast, a dark pattern, such as a misleading button or a hidden opt-out, exploits human psychology to drive a predetermined action. The difference lies in intent. Some designs empower users by providing information, while others steer them toward actions that primarily serve business goals. The internet, much like a city’s infrastructure, should be navigable with clarity and purpose. Unfortunately, too often users are led down digital paths they never intended to take. This issue becomes even more pressing as AI-driven interfaces grow more sophisticated. Unlike traditional UX, where designers manually shape experiences, AI-powered systems dynamically learn, adapt, and personalize interactions in ways that are even less transparent to users. Recommendation algorithms dictate what content we see, predictive search results influence how we think, and dynamic pricing adjusts costs based on perceived willingness to pay. These systems are not inherently harmful, but their opacity means that users often interact with them without realizing how they are being nudged, persuaded, or even manipulated. Without a fundamental awareness of how these systems operate, users are left to trust that digital experiences are designed in their best interest, an assumption that is not always justified. For UX to truly serve its purpose, it cannot remain an entirely silent force. Just as road signs equip drivers with the knowledge to navigate safely, users must be equipped with an understanding of UX to interact with digital spaces consciously. If people are unaware of how interfaces shape their decisions, then they cannot meaningfully engage with or challenge the systems that influence them. Raising awareness is not just an academic exercise, it is an essential step toward ensuring that UX remains a tool for empowerment rather than control. This belief is what led me to design an interactive card game, intended not only as an educational tool, but as a way to make the invisible visible. Through gameplay, players will dissect, discuss, and critically engage with design choices that shape their everyday digital interactions. By turning UX into something tangible and interactive, the game provides a structured but playful environment to explore how design decisions influence behavior, a necessary first step in making UX awareness as fundamental as reading street signs. THE SILENT FORCE OF UX DESIGN

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: The idea for the educational card game was born from a realization that many people remain unaware of the critical role UX design plays in their daily lives. As someone immersed in the field of UX, I found myself reflecting on how little the average person understands about the subtle ways in which design shapes their decision-making processes, emotions, and behaviors when interacting with digital products. This gap motivated me to develop a tool that could bridge this knowledge divide, bringing design principles to life for non-designers. This card game is an accessible, engaging way to make learning about design more approachable to a broader audience. It offers an interactive experience that introduces key design principles, elements, and UX laws in a way that is easy to grasp for people with no prior knowledge of the field. The game’s primary goal is to educate everyday users about how every element of digital interface design plays a role in guiding them through processes. By providing players with real-life examples and scenarios, the game seeks to demystify UX's "silent” influence on everyday decisions, helping users recognize and understand the impact design has on their digital interactions. From Case Study to Proposal Through user research and interviews, I uncovered a significant gap in user awareness regarding how UX design influences their decisions, emotions, and behaviors when interacting with digital interfaces. This gap led to the idea of an educational card game, which would aim to engage users in critically examining how UX elements subtly shape their digital experiences. The findings revealed several key insights. First, participants exhibited strong emotional reactions—such as frustration, joy, and confusion—when interacting with digital interfaces. These emotional responses highlighted how deeply psychological principles are embedded within UX design, shaping users’ emotional states without their awareness. There appeared to be a generational divide in how participants interacted with and reflected on technology. Based on visual observation, I inferred that younger participants (ages ranging from 20-30 years old) tended to feel more comfortable with frequent use of digital interfaces, while older participants (ages PART 03

author: Zoe Bartholot
thesis_title: The Silent Force Of Ux Design
paragraph: ranging from 40-50 years old) approached those devices and digital interfaces with more skepticism or used it primarily for purposes they might describe as “practical,” as opposed to for leisure or recreation. This divide suggests that age and experience strongly influence perceptions of digital products. Another critical finding was the distinction participants made between using such technology for work versus personal purposes. Many noted that context and intent played a significant role in shaping their expectations and behavior when interacting with digital tools. This research and the insights gained here led to the problem statement of this design proposal: While UX design subtly shapes digital interactions, there is a significant gap in users’ awareness of how UX influences their behavior and decision-making. This unconscious influence, combined with both generational and contextual differences in technology perception, highlights the need for greater awareness and critical engagement with UX principles. Through my user research and interviews, it became clear that participants often engage with digital products without fully recognizing how design choices guide their behavior. This unconscious engagement is the central problem that this project aims to address. To bridge this gap in understanding, I developed the concept of an educational card game.
